# Typography — FULL research corpus (recovered from transcripts)

> **What this is.** The complete per-agent deep-research behind the `typography` skill, extracted
> **verbatim** from the `StructuredOutput` of every research agent across the five typography research
> workflows. The skill had **no** saved research doc — this recovers it. **De-noised by construction**
> (only StructuredOutput content; no prompts, tool logs, rate-limit/retry/token text). Deterministic extract.

> **Scale:** 111 source extractions · 301 adversarial verifications · 31 synthesis records, ~521 agents / 5 workflows (incl. cognitive/mental-load).

---

## Part A — Source extractions (111)

### Type that reads easily & pairs with the restrained palette

#### Source `a0134067` — quality: **primary** · published: 2013-11-11
- **[central]** In adults with age-related macular degeneration, doubling both line spacing and word spacing increased reading speed by ~26% for high-contrast text and ~46% for low-contrast text (p<0.001) — controlled evidence that generous leading aids low-vision reading.
  > "Double line/word spacing increased speed by ~26% with high contrast (p<0.001); Double line/word spacing increased speed by ~46% with low contrast (p<0.001)"
- **[supporting]** Line spacing (leading), not word spacing, was the dominant driver of the reading-speed gain, with a line-spacing effect size roughly 15-20x larger than word spacing; the fastest speeds occurred at double line + double word spacing.
  > "Double line spacing showed significantly greater effect size than word spacing (15-20x larger). ... For both low and high contrast text, the fastest reading speeds we measured were for passages of text with double line and double word spacing."
- **[supporting]** Enhanced (double) line/word spacing more than halved reading errors relative to single-spaced text in macular-disease readers.
  > "Double line/word spacing "more than halved the number of reading errors obtained with single spaced text""
- **[supporting]** Increasing letter spacing (tracking) does not increase reading speed — a finding from prior controlled research the authors invoke, bounding the line-spacing benefit and contradicting the belief that wider letter-tracking improves reading.
  > "The study cites prior work finding that "increased letter spacing does not lead to an increase in reading speed," supported by multiple citations (references 38-42)."

#### Source `a07b05c6` — quality: **secondary** · published: 2022-05-10
- **[central]** Baymard recommends body-text lines of 80 characters or fewer for readability and accessibility, aligning their guidance with WCAG 1.4.8 rather than with a tighter optimum.
  > "WCAG guideline 1.4.8: "Lines of text should be 80 or fewer characters" ... Baymard's recommendation: "80 characters or fewer" to support readability and accessibility"
- **[central]** The commonly cited optimal measure of 50-60 characters per line is attributed to Swiss designer Emil Ruder (mid-20th-century 'Typographie'), i.e. it is a typographic convention, not a controlled finding.
  > "Emil Ruder (Swiss graphic designer): "The optimal line length for body text is 50–60 characters per line, including spaces""
- **[supporting]** Up to 75 characters per line is treated as an acceptable upper bound for body text, consistent with the ~45-75 character convention.
  > "Other sources: Up to 75 characters is acceptable"
- **[supporting]** This source frames over-long lines as harming reader engagement and willingness to read rather than presenting measured reading-speed or comprehension data, bounding the strength of the line-length claim.
  > "The article emphasizes that long lines negatively impact user willingness to read and likelihood to understand products, but doesn't present specific speed or comprehension test data."
- **[tangential]** Too-short lines are claimed to break reading rhythm by forcing the eye to return too frequently, a mechanism-based (not measured) rationale for a lower bound.
  > "Too-short lines: "The eye will have to travel back too often, breaking the reader's rhythm""

#### Source `a08f5c62` — quality: **blog** · published: 2009-05-07
- **[central]** The common design rule that line length should be constrained to ~45-75 characters is not supported by web reading research; readers handle significantly longer measures on screen than the print convention prescribes.
  > "But research doesn't support this claim, at least on the web. Instead, users are able to read significantly longer line lengths on the web"
- **[central]** A controlled Wichita State (SURL) study, 'The Effects of Line Length on Reading Online News' (2005), found on-screen reading speed was highest at 95 characters per line and lowest at 35 cpl — the opposite of the short-measure ideal.
  > "Reading speed was highest at 95cpl, and lowest at 35cpl on screen"
- **[central]** In the same study, line length had no measurable effect on reading comprehension on screen, separating the speed/preference effect from comprehension.
  > "They found that line length no impact on comprehension on screen"
- **[supporting]** The widely cited ~66-character ideal measure originates as a print typography convention (Bringhurst), not a screen-validated finding.
  > "The 66-character line (counting both letters and spaces) is widely regarded as ideal"
- **[tangential]** The author proposes a revised web 'standard' nearer ~100 characters per line rather than the traditional short measure, while conceding the exact value is uncertain.
  > "What is the new standard? Tough to say, but 100cpl seems to be within the range of feasibility"

#### Source `a168b0c9` — quality: **blog** · published: ?
- **[central]** [CONVENTION] Tabular figures (fixed-width numerals) are required for vertically aligned columns of numbers such as tables and grids — directly supporting tabular figures for data tables/dashboards. Source: Matthew Butterick.
  > "In body text, proportional figures are preferred because they tend to have more even spacing and a more consistent appearance. But tabular figures are essential for one purpose: vertically aligned columns."
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION] In running body text, proportional figures are preferred over tabular because their varying widths give more even spacing and a more consistent appearance. Source: Matthew Butterick.
  > "In body text, proportional figures are preferred because they tend to have more even spacing and a more consistent appearance."
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION] Lining figures are the safe default usable in any context and are specifically preferred in all-caps settings because they match cap height; old-style figures should not be used with caps. Source: Matthew Butterick.
  > "Lining figures can be used in any situation. Lining figures are always preferred for all caps text because they come closest to cap height."
- **[tangential]** [CONVENTION] Old-style (text) figures, with their ascending/descending forms mimicking lowercase, suit lowercase body text but look wrong combined with capital letters. Source: Matthew Butterick.
  > "With caps, however, you should not use old-style figures. They look wrong."

#### Source `a180efa6` — quality: **secondary** · published: ?
- **[central]** A line of 45 to 75 characters is widely regarded as a satisfactory measure for a single-column page of serifed text; this is presented as established typographic convention (Bringhurst), not as a controlled-research finding.
  > "Anything from 45 to 75 characters is widely regarded as a satisfactory length"
- **[central]** The 66-character line (counting letters and spaces) is held up as the ideal single-column measure — again as convention ('widely regarded'), not empirical proof.
  > "The 66-character line (counting both letters and spaces) is widely regarded as ideal"
- **[supporting]** For multiple-column layouts a shorter measure of roughly 40 to 50 characters is recommended as the better average.
  > "For multiple column work, a better average is 40 to 50 characters"
- **[supporting]** A concrete CSS/em rule of thumb: one character occupies about 0.5em, so a column set to 33em yields approximately 66 characters per line — giving a directly implementable formula for hitting the convention.
  > "One character takes up 0.5 em so this box will have a measure of 66 characters per line"

#### Source `a260839e` — quality: **primary** · published: 2025-10-01
- **[central]** WCAG 2.x SC 1.4.12 (Level AA) requires that content remain readable and functional when users override text spacing to: line height at least 1.5x font size, paragraph spacing at least 2x font size, letter spacing at least 0.12x font size, and word spacing at least 0.16x font size.
  > "Line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size ... Spacing following paragraphs to at least 2 times the font size ... Letter spacing (tracking) to at least 0.12 times the font size ... Word spacing to at least 0.16 times the font size"
- **[supporting]** The criterion does NOT require authors to use these spacing values; it only requires that content not break when a user overrides the authored spacing.
  > "Content is not required to use these text spacing values. The requirement is to ensure that when a user overrides the authored text spacing, content or functionality is not lost."
- **[central]** The numeric spacing thresholds are grounded in empirical reading-speed research (the McLeish study), not pure convention — letter-spacing tests ran 0.04 to 0.25 em with reading speed increasing up to ~0.25 em but flattening around 0.20 em.
  > "The metrics chosen as measures are based on the McLeish study. She ran from .04 to .25 em tests. McLeish found an increasing curve in reading speed of actual materials up to .25, but it started to flatten at .20."
- **[supporting]** The purpose of the criterion is to preserve readability/operability specifically when readers personalize spacing for their own reading needs (e.g., low vision, dyslexia).
  > "when people override author-specified text spacing to improve their reading experience, content is still readable and operable"

#### Source `a2d927ef` — quality: **blog** · published: ?
- **[central]** [CONVENTION — Matthew Butterick] Optimal line spacing (leading/line-height) for most body text is 120%–145% of the point size.
  > "For most text, the optimal line spacing is between 120% and 145% of the point size."
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION — Matthew Butterick] Single-spacing is too tight and double-spacing is too loose for comfortable reading; both fall outside the optimal range, a habit inherited from typewriters.
  > "single-spacing is "dense and hard to read" while double-spacing "is still looser than optimal.""
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION — Matthew Butterick] Because different fonts at the same point size appear different visual sizes, a font that looks small on the page should be set with less line spacing.
  > "Different fonts set at the same point size may not appear the same size on the page"
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION — Matthew Butterick] In CSS, line spacing should be set with the line-height property, preferably as a unitless multiplier.
  > "CSS: Use the `line-height` property preferably without units"

#### Source `a474464d` — quality: **primary** · published: 2019 (typeface release year; no explicit page publish date)
- **[supporting]** Soehne is a contemporary lineal grotesque sans (released 2019) explicitly designed as a reinterpretation of Akzidenz-Grotesk and Helvetica, anchoring it as a 'fresh-but-familiar' modern UI/body sans in the same lineage as the workhorse grotesques.
  > "Soehne is the memory of Akzidenz-Grotesk framed through the reality of Helvetica."
- **[central]** Soehne ships tabular (monospaced-width) numerals via the tnum OpenType feature, making it suitable for aligned data displays, tables, and dashboards.
  > "Numerals: Includes tabular numerals (`tnum` feature) suitable for aligned data displays"
- **[supporting]** Soehne includes manual VTT hinting and optimized WOFF2 web files (34-39kb per style), i.e. the foundry treats screen rendering and web-font payload as explicit design concerns.
  > "Hinting: Manual VTT hinting for screen rendering ... Optimized WOFF2 web formats ranging from 34-39kb per style."
- **[tangential]** Soehne is in production use on major interface/digital products including Stripe, evidencing practitioner acceptance as a UI typeface (a convention/usage signal, not controlled legibility evidence).
  > "Documented implementations include Stripe, Exit Strategy, and other digital/interface projects, suggesting established suitability for contemporary web and UI applications."

#### Source `a48f5f5a` — quality: **primary** · published: 2013 (published by Five Simple Steps; PDF metadata creation date 2013-04-23; later mirrored on blog.typekit.com in 2016)
- **[central]** [CONVENTION — Tim Brown] The common typeface-pairing strategies are: stay within one family, use a sans+serif superfamily, limit to one historical period, or use one designer's faces — but Brown argues these only make 'adequate' pairings easy and don't explain why a combination actually works. This is a named-authority caution against treating pairing rules as proof of success.
  > "stick with a single type family (making use of its various weights, widths and features); use a superfamily that includes sans and serif siblings; limit yourself to a specific historical period; use typefaces from the same type designer. That's all fine advice because it makes adequate combinations easy to identify. But it also robs us of the opportunity to truly understand why a combination works or doesn't, and can lead to a false sense of completion."
- **[central]** [CONVENTION — Tim Brown] In a multi-typeface system the body/text typeface should be the 'anchor', because text faces are engineered to survive varied settings whereas display faces fail at small sizes and on coarse/low-resolution screens. This is a concrete, falsifiable hierarchy rule for choosing the primary UI/body face.
  > "Make your body text typeface the anchor if possible, because this text represents a majority of your content—and because text faces are built to withstand a variety of settings, whereas display faces can rarely be used at small sizes or at coarse resolutions."
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION — Tim Brown] Typefaces should be paired on shared underlying proportions (x-height, extender length, character width, aperture, stroke contrast), not on surface similarity — 'compatibility and similarity are not the same thing.' If structure/proportion match, shapes and features may safely differ.
  > "If you can identify typefaces that share common structures and spatial divisions, then many other aspects about the letterforms, such as their shapes and features, can differ because they are deeply related by proportion."
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION — Tim Brown] Type is job-specific: text faces 'knit multiline text into a smooth block of words', display faces use fine detail that is lost or harmful at small sizes, and optical/'Reading Edge' cuts (e.g., Garamond Premier Display/Subhead/Caption; Antenna RE for coarse low-resolution screens) exist to address size and screen-resolution constraints. Supports choosing different cuts for body vs display vs small/dense data UI.
  > "Text faces are designed to knit multiline text into a smooth block of words that can be read comfortably. Font Bureau has even made "Reading Edge" versions of some of its typefaces—like Antenna RE, meant for use at text sizes on coarse, low-resolution screens"
- **[tangential]** [CONVENTION — Tim Brown, 2013] Specific typefaces Brown names as excellent for body/long-form text include Kepler, Rooney, JAF Bernino Sans, and FF Tisa; FF Meta Serif is cited as a comfortable reading anchor. These are concrete named recommendations, though dated to the 2013 Typekit library.
  > "Kepler, Rooney and JAF Bernino Sans (from left to right) are excellent body text typefaces."

#### Source `a52a44fd` — quality: **primary** · published: 2022 (ACM TOCHI, DOI 10.1145/3502222; pre-print citation dated January 2021)
- **[central]** In a perceptually size-normalized study of 16 fonts, each individual's reading speed increased ~35% comparing their fastest vs. slowest font, with no loss of reading comprehension — i.e., 'one font does not fit all' (individuated readability). This is a within-individual best-vs-worst gap, not a universal font ranking.
  > "Participants' reading speeds (measured in WPM) increased by 35% when comparing fastest and slowest fonts without affecting reading comprehension. High WPM variability across fonts suggests that one font does not [fit] all."
- **[central]** Font PREFERENCE does not predict reading SPEED, even when font size is controlled — preference and effectiveness are decoupled. Directly supports labeling 'this font looks/feels better' as taste, not proven performance.
  > "Our results show that no single factor predicts reading speed, and interestingly, preference does not predict speed even when font size is controlled. Our main takeaway is that different fonts work best for different people."
- **[central]** There is NO set of consistently faster (or slower) fonts across the population as a whole — the font effect emerges per individual, not as a universal ranking. (In the mixed-effects model the population-level font effect was non-significant for younger readers and only weakly significant for older readers, R2_beta=.005.) This bounds and partly disconfirms any 'best font for everyone' or simple serif-vs-sans speed claim.
  > "indicating that for these factors, reading speed did not trend in a consistent direction across the population. Regarding fonts, this means that there is not a set of consistently faster fonts (conversely, slower) across the population as a whole."
- **[supporting]** Even named 'fastest' fonts win only marginally above chance across people: Open Sans was the slowest font on average (254 WPM) yet individuals read faster in it 51% of the time; EB Garamond and Open Sans were the best/worst performers among older readers. Concrete evidence that single-font recommendations are weak at the population level.
  > "Open Sans was the slowest font on average at 254 WPM, while individual participants read faster in it 51% of the time."
- **[supporting]** Participants read ~14% faster in their objectively fastest font (314 WPM average) than in their self-selected most-preferred font — quantifying the speed cost of choosing type by preference rather than measured performance.
  > "Participants read 14% faster in their fastest font (314 WPM, on average) compared to their most preferred"

#### Source `a537670c` — quality: **primary** · published: 2018 (Annals of Dyslexia, vol. 68; published online December 2017)
- **[central]** In a controlled study of 170 children with dyslexia, the Dyslexie font produced no reading-speed or accuracy benefit over standard Arial — directly disconfirming the marketing claim that dyslexia-specific fonts ease reading.
  > "children with dyslexia (n = 170) did not read text written in Dyslexie font faster or more accurately than in Arial font"
- **[central]** The study's overall conclusion is that the Dyslexie font is reading-neutral: it gives no measurable benefit but also does no harm, for readers both with and without dyslexia.
  > "the Dyslexie font neither benefits nor impedes the reading process of children with and without dyslexia"
- **[supporting]** Any apparent advantage of the Dyslexie font is attributable not to its distinctive letterforms but to increased spacing — meaning the special letter shapes are not the active ingredient, and ordinary spacing/measure adjustments would do the same.
  > "if the font Dyslexie aids reading, it is not because of its shape but rather because of the increase in word spacing"
- **[supporting]** Reader preference ran toward conventional fonts, not the dyslexia-specific one, and preference did not predict reading performance.
  > "The majority preferred reading in Arial and preference was not related to reading performance"
- **[supporting]** At the isolated-word level across three fonts (Dyslexie, Arial, Times New Roman) in 102 children with and 45 without dyslexia, Dyslexie again showed no speed or accuracy advantage.
  > "Words written in Dyslexie font were not read faster or more accurately. Moreover, participants showed a preference for the fonts Arial and Times New Roman rather than Dyslexie"

#### Source `a6761d1f` — quality: **primary** · published: 2022-11
- **[central]** Under matched conditions (equal size, spacing, layout, word/syllable/character count), Times New Roman (serif) and Helvetica (sans-serif) produced NO statistically significant difference in reading time (p=0.50) or reading speed (p=0.56) — direct disconfirmation of the 'serif reads faster / sans reads faster' belief. Bound: stimuli were PRINTED (paperback/newspaper), not on-screen, so this constrains rather than proves the screen-specific case.
  > "For the paperback layout, no significant difference in reading time (p = 0.50) or reading speed (p = 0.56) was found between the two fonts. The correlation between the two fonts was high for both reading time and speed (r = 0.93)."
- **[central]** The authors explicitly conclude serif vs sans-serif legibility is equivalent and the two are interchangeable, supporting a [MYTH] tag on any blanket 'serif vs sans reads better' claim.
  > "The legibility of Helvetica and Times New Roman is similar when investigated under equivalent conditions. Thus, these two font types can be used as interchangeable standard typefaces."
- **[supporting]** Reading accuracy was identical between the serif and sans-serif fonts: mean 0.31 ± 0.58 errors per text for both, reinforcing that the serif/sans distinction did not affect comprehension-adjacent error rate.
  > "The mean number of reading errors was the same (0.31 ± 0.58 errors/text) for both fonts."
- **[supporting]** Layout (newspaper vs paperback format) DID produce a significant difference in reading time and speed, even though font family did not — evidence that line-length/measure and layout can dominate over typeface choice. Relevant to the measure/leading topic.
  > "There was a significant difference in reading time and speed between the paperback and the newspaper layout."
- **[supporting]** Evidence-strength bound: the finding rests on a controlled within-subject Latin-square design with only 36 young (18-38) best-corrected-acuity participants reading PRINTED paragraphs — modest n, narrow age band, and print medium limit generalization to older readers, low vision, and screen UI.
  > "The paragraphs were presented in random order (Latin square design) to 36 participants between 18 and 38 years of age (wearing their best-corrected visual acuity). Reading duration was measured with a stopwatch."

#### Source `a68ddd32` — quality: **primary** · published: 2005-11
- **[central]** Controlled legibility testing found no difference in legibility between typefaces that differ only in the presence or absence of serifs, directly disconfirming the popular belief that serifs aid reading. [MYTH]
  > "our data exhibited no difference in legibility between typefaces that differ only in the presence or absence of serifs."
- **[central]** Reading speed (both RSVP and continuous reading) showed no effect of serifs, so serif vs sans-serif does not measurably change how fast text is read.
  > "RSVP and continuous reading speeds showed no effect of serifs."
- **[supporting]** Any small legibility advantage of serif fonts is attributable to the increased inter-letter spacing serifs impose, not to the serifs themselves; serifs may even slightly interfere with legibility.
  > "serifs may actually interfere ever so slightly with legibility. This reduction is more than offset by an enhancement of legibility caused by the increased spacing."
- **[central]** At typical print sizes under normal viewing conditions, the presence or absence of serifs has no significant effect on legibility; any benefit only appears for very small or distant text and is tiny.
  > "When text is small or distant, serifs may...produce a tiny legibility increase due to the concomitant increase in spacing. However, it is unlikely to be of significance at typical print sizes viewed under normal conditions."

#### Source `a9338c28` — quality: **blog** · published: ?
- **[central]** Butterick names the specific professional typefaces he recommends for body/UI/professional documents: Equity, Valkyrie, Century Supra, Concourse, Hermes Maia, Heliotrope, Advocate, and (monospaced) Triplicate — these are his own commercial families, plus Charter as a quality option.
  > "Equity ... Valkyrie ... Century Supra ... Concourse ... Hermes Maia ... Heliotrope ... Triplicate ... Advocate"
- **[central]** Butterick holds that switching to a professional (paid) font is the single highest-leverage typography upgrade, and that for writers who care about typography professional fonts are essential rather than optional.
  > "If you want the fastest, easiest and most obvious upgrade to your typography, nothing beats a professional font."
- **[supporting]** Butterick bounds the importance of font choice: picking a good font is only one element of typography and does not substitute for mastering composition and text formatting — disconfirming any belief that font selection alone produces good typography.
  > "messing around with the font menu on your computer isn't a substitute for knowing the fundamentals of type composition and text formatting"
- **[supporting]** Butterick concedes system fonts are the right choice in specific contexts (emails, draft documents), so his pro-professional-font stance is situational, not absolute.
  > "there are situations, like emails and draft documents, where system fonts are your best option"
- **[tangential]** Triplicate is the monospaced family Butterick recommends (relevant to type for code/data), and he distinguishes monospaced from his text faces by naming it separately.
  > "Triplicate"

#### Source `aa3459b5` — quality: **secondary** · published: 2019-12-01 (Vision Research, vol. 165; available online May 2019)
- **[supporting]** PROVENANCE CORRECTION: This source is a single-author REVIEW/survey article by Charles Bigelow (the 'Bigelow' of Bigelow & Holmes, a named authority in the research question), published in Vision Research, Dec 2019 (PubMed 31078662, DOI 10.1016/j.visres.2019.05.003) — NOT a Beier/Bernard/Castet controlled study as the task metadata stated. It is a historiographic survey, so its surveyed findings are authority-framed synthesis to be traced to the underlying primary studies, not [STRONG] controlled data in themselves.
  > "For most of the 20th century, legibility studies were aimed at determining legible typefaces and type sizes to aid publishing, education, and other areas of reading."
- **[supporting]** Twentieth-century legibility research systematically tested a defined set of traditional type variables — case (capitals vs lowercase), style (roman vs italic), weight (light vs bold), width, and size. These are the features with the longest empirical research tradition; notably serif-vs-sans, x-height, and letter-spacing/tracking are NOT in this classic experimental list, so claims about them rest on a narrower/newer evidence base.
  > "Experimental font variables were usually features familiar in traditional typographic fonts, such as capitals versus lowercase, roman versus italic, light versus bold weight, width, and size."
- **[supporting]** Modern legibility research quantifies letterform/character features objectively using image-processing metrics such as spatial frequency and perimetric complexity — i.e., legibility-relevant properties of a typeface can be measured from the forms rather than only judged subjectively, supporting an evidence-based (not taste-based) approach to type selection.
  > "the use of imaging processing methods to investigate font and character features like spatial frequency and perimetric complexity"
- **[tangential]** Specialized experimental fonts have been designed and tested specifically for low-vision readers, distinct from fonts for normal vision — establishing low-vision legibility as its own research area (relevant to the skill's accessibility-of-type topic, separate from normal-vision UI/body legibility).
  > "the creation of experimental fonts for low vision as well as normal vision, a closer rapprochement of legibility study with other fields of vision research"

#### Source `ab4462f6` — quality: **primary** · published: 2020
- **[central]** Wider letter-spacing (here +2.5 pt added to Times New Roman 12pt) speeds single-word processing: it reduced first fixation durations, gaze durations, and total fixation time for all readers during silent reading.
  > "Wider spacing reduced first fixation durations, gaze durations, and total fixation time for all participants."
- **[central]** Faster single-word processing does NOT translate into faster reading; wider spacing gave no words-per-minute benefit and actually slowed readers (average ~9.45 wpm reduction overall) because more words had to be fixated.
  > "In terms of words read per minute, wider letter spacing did not provide a benefit, and faster readers in particular were slowed down."
- **[central]** The cost of wider letter-spacing scales with reading skill: individual letter-spacing effect correlated negatively with reading speed (r = -0.62), and a 347-wpm reader lost about 27 wpm under wider spacing.
  > "Individual differences in letter spacing effect correlated negatively with individual wpm values"
- **[supporting]** Wider spacing changed eye-movement behavior by reducing word-skipping and increasing the number of fixations, especially for faster readers, explaining why net reading rate dropped despite shorter individual fixations.
  > "wider letter spacing also reduced skipping probabilities and elicited more fixations, especially for faster readers"
- **[supporting]** Study is small and narrow: 24 German university students reading newspaper texts in Times New Roman 12pt via 1000 Hz eye-tracking, bounding generalization of the letter-spacing findings.
  > "Participants: 24 German university students (mean age 21.29; 19 female)"

#### Source `ab8f155f` — quality: **primary** · published: 2001
- **[central]** In a controlled study, three screen line lengths were tested (25, 55, and 100 characters per line); 55 CPL produced significantly better comprehension scores than the longest line length of 100 CPL, giving controlled evidence that overly long measures hurt reading.
  > "line length influences readers' comprehension with documents at 55 characters per line producing better comprehension scores than the longest line length (100 characters per line)"
- **[central]** A medium line length of ~55 characters per line supported the most effective reading across both normal and fast reading speeds, grounding the conventional ~45–75 (or '66') character measure rule in controlled comprehension data rather than taste.
  > "a medium line length (55 characters per line) appears to support effective reading at normal and fast speeds"
- **[supporting]** Medium line length (~55 CPL, about 4 inches) facilitated more effective reading at normal speed than short lines (~25 CPL), indicating very short measures also impede reading — the optimum is a middle band, not 'shorter is always better'.
  > "Medium line lengths (55 CPL, which is approximately 4-inches) facilitated more effective reading at normal reading speed than shorter line lengths (24 CPL)."
- **[supporting]** Reading at a fast speed reduced overall comprehension, but the type of information recalled did not depend on speed — bounding the line-length effect within a separate, independent speed effect.
  > "a reduction in overall comprehension was found when reading fast, but the type of information recalled was not dependent on speed"

#### Source `ab8fecd7` — quality: **secondary** · published: 2023-11-01
- **[central]** WOFF2 has the best compression of any web-font format (up to 30% better than WOFF), enjoys wide browser support, and is often the only format needed for full compatibility across modern browsers; older formats (WOFF, EOT, TTF) are only required for legacy browsers.
  > "WOFF2 enjoys wide browser support and the best compression—up to 30% better than WOFF ... The WOFF2 format is often the only one needed for full compatibility across modern browsers"
- **[central]** The font-display values produce distinct FOIT/FOUT outcomes: block blocks rendering up to ~3 seconds in Chromium/Firefox (indefinitely in Safari); swap shows fallback text immediately then swaps in the web font; fallback blocks for a very short period; optional only uses the web font if it downloads within 100 milliseconds.
  > "Chromium and Firefox block rendering for up to a maximum of 3 seconds before using a fallback. Safari blocks indefinitely ... swap does not block rendering, and shows the text immediately in a fallback before swapping in the specified web font ... optional ... only uses the web font resource if it downloads within 100 milliseconds"
- **[supporting]** Self-hosting web fonts is in most cases faster than loading them from a cross-origin third party; if a third party is used, a preconnect resource hint mitigates the overhead.
  > "In most cases, self-hosting web fonts is faster than downloading them from a cross-origin"
- **[supporting]** Preloading fonts makes them discoverable early in page load, but overuse can divert bandwidth from other critical resources, and a crossorigin attribute must be specified when preloading fonts.
  > "preload directive makes web fonts discoverable early during page load ... Overuse of the preload directive may divert bandwidth from other critical resources ... you must specify the crossorigin attribute when preloading fonts"
- **[tangential]** swap is the most widely used font-display value on the web (per HTTP Archive 2022 data).
  > "swap is the most widely used font-display value"

#### Source `ab90c237` — quality: **primary** · published: 2024-11-16
- **[central]** Inter is a sans-serif typeface designed specifically for computer screens, with a deliberately tall x-height intended to aid readability of mixed-case and lower-case text. This is the designer's stated design intent (Rasmus Andersson), not controlled evidence — supports a [CONVENTION] claim that Inter is a screen/UI-optimized readable typeface.
  > "Inter is a typeface carefully crafted & designed for computer screens ... Inter features a tall x-height to aid in readability of mixed-case and lower-case text"
- **[central]** Inter is a variable font that ships OpenType features purpose-built for data UIs, including tabular numbers and a slashed zero to disambiguate '0' from 'o', plus contextual alternates. This directly grounds the data-typography topic (tabular figures, disambiguated numerals) and the variable-font topic.
  > "Inter is a variable font with several OpenType features, like contextual alternates that adjusts punctuation depending on the shape of surrounding glyphs, slashed zero for when you need to disambiguate '0' from 'o', tabular numbers, etc."
- **[supporting]** Inter includes small-scale glyph optimizations ('pits' and 'traps') intended to improve rasterization and legibility at small sizes — relevant to type for dense data UIs and axis labels at small sizes.
  > "small-scale glyph optimizations (like 'pits' and 'traps') that help rasterization"
- **[supporting]** Inter is classified as a geometric neo-grotesque, placing it in the same stylistic family as Roboto, Apple's San Francisco, Akkurat, Asap, and Lucida Grande — useful context for typeface-selection/pairing as a 'fresh and modern' but conventional UI sans.
  > "Inter can be classified as a geometric neo-grotesque, similar in style to Roboto, Apple San Francisco, Akkurat, Asap, Lucida Grande"
- **[tangential]** Inter is distributed both as a variable font and as discrete static font files spanning nine weights (Thin through Black) with matching italics; the current release is Version 4.1 (Nov 16, 2024).
  > "Inter is a variable font ... distributed both as a variable font and as a set of traditional distinct font files"

#### Source `abac5e4a` — quality: **primary** · published: 2007-08-06
- **[central]** Contrary to the conventional belief that ALL-CAPS reads worse, upper-case text was found MORE legible than mixed-case or lower-case — especially for low-vision readers — because it tolerates smaller letter sizes with no loss of legibility. This bounds/qualifies the popular [MYTH] that uppercase is simply less legible; the disadvantage is size-economy-driven, not absolute.
  > "Upper-case is more legible than the other case styles, especially for visually-impaired readers, because smaller letter sizes can be used than with the other case styles, with no diminution of legibility."
- **[central]** The uppercase legibility/reading-speed advantage appears only at small text sizes (low acuity reserve) and disappears once text is large enough — so at comfortable body-text sizes there is no uppercase reading-speed benefit. This is the key bound: case effects are size-dependent.
  > "the upper-case advantage disappears, when text is large enough"
- **[supporting]** Lower-case acuity thresholds were roughly 0.1 log unit higher than upper-case, quantifying the x-height/size economy of uppercase letters at threshold legibility.
  > "Lower-case thresholds were roughly 0.1 log unit higher than upper-"
- **[tangential]** A word superiority effect was present (words identified more accurately than random letter strings), but it did not override the raw size benefit of uppercase letters — i.e., word-shape recognition alone does not make mixed-case more legible at small sizes.
  > "words were identified more accurately than random letter strings"

#### Source `ad3ae80f` — quality: **primary** · published: 2017 (Annals of Dyslexia, vol. 67; published online 2016)
- **[central]** The specialized dyslexia font OpenDyslexic produced no improvement in reading rate or accuracy for students with dyslexia, neither for individuals nor the group as a whole, compared to Arial and Times New Roman.
  > "Results from this alternating treatment design show no improvement in reading rate or accuracy for individual students with dyslexia, as well as the group as a whole."
- **[central]** OpenDyslexic actually performed worse than standard fonts (Arial, Times New Roman) across all three reading tasks, with negative effect sizes (-49.65% to -88.65% for fluency, -53.89% to -75.81% for accuracy).
  > "effect sizes ranging from -49.65% to -88.65% for fluency measures and -53.89% to -75.81% for accuracy, indicating OD performed worse than standard fonts across all three reading tasks"
- **[supporting]** No participant preferred reading material in OpenDyslexic, undercutting the preference rationale for the font; some only noted it was 'new' or 'different.'
  > "While some students commented that the font was 'new' or 'different', none of the participants reported preferring to read material presented in that font."
- **[central]** The authors found no evidence of any positive effect of OpenDyslexic and warn that adopting such unproven interventions diverts resources from evidence-based practices.
  > "Based on visual and statistical analysis, there is 'no evidence' of OD having a positive effect on reading speed or accuracy"
- **[supporting]** The study was a controlled alternating-treatment-design experiment with 12 elementary students (grades 3-6) carrying confirmed dyslexia diagnoses, comparing OpenDyslexic against Arial and Times New Roman.
  > "12 elementary students (grades 3-6) with confirmed dyslexia diagnoses ... Fonts Compared: OpenDyslexic (OD), Arial, and Times New Roman (TNR)"

#### Source `ad3ff620` — quality: **primary** · published: 2026-06-23
- **[central]** Carbon's type scale is NOT a fixed geometric/modular ratio but a custom incremental equation Xn = Xn-1 + {INT[(n-2)/4] + 1} * 2, seeded at y0 = 12px — a counterexample to the assumption that a 'modular scale' must be a constant ratio. This grounds topic 3 (is a strict ratio scale evidence-based or taste?): a major design system deliberately uses a non-ratio scale.
  > "The IBM type scale is built on a single equation. The formula for our scale was created to provide hierarchy for all types of experiences. The formula assumes that y₀=12 px. ... Xn = Xn-1 + {INT[(n-2)/4] + 1} * 2"
- **[central]** Carbon builds its entire UI typography on IBM Plex as a superfamily (Sans, Serif, Mono) with explicit fallback stacks (e.g. 'IBM Plex Sans', 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif), naming a specific typeface experts/institutions treat as both modern and readable for product UI — directly relevant to topic 4 (choosing/pairing typefaces, superfamilies) and topic 6 (font stacks).
  > "Carbon uses the open-source typeface IBM Plex. It has been carefully designed to meet IBM's needs as a global technology company... font-family: 'IBM Plex Sans', 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif;"
- **[supporting]** Carbon recommends only three weights for digital UI — Light (300), Regular (400), SemiBold (600) — and explicitly bars SemiBold from long text, reserving it for section headers. Bears on topic 1 (weight effects) and topic 3 (hierarchy by weight).
  > "We suggest using IBM Plex Light, Regular, and SemiBold for digital experiences. The semibold weight is ideal for section headers, but should not be used for long text."
- **[supporting]** Hierarchy via weight is not absolute: a lighter weight can outrank a bolder one if it is sufficiently larger, i.e. size can override weight in the hierarchy. Concrete convention for topic 3 (hierarchy by size/weight/space/colour).
  > "A bold weight will always have more emphasis than a lighter weight font of the same size. However, a lighter weight font can rank hierarchically higher than a bold font if the lighter weight type size is significantly larger than the bold one."
- **[supporting]** Carbon keeps running text in a neutral type color and reserves one accent (primary blue) for primary actions and links — a restraint convention that composes cleanly with the existing 'one reserved accent / muted ground' colour system the skill must pair with.
  > "Keep type color neutral in running text. Use primary blue for primary actions. Core blue colors are used for text links and primary actions"

#### Source `ae38e4f7` — quality: **blog** · published: 2022-09-12
- **[central]** For data visualizations, use tabular figures (every digit the same width) rather than proportional figures, because equal-width digits let readers compare magnitudes in tables, tooltips, and axis ticks.
  > "Tabular figures are, as the name implies, great for tables...every number is the same width. ... For most of your data visualizations, use tabular figures."
- **[central]** For data visualizations, use fonts with lining figures (all digits the same height) rather than oldstyle figures, because oldstyle digits that rise above and drop below the line are hard to read in a table, tooltip, or as an axis tick.
  > "Lining numbers 'line up': They're all the same height. Oldstyle numbers go below and above the 'line.' ... For your data visualizations, use fonts with lining figures."
- **[central]** Sans-serif typefaces are most often the better choice for data visualization (over serif), being cleaner and easier to skim, especially for numbers; recommended readable defaults include Roboto, Lato, Open Sans, Source Sans Pro, and Noto Sans, with serif reserved for titles/accent use.
  > "For data visualizations, sans-serif ('without serifs') typefaces are most often the better choice. ... They look cleaner and are often easier to skim than serif fonts, especially when it comes to numbers."
- **[supporting]** Type below roughly 12px tends to be too small for charts, though there is no universal minimum because legibility depends on font family, weight, case, letter-spacing and color; Datawrapper defaults to 14px Roboto for text annotations.
  > "Depending on the font family, everything below 12px will likely be too small."
- **[supporting]** Bold weight should be reserved for emphasis/titles only, and very thin/light weights are hard to read because delicate strokes look like a lighter color; multiplexed fonts (same character width across weights, e.g. Recursive) preserve table alignment when bolding.
  > "Use bold fonts only for emphasis. ... Thin or light font weight has such delicate strokes that it looks as if it's set in a brighter color."

#### Source `af4b0e07` — quality: **primary** · published: ?
- **[central]** Material Design 3's type scale is structured as 5 roles (display, headline, title, body, label) each available in 3 sizes (large, medium, small), yielding a 15-style type ramp built to create page hierarchy across devices.
  > "display, headline, title, label, body. Each role has three sizes: large, medium, and small."
- **[central]** Material 3 recommends a line-height ratio of 1.2x the type size for display/headline/title styles and roughly 1.5x the type size for body/label styles.
  > "For display/headline/title: "a line height ratio of 1.2 times the type size" ... For body/label: "a line height ratio around 1.5 times the type size""
- **[supporting]** Material 3 prescribes hierarchy by role and emphasis, not arbitrary sizing: display is reserved for short, high-importance text/numerals on large screens, while body styles are for longer passages.
  > "Display: "reserved for short, important text or numerals. They work best on large screens." ... Body: "used for longer passages of text in your app.""
- **[supporting]** Material 3 explicitly distinguishes body typefaces (for readability at small sizes and long passages) from display typefaces (for short emphasis), reinforcing a body-vs-display typeface-selection convention.
  > "typefaces intended for body styles, which are readable at smaller sizes and can be comfortably read in longer passages."
- **[tangential]** Material 3 sets text contrast targets of 3:1 for large text and 4.5:1 for small text (matching WCAG AA), though luminance/contrast is deferred to the colour skill.
  > "Material targets "3:1 for large text" and "4.5:1 for small text""

#### Source `afe54ebf` — quality: **primary** · published: ?
- **[central]** ONS sets a minimum chart text size of 14px for legibility, allowing 12px only in small-multiple charts. [CONVENTION — ONS Service Manual]
  > "The minimum font size for chart text elements is 14 pixels (px) for legibility. In small multiple charts, you can go down to 12px if necessary."
- **[supporting]** ONS specifies a sans-serif stack (Open Sans, Helvetica, Arial) with OpenType stylistic set 1 (ss01) enabled specifically for glyph-level accessibility — an opentail lowercase 'g' and a serifed capital 'I' to aid disambiguation. [CONVENTION — ONS Service Manual]
  > "We use stylistic set 1 because it includes the more accessible opentail lowercase 'g', and serifed capital 'I'."
- **[supporting]** ONS requires all chart text in sentence case and running horizontally, avoiding diagonal or vertical text (reducing axis increments instead). [CONVENTION — ONS Service Manual]
  > "All text in charts should use sentence case and run horizontally"
- **[supporting]** ONS sets line-height for chart text at 1.2x to 1.3x the font size depending on text type, and uses a graded type ramp (e.g. titles 700/20px, subtitles 600/16px, axis labels 400/14px) to build hierarchy via size, weight and grey color rather than size alone. [CONVENTION — ONS Service Manual]
  > "Line height: 1.2x to 1.3x font size depending on text type"
- **[tangential]** ONS sets default text color to off-black #222222 rather than pure black #000000, with secondary text in named greys (#707071, #414042) — a restraint that composes with muted/off-white data-viz palettes. [CONVENTION — ONS Service Manual]
  > "The default font colour (primarily for titles and source) is Black #222222. Ensure this is used, and not full black #000000."

### Typography gaps — legibility, measure, scale, pairing, typeface choice, numerals

#### Source `a1c5f105` — quality: **blog** · published: 2020-01-12 (updated 2022-06-28)
- **[central]** Browser HTTP cache partitioning (double-keyed/per-domain cache) implemented by Safari and Chrome eliminated the old shared-CDN cache benefit, so a font loaded from a public CDN on one site is no longer reused on another site. [CONVENTION/MYTH-buster]
  > "Safari has a unique HTTP cache per domain visited...for privacy reasons (called a double-keyed cache) and Chrome soon will have too"
- **[central]** Self-hosting fonts is measurably faster than loading from the Google Fonts CDN because the CDN forces extra third-party connections (fonts.googleapis.com then fonts.gstatic.com); the Web Almanac test showed page load drop from ~6s to ~3s and Start Render from 6s to 2.5s. [CONVENTION, with measurement]
  > "yes it's better to self-host as the performance gains are substantial"
- **[supporting]** Google Fonts' CSS is render-blocking and the CDN adds roughly 3 seconds of latency before font download even begins (≈1s to connect to fonts.googleapis.com, ≈0.5s CSS download/parse, ≈1.25s to connect to fonts.gstatic.com) on a slow-3G/Chrome test.
  > "you have to connect to `fonts.googleapis.com`, download the CSS, then connect to `fonts.gstatic.com` to download the actual fonts"
- **[supporting]** Self-hosting without re-implementing Google's optimizations costs bytes: naive self-hosting can make font files up to ~74% larger (both latin and latin-ext in one file, hinting, multiple formats), e.g. Lato roughly doubles (~20KB to ~40KB) when Windows hints are kept. Proper self-hosting must add font-display:swap, unicode-range subsetting, and WOFF2-only.
  > "However, Google Fonts is not just a repository...it is also a clever delivery mechanism...In moving to self-hosting you ideally want to re-implement as many of those improvements"
- **[supporting]** Adding <link rel=preconnect href=https://fonts.gstatic.com crossorigin> saves over 1 second (fonts start downloading at ~4s instead of ~5.25s); preload of Google-hosted fonts is unreliable because Google uses hashed filenames that can change.
  > "Adding `<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>` saved >1 second"

#### Source `a1e75a70` — quality: **primary** · published: 2023
- **[central]** The BDA recommends common sans-serif fonts (Arial, Comic Sans, Verdana, Tahoma, Century Gothic, Trebuchet, Calibri, Open Sans) for dyslexic readers — and nowhere in the guide does it recommend or even mention any specially-designed 'dyslexia font' (OpenDyslexic, Dyslexie). This is disconfirming evidence against the 'you need a special dyslexia typeface' belief: the leading dyslexia authority's own advice is plain sans-serif.
  > "Use sans serif fonts, such as Arial and Comic Sans, as letters can appear less crowded. Alternatives include Verdana, Tahoma, Century Gothic, Trebuchet, Calibri, Open Sans."
- **[central]** BDA's recommended body text size is 12-14 point, which it equates to 1-1.2em / 16-19px — placing the common 16px web convention at the bottom of the recommended range, with some readers needing larger.
  > "Font size should be 12-14 point or equivalent (e.g. 1-1.2em / 16-19 px). Some dyslexic readers may request a larger font."
- **[central]** BDA gives concrete spacing targets: inter-letter (tracking) ~35% of average letter width (with a caution that excessive spacing reduces readability), inter-word spacing at least 3.5x the inter-letter spacing, and line spacing of 1.5 / 150%. These align directionally with WCAG 1.4.12 Text Spacing (line-height ≥1.5x, letter-spacing ≥0.12em, word-spacing ≥0.16em).
  > "Larger inter-letter / character spacing (sometimes called tracking) improves readability, ideally around 35% of the average letter width. If letter spacing is excessive it can reduce readability. Inter-word spacing should be at least 3.5 times the inter-letter spacing. Some dyslexic people find that larger line spacing improves readability. It should be proportional to inter-word spacing; 1.5 / 150% is preferable."
- **[supporting]** BDA recommends left-aligned, ragged-right text and explicitly advises against justification, on the grounds that even word spacing and locatable line ends aid reading — directly supporting the left-aligned-over-justified accessibility guidance.
  > "Left align text, without justification. This makes it easier to find the start and finish of each line and ensures even spacing between words."
- **[supporting]** BDA states an optimal line length / measure of 60 to 70 characters for dyslexic readers, giving a concrete measure target.
  > "Write short simple sentences: 60 to 70 characters is optimal."

#### Source `a1eca82e` — quality: **primary** · published: 2019-06-06
- **[central]** For accessibility (low-vision and other readers), the W3C Low Vision Task Force recommends avoiding full justification and avoiding centered justification for blocks of text longer than one sentence; left-aligned (ragged-right) is the default and recommended for left-to-right scripts.
  > "Avoid full justification. Avoid centered justification for blocks of text (more than one sentence of text)."
- **[central]** Full justification harms readability by creating uneven word spacing and inconsistent letter spacing, producing vertical 'rivers of white' that disrupt the reader's ability to track along and between lines.
  > "extra space between words that makes it difficult to follow along on one line ... inconsistent letter spacing that makes it harder to distinguish letters and words ... Creates "rivers of white" that "disrupts tracking""
- **[supporting]** Justification problems get worse, not better, when users enlarge text and it reflows — directly relevant to WCAG resize/reflow scenarios (e.g., a 20/400 user at 400% magnification).
  > "problems with full justification are usually much worse when users increase the text size (and text reflows as it should..."
- **[supporting]** Centered text is harder to read across multiple lines because the ragged left edge makes it difficult to find the start of each successive line and sustain reading flow.
  > "more difficult for most people to read, when there are multiple lines of text. It is harder to find the beginning of the next line of text and keep a smooth reading flow..."
- **[supporting]** Left-justified text is easier to read for left-to-right scripts (right-justified for right-to-left scripts), and this is the CSS default when text-align is not explicitly set.
  > "for left-to-right scripts, left justified text is easier to read; for right-to-left scripts, right justified is easier to read"

#### Source `a382ef54` — quality: **primary** · published: 2013 (ASSETS '13; PDF metadata dated 2013-07-13)
- **[central]** Controlled eye-tracking study: 48 dyslexic readers, within-subject design, read 12 texts in 12 fonts (Arial, Arial It., CMU, Courier, Garamond, Helvetica, Myriad, OpenDyslexic, OpenDyslexic It., Times, Times It., Verdana), measuring Reading Time and Fixation Duration. This is [STRONG] controlled evidence, not opinion.
  > "Using a within-subject design, 48 subjects with dyslexia read 12 texts with 12 different fonts."
- **[central]** [MYTH busted] The dyslexia-specific font OpenDyslexic did NOT improve readability or reading speed for dyslexic readers; mainstream fonts (Arial, Helvetica, Verdana) did better. Participants significantly preferred Verdana/Helvetica over OpenDyslexic.
  > "The fonts designed specifically for dyslexia, OpenDys and OpenDys It., did not lead to a better or worse readability. As in [21], OpenDys did not lead to a faster reading."
- **[central]** Subjective font preference does NOT predict reading performance: Preference Ratings vs Reading Time had a Pearson correlation of only -0.13 (near zero). Preference is not a proxy for reading speed.
  > "Preference Ratings and Reading Time had a Pearson correlation of -0.13... which implies that there is almost no correlation between the reading time and the participants preferences."
- **[central]** [STRONG] Sans-serif, monospaced and roman styles produced significantly shorter FIXATION DURATIONS than serif/proportional/italic, but did NOT significantly change total reading TIME — so the speed-advantage conclusion is explicitly weak. Serif had no significant effect on Reading Time (W=11852, p=0.2021); only fixation differed (0.26s vs 0.24s, p=0.008). This supports 'no robust inherent serif/sans reading-speed advantage.'
  > "Although sans serif, monospaced and roman fonts lead to significant shorter fixation durations, we did not find a significant difference in reading time. Hence, our conclusions towards these characteristics are weaker."
- **[supporting]** Recommended fonts for dyslexic readers (by performance + preference): Helvetica, Courier, Arial, Verdana, Computer Modern. Italic should be avoided — Arial Italic significantly decreased readability and gave longer fixations/reading times. Helvetica and Courier gave the shortest fixation durations.
  > "Good fonts for people with dyslexia are Helvetica, Courier, Arial, Verdana and CMU... In particular, Arial It. should be avoided since it significantly decreases readability."

#### Source `a3b181cc` — quality: **primary** · published: 2025-10-01
- **[central]** WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.12 Text Spacing (Level AA) requires that no loss of content or functionality occurs when the user sets line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size, with no other style property changed.
  > "Line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size ... No loss of content or functionality occurs by setting all of the following and by changing no other style property"
- **[central]** SC 1.4.12 requires support for paragraph spacing of at least 2 times the font size and letter spacing (tracking) of at least 0.12 times the font size without loss of content or functionality.
  > "Spacing following paragraphs to at least 2 times the font size ... Letter spacing (tracking) to at least 0.12 times the font size"
- **[central]** SC 1.4.12 requires support for word spacing of at least 0.16 times the font size without loss of content or functionality.
  > "Word spacing to at least 0.16 times the font size"
- **[supporting]** Text Spacing is a Level AA success criterion, conditioning conformance on the four spacing overrides being applicable.
  > "Conformance Level: AA"
- **[supporting]** The criterion's stated beneficiaries are people with low vision who need increased spacing to read, and people with dyslexia who may increase spacing to increase reading speed.
  > "People with low vision who require increased space between lines, words, and letters are able to read text ... People with dyslexia may increase space between lines, words, and letters to increase reading speed"

#### Source `a48f9e42` — quality: **primary** · published: ?
- **[central]** WCAG SC 1.4.4 Resize Text is a Level AA success criterion requiring that text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200% without loss of content or functionality.
  > "Text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200 percent without loss of content or functionality."
- **[central]** The exact threshold for SC 1.4.4 is 200% — content satisfies the criterion if it can be scaled up to twice the width and height.
  > "Content satisfies the success criterion if it can be scaled up to 200% - that is, up to twice the width and height."
- **[supporting]** The only exceptions to the resize-to-200% requirement are captions and images of text.
  > "Exemptions apply to: "captions and images of text" only. Standard text must be resizable without requiring assistive technology."
- **[supporting]** SC 1.4.4 does not mandate a single resizing mechanism; zoom (whole page), text-only resizing, or magnification all qualify, and relative units (em/percent) are listed as preferred sufficient techniques for satisfying it.
  > "The sufficient techniques recommend relative units via: C28: "Specifying the size of text containers using em units""
- **[tangential]** If incremental resizing steps exist between 100% and 200%, there must be no loss of content or functionality at any of those intermediate steps.
  > "If the resizing mechanism offers incremental resizing steps between 100% and 200%, authors must ensure that there is no loss of content or functionality at these incremental steps."

#### Source `a4b41ea0` — quality: **primary** · published: 2026-03-09
- **[central]** WCAG SC 1.4.8 Visual Presentation is a Level AAA criterion (not AA), and its measure requirement caps line width at no more than 80 characters or glyphs (40 for CJK scripts).
  > "Width is no more than 80 characters or glyphs (40 if CJK)."
- **[central]** Under SC 1.4.8, line spacing (leading) must be at least space-and-a-half (150%) within paragraphs, and paragraph spacing must be at least 1.5 times larger than the line spacing.
  > "Line spacing (leading) is at least space-and-a-half within paragraphs, and paragraph spacing is at least 1.5 times larger than the line spacing"
- **[central]** SC 1.4.8 requires that text not be justified to both margins (i.e., favors left-aligned/ragged-right for accessibility).
  > "Text is not justified (aligned to both the left and the right margins)"
- **[supporting]** SC 1.4.8 requires that foreground and background colors be user-selectable.
  > "Foreground and background colors can be selected by the user"
- **[supporting]** SC 1.4.8 requires text to be resizable up to 200% without assistive technology in a way that does not force horizontal scrolling to read a line.
  > "Text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200 percent in a way that does not require the user to scroll horizontally to read a line of text"

#### Source `a5069f75` — quality: **blog** · published: 2020-11-30
- **[central]** Browser HTTP cache partitioning (Chrome v86, Oct 2020) eliminated the cross-site shared-cache benefit Google Fonts relied on; fonts are now redownloaded per-site rather than reused from a shared CDN cache.
  > "Since Chrome v86, released October 2020, cross-site resources like fonts can't be shared on the same CDN anymore."
- **[central]** Cache partitioning is now near-universal across browsers, not a Chrome-only quirk — Safari since 2013, Firefox since v85 (Jan 2021), Edge since v1.34 (Dec 2021) — so the shared-cache argument is dead everywhere.
  > "Safari: since 2013 ... Firefox: since v85 (January 2021) ... Edge: since v1.34 (December 2021)"
- **[supporting]** The historical Google Fonts performance pitch was cross-site cache reuse: load a font once on any site and reuse it everywhere — a claim the article identifies as no longer valid.
  > "Our cross-site caching is designed so that you only need to load a font once, with any website, and we'll use that same cached font on any other website that uses Google Fonts."
- **[central]** The author's overall recommendation is to self-host fonts because the old shared-cache performance advantage of Google Fonts no longer exists.
  > "Self-host your fonts for better performance. The old performance argument is not valid anymore."
- **[tangential]** Google Fonts is widely deployed (cited as over 42M websites), giving the cache-partitioning change broad practical impact.
  > "Google Fonts used on "over 42M websites""

#### Source `a509b549` — quality: **blog** · published: 2021-05-25
- **[central]** When a web font finishes downloading and swaps in, characters expand or contract relative to the fallback font (a 'hydration effect'), and this size mismatch is the mechanism that produces Cumulative Layout Shift during font loading.
  > "the 'hydration effect' when the web font downloads and characters expand (or contract) due to differences between the fonts"
- **[central]** font-display: swap is a poor choice for avoiding layout-shift penalties because the swap itself causes the shift that hurts Core Web Vitals (CLS).
  > "using `font-display: swap` is a poor choice because of that"
- **[central]** The @font-face metric-override descriptors — ascent-override, descent-override, line-gap-override, and size-adjust — let you tune a fallback font's metrics to the primary web font; size-adjust applies a percentage scale factor to glyphs so the fallback and web font occupy the same space, eliminating the swap-induced shift.
  > "allows us to reduce layout shifts by matching up a fallback font and primary web font through a scale factor for glyphs (percentage)"
- **[supporting]** A concrete metric-matched fallback for Lato over Arial uses size-adjust: 97.38% and ascent-override: 99% on the @font-face rule, with the fallback chained after the web font in the font-family list (font-family: Lato, Lato-fallback, sans-serif).
  > "size-adjust: 97.38%;
    ascent-override: 99%;
    src: local("Arial");"
- **[tangential]** At publication (May 2021), ascent-override/descent-override/line-gap-override had shipped in Chrome since version 87, but size-adjust was not yet in any stable browser, with Chrome intending to ship it in Chrome 92 (July 20, 2021); Firefox had it behind a flag.
  > "Three of these settings have already been shipped in Chrome since version 87, though the key `size-adjust` descriptor is not yet available in any stable browser"

#### Source `a667f7ed` — quality: **blog** · published: 2024-06-06 (updated 2025-11-30)
- **[central]** A web-font layout shift (CLS) occurs at swap time because the fallback font differs in sizing/character dimensions from the web font; the metric-override @font-face descriptors (size-adjust, ascent-override, descent-override) reduce it by making the fallback better match the web font.
  > "When a fallback font is significantly different in sizing or character dimensions from the web font, the transition between the fallback font and web font can cause a layout shift. ... To further reduce such layout shifts, we can use font face descriptors to make the fallback font better match the web font."
- **[central]** font-display: swap renders the fallback font immediately and swaps to the web font once loaded, trading FOIT for a less-jarring font swap; the article recommends it as a balance between fast text and the desired font.
  > "This tells the browser to display the fallback font immediately, then swap to the web font once the web font loads. ... a good compromise between displaying text quickly and using the desired web font"
- **[central]** Concrete metric-override values can be derived from a tool like Capsize: for the web font Victor Mono with Courier New as fallback, the article uses ascent-override: 110% and descent-override: 25%.
  > "Example provided: For "Victor Mono" with "Courier New" fallback, the article recommends `ascent-override: 110%;` and `descent-override: 25%;` based on Capsize tool measurements."
- **[supporting]** font-display: optional can avoid the swap layout shift because the browser may choose not to use the web font at all when it does not load fast enough.
  > "In certain cases, when using `font-display: optional`, the browser may choose to not use the web font at all."
- **[supporting]** font-display: block causes a flash of invisible text (FOIT) while the slow web font loads, even though space is reserved using fallback font metrics.
  > "Causes "flash of invisible text" (FOIT) when web font loads slowly ... Browser reserves space based on fallback font metrics, even though text is invisible to users"

#### Source `a7d4a544` — quality: **primary** · published: 2020-10-06
- **[central]** Chrome partitions the HTTP cache so a resource cached while visiting one site is NOT served from cache on a different site, even when the resource URL is identical — defeating the historical shared-CDN caching benefit (e.g. a font fetched from a shared CDN on site A is re-downloaded on site B).
  > "The same user visits a different page (https://b.example) which requests the same image (https://x.example/doge.png). Though the same image was loaded in the previous example, since the key doesn't match it will not be a cache hit."
- **[central]** The cache partition key is a 'Network Isolation Key' composed of the top-level site and the current-frame site, combined with the resource URL; sites are identified by scheme://eTLD+1 (ignoring subdomains and ports).
  > "With cache partitioning, cached resources will be keyed using a new "Network Isolation Key" in addition to the resource URL. The Network Isolation Key is composed of the top-level site and the current-frame site."
- **[supporting]** HTTP cache partitioning shipped starting in Chrome 86 (the post that announces it is dated October 6, 2020).
  > "To mitigate these risks, Chrome will partition its HTTP cache starting in Chrome 86."
- **[supporting]** The measured cost of partitioning is small: overall cache miss rate rises ~3.6%, First Contentful Paint changes ~0.3%, and the fraction of bytes loaded from the network rises ~4%.
  > "The overall cache miss rate increases by about 3.6%, changes to the FCP (First Contentful Paint) are modest (~0.3%), and the overall fraction of bytes loaded from the network increases by around 4%."
- **[tangential]** Cache partitioning is not Chrome-only: Safari partitions using the top-level eTLD+1, and Firefox planned a similar partitioning approach — so cross-site shared caching of CDN fonts is broadly defeated across browsers.
  > "Safari: Uses top-level eTLD+1 only ... Firefox: Planning implementation with top-level scheme://eTLD+1 and considering a second key like Chrome"

#### Source `a94a8072` — quality: **secondary** · published: 2022-10-04
- **[central]** font-display controls the FOIT/FOUT tradeoff via defined block/swap periods: optional (~100ms block, no swap) is the most performance-favoring and recommended for body text; swap (0ms block, infinite swap) shows fallback text immediately and is recommended for branding/display elements; block delays text 2-3s. auto is browser-dependent and discouraged.
  > "font-display: optional provides 'the most performant approach' ... For body text, use optional; for branding/display elements, use swap—but deliver the font early to minimize layout shifts."
- **[central]** Font swapping can cause Cumulative Layout Shift when the fallback and web font occupy different space; the fix is aligning fallback metrics to the web font using the CSS @font-face metric-override properties size-adjust, ascent-override, descent-override, and line-gap-override.
  > "The practice of font swapping has the potential to cause layout shifts ... Use CSS properties to align fallback and web font metrics: size-adjust, ascent-override, descent-override, line-gap-override."
- **[supporting]** Contrary to the common 'always self-host' belief, the 2022 Web Almanac data cited here found sites using third-party fonts rendered faster than self-hosted ones; self-hosting is advised only when paired with a CDN and HTTP/2.
  > "The Web Almanac found sites using third-party fonts rendered faster than self-hosted. Recommendation: self-host only if using a CDN and HTTP/2; otherwise, third-party providers (e.g., Google Fonts) likely perform better."
- **[supporting]** WOFF2 is the only web font format worth shipping because it compresses roughly 30% better than WOFF.
  > "Use only WOFF2 and forget about everything else... WOFF2 compresses 30% better than WOFF."
- **[supporting]** Variable fonts do not automatically save bytes: a single variable file often has a larger size than one single-weight file, so they only reduce total bytes when multiple styles and weights are actually used.
  > "Variable fonts: Consolidate multiple font files into one. Caveat: variable fonts often have larger file sizes than single-weight fonts; only beneficial if using multiple styles and weights."

#### Source `a95c365a` — quality: **primary** · published: ?
- **[central]** WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.12 Text Spacing requires that no loss of content or functionality occur when a user sets line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size. This is a normative minimum users must be able to apply.
  > "Line height: "at least 1.5 times the font size" ... These are minimum thresholds users must be able to set without breaking the layout or hiding content."
- **[central]** SC 1.4.12 requires content to adapt without loss when spacing following paragraphs is set to at least 2 times the font size, letter spacing (tracking) to at least 0.12 times the font size, and word spacing to at least 0.16 times the font size.
  > "Paragraph spacing: "at least 2 times the font size" - Letter spacing: "at least 0.12 times the font size" - Word spacing: "at least 0.16 times the font size""
- **[supporting]** The intent of the criterion is that people with low vision who need increased space between lines, words and letters can read text, and people with dyslexia may increase that spacing to increase reading speed — without the author's layout breaking.
  > "Low Vision: "People with low vision who require increased space between lines, words, and letters are able to read text." Dyslexia: "People with dyslexia may increase space between lines, words, and letters to increase reading speed.""
- **[supporting]** The 0.12em letter-spacing threshold is grounded in empirical reading-speed research (the McLeish study), which found reading speed increased with letter spacing up to about 0.25em but began to flatten around 0.20em.
  > "The metrics derive from the McLeish study. The document states: "She ran from .04 to .25 em tests. McLeish found an increasing curve in reading speed of actual materials up to .25, but it started to flatten at .20.""

#### Source `a9895437` — quality: **primary** · published: 2017 (Annals of Dyslexia; published online March 2016, journal volume 2017)
- **[central]** A controlled alternating-treatment study found the OpenDyslexic font produced no improvement in reading rate or accuracy for children with dyslexia — directly contradicting the popular 'dyslexia-friendly font' marketing claim ([MYTH] per controlled evidence).
  > "Results from this alternating treatment experiment show no improvement in reading rate or accuracy for individual students with dyslexia, as well as the group as a whole."
- **[central]** OpenDyslexic actually performed worse than standard fonts (Arial, Times New Roman), with statistically significant deficits (IRD -49.65% to -88.65% on fluency; -53.89% to -75.81% on accuracy, CIs excluding zero), so the specialized font may hurt rather than help.
  > "Effect sizes showed OpenDyslexic performed worse, with IRD (Improvement Rate Difference) ranging from -49.65% to -88.65% on fluency measures and -53.89% to -75.81% on accuracy—all confidence intervals excluding zero, indicating statistically significant deficits."
- **[central]** There is no evidence base supporting specialized dyslexia fonts as an effective practice, and relying on them risks diverting resources from proven interventions.
  > "Currently, there is no documentation to support a specialized font is an evidence-based practice."
- **[supporting]** What actually helps dyslexic readers is intensive, systematic, multisensory instruction in phonological awareness, phonics, and fluency — not font swaps.
  > "intensive systematic intervention...direct multisensory instruction in the areas of phonological awareness, phonics, and fluency...sequential and cumulative, and taught to automaticity"
- **[supporting]** The study was small and font-specific: 12 elementary students (grades 3-6) with confirmed dyslexia, comparing OpenDyslexic against Arial and Times New Roman across letter naming, real-word, and nonsense-word decoding — bounding the strength/generalizability of the finding.
  > "12 elementary students (grades 3-6) with confirmed dyslexia diagnoses ... OpenDyslexic (OD) compared against Arial (sans serif) and Times New Roman (serif)"

#### Source `a9ac30c5` — quality: **blog** · published: 2021-03-02
- **[central]** font-display: swap instructs browsers to render fallback/system text immediately and replace it with the web font once loaded (i.e. it produces FOUT, not FOIT), with support in Chrome 60+, Firefox 58+, Safari 11.1+, Edge 79+.
  > "font-display: swap ... instructs browsers to display the fallback text before web fonts get loaded."
- **[central]** The author's recommended default loading strategy for most sites is 'FOUT with Class', while 'Critical FOFT' (self-hosted only) is positioned as the most performant strategy.
  > "FOUT with Class: Best approach for most situations ... Critical FOFT: Most performant approach"
- **[supporting]** FOUT works by first showing a system/fallback font and swapping to the web font when it finishes loading, whereas FOIT hides text until the web font loads.
  > "Show users a system font. When the web font gets loaded, we change the text back"
- **[supporting]** Preloading fonts is not free: preloading more than one font can hurt the initial page load time, so it should be used sparingly.
  > "If we load more than one, we can adversely affect the initial load time"
- **[tangential]** Self-hosting fonts is only legally permissible when you hold the appropriate license for the font.
  > "You can only self-host your fonts if you have the license to do so"

#### Source `aa377ed3` — quality: **blog** · published: 2024-04-25
- **[central]** A subsetted variable font replaced five subsetted static weights and cut the per-visitor font transfer from ~90kB to 25.8kB — a 71% drop to 26kB total — demonstrating variable fonts save bytes when several weights of one family are used.
  > "Before subsetting for five different weights, the static font file was 104kB...After subsetting, it came down to 90.1kB. Using a subsetted variable font cut the file size further to just 25.8kB"
- **[central]** Variable fonts deliver any number of weights from a single file, whereas traditional static fonts need a separate file per weight.
  > "With variable fonts, you need just one file regardless of how many font weights you use"
- **[supporting]** The CSS unicode-range descriptor does not subset a font — it only restricts which characters the browser uses while the full glyph set is still downloaded; real byte savings require actual subsetting (generators, Python scripts, or Google Fonts &text=).
  > "using `unicode-range: [...]` simply forbids the browser from using characters from outside the range...But those characters are still there and they're still downloaded."
- **[supporting]** Switching font-display from swap to optional reduced Cumulative Layout Shift in their implementation.
  > "Display setting changed from `swap` to `optional` to minimize Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)"
- **[supporting]** Preloaded font files must carry the crossorigin attribute or the browser downloads them twice (preload plus the actual @font-face fetch).
  > "without the `crossorigin` argument, font files were downloaded twice"

#### Source `aa52d1e3` — quality: **primary** · published: 2020
- **[central]** A font with dyslexia-friendly letterform features produced NO reading-performance difference versus a standard font, for both dyslexic children and typical readers — disconfirming the 'dyslexia font helps' belief.
  > "The data failed to show any effect from the letterform... The model did not show a main effect of letterform (F(1,875) = 0.496, p = 0.482)...
