Both children with dyslexia and typical readers did not show differences in reading performance...when they read texts written with the DF letterform or with the standard letterform."
- **[central]** Increasing inter-letter spacing WITHOUT a matched increase in inter-word spacing slowed reading: dyslexic readers dropped from 3.22 to 3.04 syllables/second (t(63)=-4.258, p<0.001, d=-0.532) — contrary to the simple 'more spacing always helps' claim.
  > "reading speed is impaired by an increase in inter-letter spacing not combined with an adequate increase in inter-word spacing... Dyslexics with increased inter-letter + default inter-word spacing: 3.04 syll/s [vs] default inter-letter + default inter-word spacing: 3.22 syll/s... t(63) = -4.258, p < 0.001, d = -0.532"
- **[supporting]** Spacing helped only when inter-letter AND inter-word spacing were increased together; that combined condition was read faster than increased inter-letter spacing alone.
  > "texts presented in the condition with increased inter-letter spacing and increased inter-word spacing were read faster [compared to increased inter-letter + default inter-word spacing]"
- **[supporting]** The spacing increases tested were modest (~7% inter-letter, ~15% inter-word on 14pt) and smaller than prior studies (Zorzi et al.) that found benefits, suggesting earlier positive spacing results relied on much larger spacing or shorter sentences.
  > "The difference between default and increased spacing employed in the present study was smaller than that used in the study by Zorzi et al... Previous studies showing spacing benefits used exceptionally wider spacing or shorter sentences (which independently aid reading)."
- **[supporting]** The study is a controlled 2x2x2 factorial experiment with 128 children (64 dyslexic, 64 control, mean age 12.4), giving it strong [STRONG]-tier weight on the dyslexia-font and spacing questions.
  > "Sample: 128 children (64 with dyslexia, 64 controls), mean age 12.4 years; Design: 2×2×2 factorial with 8 reading conditions"

#### Source `ab23458c` — quality: **primary** · published: 2023-02-27
- **[central]** font-display:swap causes layout shift / poor CLS because the fallback and web font differ in character size, shifting content when the swap happens — this is the modern motivation for metric-override fallbacks (TOPIC A: CLS from font swap).
  > "The biggest issue with `swap` is the jarring effect, where the difference in character sizes of the two fonts results in screen content shifting around... This leads to poor CLS scores, especially for text-heavy websites."
- **[central]** The fix is the @font-face metric-override technique: size-adjust, ascent-override, descent-override, and line-gap-override let a fallback font be tuned to match the web font's metrics, preventing the layout shift on swap.
  > "Properties such as `size-adjust`, `ascent-override`, `descent-override`, and `line-gap-override`...can help override the metrics of a fallback font"
- **[supporting]** Framework/tooling now automates the metric-matching: Next.js (@next/font) enables it by default, Nuxt's @nuxtjs/fontaine auto-calculates fallback metrics and generates the fallback @font-face CSS, and Capsize's createFontStack builds the stack from font metrics.
  > "This feature is enabled by default when you load fonts using the @next/font component... @nuxtjs/fontaine automatically calculates the fallback font metric values and generates the fallback `@font-face` CSS."
- **[supporting]** The fallback font is referenced as a local system font via local() in the @font-face src (e.g. BlinkMacSystemFont / Segoe UI), so no extra network request is incurred for the metric-adjusted fallback.
  > "src: local('BlinkMacSystemFont'), local('Segoe UI')..."

#### Source `abfade1d` — quality: **blog** · published: 2016-07-12 (updated 2017-07-27)
- **[central]** font-display: swap opts a browser into FOUT (Flash of Unstyled Text — fallback shown then swapped) instead of FOIT (Flash of Invisible Text — text hidden until the web font loads), the central font-display trade-off.
  > "Add a new `font-display: swap` descriptor to your `@font-face` block to opt-in to FOUT on browsers that support it."
- **[central]** font-display: fallback or optional is the recommended choice when the web font is non-essential to the design (i.e. for body text where you would rather show the system fallback than block or swap).
  > "can be used if you consider web fonts to be unnecessary to the design"
- **[supporting]** Untreated FOIT lasts a maximum of about three seconds in most modern browsers, after which the browser falls back to FOUT and shows fallback text.
  > "Maximum three second FOIT in most modern browsers, switches to FOUT if load takes longer"
- **[central]** A concrete web-font performance budget threshold: more than 4-5 web fonts or a total file size over 100KB is the point at which heavier loading strategies (FOFT) become warranted.
  > "anything more than 4 or 5 web fonts or a total file size of more than 100KB"
- **[supporting]** Preloading fonts has a cost — each preloaded font competes with and can block initial render — so usage should be limited to the one or two most important web fonts.
  > "The more you preload, the more you can block initial render"

#### Source `ac6712fd` — quality: **blog** · published: 2021-02-17
- **[central]** Browser HTTP cache partitioning shipped in Firefox 85 and Chrome 86, ending the shared cross-site cache for resources like public-CDN fonts.
  > "As of Firefox v85 and Chrome v86, the browser cache will be partitioned."
- **[central]** Under cache partitioning, a resource used on two different sites is downloaded and stored twice, so the old cross-site cache reuse no longer applies.
  > "the same resource included on two sites will have to be downloaded from the internet twice and stored separately."
- **[central]** Google Fonts loses its shared-cache benefit specifically: fonts must now be re-downloaded per site rather than reused from a cache populated by other sites.
  > "Under the new cache partitioning implementations, these fonts will have to be downloaded for every site that uses them."
- **[supporting]** Google estimated the performance hit from cache partitioning to be minimal, about a 0.3% difference in First Contentful Paint.
  > "Google estimates that the changes in browser caching will have minimal impact, as little as a 0.3% difference in FCP (First Contentful Paint)."
- **[supporting]** The article recommends self-hosting third-party libraries (including fonts) on your own domain to avoid a second connection.
  > "Host all third-party libraries on your own domain to eliminate the need for the browser to establish a second connection."

#### Source `ad63b716` — quality: **primary** · published: 2017
- **[central]** In a controlled study of 170 children with dyslexia (ages 7-12), reading text set in the Dyslexie font was no faster and no more accurate than reading the same text in Arial — directly contradicting the marketing claim that the font eases dyslexic reading.
  > "Children with dyslexia read text written in font Dyslexie as fast as in font Arial and they made a comparable number of errors."
- **[central]** A second experiment (102 children with dyslexia + 45 without, word-level, comparing Dyslexie vs Arial vs Times New Roman) likewise found no reading benefit from Dyslexie for either group.
  > "Participants with and without dyslexia read as many words correctly during 1 min in the font Dyslexie as they did in Arial."
- **[central]** Any apparent benefit of Dyslexie comes from increased spacing, not from its special letter shapes — the active ingredient is spacing, not the bespoke glyph design (citing Marinus et al. 2016, and noting their own study did not control horizontal spacing).
  > "if the font Dyslexie aids reading, it is not because of its shape but rather because of the increase in word spacing"
- **[supporting]** Children did not even prefer the Dyslexie font (they preferred Arial), and font preference was unrelated to actual reading performance — preference is not a proxy for reading speed/accuracy.
  > "preference was not related to reading performance"

#### Source `ad948367` — quality: **primary** · published: 2025-02-21 (latest release v0.5.0)
- **[central]** Fontaine reduces CLS by scanning your @font-face rules and generating fallback @font-face rules whose metrics (ascent-override / descent-override / line-gap-override) are crafted to match a local fallback font, so the fallback occupies the same space as the web font and swap causes no reflow.
  > "Reduces CLS by using local font fallbacks with crafted font metrics. ... scan[s] your `@font-face` rules and generate[s] fallback rules with the correct metrics."
- **[supporting]** The metric-override technique is implemented purely via CSS @font-face descriptors with concrete percentage values (e.g. ascent-override: 92.7734375%, descent-override: 24.4140625%, line-gap-override: 0%) and adds no JavaScript runtime cost.
  > "ascent-override: 92.7734375%, descent-override: 24.4140625%, line-gap-override: 0% ... Pure CSS, zero runtime overhead."
- **[central]** Applying matched fallback metrics produced a large measured CLS drop in Fontaine's own demo: CLS fell from 0.34 to 0.013 and the performance score rose from 88 to 98 (self-reported demo, not an independent controlled study).
  > "CLS reduction: "0.34" → "0.013" ... Performance score: "88" → "98""
- **[supporting]** Fontaine works by appending a generated fallback family to your font-family declaration, e.g. font-family: 'Roboto' becomes 'Roboto', 'Roboto fallback'.
  > "When you use `font-family: 'Roboto'`, the system automatically appends the fallback, transforming it to `'Roboto', 'Roboto fallback'`."
- **[supporting]** Metric overrides do not eliminate layout shift on their own: if the stylesheet carrying the font-face fallback rules is not inlined, a layout shift still occurs when that stylesheet loads — so all CSS (not just the fallback rules) must be inlined for full benefit.
  > "For best performance...you will need to inline _all_ your CSS, not just the font-face fallback rules...or there will still be a layout shift when the stylesheet loads."

#### Source `afe2f8d3` — quality: **primary** · published: 2025-09-16
- **[central]** WCAG SC 1.4.10 Reflow requires content to be presentable without loss of information/functionality and without two-dimensional scrolling at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels (for vertical-scrolling content) or a height equivalent to 256 CSS pixels (for horizontal-scrolling content).
  > "Content can be presented without loss of information or functionality, and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions for: Vertical scrolling content at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels; Horizontal scrolling content at a height equivalent to 256 CSS pixels."
- **[central]** The 320 CSS pixel reflow threshold corresponds to a 1280 CSS pixel wide viewport zoomed to 400%, defining the test condition for text/content reflow.
  > "320 CSS pixels is equivalent to a starting viewport width of 1280 CSS pixels wide at 400% zoom."
- **[supporting]** Reflow exempts content that inherently requires a two-dimensional layout, including data tables (but not individual cells), images such as maps/diagrams, video, games, and toolbars that must stay in view.
  > "parts of the content which require two-dimensional layout for usage or meaning"
- **[supporting]** SC 1.4.10 governs content reflow behavior, not text enlargement magnitude — it does not mandate any specific font size.
  > "The criterion does **not** mandate specific font sizes—it focuses on content behavior, not text enlargement magnitude."

### Font pairing & deliberate multiple-typeface communication

#### Source `a07c59e9` — quality: **secondary** · published: 2024-10-07
- **[central]** Pair typefaces by clear contrast, not similarity — typefaces that are almost-but-not-quite the same clash, the classic 'too close for comfort' failure (attributed to Ellen Lupton, Thinking with Type). [CONVENTION]
  > "Warns against pairing typefaces that are "almost the same— *but not quite*— they begin to clash" ... Quoted phrase "too close for comfort" from *Thinking with Type*"
- **[central]** Limit the number of typefaces: two to three is usually sufficient, because too many letterform styles become cluttered or visually confusing (presented as best-practice convention, not evidence). [CONVENTION]
  > ""two to three is usually sufficient" for limiting choices ... "Using too many letterform styles can easily become cluttered or visually confusing""
- **[central]** Successful pairing requires ample contrast across weight, width, style, or size — e.g. thick with thin, ornate with simple, or sans-serif with serif. [CONVENTION]
  > ""Successful type pairing may be achieved by combining contrasting styles of type, such as *thick with thin, ornate with simple*, or *sans serif with serif*""
- **[supporting]** Typographic hierarchy is built by scale and visual weight: adjusting the weight of headlines versus body copy directs the reader's eye. [CONVENTION]
  > ""adjusting the visual weight of headlines vs. body copy helps direct a reader's eye" ... "two typefaces which might not work well together at the same point size may pair beautifully at contrasting sizes""
- **[supporting]** Type choice should be driven by context and content rather than aesthetics alone — context is the most important pairing factor. [CONVENTION]
  > ""The most important factor in font pairing is *context*" ... Type choice "should be determined and prioritized by content""

#### Source `a0a96630` — quality: **blog** · published: ?
- **[central]** [MYTH-counter] The rule 'never mix multiple/different typefaces in one layout' is rejected by practitioner Fernando Mello: deliberately diverse typefaces with different 'tones of voice' can form a coherent working system when each is assigned to a different layout role/hierarchy level (he demonstrates four faces — FS Aldrin, FS Pimlico, FS Dillon, FS Silas Slab — used together).
  > "by studying details and strengths of different typefaces, it is possible to create working systems which rely effectively on type designs with different tones of voice."
- **[central]** [MYTH-counter / CONTRARY VIEW] Mello disputes the widely cited 'pair by contrast, never use two similar typefaces' convention (Lupton/Bringhurst): near-similar faces can coexist without conflict if differentiated by weight and function — e.g. one sans for the headline in a heavier weight and a very similar design for running text in a thinner weight.
  > "sometimes it might be better to use one sans design for headline in a specific weight, and a very similar design for the running text in a thinner weight."
- **[supporting]** [MYTH-counter] The 'sans for headings, serif for body' formula is treated as a non-rule; for screens specifically Mello argues a sans renders and reads better than a serif, supporting sans body text with serif (or other) headings as a valid screen-first pairing.
  > "A sans, for its simpler structure of shapes, will render better and be more legible, direct and welcoming to read on screens."
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION] Mello frames font pairing as craft/judgment with no fixed rules: the correct starting point is the brief, the content to communicate, and the intended hierarchy — not a lookup of 'which fonts pair' — and proper typesetting (size, leading) is part of what makes a pairing work.
  > "There aren't, and shouldn't be strong rules or dogmas in design"
- **[tangential]** [CONVENTION] Both pairing strategies are endorsed as equally valid: a superfamily approach (one type system across sans+slab, e.g. FS Silas Sans + FS Silas Slab) and a cross-family approach (two distinct families, e.g. FS Brabo + FS Lucas) can each produce good hierarchy.
  > "it is also possible to achieve good or even better results by planning your hierarchy using different families."

#### Source `a0efc55e` — quality: **secondary** · published: 2022-06-19
- **[central]** A single typeface with multiple weights is sufficient for a professional, well-balanced design — i.e., a second font family is not required (supports the 'weight/size within one family suffices' point and undercuts the myth that you need a second typeface).
  > "A design that uses a single typeface with multiple weights can look professional and well-balanced."
- **[central]** Each typeface or style in a design must serve a distinct, defined function — supporting functional differentiation (heading vs body vs other roles).
  > "Each typeface or style should have a clear role and purpose."
- **[supporting]** Visual hierarchy is built by varying weight, style, and size — i.e., contrast within one family is the first lever for hierarchy, before adding a second family.
  > "Using multiple font weights, styles, and sizes will help define the page's visual hierarchy"
- **[supporting]** Body copy should use a single typeface; mixing two typefaces in body text reads as inconsistent/unrefined (a pitfall of over-pairing).
  > "Using two typefaces for body copy makes a design look inconsistent and unrefined"
- **[supporting]** Decorative typefaces are unreadable at small sizes and should never set body copy; pair a decorative face with a neutral serif or sans instead (decorative-pairing pitfall / readability constraint).
  > "Decorative typefaces are difficult to read at small sizes and should never be used for body copy."

#### Source `a15e8b87` — quality: **secondary** · published: ?
- **[central]** Tabular figures all share an identical horizontal width so they align vertically in columns, making numeric data in spreadsheets, financial tables, math, dates, and phone numbers more readable (the figure style for data/tabular settings). [CONVENTION — Type Network / Yves Peters]
  > "Tabular figures: "all occupy identical horizontal spaces" for vertical column alignment in spreadsheets and data tables ... "Vertical alignment is crucial for making information in spreadsheets, financial data, math, and things like lists of dates or phone numbers more readable.""
- **[central]** Proportional figures occupy widths consistent with each numeral's design and integrate harmoniously with running text, whereas tabular figures used in body copy create an uneven, jarring reading rhythm — so figure style is matched to content type (data vs. prose). [CONVENTION]
  > "Proportional figures: "all occupy horizontal spaces that are consistent with their design" and "integrate more harmoniously with the surrounding text" ... tabular figures in body copy create "a jarring rhythm for readers.""
- **[supporting]** Lining figures share a single height that lines up with the capitals and should always be used in all-caps settings; oldstyle figures follow x-height/ascender/descender logic and are suited for mixed-case text — a height-axis distinction separate from the tabular/proportional width axis.
  > "Lining figures: "all have the same height" and "line up with the capitals" ... Oldstyle figures: their "construction follows the logic of x-height, ascenders, and descenders, they're suited for mixed-case typesetting" ... "Always use the lining figures in all-caps setting"."
- **[supporting]** Proportional oldstyle figures harmonize with surrounding mixed-case body text, while oldstyle figures should be avoided in all-caps because they read as too small — pairing figure style to the typographic context.
  > "Proportional oldstyle figures "harmonize perfectly with the surrounding text in mixed-case setting." However ... avoid combining oldstyle with all-caps text since "the numbers will look too small.""

#### Source `a2ac7c63` — quality: **blog** · published: 2022-09-12
- **[central]** For data visualization, use fonts with LINING figures (all digits the same height) and TABULAR figures (every number the same width) so numbers align in tables and columns — a functional requirement for numeric/data typography.
  > "For your data visualizations, use fonts with lining figures... Lining numbers 'line up': They're all the same height... Tabular figures are, as the name implies, great for tables... every number is the same width"
- **[central]** Sans-serif typefaces are the default better choice for data-visualization body text and labels because they skim more cleanly, especially for numbers — but this is framed as industry convention/readability practice rather than inherent serif-vs-sans superiority.
  > "For data visualizations, sans-serif ('without serifs') typefaces are most often the better choice... They look cleaner and are often easier to skim than serif fonts, especially when it comes to numbers"
- **[central]** Functional differentiation by content type is endorsed: serif faces can be used for chart titles/headlines (read as classier, more serious) paired with a sans-serif for labels and body — e.g., The Guardian pairs serif headlines with sans-serif body.
  > "Serifs look a bit more classy, traditional, and serious/professional than sans-serifs... The Guardian uses serif fonts for chart titles paired with sans-serif for labels"
- **[supporting]** Coherent typographic systems in newsrooms achieve variety through weight/style changes within one typeface rather than adding many families — e.g., The Economist uses different styles from a single commissioned typeface for all text.
  > "The Economist uses different styles from the same commissioned typeface for all text"
- **[supporting]** Very thin/light font weights are hard to read and should be avoided for visualization text; weight contrast (bold) should be reserved for emphasis, with regular/medium for longer text.
  > "Thin fonts are 'really, really hard to read' (cites ACM research)... Use bold fonts only for emphasis"

#### Source `a351e139` — quality: **blog** · published: 2022-02-11
- **[central]** [CONVENTION] Limit a design to two typefaces, three at most, to avoid clutter/'typeface chaos.' This is the same 'two is plenty, rarely more than three' convention voiced elsewhere — presented here as guidance, not evidence.
  > "Stick with two fonts — three, max"
- **[central]** [CONVENTION] Pairing across serif and sans-serif is a reliable way to get contrast, but the two faces should still share some structural qualities (similar proportions, same x-height) so the pairing reads as harmony rather than conflict.
  > "Mixing serif and sans serif fonts is a safe bet ... look for typefaces that share a few qualities—like similar proportions and the same x-height"
- **[central]** [CONVENTION] Deliberate visual contrast between paired fonts builds hierarchy by directing the reader's attention to the most important text first; the 'squint test' (viewing from a distance) is offered as an informal check that hierarchy holds.
  > "Creating visual differences when pairing fonts can help highlight text that needs to attract the attention of the reader first"
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION] Each font in a pairing should be assigned a distinct role or purpose in the layout (e.g., headings vs body), supporting functional differentiation of content types.
  > "assign[ing] each font a role or purpose in the design layout"
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION] Using multiple weights and styles from a single type family is a low-risk way to get variation because the styles were designed to work together — i.e., a superfamily/same-family approach before reaching for a second family.
  > "Fonts from the same family were designed to work together"

#### Source `a67dce42` — quality: **primary** · published: 1989
- **[supporting]** Typographical cues and headings count as 'signals' — devices that emphasize a text's content or structure without adding new content; this is the recognized mechanism by which typographic emphasis communicates structure rather than meaning.
  > "Signals are writing devices that emphasize aspects of a text's content or structure without adding to the content of the text."
- **[central]** Controlled studies reviewed across many signal types (including headings and typographical cues) show signals reliably improve memory for the specific information they emphasize — concrete evidence that typographic emphasis/hierarchy aids retention of the cued content.
  > "Virtually all types of signals produce better memory for information they cue in a text, whereas memory for unsignaled information often is unaffected."
- **[supporting]** Signaling's benefit is selective, not uniform: it boosts memory for emphasized items while leaving unsignaled items roughly unchanged — i.e., emphasis works by redistributing attention to cued content, which supports using typographic contrast deliberately to mark what matters.
  > "memory for unsignaled information often is unaffected"
- **[supporting]** The evidence base is strongest for memory effects and notably thinner for attention, basic reading processes, and comprehension — so claims that typographic hierarchy aids scanning/comprehension are less directly supported than claims that it aids recall of cued items.
  > "less attention has been directed to signaling effects on other cognitive processes, such as attention, basic reading processes, and comprehension"
- **[supporting]** Typographical cues are explicitly enumerated as one studied signaling device alongside titles, headings, overviews and summaries, grounding the practice of using typographic differentiation (e.g., bold, distinct heading faces) as a structural signal in empirical literature.
  > "Findings are reviewed for studies of several different types of signaling devices, including: titles, headings, previews, overviews, summaries, typographical cues, recall sentences, number signals, importance indicators, and summary indicators."

#### Source `a8c09603` — quality: **blog** · published: 2026-01-12
- **[supporting]** Typewolf (Jeremiah Shoaf) defines a font as 'body text friendly' by a concrete set of typographic properties: it must contain regular, italic and bold styles and have low-to-moderate stroke contrast, large counters, open apertures and a large x-height. This is a [CONVENTION] checklist for which faces suit the body role versus a display/heading role.
  > "An asterisk indicates the family is body text friendly, meaning it contains regular, italic and bold styles and has low-to-moderate stroke contrast, large counters, open apertures and a large x-height."
- **[supporting]** Specific Google Fonts that Typewolf marks as body-text friendly include Inter, Work Sans, Roboto, and Merriweather, while it also catalogs monospace faces such as Inconsolata and Space Mono — concrete named candidates for the body-vs-code functional roles.
  > "Examples marked body-text friendly include Inter, Space Mono, Work Sans, Roboto, and Merriweather. ... Inconsolata is listed as a monospace option."
- **[tangential]** The page is an expert-curated selection ('The 40 Best Google Fonts') by a named typography authority (Jeremiah Shoaf / Typewolf), last updated January 12, 2026 — making it a [CONVENTION]/curation source rather than evidence, and it defers actual pairing recommendations to a separate guide rather than stating pairing principles itself.
  > "The author directs readers to "The Definitive Guide to Free Fonts" for "recommended pairings" but that detailed guide is not included in the provided webpage content."

#### Source `a9a6e77b` — quality: **blog** · published: 2016-04-29
- **[supporting]** Tim Brown, Head of Typography at Adobe Typekit, authored the 'Combining Typefaces' guide (originally 2013), making it an attributable [CONVENTION] source on typeface pairing from a named foundry authority.
  > "Tim Brown, Head of Typography for Adobe Typekit, published this guide originally in 2013 through Five Simple Steps."
- **[central]** Typeface combination is framed as partly craft/taste rather than rule-bound, supporting the research question's stance that pairing aesthetics is mostly taste with limited hard evidence.
  > "Successful typeface combinations are partly a matter of good taste, which can be tough to develop."
- **[supporting]** The recommended method offers a reasoning framework for why combinations succeed or fail rather than rigid prescriptive rules, i.e. pairing is treated as judgment, not a formula.
  > "The document provides "a framework for efficient practice" rather than rigid rules, focusing on understanding *why* combinations work or fail."
- **[central]** Typeface selection should be driven by real design goals and the functional 'jobs that typefaces do', aligning with the functional-differentiation premise (typeface as a signal of content type/role).
  > "Strategies for selecting typefaces based on real design goals ... covering designers, anatomy, families, classification, and the jobs that typefaces do"
- **[supporting]** The correct way to validate a typeface combination is empirical use and careful observation in context, not abstract pairing charts.
  > "Practical advice about identifying successful typeface combinations by using them and looking at them carefully"

#### Source `aa2530c9` — quality: **blog** · published: 2024-04-15
- **[central]** Effective font pairing requires contrast: faces must differ enough in classification (serif vs sans-serif vs script vs display), size, and weight to read as intentional, while sharing some attribute that makes them feel related.
  > "Similar enough in certain attributes as to feel related. Different enough in classification (serif, sans-serif, script, display, etc), size and weight to provide sufficient contrast."
- **[central]** Fonts that are only slightly different (near-but-not-equal) read as an unintentional mistake rather than a deliberate pairing, signalling messiness/unprofessionalism — the conflict-not-contrast pitfall.
  > "The problem comes when an undiscerning eye might just perceive them as similar, which causes a connotation of messiness."
- **[supporting]** Paired faces should be clearly distinct, not near-identical; the goal is complementary difference, not 'fraternal twins.'
  > "Make sure to choose fonts that are different enough to complement each other, without looking like they're fraternal (not identical) twins."
- **[supporting]** Convention is to limit a design to two typefaces and master two-font pairing before introducing a third.
  > "You don't need to limit fonts to two, but make sure you can master using two before moving to three."
- **[supporting]** Functional/hierarchical differentiation is achieved by assigning distinct faces to distinct roles — e.g., a slab-serif display heading (ChunkFive), a serif italic subheading (Crimson Text Italic), and a sans body (Montserrat).
  > "The largest heading is a slab-serif ChunkFive... The serif subheading Crimson Text Italic complements it... followed by the bolded Montserrat side-note and the Montserrat paragraph and link."

#### Source `aa30ef5f` — quality: **blog** · published: No date on page; part of Butterick's Practical Typography (Matthew Butterick), 2nd ed. 2019
- **[central]** [CONVENTION] Tabular figures (equal-width digits) are required for vertically aligned columns of numbers such as data tables/grids — a concrete functional-differentiation rule: data/number columns demand tabular figures where prose does not.
  > "But tabular figures are essential for one purpose: vertically aligned columns, like you find in grids of numbers."
- **[central]** [CONVENTION] In running prose, proportional figures are preferred over tabular because they space more evenly — so the correct figure style depends on content type (prose numbers vs columnar data), reinforcing typeface/figure choice as a signal of 'this is a different kind of thing.'
  > "In body text, proportional figures are preferred because they tend to have more even spacing and a more consistent appearance."
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION] Lining figures suit any context and are the right choice in all-caps settings (they match cap height), whereas oldstyle figures belong in lowercase body text and look wrong with caps — figure style should track the surrounding text style.
  > "Lining figures can be used in any situation. Lining figures are always preferred for all caps text because they come closest to cap height."
- **[tangential]** A concrete falsifiable test detects whether a font provides tabular figures: set a row of zeros above a row of ones; equal line lengths indicate tabular (fixed-width) digits.
  > "To check if your font has tabular figures, type a line of zeroes above a line of ones. If they're the same length, then your font has tabular figures."

#### Source `aa5fabec` — quality: **primary** · published: 2019-08-04
- **[central]** The 'layer-cake' scanning pattern (eye-tracking): users fixate mostly on headings and subheadings, dropping into body text only occasionally, producing alternating horizontal stripes in gaze data. This is direct eye-tracking evidence that distinct headings structure how users scan a page.
  > "fixations made mostly on the page's headings and subheadings, with deliberate occasional fixations on the (body) text in between"
- **[central]** Clear typographic hierarchy aids scanning effectiveness: NNG calls the layer-cake pattern the most effective scanning behavior and links it to users successfully finding target information when present. Supports the [STRONG] 'hierarchy-aids-scanning' claim (distinct from which-fonts-to-pair conventions).
  > "by far the most effective way to scan pages ... most of the time, it ensures that users will find the information they are looking for (if it is present on the page)"
- **[supporting]** The benefit is conditional on headings being BOTH visually distinct and descriptive (meaningful content), not merely styled differently — i.e., contrast must be in service of communication. This grounds functional differentiation: headings must visually signal 'different kind of thing' and carry scannable meaning.
  > "subheadings visually stand out on the page and are descriptive, users engage in an efficient scanning pattern"
- **[supporting]** Users deliberately skip body text, reading a body paragraph only when the adjacent heading signals relevance to their goal — the heading acts as the decision gate for whether to read prose. This explains why heading/body typographic differentiation has functional payoff.
  > "the eyes scan directly to headings and may read the related body of text if interesting or relevant to their goal"

#### Source `abf9f51c` — quality: **primary** · published: 2006-04-16
- **[central]** Users do not read web text thoroughly word-by-word; they scan, with eye movement following a dominant F-shaped path (top horizontal sweep, a shorter second horizontal sweep, then a vertical scan down the left edge), established via eye-tracking of 232 users across thousands of pages.
  > "Users won't read your text thoroughly in a word-by-word manner. Exhaustive reading is rare."
- **[supporting]** Because users scan rather than read, the most important information must be front-loaded: the first two paragraphs should carry the key message since later content is unlikely to be read.
  > "the first two paragraphs must state the most important information"
- **[supporting]** During the vertical left-side scan users notice the opening words of headings, subheads, paragraphs and bullets far more than later words, so information-carrying words should lead each element.
  > "Start subheads, paragraphs, and bullet points with information-carrying words"
- **[tangential]** The F-pattern is a rough general tendency, not a fixed rule — users sometimes scan in E-shaped or inverted-L variants depending on content and layout, so structure (headings/formatting) shapes the path rather than the page guaranteeing it.
  > "a rough, general shape rather than a uniform, pixel-perfect behavior"

#### Source `ac788a44` — quality: **secondary** · published: No publish date shown on the page; content owned by Google, reviewed by Doug Wilson, Ellen Lupton, and Thomas Phinney
- **[supporting]** A superfamily is defined as several explicitly related type families (e.g. serif, sans, slab, sometimes monospaced) that all share the same underlying skeletal structure, enabling fonts to be swapped without disrupting layouts. [CONVENTION, Google Fonts foundry]
  > "superfamilies: The collective grouping of several explicitly related type families—such as a serif, sans, and slab—that all share the same underlying structure to their design. Using superfamilies allows us to swap fonts without disrupting our layouts"
- **[central]** The variety within a single family or superfamily provides enough contrast that a second, unrelated typeface is not required — staying within one family yields shared consistency across variations. Bears directly on the 'you don't need a second font' point and weight/style contrast within one family as the first lever. [CONVENTION]
  > "we have enough contrast at our disposal to not require a pairing from another typeface. And the advantage of staying within the family means there’s a shared consistency across every variation."
- **[central]** A second/secondary typeface from a different family should only be introduced when it does something the primary typeface cannot — the rule that gates when a second family 'earns its place.' [CONVENTION]
  > "we should only introduce a secondary typeface if it can do something our primary typeface cannot."
- **[central]** Google Fonts endorses concrete superfamily/within-family pairings fitting a restrained screen register: Merriweather & Merriweather Sans, Roboto Slab & Roboto Mono, Nunito & Nunito Sans, and Quattrocento & Quattrocento Sans, with Breve (DSType) cited as a fuller superfamily example. [CONVENTION]
  > "## Merriweather & Merriweather Sans ... ## Roboto Slab & Roboto Mono ... ## Nunito & Nunito Sans ... ## Quattrocento & Quattrocento Sans"
- **[supporting]** Within-family pairing preserves harmony because the related fonts share not just skeletal structure but the same spacing, proportions, motifs (e.g. ink traps, stroke thickness, angles) and vertical metrics/x-height — explaining why near-but-different is avoided and contrast still reads as intentional. [CONVENTION]
  > "it’s not simply the skeletal structure that’s shared across the different fonts: we’ll also benefit from the same spacing settings, the same proportions, and the same motifs being used throughout the designs."

#### Source `ac81afeb` — quality: **secondary** · published: 2026-06-06
- **[central]** [CONVENTION] Pair by contrast, not similarity: two near-identical typefaces (e.g. two geometric sans-serifs) read as an error rather than an intentional choice, because viewers fixate on the tiny mismatches in x-height/terminals. Attributed to Charlie Beeson (design director, FutureBrand) and Alice Munday (design director, Curious).
  > ""Pairing two geometric sans-serifs that are very similar doesn't look like a choice, it looks like a mistake," says Charlie Beeson, design director at FutureBrand. "Viewers get hung up on those tiny yet jarring differences in x-heights or terminals, creating a visual itch." ... Alice Munday ... agrees. "Using two fonts that are too similar in style can create a disjointed feeling and the decision to be different feels like it lacks intention," she says. "Why use both if they do the same job?""
- **[central]** [CONVENTION] Functional differentiation / one-anchor-one-voice: each typeface should have a defined role — a display face carries character and presence, a body face prioritises clarity and rhythm; when both compete for attention you get tension, not hierarchy. Attributed to Mat Desjardins (founder/creative director, Pangram Pangram).
  > ""A display face can bring character and presence, while a body font should focus on clarity and rhythm. When both try to stand out, they end up competing instead of working together." ... "Let one speak, and make sure the other knows when to stay quiet.""
- **[central]** [CONVENTION] Hierarchy and weight/style WITHIN one family is the first lever: using the same style and weight across headers, subheads and body makes a design flat; exploiting a family's full range of weights/styles brings contrast even to neutral typefaces, and two-to-three distinct type styles is the recommended ceiling. Attributed to Jenny Truong (associate creative director, Park & Battery).
  > ""It's vital to take advantage of the font family's full range of weights and styles, as these bring personality and contrast to even the most neutral typefaces." ... "Sticking to no more than two or three distinct type styles is the sweet spot," Jenny advises."
- **[central]** [MYTH/CONVENTION] You do not always need a second font: a single typeface (or a typeface with optical-size/tracking-axis variations) is often enough for a distinctive, functional system, and adding fonts without reason dilutes recognition and adds complexity. Attributed to Natasha Lucas (visual-identity designer); optical-size point illustrated with Dalton Maag's Haas Recast.
  > ""One of the most common mistakes in font pairing is assuming that every brand needs multiple typefaces," says Natasha. "In many cases, a single typeface is enough to create a distinctive and highly functional identity system. Introducing additional fonts without clear consideration can dilute brand recognition and create unnecessary complexity for execution.""
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION] Superfamily pairing as the safe route for serif+sans: rather than pairing two independent serifs (which demands expertise and can confuse readers when faces are close in origin but different in expression), use a super-family containing matched serif and sans designed to work together for a more polished result. Attributed to Riccardo De Franceschi (creative director, Dalton Maag), citing Aldgate and Bankside.
  > ""Keeping it simple and opting for a super-family that includes both sans and serif families that were designed to work together will deliver a more polished user experience," he says. Riccardo points to Aldgate and Bankside, both from the Dalton Maag library ... "They both deliver a healthy visual contrast, but feel like they belong together as harmonious pairs.""

#### Source `ad2aa81e` — quality: **blog** · published: No publication date for the Inter profile; curated pairings range from December 2019 to May 2025
- **[supporting]** Inter is a neo-grotesque sans-serif designed by Rasmus Andersson specifically to work well on screens as a UI font, with a large x-height and nine weights plus a variable font version.
  > "open-source sans-serif typeface designed by Swedish designer/programmer Rasmus Andersson... designed to work well on screens as a UI font... features a large x-height... The family is available in nine weights with matching italics, as well as a variable font version."
- **[central]** Typewolf curates Inter pairings predominantly as a sans UI/body face paired with a contrasting serif display face (e.g. its suggested pairing is Inter + Bluu, and real-world examples pair Inter with serifs like Martina Plantijn, Reckless, and Editorial New).
  > "Inter + Bluu (listed under "Suggested Font Pairing")... combinations with fonts like: Martina Plantijn, Reckless, Family, National 2, Editorial New, Right Grotesk, Whyte, Space Mono, Agrandir"
- **[supporting]** Typewolf's pairing guidance is curation-by-example only: it presents 40+ real-world Inter combinations without stating any rationale for why the pairings work, so the contrast principle is implied by selection rather than argued from evidence.
  > "No explicit guidance is provided on *why* these pairings work together."
- **[tangential]** Inter's nearest comparable faces are themselves neo-grotesque UI/screen sans-serifs (Maison Neue, San Francisco, and Neue Haas Unica as the closest Adobe Fonts match), confirming its register as a quiet, modern, screen-first workhorse rather than a voice/display face.
  > "Similar Fonts Listed: Maison Neue, San Francisco... Closest Match on Adobe Fonts: Neue Haas Unica"

#### Source `ad9e08d8` — quality: **blog** · published: 2023-10-01
- **[central]** Pairing by contrast still requires at least one shared attribute (stroke weight, angle/curve, x-height, or mood) to tie the faces together; fonts that are near-but-not-identical fall into an 'uncanny valley' that reads as a mistake rather than an intentional choice.
  > "What you don't want to do, however is use something too similar...when you put together two very similar fonts you end up with the 'uncanny valley' effect where it can just look like a mistake."
- **[central]** Superfamilies are a low-risk pairing solution because the styles share one skeleton (built-in similarity) while differing in style to supply the contrast.
  > "They already have similarities built in, as they share the same skeleton, and you can find that contrast from using two different styles."
- **[supporting]** Pairing a serif with a sans serif is a canonical way to introduce contrast between two faces.
  > "Another classic example of contrast is pairing a serif with a sans serif."
- **[supporting]** The most common font-pairing scenario is a heading face combined with a body-text face (or vice versa), and an 'anchor' face should be chosen to assign these roles.
  > "The most common reasons you are looking to pair fonts are a heading with some body text (or vice versa)"
- **[tangential]** Contrast can come from combining a very wide and a very condensed face, provided they share a similarity such as weight.
  > "put together a super wide and a super condensed font (as long as they share a similarity, like weight for example)"

#### Source `add32d6c` — quality: **secondary** · published: 2013-04-23
- **[central]** Tim Brown's core method is to choose one 'anchor' typeface first, and to make the body-text face the anchor whenever possible — because body text is the majority of the content and text faces survive a range of sizes/resolutions that display faces cannot. This is the 'pick the body/text face first' model. [CONVENTION — Tim Brown]
  > "One typeface will anchor the other(s). It['ll] help set the underlying tone of your experience, and act as a reference point for every element in your composition. Find it early and know it well. Make your body text typeface the anchor if possible, because this text represents a majority of your content ... and because text faces are built to withstand a variety of settings, whereas display faces can rarely be used at small sizes or at coarse resolutions."
- **[central]** Brown insists pairing works on underlying COMPATIBILITY, not surface SIMILARITY — two shapes can look different yet share structure, which matters more than looking alike. This grounds the 'pair by contrast, not near-sameness' principle. [CONVENTION — Tim Brown]
  > "Remember that compatibility and similarity are not the same thing. For example, a square and a circle are very di[fferent], but both are strict geometric forms that have more in common with one another than either has with a more calligraphic shape."
- **[central]** Brown frames three combining strategies and is explicit that a SECOND family is not required: a single type family (exploiting its weights/widths/features) or a single typeface can supply 'a multitude of possibilities'; a superfamily (serif+sans from one designer) is a second option; multiple families a third. Weight/width within one family is the first lever. [CONVENTION — Tim Brown]
  > "Read any article or book about mixing fonts (this one included) and you['ll find] all of the following advice: stick with a single type family (making use of its various weights, widths and features); use a superfamily ... Even sticking with a single typeface (for example, the complete Proxima Nova family) provides a multitude of possibilities, particularly if that face o[ffers] ... weights, widths and styles."
- **[central]** Brown's functional-differentiation thesis: every compositional 'chunk' is a job (prose to read, headings to catch attention, navigation, data to gather quickly), and you pick the face that fits that job — e.g. a geometric sans to 'clearly and quietly' supply navigation/structured information, an editorial serif (FF Meta Serif) for a 'conversational tone, while also conveying authority' in critique/body text. Typeface signals the kind of content. [CONVENTION — Tim Brown]
  > "Compositional chunks are like job openings ... Is its purpose to catch and direct readers' attention? Is it informational, meant for readers to pore over and reference? Is its purpose to help people navigate, or gather data quickly? Whatever [you]'re trying to do, a typeface was made for that purpose. ... Geometric sans serif typefaces are great for this job ... Neutral and balanced, it clearly and quietly supplies structured information in the form of navigation"
- **[supporting]** Brown is explicit that pairing is largely craft/taste with no formula or 'correct answers' — successful combinations are 'partly a matter of good taste' developed through practice, not a rule set; he quotes Tschichold that there are 'no born masters of typography.' This matches an honest framing of pairing as mostly taste with evidence sparse. [CONVENTION — Tim Brown, citing Jan Tschichold]
  > "Successful combinations are partly a matter of good taste, which comes with experience but is tough to develop. ... there are no correct answers, and as with many aspects of the design process it[']s [iterative] ... As a rule, impeccable taste springs partly from inborn sensitivity ... there are no born masters of typography, but self-education may lead in time to mastery."

#### Source `ae0f3534` — quality: **primary** · published: 2001-07
- **[central]** In a controlled experiment, college students who read a 12-topic expository text WITH headings remembered the text's topics and their organizational structure better than students who read the identical text WITHOUT headings — controlled evidence that structural signals (headings) aid recall of content organization, the comprehension/scanning side of typographic hierarchy.
  > "participants who received training and/or read the text with headings remembered text topics and their organization better"
- **[supporting]** Headings do not merely decorate; they change how readers cognitively process text — the authors concluded the signals shift readers' encoding and recall strategies, supporting the principle that visually distinguishing structural elements communicates 'this is a different kind of thing' and alters processing.
  > "signals induce a change in readers' strategies for encoding and recalling text"
- **[supporting]** The evidence is a between-subjects controlled manipulation (heading vs. no-heading on the same 12-topic text, crossed with mental-outline training vs. none), qualifying it as [STRONG] experimental evidence rather than convention — though it tests headings as content signals, not typeface/font-pairing contrast specifically.
  > "College students read a 12-topic expository text, with half receiving training to construct mental outlines during reading. Half read text with headings; half read the same text without headings."

#### Source `ae221926` — quality: **primary** · published: 2004-04 (Learning and Instruction, Vol. 14, Issue 2; authors Jukka Hyönä and Robert F. Lorch Jr.)
- **[central]** Controlled eye-tracking evidence: adding topic headings to multi-topic expository text improved comprehension processing of topic sentences AND raised recall, measured as more topics appearing in readers' written summaries. This is direct experimental support that structural/typographic hierarchy aids comprehension and recall, not just aesthetics.
  > "The presence of topic headings facilitated the processing of topic sentences and increased the number of topics mentioned in the text summaries written after reading the texts."
- **[supporting]** Headings changed measurable eye-movement (scanning) behavior in two distinct phases: enhanced fixations during initial first-pass reading and increased deliberate look-backs to topic sentences. This ties the comprehension benefit to an observed change in how the eye scans the page, supporting the rationale for visually distinct headings.
  > "The facilitatory effect of headings was reflected both in the fixations made during the first-pass reading as well as in the later look-backs directed to the topic sentences."
- **[supporting]** The study is a within-subjects controlled experiment (each adult reader saw one text version with topic headings signaling topic shifts and one without), which qualifies the heading-benefit finding as [STRONG] controlled evidence rather than convention or opinion.
  > "Adult participants read two texts, one in which topic shifts were signaled by topic headings and one in which topic headings were excluded."
- **[tangential]** Scope caveat for the skill: the manipulation was the presence vs. absence of headings as content/structure cues, not the typeface or font pairing used to render them. The evidence supports that distinct headings aid scanning and comprehension, but does NOT speak to which fonts to pair or whether a second typeface (vs. weight/size/case contrast within one family) produces the benefit.
  > "Effects of topic headings on text processing: evidence from adult readers' eye fixation patterns"

#### Source `af44bf86` — quality: **primary** · published: Last updated August 6, 2025 (Copyright © 2024 IBM)
- **[central]** IBM Plex is a single unified superfamily spanning sans, serif, and monospace (plus a condensed sans) — one type system designed to cover heading, body, UI, and code roles from a common design, exemplifying superfamily pairing across functional differentiation rather than mixing unrelated families.
  > "Select Font IBM Plex Sans IBM Plex Sans Italic IBM Plex Mono IBM Plex Mono Italic IBM Plex Serif IBM Plex Serif Italic IBM Plex Sans Condensed IBM Plex Sans Condensed Italic"
- **[supporting]** The Plex system ships a continuous weight ramp from Thin through Black (Thin, Extra Light, Light, Regular, Text, Medium, Semi Bold, Bold, Black), supporting the 'weight contrast within one family' lever for hierarchy and emphasis without adding a second family.
  > "Select Weight Thin Extra Light Light Regular Text Medium Semi Bold Bold 12 400 Black"
- **[supporting]** Plex's stated design intent is a single corporate typeface meant to be global and versatile — i.e., one cohesive system rather than an assembled set of separate faces.
  > "IBM Plex® is our new typeface. It’s global, it’s versatile and it’s distinctly IBM"
- **[tangential]** The serif member is a slab serif whose detailing deliberately uses internal stroke/angle contrast against the rounded exterior — design provenance from IBM heritage, illustrating that the superfamily's members share a controlled, intentional contrast rather than being near-identical.
  > "The first (and most obvious) detail borrowed was the slab serif ... we adopted the vertical stroke and right angle of the interior counters, which contrast nicely against the rounded curves of the exterior stroke"

#### Source `affe27c1` — quality: **primary** · published: 2001 (Contemporary Educational Psychology, vol. 26, pp. 171-191)
- **[central]** Controlled summarization experiments show that text topics introduced by a heading (signaled) are more likely to be included in readers' summaries than topics without a heading — direct evidence that headings shape readers' representation of a text's topic structure. This is STRONG controlled evidence that hierarchy/signaling markers aid topic identification, though it concerns headings as a structural device rather than typeface contrast per se.
  > "In all experiments, topics were more likely to be included in a summary if they were signaled than if they were not signaled."
- **[central]** The heading effect is contrastive/relative, not absolute: when only half the topics carried headings, signaled topics were summarized even more often than when all topics had headings, while unsignaled topics were summarized less often than when no topics had headings — i.e., a marked-vs-unmarked contrast amplifies what reads as important. This supports the 'hierarchy by contrast' principle (selective emphasis communicates; uniform emphasis dilutes).
  > "This effect was magnified when the text was only half signaled: Signaled topics were more likely to appear in a summary if only half the text topics were signaled than if all of the topics were signaled; however, unsignaled topics were less likely to appear in a summary if half of the text topics were signaled than if none of the text topics were signaled."
- **[supporting]** The study's explicit purpose was to test whether headings change readers' mental representation of a text's topic structure (not merely aesthetics) — grounding the claim that hierarchy cues function as communication about what kind/importance of content a reader is looking at.
  > "A summarization task was used to study whether headings influence readers' representations of the topic structure of a text."
- **[supporting]** The effect was established with controlled manipulation (full headings vs. half headings vs. no headings) across multiple participant populations (college students and 6th/8th graders), qualifying it as STRONG, replicated experimental evidence rather than convention or opinion.
  > "College students (Experiments 1–3) and sixth- and eighth-graders (Experiment 3) summarized a multiple topic text that (a) included headings introducing every new subtopic, (b) included headings introducing half of the new subtopics, or (c) included no headings."

### Type in charts, graphs & tables (fonts + figures)

#### Source `a011d808` — quality: **blog** · published: 2013-07-08 (updated 2022-08-09)
- **[supporting]** There are exactly four figure-set styles formed by crossing two axes — width (tabular vs proportional) and height/style (lining vs old-style): tabular lining, tabular old-style, proportional lining, and proportional old-style.
  > "Tabular Lining (.tf suffix) ... Tabular Old-Style (.tosf suffix) ... Proportional Lining (.lf suffix) ... Proportional Old-Style (.osf suffix)"
- **[central]** These four figure sets are exposed through four standard OpenType feature tags that a font can ship: lnum (lining), onum (old-style), pnum (proportional), and tnum (tabular).
  > "the tutorial states that Glyphs auto-generates four OpenType features: lnum (lining numerals), onum (old-style numerals), pnum (proportional numerals), tnum (tabular numerals)"
- **[central]** Proportional figures are defined by varying digit widths, whereas tabular figures share a fixed width — the property that lets tabular figures align vertically in columns of numbers.
  > "If the width difference of all default figures exceeds one unit, they are considered proportional."
- **[supporting]** Old-style figures are distinguished from lining figures by having reduced-height (descending/ascending) numerals; Glyphs detects them by measuring whether the zero is markedly smaller than the eight.
  > "If the zero is more than 17 percent smaller than eight, the default figures are deemed old-style."

#### Source `a1238bf0` — quality: **blog** · published: 2019-06-18
- **[central]** Tabular figures give each numeral the same advance width so digits align in columns in data or financial tables; the OpenType feature is exposed via CSS font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums.
  > "Each numeral occupies the same size space so they line up nicely in columns when used in data or financial tables"
- **[central]** Proportional figures (the default for many faces) produce more pleasing results in running text and should be used unless numerals must line up in a table — implying proportional figures are wrong for number columns.
  > "Unless you need to line up numerals in a table, you'll get more pleasing results by specifying proportional spacing"
- **[supporting]** Lining figures all share equal height and sit on the baseline (a.k.a. titling figures), making them the standard style suited to headings/formal contexts; CSS: font-variant-numeric: lining-nums.
  > "Also known as 'Titling Figures', these are the most standard ones. They have equal heights, and line up on the baseline"
- **[supporting]** Old-style (text) figures have uneven heights with ascenders/descenders and are designed to sit inline with lowercase letters, suiting running mixed-case prose; CSS: font-variant-numeric: oldstyle-nums.
  > "Sometimes referred to as 'Text Figures', this style of numeral tends to have an older feel to them and are designed to sit in line with lower case letters"
- **[supporting]** Figure style and spacing are two independent axes (lining/old-style crossed with tabular/proportional), but not every typeface ships all four combinations, and open-source faces with the full set are hard to find.
  > "Not every typeface has all the styles and spacing of numerals, but more have them than you might realize"

#### Source `a12ff7d4` — quality: **primary** · published: 2001
- **[central]** The 'visual span' — the number of letters that can be recognized in a single glance/fixation — imposes a fundamental upper limit on reading speed; it measures ~10+ letters in central vision and shrinks to ~1.7 letters at 15deg eccentricity. This is direct vision-science support that glance-legibility of individual characters bounds reading throughput, the regime in which chart/axis/table text is read.
  > "We propose that the size of the visual span - the number of letters recognizable in a glance - imposes a fundamental limit on reading speed... The size of the visual span decreased from at least 10 letters in central vision to 1.7 letters at 15deg eccentricity"
- **[central]** For simple text read in central vision (low error rate — the normal case for a sighted reader scanning a chart), letter recognition itself is the rate-limiting bottleneck for reading speed. This grounds the typography rule that making individual letterforms/digits easy to recognize at small sizes (open apertures, disambiguated forms) is what governs fast glance-reading, not stylistic preference.
  > "When the error rate is low, typically true for simple texts in central vision, letter recognition forms the bottleneck for reading speed."
- **[supporting]** A parameter-free model that took only measured per-position letter-recognition accuracy (the visual-span profile) plus the simplest lexical-matching rule was sufficient to predict observed RSVP reading speed in central vision (model ~800–1000 wpm bracketing the human mean). This quantitatively links single-letter legibility to reading speed.
  > "the letter-recognition data, together with the simplest possible lexical-matching operation, are sufficient to account for RSVP reading speed in central vision"
- **[supporting]** Reading speed increases with print size only up to a 'critical print size' and then plateaus at a maximum; below that size, speed falls. This supports a minimum-comfortable-size floor for chart/table text — text shrunk below the legibility-limited size measurably slows reading.
  > "At all eccentricities, reading speed rose with print size until a critical print size was reached, and then leveled out at a maximum value."
- **[tangential]** The study rendered all stimuli in a single monospaced typeface (Courier Bold), dark-on-white at ~90% contrast, and varied eccentricity, size and exposure time — not typeface. It therefore offers NO typeface-comparison evidence and cannot be cited for choosing one font over another; its contribution is the letter-recognition-to-reading-speed mechanism, not font recommendations.
  > "The words were rendered in Courier Bold and were displayed as dark letters on a white (110 cd/m2) background with a contrast of ca. 90%."

#### Source `a4234bb5` — quality: **primary** · published: ?
- **[central]** ONS sets a 14px floor for chart text elements, with 12px allowed only in small multiples [CONVENTION].
  > "The minimum font size for chart text elements is 14 pixels (px) for legibility."
- **[central]** The only sanctioned exception to the 14px minimum is small-multiple charts, which may drop to 12px [CONVENTION].
  > "In small multiple charts, you can go down to 12px if necessary."
- **[supporting]** All chart text should be sentence case and run horizontally — i.e. avoid ALL-CAPS and rotated labels [CONVENTION].
  > "All text in charts should use sentence case and run horizontally"
- **[supporting]** ONS uses Open Sans (fallbacks Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif) with Stylistic Set 1 enabled for an accessible lowercase g and a serifed capital I, and ships weights 400/600/700 — letterform-disambiguation and weight restraint via OpenType features rather than heavy display faces [CONVENTION].
  > "Font: Open Sans with fallbacks: "Open Sans, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif" ... Stylistic Set 1 recommended for accessibility features (accessible lowercase "g" and serifed capital "I")"
- **[supporting]** Body/chart text uses off-black #222222 rather than pure black, with muted greys (#707071 axis labels, #414042 category labels/legends) — text contrast is deliberately softened so labels defer to the data [CONVENTION].
  > "Default text: Black #222222 (not pure black #000000) - Axis labels: Grey 75 (#707071) - Category labels/legends: Grey 100 (#414042)"

#### Source `a48ad48f` — quality: **secondary** · published: ?
- **[central]** Tabular lining numerals are the recommended figure style for tables, charts, and timetables — any context where numbers must line up vertically. This directly supports the skill's default for tables/axes/number columns.
  > "tabular lining numerals work well for tables, charts, timetables, or any scenario where figures are required to line up vertically."
- **[central]** Tabular numerals work by giving every digit identical horizontal width (i.e., monospaced figures), which keeps columns aligned even when the displayed values change.
  > "Tabular lining numerals have uppercase-like glyphs that take up exactly the same horizontal space. Because of this, they're sometimes referred to as monospaced numerals."
- **[supporting]** Proportional lining numerals are the recommended catch-all default for running body copy, because type is used for prose far more often than for tables; this delimits where proportional figures are appropriate (prose, not number columns).
  > "If in doubt, use proportional lining numerals: They're the best catch-all for the majority of situations. Type is used for body copy more often than it's used for tables"
- **[supporting]** The four figure styles are not always present in a font (the designer often draws only one); when present they are selected via OpenType feature controls, and the font's default figure style reflects the designer's intended use. This grounds the pitfall of relying on a font's (often proportional) default in tables and the use of OpenType figure features.
  > "if they're present in the file, you can choose the one you want using OpenType feature controls. If in doubt, use the figure style the type designer has chosen as the default."
- **[supporting]** The preference for lining over oldstyle figures is convention, not a demonstrated reading advantage; oldstyle figures are described as more comfortable in body text because their ascenders/descenders don't interrupt the lowercase reading flow. This matches the prior typography finding that preference is not the same as measured reading performance.
  > "while there's no real reason lining numerals are preferable to oldstyle, they're commonly accepted as the norm."

#### Source `a55fe002` — quality: **primary** · published: Font released 2019; page has no explicit publish date (references a 2024 Smithsonian acquisition, so page current as of 2024+)
- **[supporting]** Atkinson Hyperlegible was created by the Braille Institute (2019, named after founder J. Robert Atkinson) expressly to improve legibility for low-vision readers — establishing WHO backs the face for topic (3): an institution-backed legibility-first typeface, not a foundry aesthetic pick.
  > "improve legibility and readability for individuals with low vision."
- **[central]** The face's core design goal is letterform disambiguation — making each character unique and pulling apart confusable shapes (its specimens show pairs like B/8, O/0, 1/I/i/l, p/q). This is direct design rationale for topic (2): disambiguated digits/letters to reduce reading errors.
  > "special design principles to differentiate characters and make each one unique"
- **[supporting]** The specific disambiguation mechanisms are concrete and checkable: distinctly differentiated similarly-shaped uprights (bears on 1 vs l vs I), large open counters, and angled spurs plus longer tails — the same legibility levers (open apertures, distinct letterforms) the research question asks about for small chart/axis/table text.
  > "Angled spurs and longer tails help increase differentiation."
- **[tangential]** Note for evidence-tier tagging: these are the creator/institution's own design assertions, not controlled-study results — so they ground a [CONVENTION] rule (institution-backed legibility face) rather than [STRONG] peer-reviewed evidence; the page cites adoption and awards (153K+ downloads, Fast Company 2019, Smithsonian 2024), not reading-speed or error-rate trials.
  > "Matching letter pairs are clearer and easier to identify."

#### Source `a5621874` — quality: **primary** · published: 2026-06-23
- **[central]** [CONVENTION] IBM Carbon recommends direct labelling on the chart over legends where possible, supporting Topic 4's direct-labels-vs-legends point and consistent with the charting skill.
  > "When possible, use labels directly on the chart to avoid long legends."
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION] Carbon prescribes concise chart text, aligning with keeping chart type/labels quiet so they defer to the data.
  > "All the text should be concise and easy to understand."
- **[supporting]** [CONVENTION] Carbon warns against overcrowding the graph frame, echoing the data-ink/anti-chartjunk principle of the charting skill.
  > "Avoid filling the chart frame with too many elements, as it impacts the user’s ability to interpret the data."
- **[tangential]** [CONVENTION] Carbon defines the KPI/big-number pattern as a number plus a single-word description, relevant to dashboard data typography.
  > "A key performance indicator (KPI) consists of a number with a single word description."

#### Source `a59357b1` — quality: **primary** · published: 2019-12 (Vision Research, vol. 165, pp. 162–172; published online 2019-11-16). DOI 10.1016/j.visres.2019.05.003. NOTE: sole author is Charles Bigelow, not 'Beier, Sawallich et al.' as the task's attribution stated.
- **[supporting]** Through most of the 20th century, type-legibility experiments manipulated traditional typographic variables — capitals vs lowercase, roman vs italic, light vs bold weight, width, and size — to determine which typefaces and type sizes were legible. This establishes the canonical, evidence-tested variable set (case, weight, width, size) that a chart-typography skill draws on for rules like 'avoid heavy/ALL-CAPS labels' and 'set a minimum size.'
  > "For most of the 20th century, legibility studies were aimed at determining legible typefaces and type sizes to aid publishing, education, and other areas of reading. Experimental font variables were usually features familiar in traditional typographic fonts, such as capitals versus lowercase, roman versus italic, light versus bold weight, width, and size."
- **[supporting]** Modern legibility research uses image-processing methods to quantify character features such as spatial frequency and perimetric complexity — the measurable letterform properties (complexity, stroke/aperture structure) that underlie small-size legibility and letter/digit disambiguation. This grounds the claim that legibility-at-a-glance can be measured objectively rather than by preference.
  > "the use of imaging processing methods to investigate font and character features like spatial frequency and perimetric complexity"
- **[tangential]** Digital typography tools, precision measurement of type forms, and non-paper reading media enabled new legibility research, including purpose-designed experimental fonts for low vision as well as normal vision — i.e., legibility can be engineered into letterforms (the premise behind hyperlegible/disambiguated faces).
  > "These advances have resulted in new areas of legibility research, including the creation of experimental fonts for low vision as well as normal vision, a closer rapprochement of legibility study with other fields of vision research"
- **[tangential]** Recent work tests non-traditional reading conditions and font features absent from print typography — RSVP (rapid serial visual presentation) displays, and color and animation in fonts — signaling that legibility findings from static print do not automatically transfer to screen/dense-UI contexts.
  > "the use of computer-aided reading displays like RSVP (Rapid Serial Visual Presentation), use of color and animation in fonts, rare in traditional typography except in movie titles and advertising text"

#### Source `a60d0e07` — quality: **primary** · published: ?
- **[central]** The CSS value tabular-nums (OpenType tnum) selects figures that are all the same width, allowing numbers to be easily aligned like in tables — the mechanical basis for using tabular figures in columns of numbers, axes, and data tables.
  > "tabular-nums ... activating the set of figures where numbers are all of the same size, allowing them to be easily aligned like in tables"
- **[central]** The CSS value proportional-nums (OpenType pnum) selects figures of varying widths (numbers not all the same size), which is why proportional figures break vertical alignment in number columns and are suited to running prose rather than tables.
  > "proportional-nums ... activating the set of figures where numbers are not all of the same size"
- **[central]** lining-nums (OpenType lnum) selects figures that all sit on the baseline, while oldstyle-nums (onum) selects figures where some digits (3, 4, 7, 9) have descenders — distinguishing lining figures (default for data) from old-style figures (suited to running text).
  > "lining-nums ... activating the set of figures where numbers are all lying on the baseline ... oldstyle-nums ... activating the set of figures where some numbers, like 3, 4, 7, 9 have descenders"
- **[supporting]** slashed-zero (OpenType zero) forces a zero with a slash, which is useful when a clear distinction between the letter O and the digit 0 is needed — the CSS mechanism for letterform/digit disambiguation in data text.
  > "slashed-zero ... forces the use of a 0 with a slash; this is useful when a clear distinction between O and 0 is needed"
- **[tangential]** CSS provides diagonal-fractions (frac) and stacked-fractions (afrc) that render numerator/denominator smaller separated by a slash or a horizontal line, plus ordinal (ordn) glyphs for markers like 1st/2nd/3rd — OpenType numeric features available for figure settings beyond tabular/lining choices.
  > "diagonal-fractions ... activating the set of figures where the numerator and denominator are made smaller and separated by a slash ... stacked-fractions ... made smaller, stacked and separated by a horizontal line"

#### Source `a7147e33` — quality: **blog** · published: 2022-09-28
- **[central]** Datawrapper (Lisa Charlotte Muth) recommends chart text default to web-typical settings: sans-serif, regular weight, sentence case (not ALL CAPS), neither overly narrow nor wide, larger than 12px, and (almost) black. This is a [CONVENTION] floor for size/weight/case in chart text.
  > "sans-serif, regular, sentence case, neither overly narrow nor wide, >12px, (almost) black text"
- **[supporting]** Use only two clearly-distinct levels of text hierarchy in a chart, exemplified by a smaller gray label vs. a larger black label (e.g., 12px gray and 14px black) — concrete px guidance for label vs. secondary text. [CONVENTION]
  > "Use only two levels of hierarchy that are clearly different from each other—like a 12px gray and a 14px black"
- **[central]** Direct labelling is preferred over a separate color key/legend: remove the legend and place category labels as close as possible to the elements they describe, so readers don't track back and forth. Consistent with the charting skill's direct-labelling rule. [CONVENTION]
  > "Remove the color key and directly label your categories"
- **[supporting]** Left- or right-aligned text reads as tidier than center-aligned because all lines share a common x-position; center/right alignment should be avoided for text above ~10 words. [CONVENTION] on label alignment.
  > "Left- or right-aligned text looks tidier than center-aligned text"
- **[supporting]** Narrow/condensed typefaces and shrinking font size to fit are discouraged; the recommended fix for cramped labels is tooltips or relocating text rather than reducing legibility. [CONVENTION] cautioning against condensed faces / too-small text.
  > "There is almost always another solution"

#### Source `a7e42610` — quality: **primary** · published: 2022-05 (Applied Ergonomics vol. 101, art. 103709; published online 2022-02-22)
- **[central]** [STRONG] Controlled experiment: letters with CLOSED apertures (counters) are recognized less accurately than the same letters with OPEN apertures — direct empirical support for the rule that data-viz faces should have open apertures for small/glance legibility.
  > "The findings showed that participants had more trouble identifying the letter if it was set in a font variation with closed apertures."
- **[central]** The long-standing typographic CONVENTION that large counters/apertures aid legibility was the explicit hypothesis under test here — this paper is the evidence that converts that convention toward [STRONG].
  > "An often-repeated piece of advice when choosing fonts for great legibility is to use fonts with large counters and apertures."
- **[supporting]** Method: aperture effect was isolated by redrawing the letters a, c, e, r, s, t, f in the serif font Pyke at three aperture levels (Open, Medium, Closed) — so the effect is attributable to aperture openness, not to confounded typeface differences.
  > "To identify effects of open and closed apertures on the letters 'a', 'c', 'e', 'r', 's', 't' and 'f', we ran an experiment using the serif font Pyke as stimulus. The letters in focus were designed for this experiment with three variations of open apertures (Open, Medium and Closed)."
- **[supporting]** Stimuli were shown in the parafovea at 2 degrees eccentricity, with and without flankers (crowding) — i.e. degraded, peripheral, glance-like viewing, which matches how axis/tick/legend text in charts is read; aperture openness mattered under exactly these hard conditions.
  > "The experimental paradigm was to present a letter either with or without flankers in the parafovea at 2° eccentricity."

#### Source `aa4bfea5` — quality: **secondary** · published: 2012-07-01
- **[central]** Legibility research is inconclusive on whether serif fonts are better than sans-serif, and the reading-speed difference between them is small — supporting the prior finding of no inherent serif/sans advantage.
  > "Legibility research is inconclusive as to whether serif fonts are truly better than sans serif... the difference in reading speed between serif and sans serif is apparently quite small."
