*Evidence:* The claim faithfully restates official Material 3 guidance and is well-attributed. The M3 "Applying Type" page (m3.material.io/styles/typography/applying-type) recommends a line-height ratio of ~1.2x type size for larger styles (title/headline/display) and ~1.5x for smaller styles (body/label) — corroborated by two independent searches returning the same language. The "1.2x" framing is confirmed as Google's own: the Wear OS typography page (developer.android.com) states verbatim "It's usually best to have a line height ratio of 1.2 times (120%) the type size." Actual M3 type-scale tokens support the general pattern: Display Large 57/64=1.12x, Body Large 16/24=1.5x (search-confirmed); computed across the scale, display ≈1.12–1.22x, body ≈1.33–1.5x. Source quality matches claim strength: it is a [CONVENTION] citation to a named major type system, not an empirical claim, and it cites the correct primary source. The claim is current and appropriately hedged ("roughly 1.5x"). CAVEAT (nuance, not refutation): M3's own tokens do not strictly conform — Title Medium (16/24)=1.5x and Title Small (14/20)=1.43x fall in the "body" range, and Body Small (12/16)=1.33x, so the 1.2/1.5 split is a documented heuristic simplification rather than a property every token obeys. The skill should present it as "M3's stated heuristic" and note that title-size tokens often use ~1.4–1.5x in practice.
*Counter-source:* Direct verification of the JS-rendered m3.material.io page was not possible (relied on convergent search-engine reads); and M3's own type-scale tokens show Title Medium/Small at 1.43–1.5x and Body Small at 1.33x, so the clean 1.2x-vs-1.5x bifurcation is a simplification of the actual token values — a qualification to footnote, not grounds to reject the attributed recommendation.

*Claim under review:* "Inter is a variable font that ships OpenType features purpose-built for data UIs, including tabular numbers and a slashed zero to disambiguate '0' from 'o', plus contextual alternates. This directly grounds the data-typography topic (tabular figures, disambiguated numerals) and the variable-font topic."

**Source:** https://github.com/rsms/inter (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Inter is a variable font with several OpenType features, like contextual alternates that adjusts punctuation depending on the shape of surrounding glyphs, slashed zero for when you need to disambiguate '0' from 'o', tabular numbers, etc."

*Evidence:* Claim is a verbatim quote from the primary source. The rsms/inter README/website states: "Inter is a variable font with several OpenType features, like contextual alternates that adjusts punctuation depending on the shape of surrounding glyphs, slashed zero for when you need to disambiguate '0' from 'o', tabular numbers, etc." Independent WebSearch confirmed the underlying OpenType feature tags: calt (contextual alternates), tnum (tabular figures), zero (slashed zero), plus stylistic sets/fractions. Variable-font status confirmed (weights 100–900). Source is the typeface designer's own actively maintained repo (Inter v4.x), so source quality matches the factual claim strength; not outdated, not marketing fluff. Only caveat: "purpose-built for data UIs" is the claim author's editorial gloss not present in the source, and tabular figures/slashed zero are standard OpenType features not unique to Inter — but the claim asserts neither uniqueness nor a source quote for that phrase, and Inter was in fact designed as a UI typeface (originally "Inter UI" by Rasmus Andersson at Figma), making the framing defensible. Not enough to refute.

#### V11 `a26b9b84` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "At typical print sizes under normal viewing conditions, the presence or absence of serifs has no significant effect on legibility; any benefit only appears for very small or distant text and is tiny."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4612630/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "When text is small or distant, serifs may...produce a tiny legibility increase due to the concomitant increase in spacing. However, it is unlikely to be of significance at typical print sizes viewed under normal conditions."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported by its primary source and corroborated by independent studies. Source confirmed as Arditi & Cho (2005), "Serifs and font legibility," Vision Research — a primary peer-reviewed controlled experiment using a parametric font where serifs were added/removed while holding all else constant. The supporting quote is accurate; the article's full findings reinforce it: "we did find a small effect of serifs on size thresholds, but it is unlikely to be of significance at typical print sizes viewed under normal conditions" and "RSVP and continuous reading speeds showed no effect of serifs." Adversarial search for disconfirming evidence (sources claiming serifs significantly aid legibility / "guide the eye" at normal sizes) returned NONE; instead found independent corroboration: e-commerce usability study (PMC9680897), on-screen eye-tracking study (Springer 2016, 10.1007/978-3-319-40355-7_55), the 2019 Vision Research typeface-features review, and children's-typography research all concur serifs have little/no effect on reading speed or comprehension in well-set text. This is mainstream consensus, not an extraordinary claim, so a single primary controlled study is sufficient source quality. Not outdated: the 2005 perceptual finding is stable and reinforced by 2016–2022 work. Nuances that do NOT refute: (a) the article attributes even the tiny small-size effect to the inter-letter SPACING serifs add rather than serifs themselves — making the claim slightly generous to serifs, not overstated against them; (b) a low-vision exception exists (some reviews favor sans-serif for low vision), but the claim explicitly scopes to "normal viewing conditions," so it is properly bounded. For a typography skill this should be tagged [STRONG] for the no-significant-difference-at-normal-sizes finding and [MYTH] for "serifs read better." Consistent with restraint-based color/charting skills; no conflict.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found. The only qualification is a low-vision exception (e.g., Arditi's low-vision typeface reviews favoring sans-serif), which does not contradict the claim because the claim is scoped to normal viewing conditions.

#### V12 `a32c6c1d` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Controlled legibility testing found no difference in legibility between typefaces that differ only in the presence or absence of serifs, directly disconfirming the popular belief that serifs aid reading. [MYTH]"

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4612630/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "our data exhibited no difference in legibility between typefaces that differ only in the presence or absence of serifs."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and not refuted. The supporting quote is verbatim from Arditi & Cho (2005), "Serifs and font legibility," Vision Research (peer-reviewed). The study isolated the serif variable by custom-building fonts varying only in serif size (0%, 5%, 10% of cap height) and tested size thresholds, RSVP, and continuous reading; it found no serif effect on reading speed. Corroborated by Lund's (1999) critical review of 72 typeface studies finding "no valid conclusion in favour of either serif or sans serif," and by broader screen/paper reading consensus. The popular "serifs guide the eye / aid reading" mechanism is empirically unsupported. Critically, the disconfirming evidence I searched for cuts AGAINST serifs aiding reading (sans-serif ~20% faster at very small RSVP sizes; sans-serif slightly better for low-vision readers), so it strengthens the [MYTH] label rather than refuting it. No credible source claims serifs reliably improve reading. Bounds to surface in the skill (caveats, not refutations): (a) the primary study has a small sample, n=6 (4 normal-sighted, 2 with AMD), but is backed by Lund's 72-study review; (b) "directly disconfirming" is slightly strong for a null result; (c) the no-difference finding applies to well-set body reading at reasonable sizes — in extreme conditions sans-serif, not serif, has a slight edge. Source quality (Vision Research, recognized author Aries Arditi, plus a 72-study meta-review) matches the myth-debunking claim strength; not outdated.
*Counter-source:* Searched for sources claiming serifs DO aid reading; none credible found. Contrary evidence (RSVP small-size sans-serif advantage ~20%; low-vision sans-serif preference; Lund 1999 review of 72 studies) all favors sans-serif or null, reinforcing the myth label rather than disputing it. Only genuine limitation is the primary study's small sample (n=6), mitigated by corroborating literature.

#### V13 `a3445cc1` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "At typical print sizes under normal viewing conditions, the presence or absence of serifs has no significant effect on legibility; any benefit only appears for very small or distant text and is tiny."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4612630/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "When text is small or distant, serifs may...produce a tiny legibility increase due to the concomitant increase in spacing. However, it is unlikely to be of significance at typical print sizes viewed under normal conditions."

*Evidence:* Source confirmed: Arditi & Cho (2005), "Serifs and font legibility," Vision Research (peer-reviewed); Aries Arditi is a recognized low-vision/legibility scientist. The supporting quote is verbatim and the claim is a faithful, near-word-for-word paraphrase — not an overreach. Arditi & Cho used parametric serif manipulation and found only a marginal size-threshold advantage for the 5% serif condition, attributed to extra spacing, and explicitly judged it negligible at normal sizes. The claim's two bounds (no significant effect at typical sizes; benefit only at small/distant text and tiny) match the source exactly. Searching for contradicting evidence INSTEAD confirms the claim: Bernard et al. (2002), Weisenmiller (1999), and RSVP/continuous-reading studies find no significant serif vs sans difference in reading speed or comprehension at normal sizes; the only contrary findings concern extreme/threshold conditions (small, distant, low-res, smoke/darkness), which the claim already concedes. Minor framing tension (some reviews credit the threshold edge to sans, Arditi/Cho to serif spacing) does not affect the claim's bound — both agree the effect is tiny and threshold-only. Source quality (primary peer-reviewed + consensus body) exceeds what this moderate, well-bounded claim requires; 2005 but the conclusion has held up in later on-screen eye-tracking work. No contradiction with the colour or charting skills.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found; broader literature (Bernard 2002, Weisenmiller 1999, RSVP/continuous-reading reviews) corroborates. Contrary findings exist only for extreme/threshold conditions, which the claim already carves out.

#### V14 `a361b936` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Reading speed (both RSVP and continuous reading) showed no effect of serifs, so serif vs sans-serif does not measurably change how fast text is read."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4612630/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "RSVP and continuous reading speeds showed no effect of serifs."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and current. Primary source = Arditi & Cho (2005), "Serifs and font legibility," Vision Research (PMC4612630). Abstract states verbatim: "RSVP and continuous reading speeds showed no effect of serifs"; body adds "In Experiments 2 and 3, the presence or absence of serifs made no difference in reading speed, for all participants" (both normally sighted and low-vision). The quote accurately matches the claim. The study isolated serifs cleanly via parametric fonts (serif size 0/5/10% cap height) — a stronger causal test than typeface comparison; serifs showed only a tiny acuity/size-threshold effect attributable to spacing, but ZERO reading-speed effect, so the claim's scope (reading speed) is correct, not overreach. Independent corroboration: Daxer et al. 2022 (Ophthalmic & Physiological Optics) compared real serif (Times New Roman) vs sans (Helvetica) equalized for x-height/layout and found no significant reading-speed difference (p=0.56, r=0.93); Tullis et al. and Arial-vs-TNR studies concur; multiple reviews report no consistent reliable serif/sans speed difference. Source is peer-reviewed primary (top vision-science journal), matches claim strength; 2005 finding re-confirmed in 2022, not outdated; not marketing/cherry-picked. No credible contradicting evidence found.
*Counter-source:* Daxer et al. 2022 (Ophthalmic & Physiological Optics, doi:10.1111/opo.13039) — searched specifically for a serif-advantage contradiction but it CONFIRMS no difference (reading speed p=0.56); no credible refuting source located.

#### V15 `a36b8425` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Controlled legibility testing found no difference in legibility between typefaces that differ only in the presence or absence of serifs, directly disconfirming the popular belief that serifs aid reading. [MYTH]"

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4612630/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "our data exhibited no difference in legibility between typefaces that differ only in the presence or absence of serifs."

*Evidence:* Source verified: PMC4612630 = Arditi & Cho (2005), "Serifs and font legibility," Vision Research 45(23):2926-2933, peer-reviewed primary source. The supporting quote is accurate and verbatim: "our data exhibited no difference in legibility between typefaces that differ only in the presence or absence of serifs." The claim accurately restates the paper's central conclusion and the [MYTH] tag (popular belief unsupported by evidence) is appropriate.

Corroboration (NOT contradiction): the broader literature backs the claim. My WebSearch surfaced the mainstream consensus that "in well-laid-out text there is no consistent significant difference in reading/comprehension between serif and sans-serif; layout/lighting/contrast matter more than typeface." Where studies do find a difference (e.g., eye-tracking, small-size RSVP), the advantage runs to SANS-SERIF, which still refutes the popular belief that *serifs* aid reading. This aligns with the well-known reviews (Lund 1999; Poole's legibility survey) that find no reliable serif advantage. So no credible source contradicts the claim; it is the mainstream evidence-based position.

Bounding caveats the skill should carry, not refutations:
1) WORDING OVERREACH: "directly disconfirming" slightly overstates a null result. A no-difference finding fails to support the belief rather than logically "disconfirming" it (absence of evidence). Safer phrasing: "found no legibility advantage for serifs, undercutting the belief that serifs aid reading."
2) SMALL N: the study used only 6 participants (4 sighted, 2 with AMD). N=6 is normal for within-subject psychophysics (many trials each) but is weak power for a null on its own — the claim's strength rests on the corroborating literature, not this single study. Don't tag the single study [STRONG] in isolation; the [MYTH]/CONVENTION-overturned status is what the body of evidence supports.
3) NUANCE: the paper DID find a small statistically significant effect in letter-size acuity thresholds, but attributed it to the increased inter-letter spacing serifs impose, not to serifs per se — which strengthens, not weakens, the "serifs don't help reading" conclusion.

No conflict with the colour or charting skills (this is type legibility, orthogonal to luminance/contrast and data-ink). Verdict: claim is well-supported, current enough (2005 conclusion has held), and source quality matches the [MYTH] strength. Not refuted; recommend the skill temper "directly disconfirming" to "found no serif legibility advantage" and cite the corroborating reviews alongside Arditi & Cho.
*Counter-source:* https://www.makingthemuseum.com/newsletter/serif-or-sans-serif-what-science-says (consensus summary, corroborates rather than contradicts); Lund, O. (1999) PhD review of legibility studies; Arditi & Cho (2005) Vision Research (primary source)

#### V16 `a393e925` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "In a controlled study, three screen line lengths were tested (25, 55, and 100 characters per line); 55 CPL produced significantly better comprehension scores than the longest line length of 100 CPL, giving controlled evidence that overly long measures hurt reading."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1071581901904586 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "line length influences readers' comprehension with documents at 55 characters per line producing better comprehension scores than the longest line length (100 characters per line)"

*Evidence:* SUPPORTED. Source is Dyson & Haselgrove (2001), "The influence of reading speed and line length on the effectiveness of reading from screen," Int. J. Human-Computer Studies 54:585-612 (doi:10.1006/ijhc.2001.0458) — a peer-reviewed controlled experiment, a primary source matching the claim's strength. Every element triangulates across independent sources: (1) Design — line lengths of ~25, 55, and 100 cpl crossed with two reading speeds; confirmed verbatim by the journal abstract as quoted by multiple sources. (2) Comprehension — abstract states "line length influences readers' comprehension with documents at 55 characters per line producing better comprehension scores than the longest line length (100 characters per line)." (3) Significance — a secondary description reports the pairwise test directly: "Multiple comparisons using Newman-Keuls test showed that comprehension of 55 characters per line documents was significantly better than 100 characters per line documents (p<0.05)," and that the comprehension difference was "not cancelled out by differences in reading rate... no speed-accuracy trade-off." So the word "significantly" in the claim is justified. The claim is correctly BOUNDED to comprehension and to this study; it does not overstate.

CAVEAT for the skill (does not refute the claim, but bounds the generalization): "overly long measures hurt reading" is true only for COMPREHENSION here — Dyson & Haselgrove also found long lines (100 cpl / 4-inch) were read FASTER than short lines, so long lines were not uniformly worse. And a contrary controlled study, Shaikh & Chaparro (2005, "Effects of Line Length on Reading Performance of Online News Articles," 35/55/75/95 cpl) found NO comprehension or efficiency difference across line lengths and that 95 cpl was read fastest, with reader preference (not performance) favoring short or long extremes. So the literature on "long lines hurt reading" is mixed; the specific Dyson & Haselgrove comprehension finding the claim cites is real and significant, but the broader rule should be presented as one controlled result among conflicting evidence, not a settled universal. I could not open the primary PDF directly (403 / a mis-cached file), so the p<0.05 detail rests on a secondary description, while the 25/55/100 design and the 55>100 comprehension finding come straight from the journal abstract quoted by multiple independent sources and match the well-known characterization of this study.
*Counter-source:* Shaikh & Chaparro (2005), "The Effects of Line Length on Reading Performance of Online News Articles" (35/55/75/95 cpl, n=20) found NO line-length effect on comprehension or reading efficiency and that 95 cpl was read fastest — contradicting any general "long lines hurt reading" rule, though it does not contradict Dyson & Haselgrove's specific comprehension finding.

#### V17 `a3d2a159` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Carbon builds its entire UI typography on IBM Plex as a superfamily (Sans, Serif, Mono) with explicit fallback stacks (e.g. 'IBM Plex Sans', 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif), naming a specific typeface experts/institutions treat as both modern and readable for product UI — directly relevant to topic 4 (choosing/pairing typefaces, superfamilies) and topic 6 (font stacks)."

**Source:** https://carbondesignsystem.com/elements/typography/overview/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Carbon uses the open-source typeface IBM Plex. It has been carefully designed to meet IBM's needs as a global technology company... font-family: 'IBM Plex Sans', 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif;"

*Evidence:* Core claim confirmed by primary sources. (1) IBM Plex IS a superfamily with Sans/Serif/Mono: Wikipedia states "IBM Plex is an open source typeface superfamily... comes in Sans, Serif, Mono, and Sans Condensed"; the IBM/plex GitHub repo and Carbon's own docs/guides/ibm-plex.md (lists Sans, Serif, Mono packages) confirm. Designed by Mike Abbink/IBM, replaced Helvetica as IBM's corporate typeface in 2017 — supports "a specific typeface treated as modern/readable for product UI." (2) Carbon uses IBM Plex as default typeface via @carbon/type ($carbon--font-families, type.font-family()). (3) Explicit fallback stacks exist. CAVEAT bounding the claim: the quoted stack 'IBM Plex Sans','Helvetica Neue',Arial,sans-serif does NOT match Carbon's actual current stack, which is `IBM Plex Sans, system-ui, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, .SFNSText-Regular, sans-serif`. The Helvetica/Arial string is illustrative (claim hedges with "e.g."); the existence of explicit fallback stacks — the real assertion — holds. A skill should cite Carbon's actual system-ui stack, not the Helvetica/Arial example. Source quality high, current, uncontradicted; relevance to topics 4 and 6 accurate.
*Counter-source:* Carbon's actual published fallback stack (IBM Plex Sans, system-ui, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, .SFNSText-Regular, sans-serif) differs from the quoted Helvetica Neue/Arial example, per @carbon/type source and Carbon typography docs — a wording inaccuracy in the example, not a refutation of the superfamily/fallback-stack claim.

#### V18 `a3d52955` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Material Design 3's type scale is structured as 5 roles (display, headline, title, body, label) each available in 3 sizes (large, medium, small), yielding a 15-style type ramp built to create page hierarchy across devices."

**Source:** https://m3.material.io/styles/typography/applying-type (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "display, headline, title, label, body. Each role has three sizes: large, medium, and small."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and factually accurate. The official Material Design 3 typography page (m3.material.io/styles/typography/applying-type) states the type scale has five roles "named to describe their purposes: display, headline, title, label, body" and that "each role has three sizes: large, medium, and small" — yielding 5 × 3 = 15 styles. This is directly confirmed by the primary source and corroborated by independent secondary sources (Egor Tarasov's "Material 3 You Typography Cheatsheet," Android Developers Jetpack Compose docs). The claim's role list (display, headline, title, body, label) is the identical set to the quote (display, headline, title, label, body) — only ordering differs, which is immaterial. The "create page hierarchy" framing matches M3's own description (roles convey emphasis/hierarchy: display = largest/most important, label = smallest/utilitarian). The "across devices" qualifier is consistent with Material being a cross-platform/responsive design system. This is a [CONVENTION]-tier descriptive claim about a named, recognized type system (Material 3), correctly attributed — not an extraordinary claim, and source quality (primary vendor documentation) exceeds the bar. No contradicting or qualifying evidence found. Note for the skill: this is a design-system convention, not [STRONG] controlled evidence that a 15-style ramp improves reading outcomes — the count and structure are factual, but the hierarchy benefit is convention, not a proven readability result. Does not contradict the colour or charting skills.

The claim's substance — Söhne provides tnum tabular (uniform/monospaced-width) numerals suitable for aligned tables/dashboards — is fully supported; tabular figures by definition set all digits to one advance width so columns align, which is the textbook use corroborated by independent sources (preusstype OpenType reference: "tnum replaces proportional-width figure glyphs with uniform tabular widths... useful for tables and financial data"). Söhne's default figures are proportional lining and tabular is feature-activated, exactly matching "via the tnum OpenType feature." Caveat: the provided "supporting quote" is a paraphrase, not verbatim — the page uses the label "Tabular numerals (tnum)" rather than that sentence — but it is faithful, not an overreach. Source released 2019, still current; no contradicting evidence found; no conflict with the color or charting skills.

**Supporting quote:** "Inter is a typeface carefully crafted & designed for computer screens ... Inter features a tall x-height to aid in readability of mixed-case and lower-case text"

The claim is a faithful paraphrase, NOT an overreach: it explicitly frames this as the designer's STATED INTENT (not controlled evidence) and tags it [CONVENTION] — which is precisely correct, since a designer's own repo is the appropriate source for a stated-intent/convention claim. Critically, the claim does NOT assert proven readability gains, so it needs no experimental backing. Adversarial search for contrary evidence found only one criticism (Inter "looks dull / falls apart at 24-30px and beyond"), which concerns LARGE display sizes and actually reinforces that Inter is optimized for small UI sizes — it does not refute the screen/small-size readability intent. Independent corroboration: Inter has "one of the tallest x-heights of any popular sans-serif." A real nuance exists in the literature (Sofie Beier's work: very tall x-heights can reduce word-shape distinctiveness), but it does not refute a claim that makes no proven-readability assertion. Not outdated (Inter actively developed, now v4 with optical-sizing axis, still a leading UI typeface in 2026). Not marketing-as-proof: correctly presented as convention attributed to a named source. Composes cleanly with colour/charting skills.
*Counter-source:* Sofie Beier (typography readability research) notes very tall x-heights can trade off word-shape distinctiveness; one usage criticism that Inter "falls apart at 24-30px and beyond" (pimpmytype.com) — but both concern bounds, not the stated screen/small-size design intent, so neither refutes the [CONVENTION] claim.

#### V21 `a42410d5` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "There is NO set of consistently faster (or slower) fonts across the population as a whole — the font effect emerges per individual, not as a universal ranking. (In the mixed-effects model the population-level font effect was non-significant for younger readers and only weakly significant for older readers, R2_beta=.005.) This bounds and partly disconfirms any 'best font for everyone' or simple serif-vs-sans speed claim."

**Source:** https://shaunwallace.org/files/Readability__TOCHI.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "indicating that for these factors, reading speed did not trend in a consistent direction across the population. Regarding fonts, this means that there is not a set of consistently faster fonts (conversely, slower) across the population as a whole."

*Evidence:* Verified directly against the primary source PDF (Wallace et al., "Towards Individuated Reading Experiences," ACM TOCHI 2022). The core claim is verbatim-supported: §6.1.2 "Reading speed across all participants" reports font non-significant by LME analysis → "there is not a set of consistently faster fonts (conversely, slower) across the population as a whole," with the paper's thesis (and title) being that different fonts help different individuals. The cited statistic is accurate: §6.1.3 "Reading speed across older participants (age >=35)" reports "font was found to significantly affect reading speed (p=0.01, R2b=.005)" — matching the claim's R2_beta=.005, and "weakly significant" fairly describes p=0.01 with a tiny ~0.5%-variance effect. Source quality is excellent (peer-reviewed ACM TOCHI, large-n mixed-effects model, 2022, not outdated, not marketing). TWO minor imprecisions, neither fatal: (1) the non-significant font result was for "all participants," not a separate "younger readers" cohort — the paper ran no younger-only analysis, though since the sample is dominated by 20s-30s readers and only the >=35 subgroup shows significance, "younger readers" is a defensible inference; (2) the claim slightly undersells that for older readers the paper says "we can make population-level recommendations, as reading speeds trend in a consistent direction depending on the font" (EB Garamond best, Open Sans worst) — so "no universal ranking" is bounded to the general population and partly breaks down for older readers, which the claim's own hedge ("only weakly significant for older readers") acknowledges. Related critiques (small effect sizes; the headline "35% faster" is within-subject fastest-vs-slowest, not a typical population gain) reinforce rather than refute the individuation thesis.
*Counter-source:* No credible source contradicts the claim. The only nuance is internal to the same paper (§6.1.3): for older readers (age>=35) font IS significant and population-level font recommendations are possible, bounding the "no universal ranking" assertion for that subgroup. The claim's "younger readers" label is the paper's "all participants," a minor misattribution that does not change the conclusion.

#### V22 `a440cd55` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Inter is a variable font that ships OpenType features purpose-built for data UIs, including tabular numbers and a slashed zero to disambiguate '0' from 'o', plus contextual alternates. This directly grounds the data-typography topic (tabular figures, disambiguated numerals) and the variable-font topic."

**Source:** https://github.com/rsms/inter (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Inter is a variable font with several OpenType features, like contextual alternates that adjusts punctuation depending on the shape of surrounding glyphs, slashed zero for when you need to disambiguate '0' from 'o', tabular numbers, etc."

*Evidence:* Claim fully supported by the primary source (rsms/inter repo) and independently corroborated. The repo states verbatim: "Inter is a variable font with several OpenType features, like contextual alternates that adjusts punctuation depending on the shape of surrounding glyphs, slashed zero for when you need to disambiguate '0' from 'o', tabular numbers, etc." Inter's OpenType feature list independently confirms calt (Contextual Alternates), tnum (Tabular Figures), zero (Slashed Zero), and pnum (Proportional Figures) — directly grounding both the data-typography topic (tabular figures, disambiguated numerals) and the variable-font topic. Source is primary/authoritative (the maintainer's own repo), current, and actively maintained as of 2026; not marketing. Only minor gloss: the claim's phrase "purpose-built for data UIs" is slightly stronger than Inter's own framing ("crafted & designed for computer screens"/UI generally), but the named features (tabular figures, slashed zero) are precisely data-display features, so this is a fair characterization, not a material overreach. Suitable as [CONVENTION]/feature-documentation grounding (named source: rsms/Rasmus Andersson, Inter), not a [STRONG] readability claim — which is consistent with how the skill should tag it.

#### V23 `a4642d35` — **REFUTED** · confidence: medium
*Claim under review:* "A medium line length of ~55 characters per line supported the most effective reading across both normal and fast reading speeds, grounding the conventional ~45–75 (or '66') character measure rule in controlled comprehension data rather than taste."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1071581901904586 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "a medium line length (55 characters per line) appears to support effective reading at normal and fast speeds"

*Evidence:* The CORE finding is well-supported: Dyson & Haselgrove (2001, Int. J. Human-Computer Studies, pii S1071581901904586) tested 25/55/100 cpl at normal+fast speeds; 55 cpl "produced the highest level of comprehension and was also read faster than short lines," and the abstract concludes "a medium line length (55 characters per line) appears to support effective reading at normal and fast speeds." So the quote backs "55 cpl supports effective reading." BUT the claim OVERREACHES in its framing — that this "grounds the conventional ~45-75 (or '66') character measure rule in controlled comprehension data rather than taste": (1) The study tested only 25/55/100; 66 and the 55-75 upper band were NEVER tested. It fuses a study value (55) with Bringhurst's PRINT-convention value (66) from a different domain. (2) Direct contradicting controlled evidence exists: Dyson & Kipping (1998), same lead author, found LONGER lines (~75-100 cpl) were read FASTER than narrow ones; Shaikh & Chaparro (2005) and Bernard et al. report mixed results with comprehension differences often non-significant and speed favoring longer lines. The controlled line-length literature does NOT converge on "55-66 optimal," so the convention is not cleanly "grounded in controlled data rather than taste." A single 2001 CRT-era study cannot ground a convention against a contradictory body of controlled work. The accurate framing: 55 cpl is [STRONG] for a medium measure beating extremes; the precise "66"/45-75 rule remains [CONVENTION] (Bringhurst) atop a mixed evidence base.
*Counter-source:* Dyson & Kipping (1998) found longer lines (~75-100 cpl) read FASTER than narrow; Shaikh & Chaparro (2005, journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/154193120504900514) and the broader mixed literature show no clean controlled consensus on optimal measure; the study itself tested 55 not the claimed 66.

#### V24 `a4667e21` — **upheld** · confidence: medium
*Claim under review:* "A medium line length of ~55 characters per line supported the most effective reading across both normal and fast reading speeds, grounding the conventional ~45–75 (or '66') character measure rule in controlled comprehension data rather than taste."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1071581901904586 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "a medium line length (55 characters per line) appears to support effective reading at normal and fast speeds"

*Evidence:* The source is the primary peer-reviewed study: Dyson & Haselgrove (2001), "The influence of reading speed and line length on the effectiveness of reading from screen," Int. J. Human-Computer Studies (the cited ScienceDirect S1071581901904586). Confirmed across the full-text PDF listing, ResearchGate, and Semantic Scholar: 48 participants, ~800-word passages, six line lengths from 25 to 100 cpl. 55 cpl produced BETTER comprehension than 100 cpl, and the paper's own conclusion is verbatim "a medium line length (55 characters per line) appears to support effective reading at normal and fast speeds." So the quote directly supports the core claim, and the source quality matches a STRONG tag (controlled study, primary source). Two bounding caveats keep this from being airtight rather than refuting it: (1) The word "most" overstates — on RAW SPEED the LONGER line (100 cpl) was read fastest (the documented "people read faster with longer lines but comprehend/prefer shorter" effect, Weinschenk, 100 Things; Dyson's broader work). 55 cpl is the best COMPOSITE of speed+comprehension, which is exactly what the study concludes, not the fastest. (2) The study only shows a MEDIUM measure (~55, which sits inside the conventional band) beats a very long one across three-to-six discrete points; the precise "~45-75 / 66" figure is Bringhurst CONVENTION, not something this study pins down. So the claim legitimately grounds "medium measure has controlled comprehension evidence, not just taste," but the exact 45-75/66 number remains convention. A faithful skill should split the STRONG 55-cpl finding from the CONVENTION 66 figure and drop "most." None of this contradicts the colour or charting skills.
*Counter-source:* Weinschenk, 100 Things Every Designer Needs to Know About People, item 18 "People Read Faster with a Longer Line Length, but they Prefer a Shorter Line Length" (oreilly.com/library/view/100-things-every/9780132658638/ch18.html), citing Dyson: ~100 cpl is fastest for on-screen reading speed while readers prefer/comprehend better at 45-72 cpl — qualifies "most effective… at fast speeds."

#### V25 `a4b184f5` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Font PREFERENCE does not predict reading SPEED, even when font size is controlled — preference and effectiveness are decoupled. Directly supports labeling 'this font looks/feels better' as taste, not proven performance."

**Source:** https://shaunwallace.org/files/Readability__TOCHI.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Our results show that no single factor predicts reading speed, and interestingly, preference does not predict speed even when font size is controlled. Our main takeaway is that different fonts work best for different people."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported by a peer-reviewed primary source: Wallace et al., "Towards Individuated Reading Experiences: Different Fonts Increase Reading Speed for Different Individuals," ACM TOCHI 2022 (DOI 10.1145/3502222), hundreds of crowdsourced participants. (1) Quote support: the paper's own framing, reproduced near-verbatim across sources, is "no single factor predicts reading speed, and interestingly, preference does not predict speed even when font size is controlled." (2) Font size control confirmed: abstract states "Our studies normalize font size by human perception." (3) Corroborating numbers: 80% believed their preferred font would be most effective, true only 18% of the time; readers were ~14% faster in their fastest font (314 WPM) than their preferred font (275 WPM). (4) Independent corroboration, no contradiction: a second Wallace et al. (2020) paper "Individual Differences in Font Preference & Effectiveness" and NN/G's "Best Font for Online Reading: No Single Answer" agree; a targeted adversarial search for criticism/contradiction returned only confirming sources. Source quality matches claim strength (peer-reviewed ACM journal, 2022, current). Scope bound to carry into the skill: "effectiveness" here = reading SPEED with comprehension held constant, on screen, short passages — the claim correctly limits itself to speed, so this bounds rather than refutes it. Labeling "looks/feels better" as taste rather than proven performance is a fair, bounded reading of the preference-vs-speed decoupling.

#### V26 `a4b5211d` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "In a perceptually size-normalized study of 16 fonts, each individual's reading speed increased ~35% comparing their fastest vs. slowest font, with no loss of reading comprehension — i.e., 'one font does not fit all' (individuated readability). This is a within-individual best-vs-worst gap, not a universal font ranking."

**Source:** https://shaunwallace.org/files/Readability__TOCHI.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Participants' reading speeds (measured in WPM) increased by 35% when comparing fastest and slowest fonts without affecting reading comprehension. High WPM variability across fonts suggests that one font does not [fit] all."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported by the primary peer-reviewed source (Wallace, Bylinskii, Dobres, Kerr et al., ACM TOCHI 2022, "Towards Individuated Reading Experiences"). Extracted PDF text confirms every component. (1) Within-individual framing is CORRECT and verbatim: body states "participants read 35% faster in their fastest font compared to their slowest font (232 WPM, on average)" and "By focusing our results on the individual, we see a potential 35% increase in reading speed (comparing fastest to slowest fonts) while maintaining comprehension." So it is each reader's own best-vs-worst gap, not a population font ranking — the claim is actually MORE precise than the abstract, which reads ambiguously ("comparing fastest and slowest fonts"). (2) Perceptually size-normalized: confirmed ("Our studies normalize font size by human perception"). (3) Sixteen fonts: confirmed by EurekAlert/UCF release from the authors' institution ("Sixteen common fonts used online, in newsprint and in PDFs were tested"). (4) Comprehension maintained: confirmed ("without affecting reading comprehension"). (5) "one font does not fit all" / individuation: verbatim in abstract. Source quality (peer-reviewed ACM TOCHI, reputable readability research group) matches the claim's modest strength; 2022, current. Not marketing. Bounding caveats (do not refute, but the skill should note them): the headline 35% within-individual figure derives from an analysis where each participant read 5 fonts ("out of the five fonts tested"), a subset of the 16-font program; the paper itself calls the gain "potential" and flags a "rough sanity check" / Cohen's d caveat ("within-participant analysis, lack of independence, and few data points"); comprehension was measured with only 2 three-option multiple-choice questions per passage (coarse). Also note the paper DOES report average per-font speed differences, so population-level effects exist — the claim's point is that the 35% specifically is the individual gap, which is accurate.
*Counter-source:* No credible third-party rebuttal found (positive secondary coverage: phys.org, EurekAlert, UCF CECS). Only "counter" pressure is internal: the paper's own hedges ("potential" 35%, Cohen's d "rough sanity check"), the coarse 2-question comprehension measure, online-crowdsourced WPM noise, and the 5-fonts-per-participant subset behind the 35% vs the 16-font program — all bounds on strength, not contradictions.

#### V27 `a4fe68f2` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Carbon's type scale is NOT a fixed geometric/modular ratio but a custom incremental equation Xn = Xn-1 + {INT[(n-2)/4] + 1} * 2, seeded at y0 = 12px — a counterexample to the assumption that a 'modular scale' must be a constant ratio. This grounds topic 3 (is a strict ratio scale evidence-based or taste?): a major design system deliberately uses a non-ratio scale."

**Source:** https://carbondesignsystem.com/elements/typography/overview/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The IBM type scale is built on a single equation. The formula for our scale was created to provide hierarchy for all types of experiences. The formula assumes that y₀=12 px. ... Xn = Xn-1 + {INT[(n-2)/4] + 1} * 2"

*Evidence:* VERIFIED, not refuted. The formula is real, primary-sourced, current, and the non-ratio framing is mathematically correct.

(1) Source/quote: The exact equation "Xn = Xn-1 + {INT[(n-2)/4] + 1} * 2" with "y₀=12 px" appears on IBM/Carbon official docs. Confirmed verbatim via WebSearch quoting the current page (carbondesignsystem.com/elements/typography/overview/, Carbon v11) AND directly fetched from v10.carbondesignsystem.com/guidelines/typography/overview/, which also lists the scale: 12, 14, 16, 18, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36, 42, 48, 54, 60, 68, 76, 84, 92 px.

(2) Math check (the load-bearing part of the claim — that this is NOT a constant ratio): Seeding y0=12 at n=1 and applying the formula reproduces every published value exactly (n=6: 20+(INT[4/4]+1)*2 = 24; n=10: 36+(INT[8/4]+1)*2 = 42, etc.). Increments are 2,2,2,2,4,4,4,4,6,6,6,6,8,8,8,8 — additive, growing +2 every 4 steps. Consecutive-step RATIOS are non-constant: 1.167, 1.143, 1.125, 1.111, 1.20, 1.167... A geometric/modular scale requires Xn = Xn-1 * r (constant r). Carbon's is Xn = Xn-1 + (growing increment). So it is provably a non-ratio scale. Claim's core assertion holds.

(3) Source quality matches claim strength: This is a descriptive [CONVENTION]-tier claim ("a major design system deliberately uses a non-ratio scale"), backed by the primary design-system documentation itself — appropriate and sufficient. It does NOT overreach into a [STRONG] empirical claim about readability superiority; it is correctly used only as a counterexample to the convention "modular scale must be a constant ratio."

(4) Currency: Confirmed on both current v11 (via search of the live URL) and historical v10. Not outdated.

Two minor bounds (not refutations): (a) Carbon itself labels this a "type scale built on a single equation," NOT a "modular scale" — so the claim does not misattribute terminology to Carbon; it uses Carbon to disconfirm the broader assumption, which is fair. (b) The claim is descriptive, not normative — it does not assert non-ratio scales read better, only that a non-ratio scale exists in a major system, which is exactly what grounds topic 3 ("strict ratio scale is taste, not evidence"). No contradicting source found.
*Counter-source:* No credible contradicting source found. WebSearch and direct fetch of both Carbon v10 and the current v11 page corroborate the formula and values; the only nuance is terminological (Carbon calls it a "type scale," not a "modular scale"), which does not undermine the claim.

#### V28 `a525d716` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "In a controlled study, three screen line lengths were tested (25, 55, and 100 characters per line); 55 CPL produced significantly better comprehension scores than the longest line length of 100 CPL, giving controlled evidence that overly long measures hurt reading."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1071581901904586 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "line length influences readers' comprehension with documents at 55 characters per line producing better comprehension scores than the longest line length (100 characters per line)"

*Evidence:* Claim accurately describes Dyson & Haselgrove (2001), Int. J. Human-Computer Studies 54(4):585-612 — the exact ScienceDirect article (S1071581901904586). Verified across multiple independent sources: (1) it is a peer-reviewed controlled factorial experiment crossing line length (25/55/100 CPL) with reading speed; (2) the journal-level finding, quoted verbatim in secondary sources, is "55 characters per line producing better comprehension scores than the longest line length (100 characters per line)"; (3) sources confirm "there was no speed-accuracy trade-off," indicating the comprehension difference was a genuine, tested effect — supporting the "significantly" qualifier. The quote fully supports the claim; line lengths and direction (55>100 comprehension) are correct. Source is primary and peer-reviewed, matching claim strength. Minor bounding (not refuting): the same study found longer lines/more chars-per-line were read FASTER, so "hurt reading" applies to comprehension, not raw speed; the claim's supporting quote is correctly scoped to comprehension. Dyson's later non-replication concerns the SPEED finding, not the comprehension finding. Broader literature (Shaikh & Chaparro, Bernard) is mixed on optimal width but does not contradict this specific controlled result. Only mild overreach is wording "significantly" vs the abstract's "better," but the paper reports line length as a significant comprehension effect.
*Counter-source:* Dyson's own follow-up (cited in designregression.com) notes she "did not replicate [the] results when [she] changed some aspects of [her] original study; long lines were not read consistently faster" — but this non-replication concerns reading SPEED, not the comprehension finding the claim rests on, so it does not refute the claim.

#### V29 `a52a816e` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Contrary to the conventional belief that ALL-CAPS reads worse, upper-case text was found MORE legible than mixed-case or lower-case — especially for low-vision readers — because it tolerates smaller letter sizes with no loss of legibility. This bounds/qualifies the popular [MYTH] that uppercase is simply less legible; the disadvantage is size-economy-driven, not absolute."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2016788/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Upper-case is more legible than the other case styles, especially for visually-impaired readers, because smaller letter sizes can be used than with the other case styles, with no diminution of legibility."

*Evidence:* Source verified as primary/peer-reviewed: Arditi & Cho (2007), "Letter case and text legibility in normal and low vision," Vision Research; PubMed PMID 17675131. The supporting quote ("Upper-case is more legible than the other case styles, especially for visually-impaired readers, because smaller letter sizes can be used...with no diminution of legibility") is verbatim from the abstract and accurately represented by the claim. Confirmed findings: (1) size thresholds — lower-case ran ~0.1 log unit HIGHER (worse) than upper-case, so uppercase is genuinely more legible at the recognition-threshold level; (2) the uppercase advantage is SIZE-CONDITIONAL — per the paper's own continuous-reading measure, uppercase was faster only at small sizes (~2x acuity) and "at larger sizes, the upper-case advantage disappeared." This is why the contrary evidence does NOT refute the claim: Tinker (1955) and a 2019 replication (lowercase ~13% faster) measured CONTINUOUS reading at NORMAL sizes — exactly the regime where Arditi & Cho also found no uppercase benefit. The claim is explicitly bounded to "smaller letter sizes," "low-vision readers," and "size-economy-driven, not absolute," so it correctly qualifies (rather than overturns) the ALL-CAPS convention. Caveat (below the refutation bar): the phrasing is slightly imprecise — it is the uppercase ADVANTAGE that is size-dependent, not the "disadvantage" — and the headline "contrary to ALL-CAPS reads worse" could mislead if stripped of the small-size/low-vision caveat; the skill should present it strictly as a low-vision/small-type exception, never as license for ALL-CAPS body text. Claim is well-supported, current enough (finding not overturned), and source quality matches strength.
*Counter-source:* Tinker (1955, Legibility of Print) and a 2019 replication found ALL-CAPS slows continuous reading ~10-20% (lowercase ~13% faster) — but at NORMAL reading sizes, consistent with Arditi & Cho's finding that the uppercase advantage vanishes at larger sizes; therefore it bounds rather than refutes the claim.

#### V30 `a532a3f1` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Carbon builds its entire UI typography on IBM Plex as a superfamily (Sans, Serif, Mono) with explicit fallback stacks (e.g. 'IBM Plex Sans', 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif), naming a specific typeface experts/institutions treat as both modern and readable for product UI — directly relevant to topic 4 (choosing/pairing typefaces, superfamilies) and topic 6 (font stacks)."

**Source:** https://carbondesignsystem.com/elements/typography/overview/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Carbon uses the open-source typeface IBM Plex. It has been carefully designed to meet IBM's needs as a global technology company... font-family: 'IBM Plex Sans', 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif;"

*Evidence:* Verified against Carbon's primary source code, not just docs. In packages/type/src/fontFamily.ts (current main, Copyright IBM 2026), Carbon defines IBM Plex as a superfamily with explicit fallback stacks: sans = "'IBM Plex Sans', system-ui, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, '.SFNSText-Regular', sans-serif"; serif = "'IBM Plex Serif', 'Georgia', Times, serif"; mono = "'IBM Plex Mono', 'Menlo', 'DejaVu Sans Mono', 'Bitstream Vera Sans Mono', Courier, monospace" (plus sansCondensed and sansHebrew variants). The exact stack in the supporting quote, "'IBM Plex Sans', 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif", is verbatim the Sans stack in Carbon v10 (tag v10.58.0, fontFamily.js: sans: "'IBM Plex Sans', 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif") and is also verbatim the Sans Condensed stack in current main — so it is a genuine, not fabricated, Carbon declaration. Core claim (IBM Plex superfamily Sans/Serif/Mono + explicit fallback stacks) holds in every version. Carbon/IBM Plex is one of the major type systems the research criteria explicitly permits as a [CONVENTION] source for topics 4 (superfamilies/pairing) and 6 (font stacks). Only caveat (a version nuance, not a refutation): current main switched the PRIMARY Sans fallback chain from 'Helvetica Neue', Arial to a system-ui chain, so a skill should cite the stack as version-dependent. Source quality is high (primary: official repo source + GitHub code search across versions). Not outdated (Plex has been Carbon's typeface v10 2018 through 2026), not marketing fluff, not contradicted.
*Counter-source:* Version drift: current carbon-design-system/carbon main fontFamily.ts uses sans="'IBM Plex Sans', system-ui, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, '.SFNSText-Regular', sans-serif", which differs from the quoted v10 stack 'IBM Plex Sans', 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif. This refines (does not refute) the claim — cite the stack as version-specific. Also note Carbon now offers a feature request/path to override Plex with custom fonts (issue #22118), but Plex remains the default superfamily.

#### V31 `a592d829` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The cost of wider letter-spacing scales with reading skill: individual letter-spacing effect correlated negatively with reading speed (r = -0.62), and a 347-wpm reader lost about 27 wpm under wider spacing."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7090332/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Individual differences in letter spacing effect correlated negatively with individual wpm values"

*Evidence:* The primary source (PMC7090332, "Wider Letter-Spacing Facilitates Word Processing but Impairs Reading Rates of Fast Readers," peer-reviewed eye-tracking study, n=24 native German speakers) confirms every specific number verbatim. (1) r=−0.62, t(22)=−3.73, p<0.01 for the correlation between individual letter-spacing effect and individual wpm — exact match. (2) The 347-wpm/27-wpm example is a direct quote: "a very fast reader... who read on average 347 words per minute under normal spacing conditions showed a strong decrease in reading rates of 27 words less per minute when letter-spacing was wider." (3) "Letter spacing effect" is operationalized as (wider − normal) wpm per participant, so the negative correlation correctly means faster readers lost more, supporting "cost scales with reading skill." Overall wider spacing cost −9.45 wpm on average. The supporting quote supplied was only a fragment but the full text contains the exact figures. Source quality matches claim strength; not outdated; not marketing. Caveat (bound, not refutation): small sample of young adult skilled German readers, lab eye-tracking — generalization beyond skilled adult Latin-script readers is not established by this single study, and "reading skill" = wpm.

#### V32 `a5affd10` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "At typical print sizes under normal viewing conditions, the presence or absence of serifs has no significant effect on legibility; any benefit only appears for very small or distant text and is tiny."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4612630/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "When text is small or distant, serifs may...produce a tiny legibility increase due to the concomitant increase in spacing. However, it is unlikely to be of significance at typical print sizes viewed under normal conditions."

*Evidence:* Source verified as Arditi & Cho (2005), "Serifs and font legibility," Vision Research 45(23):2926-2933 — a peer-reviewed CONTROLLED experiment (custom fonts varying only serif size/spacing; tested via size thresholds, RSVP, and continuous paper reading). The supporting quote is verbatim and the claim faithfully restates it: reading speed showed NO serif effect at all (incl. AMD patients); only a small statistical effect in size-threshold tests, which the authors explicitly say is "unlikely to be of significance at typical print sizes viewed under normal conditions." Broader literature corroborates (multiple studies + eye-tracking find no significant serif vs sans difference when text is well laid out); the belief this refutes is the OPPOSITE myth ("serif reads better"). No credible source contradicts. Claim is properly scoped to print/normal viewing, does not overreach to screens. Primary peer-reviewed source matches the claim's strength; foundational legibility finding, not outdated. Minor nuance: the paper hints serifs may slightly INTERFERE at tiny sizes (improvement exceeded what spacing predicts), making the claim if anything generous to serifs — still within bounds.

#### V33 `a5b73bc4` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Contrary to the conventional belief that ALL-CAPS reads worse, upper-case text was found MORE legible than mixed-case or lower-case — especially for low-vision readers — because it tolerates smaller letter sizes with no loss of legibility. This bounds/qualifies the popular [MYTH] that uppercase is simply less legible; the disadvantage is size-economy-driven, not absolute."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2016788/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Upper-case is more legible than the other case styles, especially for visually-impaired readers, because smaller letter sizes can be used than with the other case styles, with no diminution of legibility."

*Evidence:* PRIMARY SOURCE VERIFIED. The quote is verbatim from the abstract of Arditi A, Cho J, "Letter case and text legibility in normal and low vision," Vision Research 2007;47(19):2499-2505 (PMID 17675131, PMC2016788) — a peer-reviewed primary study. Source quality matches the claim's strength.

QUOTE SUPPORTS THE CLAIM (1-for-1): The abstract's verbatim sentence — "the results suggest that upper-case is more legible than the other case styles, especially for visually-impaired readers, because smaller letter sizes can be used than with the other case styles, with no diminution of legibility" — directly supports every element of the claim: (a) upper-case MORE legible, (b) especially low-vision, (c) mechanism = tolerates smaller sizes with no loss of legibility.

MECHANISM/NUMBERS CONFIRMED: Lower-case size thresholds were ~0.1 log unit higher than upper-case (uppercase readable at smaller sizes). At small print (2x acuity limit), uppercase was read significantly faster by both normal and low-vision readers; "at larger sizes the upper-case advantage disappeared." This is exactly the size-regime dependence the claim captures with "the disadvantage is size-economy-driven, not absolute."

NOT AN OVERREACH: The claim is carefully bounded — it does not say uppercase is universally better, and it explicitly attributes the effect to size economy and scopes it to low vision. It does NOT contradict the classic Tinker reading-speed-of-running-text finding (all-caps ~13% slower for normal vision at normal sizes), because that lives in the large-size/normal-vision regime where Arditi & Cho found the uppercase advantage simply DISAPPEARS (a wash), not a reversal. The two findings address different size regimes, so no contradiction; the claim's framing is faithful.

MINOR CAVEAT (not refuting): The claim's opener conflates "reads worse" (continuous-reading SPEED) with "legible" (acuity/recognition threshold). The paper measured both and used the same conflation in its own title/conclusions ("upper-case text is simply more legible"), and the claim itself partially corrects this with "not absolute." This nuance does not rise to the level of misrepresentation. Independent search corroborates direction (e.g., popular coverage of the "all-caps is a myth" framing) though those are weaker secondary sources not relied upon.

CURRENCY: 2007, but vision-science findings on acuity-limited legibility are stable and the paper remains the canonical cite for case x legibility; no newer study overturns it.
*Counter-source:* Tinker (1963) tradition holds all-caps slows continuous reading speed ~13% for normal-vision readers at normal sizes — but this addresses a different size regime (supra-threshold/large) where Arditi & Cho found NO uppercase advantage (a wash), so it bounds rather than refutes the claim.

#### V34 `a5c81874` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Faster single-word processing does NOT translate into faster reading; wider spacing gave no words-per-minute benefit and actually slowed readers (average ~9.45 wpm reduction overall) because more words had to be fixated."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7090332/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "In terms of words read per minute, wider letter spacing did not provide a benefit, and faster readers in particular were slowed down."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported by the primary peer-reviewed source (Weiss, Knebel, Gerth, Fiebach et al., "Wider Letter-Spacing Facilitates Word Processing but Impairs Reading Rates of Fast Readers," Frontiers in Psychology, 2020; PMC7090332). The paper's TITLE itself states the claimed dissociation. Exact numbers verified: normal spacing M=209.88 wpm (SD=51.49) vs wide spacing M=200.43 wpm (SD=45.93), a significant disadvantage; 209.88−200.43 = exactly 9.45 wpm, and the text states verbatim "participants read on average 9.45 words less per minute when letter spacing was wider." Mechanism matches the claim: single-word processing IMPROVED (first-fixation duration −15.81 ms, gaze duration −7.20 ms) but reading rate FELL because wider spacing "reduced skipping probabilities and elicited more fixations" — "more words must be fixated." Fast readers slowed most (20–30 wpm). Independent WebSearch corroborated the mechanism and noted replication of Slattery et al. 2016 across Spanish/Hungarian/English. No credible contradicting source found. Bound for the skill (not a refutation): finding applies to typically-developed adult silent readers; a separate literature (Zorzi et al. 2012) shows extra letter-spacing HELPS dyslexic readers, so the claim must not be generalized to all populations. The claim as written stays in scope and the "~9.45 wpm overall" figure is accurate.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found for typically-developed readers. Adjacent qualifier: Zorzi et al. 2012 (PNAS) finds increased letter-spacing improves reading in dyslexic children — different population, does not contradict this claim.

#### V35 `a5e59c3a` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Material 3 recommends a line-height ratio of 1.2x the type size for display/headline/title styles and roughly 1.5x the type size for body/label styles."

**Source:** https://m3.material.io/styles/typography/applying-type (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "For display/headline/title: "a line height ratio of 1.2 times the type size" ... For body/label: "a line height ratio around 1.5 times the type size""

*Evidence:* The claim faithfully reproduces the primary source. Two independent WebSearches of the official M3 page (https://m3.material.io/styles/typography/applying-type) returned matching near-verbatim text: "For larger type... title, headline, and display, Material Design 3 recommends a line height ratio of 1.2 times the type size... for smaller body copy using styles like body and label, a line height ratio around 1.5 times the type size." The claim's wording ("1.2x ... for display/headline/title" / "roughly 1.5x ... for body/label") matches this, including the hedge "roughly" mirroring the source's "around." Source quality is sufficient: this is a [CONVENTION]-tier claim ("Material 3 recommends X"), explicitly permitted by the criteria as a "major type system," and it is verified against that system's own primary docs — no extraordinary-evidence bar applies. It is current (Material 3 is the live Material spec in 2026) and not marketing fluff. CAVEAT/BOUND: M3's actual per-token line heights are fixed dp/sp values, not literal ratios, and they deviate from the neat 1.2/1.5 split. Computed ratios: Display 57/64=1.12, 45/52=1.16, 36/44=1.22; Headline 32/40=1.25, 28/36=1.29, 24/32=1.33; Title 22/28=1.27, 16/24=1.50, 14/20=1.43; Body 16/24=1.50, 14/20=1.43, 12/16=1.33; Label 14/20=1.43, 12/16=1.33, 11/16=1.46. So the "1.2 for title/headline/display" generalization actually runs 1.12–1.50 (Title Medium is 1.5, not 1.2; Headline is 1.25–1.33), and "1.5 for body/label" actually runs 1.33–1.50. The claim should be presented as M3's stated general guidance, not as exact per-style ratios — which the page itself frames loosely ("around") and the claim hedges ("roughly"). The skill should label this [CONVENTION], not [STRONG], and note the per-token deviation rather than implying a strict rule.
*Counter-source:* https://m3.material.io/styles/typography/type-scale-tokens (actual per-token line-height/font-size values deviate from the neat 1.2/1.5 ratios, e.g. Title Medium 16/24 = 1.5, Body Small 12/16 = 1.33)

#### V36 `a60dceeb` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Carbon's type scale is NOT a fixed geometric/modular ratio but a custom incremental equation Xn = Xn-1 + {INT[(n-2)/4] + 1} * 2, seeded at y0 = 12px — a counterexample to the assumption that a 'modular scale' must be a constant ratio. This grounds topic 3 (is a strict ratio scale evidence-based or taste?): a major design system deliberately uses a non-ratio scale."

**Source:** https://carbondesignsystem.com/elements/typography/overview/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The IBM type scale is built on a single equation. The formula for our scale was created to provide hierarchy for all types of experiences. The formula assumes that y₀=12 px. ... Xn = Xn-1 + {INT[(n-2)/4] + 1} * 2"

*Evidence:* VERIFIED against primary source. The current Carbon Design System docs (carbondesignsystem.com/elements/typography/overview/, v11) state verbatim: "The IBM type scale is built on a single equation... The formula assumes that y₀=12 px" with formula Xn = Xn-1 + {INT[(n-2)/4] + 1} * 2. The claim reproduces the formula and seed (12px) character-for-character — no overreach. The non-constant-ratio characterization is a mathematical fact, not interpretation: the formula generates tokens 12,14,16,18,20,24,28,32,36,42,48... whose step ratios vary (14/12=1.167, 16/14=1.143, 18/16=1.125, 20/18=1.111, 24/20=1.20) because it is ADDITIVE with an increment that grows in blocks of four (+2,+2,+2,+2,+4,+4...), not multiplication by a fixed factor. A constant geometric/modular ratio is impossible from this equation. Corroborated by v10 Carbon docs and the IBM Design Language type-scale page (both surfaced in search). Source is IBM's own primary documentation — quality matches the claim strength (a factual statement about a documented formula). Current, not deprecated. No credible source disputes it. Minor framing nuance: a strict "modular scale" (Tim Brown sense) IS by definition constant-ratio, so Carbon is technically not a modular scale at all — but this only strengthens the claim's use of Carbon as a counterexample to the assumption that major design systems use fixed-ratio scales (Material 3 likewise abandons strict ratios). Properly tagged territory: this is a [CONVENTION]-level documented design decision by a named major system, used to bound topic 3's question, not presented as controlled evidence.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found. Closest nuance: "modular scale" strictly means constant-ratio (Tim Brown), so Carbon isn't a modular scale at all — which supports, not refutes, the claim.

#### V37 `a6112c38` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Reading speed (both RSVP and continuous reading) showed no effect of serifs, so serif vs sans-serif does not measurably change how fast text is read."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4612630/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "RSVP and continuous reading speeds showed no effect of serifs."

*Evidence:* Quote is verbatim from the abstract of Arditi & Cho (2005), "Serifs and font legibility," Vision Research 45(23):2926-2933 (PMC4612630) — a peer-reviewed primary controlled study. The design isolates serifs ("typefaces that differ only in the presence or absence of serifs"), so the generalization is licensed, not an overreach; the paper's own conclusion is "no difference in legibility between typefaces that differ only in the presence or absence of serifs." The only serif effect found was a small (~5%) acuity-threshold advantage in letter identification at the legibility limit — NOT reading speed — so it bounds but does not contradict the claim. Corroborated by a 246-participant 2022 e-commerce study (PMC9680897): serif vs sans within the same family had no impact on reading speed. The one contrary result (an eye-tracking study with sans read faster) used only 10 grad students and confounded whole-typeface design with serifs. Source quality matches claim strength; finding is stable in a slow-moving field.
*Counter-source:* Small contrary eye-tracking study (n=10 grad students) reporting sans-serif read faster, but underpowered and confounds typeface design with serifs; also Arditi & Cho found a small ~5% serif acuity-threshold advantage (letter ID, not speed).

#### V38 `a679ef94` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Faster single-word processing does NOT translate into faster reading; wider spacing gave no words-per-minute benefit and actually slowed readers (average ~9.45 wpm reduction overall) because more words had to be fixated."

**Supporting quote:** "In terms of words read per minute, wider letter spacing did not provide a benefit, and faster readers in particular were slowed down."

*Evidence:* Claim is accurately supported by the primary peer-reviewed source PMC7090332 (Korn et al., "Wider Letter-Spacing Facilitates Word Processing but Impairs Reading Rates of Fast Readers"; 24 German adults, silent reading). The exact "9.45 wpm reduction" matches verbatim (normal 209.88 wpm vs wider 200.43 wpm). The dissociation the claim describes — wider spacing sped single-word processing (reduced first-fixation, gaze, and total-fixation durations) yet gave NO wpm benefit and slowed readers — is the paper's literal thesis and title. The stated mechanism ("more words had to be fixated") matches the paper's "reduced skipping probabilities and elicited more fixations, especially for faster readers." Faster readers were slowed most (up to 20-30 wpm), also matching. Source is the originating primary study, peer-reviewed, 2020, not marketing — quality matches claim strength. Bounding note (context, not refutation): this is one 24-participant study of UNIMPAIRED ADULT readers; a parallel literature (Zorzi et al. 2012 PNAS; Sjoblom 2016) shows extra-large spacing HELPS dyslexic/high-crowding readers, and PMC7090332 itself found slower readers showed positive-or-null effects. So the finding is population-dependent and should be tagged [STRONG] scoped to typical adult readers, not generalized to "wider spacing harms everyone." The claim as written does not over-generalize, so it stands.
*Counter-source:* https://www.pnas.org/content/109/28/11455 (Zorzi et al. 2012 — extra-large spacing helps dyslexic children; bounds but does not refute the claim, which concerns typical adult readers)

#### V39 `a6b654d3` — **upheld** · confidence: high

This directly grounds the data-typography topic (tabular figures, disambiguated numerals) and the variable-font topic."

**Supporting quote:** "Inter is a variable font with several OpenType features, like contextual alternates that adjusts punctuation depending on the shape of surrounding glyphs, slashed zero for when you need to disambiguate '0' from 'o', tabular numbers, etc."

*Evidence:* The claim is well-supported by the primary source and corroborated by independents. The rsms/inter GitHub README states verbatim: "Inter is a variable font with several OpenType features, like contextual alternates that adjusts punctuation depending on the shape of surrounding glyphs, slashed zero for when you need to disambiguate '0' from 'o', tabular numbers, etc." The claim restates this faithfully (variable font; tnum tabular numbers; slashed zero for 0-vs-o; contextual alternates). Secondary sources (lexingtonthemes, MDN font-variant-numeric, font-feature-settings docs) confirm the real OpenType tags: zero (slashed zero), tnum (tabular figures), calt (contextual alternates), plus aalt, c2sc, ss01-ss08, cv01-cv13. Source quality is primary (the font author's own canonical repo) — sufficient for a factual claim about a font's features. Not outdated (Inter is actively maintained; these features long-standing). Not marketing — these are objectively verifiable OpenType feature tags, not subjective benchmarks. Two minor bounds, neither refuting: (1) "purpose-built for data UIs" is the verifier's/claim's editorial framing, not in the quote — but it is a fair characterization since tabular figures + slashed zero are exactly the table/dashboard features. (2) Inter's defaults are proportional lining figures; tabular numbers and slashed zero are opt-in features invoked via font-feature-settings/font-variant-numeric, not the default rendering — so "ships features" is correct but they are selectable, not automatic. Both topic groundings (data-typography: tabular figures + disambiguated numerals; variable-font) are accurately supported.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found; secondary sources (MDN, lexingtonthemes Inter feature guide) corroborate rather than dispute. Only nuance: defaults are proportional lining figures with tnum/zero as opt-in features, which qualifies but does not refute the claim.

#### V40 `a6b9efa5` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Controlled legibility testing found no difference in legibility between typefaces that differ only in the presence or absence of serifs, directly disconfirming the popular belief that serifs aid reading. [MYTH]"

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4612630/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "our data exhibited no difference in legibility between typefaces that differ only in the presence or absence of serifs."

*Evidence:* Quote verified VERBATIM in the primary source: Arditi & Cho (2005), "Serifs and font legibility," Vision Research 45(23):2926-2933 (peer-reviewed; PMC4612630). The abstract states "our data exhibited no difference in legibility between typefaces that differ only in the presence or absence of serifs." The study built 9 parametric fonts varying ONLY serif size and letter-spacing, then tested legibility 3 ways (size thresholds, RSVP, continuous reading); RSVP and continuous reading showed no serif effect. Because the serif variable was isolated parametrically, "directly disconfirming" is justified. The [MYTH] tag matches a broad, independent consensus: multiple sources confirm "no solid research backs the claim that serifs are more readable" and no significant difference in reading speed/scanning/comprehension — the "serif readability myth" is a documented, named myth, so the claim does not rest on this one paper. Qualifying evidence found but it BOUNDS rather than refutes: (a) the study itself found a tiny size-threshold edge for 5% serifs, attributed to added spacing not serifs; (b) Beier & Dyson 2014 show serifs help/hurt single-LETTER recognition by placement, not net reading; (c) a 2022 Acta Psychologica paper (Beier et al.) on serif×stroke-contrast interaction qualifies but does not overturn the consensus (could not fetch, HTTP 403). The primary's real limitations — n=6 (4 sighted + 2 AMD), single parametric font — are author-acknowledged caveats; the skill should anchor the claim on consensus + primary, not n=6 alone. None of these refute the claim as worded. Source is peer-reviewed, primary, corroborated, and not a fast-moving/marketing claim.
*Counter-source:* 2022 Acta Psychologica (S000169182200138X) serif×stroke-contrast interaction and Beier & Dyson 2014 letter-level serif effects qualify but do not overturn; primary study n=6 / single font are author-acknowledged limitations

#### V41 `a72beca7` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The authors explicitly conclude serif vs sans-serif legibility is equivalent and the two are interchangeable, supporting a [MYTH] tag on any blanket 'serif vs sans reads better' claim."

**Source:** https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/opo.13039 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The legibility of Helvetica and Times New Roman is similar when investigated under equivalent conditions. Thus, these two font types can be used as interchangeable standard typefaces."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED, NOT REFUTED. Primary source confirmed: Daxer, Radner, Radner, Benesch & Ettl (2022), "Towards a standardisation of reading charts: Font effects on reading performance—Times New Roman with serifs versus the sans serif font Helvetica," Ophthalmic & Physiological Optics 42(6), DOI 10.1111/opo.13039 (open-access full text at PMC9804255). The supporting quote is verbatim the paper's conclusion. Controlled design: 36 normal-sighted adults (18-38), three paragraphs matched on words/syllables/characters/difficulty, Latin-square randomization, equal size/spacing/layout; result = NO statistically significant difference between Helvetica (sans) and Times New Roman (serif) on reading time, reading speed (wpm), or errors. Authors explicitly state the two "font types can be used as interchangeable standard typefaces," and the title itself frames serif-vs-sans, so the claim's generalization mirrors the authors' own framing rather than overreaching. Disconfirming search REINFORCED the MYTH tag: Lund (1999) reviewed 72 typeface studies and found no valid serif/sans conclusion; Richardson (2022 book) and Tinker concur, attributing apparent differences to x-height, counters, spacing, stroke width and familiarity — not serifs. BOUND (does not refute): study is print reading-charts, German read-aloud, young normal-sighted, one x-height (1.57mm), long paragraphs; authors caveat generalization. So it SUPPORTS — does not single-handedly prove — the screen-specific myth; the claim correctly says "supporting," and the blanket-myth conclusion is carried by the broader corpus plus this study. For a screen/UI skill it should be cited as one [STRONG] data point alongside Lund's review, not as standalone proof of screen equivalence.
*Counter-source:* https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9804255/ (primary full text); corroborating: Lund 1999 72-study review and Richardson 2022 (https://library.oapen.org/bitstream/handle/20.500.12657/53344/978-3-030-90984-0.pdf), both reaching the same no-difference conclusion — no credible source contradicts the claim.

#### V42 `a73ffd57` — **upheld** · confidence: high
