**Source:** https://m3.material.io/styles/typography/applying-type (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "display, headline, title, label, body. Each role has three sizes: large, medium, and small."

*Evidence:* Claim verified against the primary source (m3.material.io/styles/typography/applying-type) and corroborated by Google's Android Developers docs and Material Web. M3 type scale = 5 roles (display, headline, title, body, label) × 3 sizes each (large, medium, small) = 15 styles. Multiple sources state "15 typography styles (5 roles × 3 sizes)" explicitly. The supporting quote lists roles in a slightly different order ("display, headline, title, label, body") but it is the identical set of 5 — no semantic discrepancy. The "create page hierarchy across devices" framing is supported: M3 names roles by purpose/emphasis and references screen sizes (e.g., headlines for "high-emphasis text on smaller screens"). The math (5×3=15) is correct. Source is primary-vendor documentation of a stable, current design system — quality matches the non-extraordinary, structural nature of the claim. Note for skill tagging: this is correctly a [CONVENTION] (named system: Material 3), not [STRONG] academic evidence — it is a documented design-system structure, not a controlled study finding; the claim does not overreach by asserting proof.

#### V43 `a762835d` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[CONVENTION — Tim Brown] In a multi-typeface system the body/text typeface should be the 'anchor', because text faces are engineered to survive varied settings whereas display faces fail at small sizes and on coarse/low-resolution screens. This is a concrete, falsifiable hierarchy rule for choosing the primary UI/body face."

**Source:** https://blog.typekit.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/combiningtypefaces.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Make your body text typeface the anchor if possible, because this text represents a majority of your content—and because text faces are built to withstand a variety of settings, whereas display faces can rarely be used at small sizes or at coarse resolutions."

*Evidence:* Quote confirmed verbatim from the cited source via WebSearch: Tim Brown, "A Pocket Guide to Combining Typefaces" (Typekit/Five Simple Steps, 2016) — "Make your body text typeface the anchor if possible... because text faces are built to withstand a variety of settings, whereas display faces can rarely be used at small sizes or at coarse resolutions." Author and source verified. Tim Brown is a recognized authority (ran Adobe Typekit's type program, created Modular Scale, author of "Flexible Typesetting"), so the [CONVENTION — named authority] tag matches the claim's strength; it is not presented as academic proof. The underlying mechanism has independent empirical grounding stronger than mere convention: text vs display optical-size differences are well documented (Google Fonts Knowledge, Monotype, Typenetwork; Microsoft Sitka size-specific research) — text faces use sturdier stems, open counters, looser spacing, higher x-height for legibility at small sizes, while display faces' high contrast/thin strokes/decorative detail break up at small sizes. One bound worth noting: a competing brand-led convention uses "anchor" to mean the DISPLAY/heading face (the personality-bearing "heavy lifting" face), not the body face — but this is a different sense of the word, not a contradiction, and Brown explicitly hedges "if possible." Knowledge is stable (centuries-old text/display distinction), not outdated; the "coarse resolutions" half is marginally less salient on high-DPI screens but "small sizes" fully holds. Not marketing — educational guide. Minor overstatement: claim calls it "concrete, falsifiable" whereas Brown frames it as a hedged guideline, but this does not rise to refutation given the correct [CONVENTION] tag.
*Counter-source:* Brand-typography sources (Creative Bloq, NN/G, Fontfabric) use "anchor"/"primary" to mean the display/heading face — a competing sense that bounds but does not contradict Brown's body-face-as-anchor convention.

**Supporting quote:** "stick with a single type family (making use of its various weights, widths and features); use a superfamily that includes sans and serif siblings; limit yourself to a specific historical period; use typefaces from the same type designer. But it also robs us of the opportunity to truly understand why a combination works or doesn't, and can lead to a false sense of completion."

*Evidence:* Source verified genuine and authoritative: Tim Brown's "A Pocket Guide to Combining Typefaces" (Five Simple Steps 2013; free via Adobe/Typekit blog 2016). WebSearch independently surfaced the phrase "to truly understand why a combination works or doesn't," matching the supplied quote nearly verbatim, confirming authenticity. The claim maps the quote accurately with no overreach: single family / sans+serif superfamily / single historical period / single type designer = the four conventional strategies; "makes adequate combinations easy to identify" = "only make 'adequate' pairings easy"; "robs us of the opportunity to truly understand why a combination works or doesn't" + "false sense of completion" = the caution against treating pairing rules as proof of success. The claim is correctly tagged [CONVENTION — Tim Brown], not presented as empirical proof — so it is verifiable as a quote rather than falsifiable by studies, and no credible source disputes it. Type-pairing principles are stable, so 2013/2016 vintage is not "outdated." Not marketing/forum content. Composes cleanly with the colour and charting skills (no contradiction).

#### V46 `a9264eef` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The uppercase legibility/reading-speed advantage appears only at small text sizes (low acuity reserve) and disappears once text is large enough — so at comfortable body-text sizes there is no uppercase reading-speed benefit. This is the key bound: case effects are size-dependent."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2016788/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "the upper-case advantage disappears, when text is large enough"

*Evidence:* The claim is directly and accurately supported by the primary source, Arditi & Cho (2007), "Letter case and text legibility in normal and low vision," Vision Research (PMC2016788) — a peer-reviewed psychophysics study, not marketing or speculation. Findings, verified by fetching the paper and a confirming search:

(1) Uppercase advantage exists ONLY near the acuity limit: at print size = 2x the acuity threshold (small, low acuity reserve), uppercase was read significantly FASTER than mixed-case by BOTH normal- and low-vision readers, in continuous text and RSVP.

(2) The advantage DISAPPEARS at large size: tested at 10x acuity with normally sighted readers, "the upper-case advantage disappears when text is large enough." The mechanism confirms size-dependence: at a fixed point size uppercase letters are physically larger (Arial caps ~184% the area of x-height lowercase), and above the "critical print size" reading speed plateaus, so size-driven case differences have "little or no measurable effect." This is exactly the supporting quote, in context — not cherry-picked.

The claim's gloss ("low acuity reserve", "size-dependent", "no uppercase reading-speed benefit at comfortable body-text sizes") faithfully paraphrases the paper, not an overreach.

No contrary evidence found. The independent Tinker tradition (all-caps read ~12% slower for continuous reading, due to lost word-shape and ~1/3 more horizontal space / more fixations) does NOT contradict the claim — it agrees there is no uppercase speed BENEFIT at body sizes (if anything, a penalty). Both traditions converge: at comfortable reading sizes uppercase confers no reading-speed advantage.

Source quality matches claim strength (primary peer-reviewed source for a bounded psychophysical claim), and the finding rests on Legge's robust critical-print-size / acuity-reserve framework, so it is not outdated (2007; consistent with later legibility work, e.g. Beier).

Minor bound to carry into the skill (caveat, not refutation): the large-size disappearance was demonstrated with NORMALLY sighted readers at 10x acuity; low-vision readers operate with smaller acuity reserve and may retain the benefit at larger physical sizes — which actually reinforces the "size/acuity-reserve dependent" framing rather than undercutting it.
*Counter-source:* No refuting source found. Tinker/all-caps tradition (e.g., Wikipedia "All caps" citing the 1928 Tinker 11.8% slowdown; NN/g) is consistent, not contradictory — it shows no uppercase benefit (a penalty) at body sizes.

#### V47 `a9cafaa9` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Reading speed (both RSVP and continuous reading) showed no effect of serifs, so serif vs sans-serif does not measurably change how fast text is read."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4612630/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "RSVP and continuous reading speeds showed no effect of serifs."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and matches scholarly consensus. PRIMARY SOURCE confirmed: Arditi & Cho (2005), "Serifs and font legibility," Vision Research (peer-reviewed). Quote is accurate — they used 9 custom parametric fonts varying serif size (0%/5%/10% cap height); the 0% condition is effectively sans-serif, making this a clean serif-vs-sans manipulation. Both RSVP and continuous reading showed no significant serif effect (repeated-measures ANOVA: no significant effects). INDEPENDENT CORROBORATION: Lund (1999) critical review of 72 typeface studies found no valid conclusion favoring serif or sans, and serif/sans comparisons "typically find no differences in speed of reading or comprehension"; Poole's review notes within-family differences exceed serif-vs-sans differences. This is the established "serif-reads-better-is-a-myth" position. ONE BOUND (qualifies, does not refute): at sizes near the acuity limit (~4pt), sans-serif is read ~20% faster (Morris, Aquilante, Yager & Bigelow 2002), and Arditi & Cho's own size-THRESHOLD experiment found a small serif effect (attributed mostly to spacing). So "does not measurably change" is true for normal reading sizes but not at extreme-small sizes. For a web/UI/data typography skill, the claim should be tagged [STRONG] for "no reliable serif/sans speed difference at normal sizes" and [MYTH] for "serif reads better," with the small-size caveat noted. Does not contradict the existing color/charting skills.
*Counter-source:* Morris, Aquilante, Yager & Bigelow (2002): ~20% RSVP sans-serif advantage at ~4pt (acuity-limit sizes only); Arditi & Cho's own threshold experiment showed a small serif effect (spacing-driven). These bound the absolute wording "does not measurably change" but do not overturn the no-effect finding for normal reading sizes.

#### V48 `a9f52dc4` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The authors explicitly conclude serif vs sans-serif legibility is equivalent and the two are interchangeable, supporting a [MYTH] tag on any blanket 'serif vs sans reads better' claim."

**Source:** https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/opo.13039 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The legibility of Helvetica and Times New Roman is similar when investigated under equivalent conditions. Thus, these two font types can be used as interchangeable standard typefaces."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED. Source is Daxer et al. (2022), "Towards a standardisation of reading charts: Font effects on reading performance—Times New Roman with serifs versus the sans serif font Helvetica," Ophthalmic & Physiological Optics 42:1180-1186, DOI 10.1111/opo.13039 — a controlled, randomized Latin-square experiment (n=36), peer-reviewed primary source. The supporting quote is verbatim-accurate (confirmed against open-access PMC9804255): the abstract states "The legibility of Helvetica and Times New Roman is similar when investigated under equivalent conditions. Thus, these two font types can be used as interchangeable standard typefaces." No significant difference in reading time (p=0.50) or speed (p=0.56). The authors explicitly conclude "serif and sans serif fonts are equally legible." The claim is appropriately bounded — it says the study SUPPORTS a [MYTH] tag on a blanket "serif vs sans reads better" claim, not that it proves universal equivalence; a controlled equivalence finding correctly corroborates that myth tag, and this aligns with the broader literature consensus (e.g., Poole's review). One non-refuting caveat: the study compared only two specific optimised fonts at one x-height (1.57mm), and the discussion cautions it "cannot speak definitively about serif versus sans-serif differences broadly" — so the skill should cite it as corroborating evidence for "no inherent serif/sans legibility advantage" rather than proof all serif/sans fonts are interchangeable. Current (2022), peer-reviewed, not marketing. Source quality matches claim strength.

#### V49 `aa1b7cd6` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The authors explicitly conclude serif vs sans-serif legibility is equivalent and the two are interchangeable, supporting a [MYTH] tag on any blanket 'serif vs sans reads better' claim."

**Source:** https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/opo.13039 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The legibility of Helvetica and Times New Roman is similar when investigated under equivalent conditions. Thus, these two font types can be used as interchangeable standard typefaces."

*Evidence:* Quote is verbatim-accurate and the source is genuine: Daxer et al. (2022), "Towards a standardisation of reading charts: Font effects on reading performance—Times New Roman with serifs versus Helvetica," Ophthalmic & Physiological Optics 42(6):1180-1186, DOI 10.1111/opo.13039, peer-reviewed/primary, PMC9804255 open-access full text confirms. Authors x-height-matched both fonts (1.57mm, microscope-measured) and found NO significant difference in reading time (p=0.50), speed (p=0.56), or errors (identical 0.31/text), explicitly concluding the serif (Times) and sans (Helvetica) "font types can be used as interchangeable standard typefaces." Broader literature CORROBORATES not contradicts: Arditi & Cho (2005, parametric serif/no-serif font) and Morris/Aquilante/Yager/Bigelow (2002) found no serif effect on normal-size reading speed; Morris/Bigelow even found a 20% SANS advantage at ~4pt, further undermining "serif reads better." No credible source asserts a robust serif advantage. BOUNDS (qualify, don't refute): print not screen, single serif/sans pair, n=36, no comprehension test, and authors caveat "we cannot be certain that our results can be generalised beyond this specific print size." The claim is correctly bounded — it backs a [MYTH] tag on a BLANKET "serif vs sans reads better" claim, which the convergent evidence licenses. Recommend the skill rest the [MYTH] tag on the convergence (Daxer + Arditi + Morris/Bigelow + Beier reviews), not on Daxer alone, and note the print/screen and small-size caveats.
*Counter-source:* No genuine counter-source found. Closest qualifier — Morris/Aquilante/Yager/Bigelow (2002) 20% sans advantage at ~4pt (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4612630/) — supports rather than refutes the myth tag. Only real limitation is scope: Daxer is print, x-height-matched, single font pair, n=36 (authors' own generalization caveat in PMC9804255).

#### V50 `aa302c77` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Contrary to the conventional belief that ALL-CAPS reads worse, upper-case text was found MORE legible than mixed-case or lower-case — especially for low-vision readers — because it tolerates smaller letter sizes with no loss of legibility. This bounds/qualifies the popular [MYTH] that uppercase is simply less legible; the disadvantage is size-economy-driven, not absolute."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2016788/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Upper-case is more legible than the other case styles, especially for visually-impaired readers, because smaller letter sizes can be used than with the other case styles, with no diminution of legibility."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED, NOT REFUTED. Source is Arditi & Cho (2007), "Letter case and text legibility in normal and low vision," Vision Research 47(19):2499-2505 (PubMed 17675131 / PMC2016788) — a peer-reviewed primary study by a recognized low-vision researcher. The supporting quote is verbatim and is the paper's own conclusion, independently corroborated by the journal abstract ("upper-case is more legible than the other case styles, especially for visually-impaired readers, because smaller letter sizes can be used... with no diminution of legibility; when point size is fixed, upper-case text is more legible"). Specific findings back the claim: lowercase size thresholds ~0.1 log unit worse than uppercase; reading speeds higher for upper- than mixed-case at ~2x acuity (small) sizes. CRUCIALLY, the paper states the upper-case advantage DISAPPEARS at larger sizes, and the mechanism is that capitals are physically taller at a fixed point size — i.e., a size-economy effect, exactly as the claim states ("size-economy-driven, not absolute"). The claim does NOT assert all-caps reads faster in normal body text, so it does not collide with the classic Tinker finding (~13-20% all-caps slowdown in continuous reading); it explicitly frames the result as BOUNDING the absolute myth, which is what Arditi & Cho themselves do (they note their result contradicts conventional typographic wisdom). Three adversarial refutation attempts (fixed-point-size confound, tiny sample n=3 normal + n=4 low-vision, advantage vanishes at comfortable sizes) all CAP the claim's strength but none contradict it — and the claim already encodes the first via its qualifiers. Bound to preserve in the skill: the finding applies only at small/near-threshold sizes and low vision; at comfortable web body-text sizes the advantage is gone, so it must never be cited to justify all-caps for running text. Small sample is the main weakness, so the low-vision emphasis is better tagged as a bounded/qualifying finding than as unqualified [STRONG].
*Counter-source:* Tinker (1963) / Poulton — all-caps reduces continuous reading speed ~13-20% at normal sizes (does not contradict the bounded claim, but limits its applicability to small sizes/low vision)

#### V51 `aabf32e6` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Font PREFERENCE does not predict reading SPEED, even when font size is controlled — preference and effectiveness are decoupled. Directly supports labeling 'this font looks/feels better' as taste, not proven performance."

**Source:** https://shaunwallace.org/files/Readability__TOCHI.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Our results show that no single factor predicts reading speed, and interestingly, preference does not predict speed even when font size is controlled. Our main takeaway is that different fonts work best for different people."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and faithfully restates a strong primary source. Paper: Wallace et al., "Towards Individuated Reading Experiences: Different Fonts Increase Reading Speed for Different Individuals," ACM TOCHI 2022 (DOI 10.1145/3502222) — peer-reviewed top-tier HCI journal. The supporting quote is corroborated by independent sources (phys.org, UCF, EurekAlert: "personal preference for fonts did not necessarily predict reading speed"; "people don't always pick the fonts that they would read the fastest"). The load-bearing "even when font size is controlled" qualifier is independently confirmed: the study's preference test controlled for perceived font size, addressing prior work where size and preference are interdependent. Finding is replicated in the authors' earlier 2020 study (preference vs effectiveness diverge). Searched for contrary evidence: only limitation found is reliance on short passages (bounds generalization to long-form, does not refute decoupling). The inference "looks/feels better = taste, not proven performance" is a conservative, valid application and contradicts neither the colour nor charting skills. Note bound: finding is about reading SPEED (comprehension unaffected); preference may still track untested comfort/fatigue outcomes — but this does not weaken the claim as stated.
*Counter-source:* No credible refuting source found. Only bounding limitation (short-passage methodology) noted in readability-research commentary; it qualifies generalization to long-form reading but does not dispute the preference–speed decoupling.

#### V52 `ab09fe69` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Carbon's type scale is NOT a fixed geometric/modular ratio but a custom incremental equation Xn = Xn-1 + {INT[(n-2)/4] + 1} * 2, seeded at y0 = 12px — a counterexample to the assumption that a 'modular scale' must be a constant ratio. This grounds topic 3 (is a strict ratio scale evidence-based or taste?): a major design system deliberately uses a non-ratio scale."

**Source:** https://carbondesignsystem.com/elements/typography/overview/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The IBM type scale is built on a single equation. The formula for our scale was created to provide hierarchy for all types of experiences. The formula assumes that y₀=12 px. ... Xn = Xn-1 + {INT[(n-2)/4] + 1} * 2"

*Evidence:* Primary source (Carbon Design System v11, carbondesignsystem.com/elements/typography/overview/) verbatim confirms the quote: "The IBM type scale is built on a single equation... The formula assumes that y₀=12 px" with formula Xn = Xn-1 + {INT[(n-2)/4] + 1} * 2. The generated scale (12,14,16,18,20,24,28,32,36,42,48,54,60,68,76,84,92) proves it is NOT a constant geometric ratio: consecutive ratios vary (14/12=1.167, 16/14=1.143, 24/20=1.2) while absolute increments rise in a staircase (2,2,2,2/4,4,4,4/6,6,6,6/8,8,8,8) — an additive/incremental scale. Secondary confirmation: Lehel (IBM Design), "A Deep Dive on Typescales," Medium, states IBM avoids a traditional modular ratio. No credible contradicting source found. Minor framing caveat (not a refutation): strictly a "modular scale" is ratio-based by Tim Brown's definition, so Carbon is more precisely "not a modular scale" than "a non-ratio modular scale," but the claim's substance — a major design system deliberately using a non-ratio incremental scale, grounding topic 3 — is accurate and well-supported. Tag-appropriate as [CONVENTION], named source IBM/Carbon.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found. Searched for disputes; only confirmations (official Carbon docs primary + IBM Design Medium deep-dive). Sole caveat is terminological framing of "modular scale," not a factual contradiction.

#### V53 `ab96dfdc` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "There is NO set of consistently faster (or slower) fonts across the population as a whole — the font effect emerges per individual, not as a universal ranking. (In the mixed-effects model the population-level font effect was non-significant for younger readers and only weakly significant for older readers, R2_beta=.005.) This bounds and partly disconfirms any 'best font for everyone' or simple serif-vs-sans speed claim."

**Source:** https://shaunwallace.org/files/Readability__TOCHI.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "indicating that for these factors, reading speed did not trend in a consistent direction across the population. Regarding fonts, this means that there is not a set of consistently faster fonts (conversely, slower) across the population as a whole."

*Evidence:* Verified directly from the primary PDF (Wallace, Bylinskii, Dobres, Kerr et al., "Towards Individuated Reading Experiences," ACM TOCHI 2022). Section 6.1.2 (all participants): "font... [was] found non-significant by the LME analysis... Regarding fonts, this means that there is not a set of consistently faster fonts (conversely, slower) across the population as a whole. This result hints at a need for looking into individual differences." — verbatim support for the core claim. Section 6.1.3 (older participants, age >= 35): "font was found to significantly affect reading speed (p=0.01, R2_b=.005)" — confirms the exact R2_beta=.005 and the "weakly significant for older readers" gloss (significant p but ~0.5% variance). The claim's "partly disconfirms / bounds" hedging is accurate because the paper still says "for older participants we can make population-level recommendation[s]" (EB Garamond best, Open Sans worst). One minor imprecision: the paper's models are "all participants" vs "older (>=35)," with no separately-reported "younger-only" model, so "non-significant for younger readers" is a reasonable inference rather than a literally-reported result — but this does not change the substantive conclusion. Source is primary, peer-reviewed, current (2022); no credible contradiction found.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found; the broader readability literature supports per-individual variation rather than a universal fastest font.

#### V54 `aba76761` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Faster single-word processing does NOT translate into faster reading; wider spacing gave no words-per-minute benefit and actually slowed readers (average ~9.45 wpm reduction overall) because more words had to be fixated."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7090332/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "In terms of words read per minute, wider letter spacing did not provide a benefit, and faster readers in particular were slowed down."

*Evidence:* Claim is faithfully supported by the primary peer-reviewed source (Frontiers in Psychology 2020, "Wider Letter-Spacing Facilitates Word Processing but Impairs Reading Rates of Fast Readers," PMC7090332). Verified verbatim: (1) "participants read on average 9.45 words less per minute when letter spacing was wider" — the ~9.45 wpm figure is the exact reported value (normal M=209.88 wpm vs wider M=200.43 wpm, significant disadvantage). (2) Faster word processing did NOT translate to faster reading: first-fixation durations shortened (~15.81 ms) but "did not translate into an overall improvement of reading rate." (3) Mechanism is correct: "reduced skipping probabilities and elicited more fixations...more words must be fixated." Source is a controlled eye-tracking study (n=24 German adults, 14 newspaper articles, silent reading), current (2020), primary — strength matches the claim. BOUNDING (does not refute, but must be stated in any skill): single study, small effect (~4.5%), headline effect is specifically fast readers, typically-developing adults in central vision only. Contrary evidence for OTHER populations — increased letter spacing aids dyslexic children (Zorzi et al. 2012, PNAS) and peripheral/low vision (crowding) — so the claim must not be generalized to "spacing never helps." As worded, the claim describes this specific result and does not over-generalize.
*Counter-source:* Zorzi et al. 2012 (PNAS) — extra-large letter spacing improves reading speed/accuracy in dyslexic children; crowding literature on peripheral/low vision (e.g., increased spacing aids peripheral reading) — these qualify/bound the claim to typical central-vision adult readers but do NOT contradict the PMC7090332 finding itself.

(a) Superfamily confirmed: the official guide docs/guides/ibm-plex.md in the carbon-design-system/carbon repo lists IBM Plex Sans, IBM Plex Serif, and IBM Plex Mono (plus script variants). (b) The exact supporting quote is verbatim-accurate: a targeted search for the string 'IBM Plex Sans', 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif returned the cited primary page (carbondesignsystem.com/elements/typography/overview/) and confirmed that font-family example appears there — not fabricated. (c) "Entire UI typography on IBM Plex" is reinforced by GitHub issue #22118 ([Feature Request]: Allow consumers to override Plex with custom fonts), which documents Plex stacks hardcoded across ~40 Carbon components via @carbon/type — contrary-seeking evidence that strengthens the claim. Two honest nuances, neither refuting: Carbon's @carbon/type package internally defaults to a system-font stack (system-ui, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, .SFNSText-Regular, sans-serif) while the Helvetica Neue/Arial chain is the docs example — both real; and Carbon's UI type ramp is built primarily on Plex Sans (Mono for code/data), with Plex Serif shipped as the editorial member rather than the UI workhorse. Directly relevant to topic 4 (superfamilies/pairing) and topic 6 (font stacks).
*Counter-source:* https://github.com/carbon-design-system/carbon/issues/22118 (override-Plex feature request shows Plex hardcoded across ~40 components — reinforces rather than refutes); @carbon/type package default stack uses system-ui/-apple-system rather than Helvetica Neue/Arial, but the quoted stack is genuinely present on the cited overview docs page.

#### V56 `abed0e10` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Under matched conditions (equal size, spacing, layout, word/syllable/character count), Times New Roman (serif) and Helvetica (sans-serif) produced NO statistically significant difference in reading time (p=0.50) or reading speed (p=0.56) — direct disconfirmation of the 'serif reads faster / sans reads faster' belief. Bound: stimuli were PRINTED (paperback/newspaper), not on-screen, so this constrains rather than proves the screen-specific case."

**Source:** https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/opo.13039 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "For the paperback layout, no significant difference in reading time (p = 0.50) or reading speed (p = 0.56) was found between the two fonts. The correlation between the two fonts was high for both reading time and speed (r = 0.93)."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED against the primary source (PMC open-access full text, PMC9804255) and an independent search summary. Daxer et al. 2022, "Towards a standardisation of reading charts: Font effects on reading performance—Times New Roman with serifs versus the sans serif font Helvetica," Ophthalmic and Physiological Optics, DOI 10.1111/opo.13039, PMID 35972034 — a peer-reviewed primary source, appropriate strength for a [STRONG] tag. The exact p-values are confirmed: paperback layout reading time p=0.50, reading speed p=0.56, no significant TNR-vs-Helvetica difference; mean errors identical (0.31±0.58/text). Matched conditions are real and rigorous: 111 words / 179 syllables / 660 characters per paragraph, matched difficulty, and x-heights EQUALIZED to 1.57 mm with identical 4.04 mm line spacing (equalizing x-height rather than nominal point size is the correct control, since TNR has a smaller x-height — this strengthens the 'matched size' framing). The bound is accurate: stimuli were "printed on single DIN A5 pages," paper-based not on-screen, so the claim's caveat ('constrains rather than proves the screen-specific case') is correct. Study's own conclusion supports the framing: "legibility of Helvetica and Times New Roman is similar... interchangeable standard typefaces." Non-refuting nitpicks only: claim says r=0.93 vs source r=0.94 (trivial; core p-values exact); study used "Helvetica Neue" but its own title calls it "Helvetica"; context is clinical reading-chart standardization, n=36, ages 18-38, short passages — these are bounds, not contradictions, and the claim already scopes itself. Corroborated by trade coverage ("Font Style Is Unlikely To Affect Reading Performance," Optometry Advisor). No credible source disputes it; consistent with the broader serif/sans literature. No conflict with the color or charting skills.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found. Independent corroboration: Optometry Advisor ("Font Style Is Unlikely To Affect Reading Performance," reporting the same Daxer study) and the PMC full text both confirm; broader serif-vs-sans reading-speed literature aligns with a null effect under matched conditions.

Bound: stimuli were PRINTED (paperback/newspaper), not on-screen, so this constrains rather than proves the screen-specific case."

**Source:** https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/opo.13039 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "For the paperback layout, no significant difference in reading time (p = 0.50) or reading speed (p = 0.56) was found between the two fonts. The correlation between the two fonts was high for both reading time and speed (r = 0.93)."

Söhne (Kris Sowersby, released 2019, actively sold) defaults to proportional lining figures with tabular available via the tnum feature, which is exactly what "ships tabular numerals via the tnum OpenType feature" states. **Supporting quote:** "The minimum font size for chart text elements is 14 pixels (px) for legibility. In small multiple charts, you can go down to 12px if necessary."

*Evidence:* The quote is verbatim from the primary source. I fetched the ONS Service Manual chart typography page (https://service-manual.ons.gov.uk/data-visualisation/build-specifications/typography) and it states exactly: "The minimum font size for chart text elements is 14 pixels (px) for legibility" and "In small multiple charts, you can go down to 12px if necessary." The claim — 14px minimum, 12px only in small-multiple charts — is a faithful reading, not an overreach: the source scopes the 12px exception specifically to small multiples, so "only in small-multiple charts" is accurate. Source quality matches the claim's strength: ONS is the UK Office for National Statistics, an official government data-visualisation standard — a recognized type system, exactly the kind of named authority the [CONVENTION] tag requires. The claim is correctly tagged [CONVENTION — ONS Service Manual], not presented as controlled academic proof, so it does not overstate. No contradicting evidence found; a 14px chart-text floor is consistent with general accessibility guidance and other systems' minimums, so nothing disputes it. The page shows no publication date, but ONS Service Manual is actively maintained and chart-text minimums are not a fast-moving field, so no outdatedness concern. Not marketing — official standards guidance. Nothing here conflicts with the colour or charting skills (it composes with data-ink/restraint guidance).

#### V61 `acd91871` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Wider letter-spacing (here +2.5 pt added to Times New Roman 12pt) speeds single-word processing: it reduced first fixation durations, gaze durations, and total fixation time for all readers during silent reading."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7090332/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Wider spacing reduced first fixation durations, gaze durations, and total fixation time for all participants."

*Evidence:* Claim is verbatim-supported by the primary peer-reviewed source (Korinth, Gerstenberger & Fiebach, Frontiers in Psychology 2020, PMC7090332), titled "Wider Letter-Spacing Facilitates Word Processing but Impairs Reading Rates of Fast Readers." Abstract states verbatim: "Wider spacing reduced first fixation durations, gaze durations, and total fixation time for all participants, with slower readers showing stronger effects" and "wide letter spacing reduces single word processing time in typically developed readers during silent reading." Methods confirm Times New Roman 12pt, spacing increased by 2.5 points. First-fixation reduction was ~15.81 ms. The claim is narrowly scoped to single-word PROCESSING (not reading rate) and does not overreach. MANDATORY BOUNDING CAVEAT (does not refute, but must travel with the claim): the SAME study found wider spacing gave NO words-per-minute benefit and slowed FAST readers (~9.45 fewer wpm), because shorter fixations were offset by more fixations and fewer word-skips. Quoting the word-processing finding alone to justify adding tracking to body text would be a misuse. Sample bound: n=24 German university students, German text, non-impaired adults — "all readers" = all participants in one study. Tag for skill: [STRONG] for the word-level facilitation; pair with [STRONG/MYTH-guard] that added tracking does NOT speed overall reading and can slow proficient readers. Does not contradict the color or charting skills.
*Counter-source:* Same paper bounds the claim: Frontiers/PMC7090332 reports wider spacing "did not provide a benefit" in words-per-minute and "faster readers in particular were slowed down" (~9.45 wpm). Adjacent contrary evidence on overall reading: Slattery & Rayner (2013) found added inter-word/inter-letter space can hurt reading efficiency.

**Supporting quote:** "Numerals: Includes tabular numerals (`tnum` feature) suitable for aligned data displays"

*Evidence:* Verified against the primary source (Klim Type Foundry's own Söhne page, klim.co.nz/fonts/soehne/), which is the authoritative record for what the font ships. The page's OpenType feature list explicitly includes "Tabular numerals: tnum" (alongside ss01, ss02, liga, calt, zero, frac, sups, sinf, ordn, case). Tabular = uniform/monospaced-width figures is definitional, and a second corroborating search confirmed tnum "replaces figure glyphs set on proportional widths with corresponding glyphs set on uniform (tabular) widths" — exactly the "monospaced-width" characterization in the claim. Suitability for aligned data displays/tables/dashboards is the textbook, universally accepted purpose of tabular figures, not opinion or marketing puffery. Source quality matches claim strength (foundry primary source). Not outdated (Söhne 2019; OpenType feature inventories are stable). One bound, not a refutation: tnum is an opt-in feature (enabled via font-feature-settings: "tnum"), so tabular figures are available rather than necessarily the default; the claim's wording ("ships ... via the tnum OpenType feature") correctly reflects opt-in availability and does not overclaim default behavior. This is appropriate [CONVENTION]-grade evidence for a data-typography skill — a verifiable technical fact about the typeface plus established typographic convention about tabular figures.

**Supporting quote:** "stick with a single type family (making use of its various weights, widths and features); use a superfamily that includes sans and serif siblings; limit yourself to a specific historical period; use typefaces from the same type designer. *Evidence:* Primary source confirmed: "A Pocket Guide to Combining Typefaces" by Tim Brown (Typekit/Five Simple Steps; orig. 2013, re-hosted at the cited blog.typekit.com/.../combiningtypefaces.pdf). Tim Brown (Adobe Typekit Type Manager, author of this guide and "Flexible Typesetting") is a named authority the criteria explicitly sanction. The claim accurately paraphrases the quote and is epistemically conservative: it tags the point [CONVENTION] and frames it as a named-authority caution against treating pairing rules as proof of success — not as empirical fact. No overreach, correct attribution, source quality matches the convention-level strength, and the conceptual point is not time-sensitive. The claim's own content (rules don't prove a pairing works) aligns with the skill's separation of proof from convention.

#### V64 `ae60e56e` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The cost of wider letter-spacing scales with reading skill: individual letter-spacing effect correlated negatively with reading speed (r = -0.62), and a 347-wpm reader lost about 27 wpm under wider spacing."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7090332/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Individual differences in letter spacing effect correlated negatively with individual wpm values"

*Evidence:* Claim is faithfully supported by the primary source (Korinth, Gerstenberger & Fiebach, "Wider Letter-Spacing Facilitates Word Processing but Impairs Reading Rates of Fast Readers," Frontiers in Psychology, 2020, PMC7090332 — peer-reviewed, reviewers include Manuel Perea). The exact numbers check out: (1) The paper states "individual difference values ... are correlated negatively with individual wpm values of the normal spacing condition, r = −0.62, t(22) = −3.73, p < 0.01" — matching the claimed r = -0.62. (2) The paper states "a very fast reader ... who read on average 347 words per minute under normal spacing conditions showed a strong decrease in reading rates of 27 words less per minute when letter-spacing was wider" — exactly matching "a 347-wpm reader lost about 27 wpm." (3) The framing "cost of wider letter-spacing scales with reading skill" is precisely what the negative correlation means and is echoed by the paper's own title and conclusion (faster readers showed greater declines). No overreach. Bounds worth flagging in the skill (not refuting): N=24 German university students, typical (non-dyslexic) adults, Times New Roman 12pt, normal vs +2.5pt wider; the 347-wpm figure is one illustrative data point (Figure 4B). The finding is also consistent with — not contradicted by — the crowding literature (e.g., Zorzi et al. 2012) where extra spacing HELPS dyslexic/slow readers; the skill divergence is real and reader-skill-dependent, which is exactly this paper's point. Source quality matches claim strength; current; not marketing.

#### V65 `ae65d99e` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "There is NO set of consistently faster (or slower) fonts across the population as a whole — the font effect emerges per individual, not as a universal ranking. (In the mixed-effects model the population-level font effect was non-significant for younger readers and only weakly significant for older readers, R2_beta=.005.) This bounds and partly disconfirms any 'best font for everyone' or simple serif-vs-sans speed claim."

**Source:** https://shaunwallace.org/files/Readability__TOCHI.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "indicating that for these factors, reading speed did not trend in a consistent direction across the population. Regarding fonts, this means that there is not a set of consistently faster fonts (conversely, slower) across the population as a whole."

*Evidence:* Verified directly against the primary-source PDF (Wallace et al. 2022, ACM TOCHI, N=352, 16 fonts). The supporting quote is confirmed verbatim: "...font, reading device, and self-reported topic interest were found non-significant by the LME analysis... Regarding fonts, this means that there is not a set of consistently faster fonts (conversely, slower) across the population as a whole." The statistics match exactly: the all-participants LME found font NON-significant (Table 2: X^2=19.38, df=15, p=0.197); the older-participants sub-model (Section 6.1.3, age >=35) found font "significantly affect reading speed (p=0.01, R2_b=.005)" — precisely the claim's "weakly significant for older readers, R2_beta=.005." The paper's own thesis ("Different Fonts Increase Reading Speed for Different Individuals") and abstract ("one font does not fit all") confirm the per-individual, not-universal framing. The interpretive part ("partly disconfirms any best-font-for-everyone or simple serif-vs-sans speed claim") is correctly hedged with "partly" and bounded to reading SPEED. One minor, non-refuting imprecision: the claim labels the non-significant model "younger readers," but it is technically the all-participants model (younger-skewed, avg age 33); the paper itself frames the younger-vs-older contrast (Figure 11), so the paraphrase is substantively accurate. Source is peer-reviewed, current (2022), well-powered, primary — matches the claim's strength. No credible counter-evidence found.
*Counter-source:* None found. Secondary coverage (phys.org, UCF CECS, Adobe/ReadabilityMatters, Fast Company) all corroborate the "individual differences > overall effect / no one-size-fits-all" finding; no source disputes it.

#### V66 `ae7f6d4f` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The cost of wider letter-spacing scales with reading skill: individual letter-spacing effect correlated negatively with reading speed (r = -0.62), and a 347-wpm reader lost about 27 wpm under wider spacing."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7090332/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Individual differences in letter spacing effect correlated negatively with individual wpm values"

*Evidence:* All three numbers are verbatim-confirmed in the primary source: Korinth, Gerstenberger & Fiebach (2020), "Wider Letter-Spacing Facilitates Word Processing but Impairs Reading Rates of Fast Readers," Frontiers in Psychology 11:444 (peer-reviewed controlled eye-movement study, n=24, t(22) matches). Paper states: individual difference values (wider minus normal wpm) "are correlated negatively with individual wpm values of the normal spacing condition, r = −0.62" (t(22)=−3.73, p<0.01); and "a very fast reader... who read on average 347 words per minute under normal spacing... showed a strong decrease in reading rates of 27 words less per minute when letter-spacing was wider." Direction confirmed: "wider letter spacing did not provide a benefit, and faster readers in particular were slowed down," while slower readers show no/weak effect. Bounding caveats (qualify, do not refute): the measured variable is reading SPEED (wpm), so "reading skill" is a loose substitution; small homogeneous sample (24 skilled German students); the r=−0.62 is a difference-score (wider−normal) correlated with its own component (normal), carrying a known mathematical-coupling/regression-to-mean bias toward negative correlation, so magnitude may be inflated; scope is silent reading in skilled adults. The dyslexia-benefit literature (Zorzi 2012) is consistent, not contradictory, since slow readers are unharmed here. No refutation trigger applies.
*Counter-source:* No credible source contradicts the specific finding; Zorzi et al. 2012 (extra-large spacing helps dyslexics) is consistent because it concerns slow/impaired readers whom this paper finds unharmed by wider spacing.

#### V67 `ae91fe2c` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The uppercase legibility/reading-speed advantage appears only at small text sizes (low acuity reserve) and disappears once text is large enough — so at comfortable body-text sizes there is no uppercase reading-speed benefit. This is the key bound: case effects are size-dependent."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2016788/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "the upper-case advantage disappears, when text is large enough"

*Evidence:* Claim is faithfully supported by the primary source: Arditi & Cho (2007), "Letter case and text legibility in normal and low vision," Vision Research (PMC2016788), a peer-reviewed psychophysics study. The paper found uppercase was read significantly faster at acuity reserve of 2 (small text near the acuity limit) by both normal- and low-vision readers, and that "the upper-case advantage disappears, when text is large enough" — at acuity reserve of 10 (normally-sighted) no case advantage remained. The authors attribute the effect to text size: above the critical print size reading speed plateaus, so the small size difference from case (uppercase letters being physically larger at a given point size) stops mattering. This is exactly the claim's bound: the uppercase reading-speed advantage is size-dependent and gone at comfortable/large sizes. The claim is, if anything, the conservative reading — it does NOT assert a general uppercase superiority (which would clash with the classic Tinker/Paterson "ALL-CAPS reads slower for continuous text" literature); instead it bounds the effect, which is consistent with both bodies of work. No credible source disputes the size-dependence. One qualification, not a refutation: for low-vision readers reading near their acuity threshold (low acuity reserve), uppercase does still help — the paper's practical point — so "comfortable body-text size" must mean normal vision at standard sizes, where the claim holds. Caveat: small N (9 participants), Arial only; standard for psychophysics but worth noting. For an evidence-based typography skill this should be tagged [STRONG] with the explicit size/acuity-reserve bound and the low-vision exception stated.
*Counter-source:* Classic Tinker/Paterson "ALL-CAPS reads ~10-20% slower for continuous reading" literature — but this reinforces, not contradicts, the "no uppercase benefit at normal sizes" half of the claim.

#### V68 `ae9776d0` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[CONVENTION — Tim Brown] In a multi-typeface system the body/text typeface should be the 'anchor', because text faces are engineered to survive varied settings whereas display faces fail at small sizes and on coarse/low-resolution screens. This is a concrete, falsifiable hierarchy rule for choosing the primary UI/body face."

**Source:** https://blog.typekit.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/combiningtypefaces.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Make your body text typeface the anchor if possible, because this text represents a majority of your content—and because text faces are built to withstand a variety of settings, whereas display faces can rarely be used at small sizes or at coarse resolutions."

*Evidence:* Quote faithfully supports the claim; no overreach. Verified the source exists and the "body text as anchor" concept is in Tim Brown's "Combining Typefaces" (Typekit/Adobe pocket guide) via independent summaries (designmodo, Designer News, Lapa.Ninja). The underlying rationale — text faces are engineered for small sizes/varied settings while display faces fail at small sizes — is one of the most settled principles in typography, corroborated independently: Walter Tracy ("text types when enlarged can be used for headings, display types, if reduced, cannot be used for text setting"), Fonts.com/MyFonts (Text v. Display), CreativePro, Monotype, EITCA. Source quality matches claim strength: tagged [CONVENTION], not [STRONG]; Tim Brown is a recognized authority (Head of Typography at Adobe Typekit/Fonts, author of "Flexible Typesetting," A Book Apart) on the skill's approved named-authority list, so no peer-review bar applies. Two bounds noted but non-refuting: (1) Brown hedges "if possible" — a brand anchored on a distinctive display face is a legitimate exception, so "falsifiable hierarchy rule" is slightly generous framing of a heuristic; (2) the "coarse resolution" rationale is softened by modern high-DPI/retina screens, but the small-size-failure half is unchanged. Not marketing, not outdated, well-attributed.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found. Searches for contrary evidence instead returned uniform corroboration (Walter Tracy via CreativePro/Fonts.com; Monotype; EITCA Academy). Could not fetch the PDF directly (exceeded size limit), so the exact verbatim wording was confirmed via independent secondary summaries plus overwhelming independent corroboration of the underlying principle rather than direct primary retrieval.

#### V69 `aecb4c62` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Material 3 recommends a line-height ratio of 1.2x the type size for display/headline/title styles and roughly 1.5x the type size for body/label styles."

**Source:** https://m3.material.io/styles/typography/applying-type (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "For display/headline/title: "a line height ratio of 1.2 times the type size" ... For body/label: "a line height ratio around 1.5 times the type size""

*Evidence:* The claim accurately reports what Material 3 says. The M3 "applying type" page (primary source) states verbatim: "For larger type legibility using styles like title, headline, and display, a line height ratio of 1.2 times the type size is recommended, while for smaller body copy using styles like body and label, a line height ratio around 1.5 times the type size is recommended" (confirmed via WebSearch surfacing m3.material.io/styles/typography/applying-type). The claim's wording matches — including "roughly 1.5x" tracking M3's "around 1.5 times." It is correctly framed as a Material 3 *recommendation* (a named major type system), which is exactly the [CONVENTION] strength being asserted, not a [STRONG] academic result. BOUND worth carrying into the skill: M3's own implemented type-scale tokens do not strictly follow this rule of thumb — measured ratios range ~1.12 to 1.5. E.g. Display Large 57/64 = 1.12 (tighter than 1.2), but Title Medium 16/24 = 1.5 and Body Large 16/24 = 1.5; Headline Small 24/32 = 1.33; Label Small 11/16 = 1.45. So 1.2x and 1.5x are M3's heuristic summary (larger type → tighter line-height, smaller type → looser), not exact per-token values, and the "title" family in particular spans both (Title Medium is actually 1.5x). This caveat qualifies but does not refute the claim, since the claim is about M3's stated recommendation, which the source does make. No contradicting source disputes that M3 issues this guidance.
*Counter-source:* Actual M3 type-scale tokens (m3.material.io/styles/typography/type-scale-tokens; Egor Tarasov M3 cheatsheet) show ratios 1.12–1.5, not a clean 1.2/1.5 split — a bound, not a refutation.

#### V70 `aed00636` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "In a perceptually size-normalized study of 16 fonts, each individual's reading speed increased ~35% comparing their fastest vs. slowest font, with no loss of reading comprehension — i.e., 'one font does not fit all' (individuated readability). This is a within-individual best-vs-worst gap, not a universal font ranking."

**Source:** https://shaunwallace.org/files/Readability__TOCHI.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Participants' reading speeds (measured in WPM) increased by 35% when comparing fastest and slowest fonts without affecting reading comprehension. High WPM variability across fonts suggests that one font does not [fit] all."

*Evidence:* Verified directly from the primary PDF text (Wallace, Bylinskii, et al., ACM TOCHI 2022, "Towards Individuated Reading Experiences", doi 10.1145/3502222). Every element of the claim matches the paper verbatim: (1) "To systematically compare 16 common fonts, we designed a study..." and "We showed participants all 16 fonts used in the study" — 16 fonts confirmed. (2) "Our studies normalize font size by human perception" (Times 16px reference + crowd-driven perceptual normalization) — perceptual size-normalization confirmed, which controls the raw-size confound. (3) The 35% is explicitly WITHIN-individual: "participants read 35% faster in their fastest font compared to their slowest font (232 WPM, on average)" and "By focusing our results on the individual, we see a potential 35% increase in reading speed (comparing fastest to slowest fonts) while maintaining comprehension"; title is "Different Fonts Increase Reading Speed for Different Individuals." (4) "without affecting reading comprehension" — comprehension preserved. (5) "High WPM variability across fonts suggests that one font does not fit all" — verbatim. The claim is responsibly bounded (it disavows a universal ranking, matching the paper's individual-focus framing). Refinement, not refutation: each participant read 5 of the 16 fonts, and the 35% is each participant's fastest-vs-slowest among their 5 ("out of the five fonts tested") — this strengthens the within-individual framing. Source is peer-reviewed top-tier HCI (54 pp, Cohen's d effect sizes, WPM exclusion 100-650 and comprehension >=0.67 thresholds), current (2022), not marketing. A targeted search for methodological critiques surfaced no published source disputing the reported finding; one related-work caveat about speed/comprehension trade-offs does not apply since this study measured and reported no comprehension loss. The one earlier conflicting read ("between-font across participants") was a small-model misinterpretation of an abstract snippet, contradicted by the full paper body.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found. A search for critiques returned only the paper itself and related readability work (e.g., "Considering the Speed and Comprehension Trade-Off in Reading Mediated by Typography"), none of which refute the within-individual 35% finding; that trade-off caveat does not apply because Wallace et al. explicitly verified comprehension was unaffected.

#### V71 `aed33089` — **REFUTED** · confidence: medium
*Claim under review:* "A medium line length of ~55 characters per line supported the most effective reading across both normal and fast reading speeds, grounding the conventional ~45–75 (or '66') character measure rule in controlled comprehension data rather than taste."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1071581901904586 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "a medium line length (55 characters per line) appears to support effective reading at normal and fast speeds"

*Evidence:* Factual core is true but the interpretive claim overreaches and ignores contradicting controlled evidence. Dyson & Haselgrove (2001) tested only THREE widely-spaced line lengths (25/55/100 cpl); 55 cpl gave best COMPREHENSION at normal and fast speeds (quote accurate). But this 3-point study cannot "ground the conventional ~45-75/'66' rule" — it only shows medium beats extremes; it cannot locate the optimum within the 45-75 band and never tested 66. Worse, the controlled reading-SPEED literature contradicts the "rather than taste" framing: Dyson & Kipping (1998, same author) found LONGER lines (100 cpl) read FASTER than 55 cpl; Shaikh & Chaparro (2005) found ~95 cpl fastest; Bernard, Fernandez & Hull (2002) found NO reliable speed difference. The "55 easiest" result is a PREFERENCE finding that explicitly dissociates from performance (readers preferred 55 while reading 100 faster). So the measure rule is a comprehension/speed/preference balance, not data that displaces taste. Salvageable narrower claim: ~55 cpl aids comprehension over very short/long lines (Dyson & Haselgrove 2001) — but NOT that it grounds the 45-75/66 convention.
*Counter-source:* Dyson & Kipping (1998) "Effects of Line Length and Method of Movement," Visible Language 32(2): 100 cpl read FASTER than 55 cpl while 55 only rated easiest (preference, not performance); also Bernard, Fernandez & Hull (2002) found no reliable speed difference and Shaikh & Chaparro (2005) found ~95 cpl fastest — contradicting "grounded in controlled comprehension data rather than taste."

#### V72 `aefae38e` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Wider letter-spacing (here +2.5 pt added to Times New Roman 12pt) speeds single-word processing: it reduced first fixation durations, gaze durations, and total fixation time for all readers during silent reading."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7090332/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Wider spacing reduced first fixation durations, gaze durations, and total fixation time for all participants."

*Evidence:* Every specific in the claim checks out against the primary peer-reviewed source (Koremann, Frontiers in Psychology 2020, PMC7090332, "Wider Letter-Spacing Facilitates Word Processing but Impairs Reading Rates of Fast Readers"):

1. "+2.5 pt added to Times New Roman 12pt" — CONFIRMED exactly. Study added 2.5 points to TNR 12pt (center-to-center 0.26° normal vs 0.38° wide).
2. "speeds single-word processing" — CONFIRMED; this is the paper's headline finding ("Facilitates Word Processing").
3. "reduced first fixation durations, gaze durations, and total fixation time" — CONFIRMED with exact magnitudes: −15.81 ms first fixation, −7.20 ms gaze, −14.97 ms total fixation, all significant across the sample.
4. "for all readers" — supported for the fixation-duration measures: the reductions were a significant main effect "for all participants." Reading speed modulated the MAGNITUDE (stronger for slow readers) but not the direction of the fixation-duration reductions.
5. "during silent reading" — CONFIRMED; silent reading of multi-line passages (155–189 words).

Source quality matches claim strength: controlled eye-tracking study, peer-reviewed, primary, 2020 (current). Not marketing, not a benchmark.

CRITICAL BOUND (must accompany the claim, but does NOT refute it): the claim is carefully scoped to single-word PROCESSING and does not assert any reading-speed benefit. The same study found wider spacing IMPAIRED overall reading rate (−9.45 wpm on average; fast readers lost 20–30 wpm; r≈−0.62 between baseline speed and harm), because of more fixations per word and less word-skipping. So the faithful skill framing is [STRONG]: "wider letter-spacing shortens per-word fixation durations, yet slows overall reading for normal/fast readers" — never "wider spacing helps reading." As worded, the claim makes no reading-rate assertion and is therefore accurate, not an overreach.
*Counter-source:* https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2020.00444/full (same study, full text — confirms word-processing benefit but reading-rate impairment, especially for fast readers)

#### V73 `af3ab46b` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The uppercase legibility/reading-speed advantage appears only at small text sizes (low acuity reserve) and disappears once text is large enough — so at comfortable body-text sizes there is no uppercase reading-speed benefit. This is the key bound: case effects are size-dependent."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2016788/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "the upper-case advantage disappears, when text is large enough"

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported by the primary peer-reviewed source. Arditi & Cho (2007), "Letter case and text legibility in normal and low vision," Vision Research (= PMC2016788), abstract states verbatim: "Reading speeds were higher for upper- than for mixed-case text at sizes twice acuity size; at larger sizes, the upper-case advantage disappeared." Legibility thresholds: lowercase ~0.1 log unit higher than uppercase (uppercase more legible only near the acuity limit). The supporting quote ("the upper-case advantage disappears, when text is large enough") is faithful to the abstract. Every component checks out: advantage exists only at small sizes / low acuity reserve (mechanism = uppercase letters are physically larger, helping only when size is the binding constraint, esp. low vision); disappears above critical print size; no uppercase reading-speed benefit at comfortable body sizes; size-dependence is a legitimate generalization of the study's upper/lower/mixed × size design. Method is strong (RSVP + continuous reading, normal + visually impaired). Not outdated — corroborated by Legge & Bigelow (2011) critical-print-size/acuity-reserve framework and NN/g glanceable-type work. Not marketing. The claim is appropriately bounded: it asserts only "no benefit" at large sizes, never a penalty, so it stays accurate even though Arditi & Cho found uppercase EQUAL (not slower) to mixed-case at large sizes — a partial challenge to the classic Tinker all-caps-slower convention.

#### V74 `af810b3a` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[CONVENTION — Tim Brown] In a multi-typeface system the body/text typeface should be the 'anchor', because text faces are engineered to survive varied settings whereas display faces fail at small sizes and on coarse/low-resolution screens. This is a concrete, falsifiable hierarchy rule for choosing the primary UI/body face."

**Source:** https://blog.typekit.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/combiningtypefaces.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Make your body text typeface the anchor if possible, because this text represents a majority of your content—and because text faces are built to withstand a variety of settings, whereas display faces can rarely be used at small sizes or at coarse resolutions."

*Evidence:* Source confirmed real: Tim Brown, "Combining Typefaces: A Pocket Guide" (Typekit/Five Simple Steps, 2016); Brown is a recognized authority (Modular Scale tool, Adobe/Typekit type lead, author of Flexible Typography) and is on the criteria's named-authority list. The claim is a faithful paraphrase of the quote and is correctly tagged [CONVENTION], not [STRONG] — source quality matches claim strength. Could not fetch the PDF directly (exceeds 10MB limit), but the substance is corroborated by multiple secondary sources: search confirms "Brown suggests using the body text as the anchor in appropriate settings like websites and magazines"; designmodo's review confirms the "anchor" concept and body text as anchor because it "normally contains the bulk of the content." The text-vs-display premise is independently corroborated as mainstream typographic doctrine, NOT one author's opinion: text faces are "designed to be legible and readable at small sizes" with strokes that "hold up at smaller sizes," whereas display faces "become distracting or illegible at smaller sizes" and "legibility suffers when small font sizes are used on a low-resolution screen" (CreativePro/Strizver, MyFonts Fontology). No credible source contradicts this; one reviewer calls it "curious" but offers a stylistic note, not a refutation. Only weakness: "coarse resolutions" (2016) is mildly dated given high-DPI screens, but the small-sizes argument and low-DPI displays keep the convention valid. Properly bounded as convention.
*Counter-source:* designmodo.com/combine-typefaces (reviewer calls body-text-as-anchor "curious" since it's the least-expressive element — stylistic observation, not a refutation of the rationale)

#### V75 `afb5d595` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Font PREFERENCE does not predict reading SPEED, even when font size is controlled — preference and effectiveness are decoupled. Directly supports labeling 'this font looks/feels better' as taste, not proven performance."

**Source:** https://shaunwallace.org/files/Readability__TOCHI.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Our results show that no single factor predicts reading speed, and interestingly, preference does not predict speed even when font size is controlled. Our main takeaway is that different fonts work best for different people."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported by the primary source and current. Verbatim quote confirmed across the ACM-indexed text and abstract: "no single factor predicts reading speed, and interestingly, preference does not predict speed even when font size is controlled" (Wallace et al., "Towards Individuated Reading Experiences," ACM TOCHI 29(4), Art. 38, 2022). Source is top-tier peer-reviewed; robust design (n=352, ages 18-71; 16 common typefaces; reading speed in WPM; 35% fastest-vs-slowest gap with comprehension maintained). The EurekAlert/UCF press release independently states "personal preference for fonts did not necessarily predict reading speed" and "people didn't always know what their best fonts were." A deliberate disconfirming search reinforced rather than contradicted the result ("8 in 10 believed their favorite font would be best, but the preferred font was seldom best"); no credible source disputes the preference-performance dissociation. The claim's paraphrase "preference and effectiveness are decoupled" is faithful, and the application to labeling aesthetic feel as taste vs. proven performance is a sound inference. Bounding caveat (not refuting): the claim correctly scopes "decoupled" to preference-vs-SPEED; the study still shows font choice has a large measurable per-individual effect, so the skill must not generalize this into "font choice doesn't matter."
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found. Disconfirming search (font preference correlates with reading speed) and the related familiarity study (Beymer/Larson, "How does typeface familiarity affect reading performance and reader preference?") both reinforce the preference-performance dissociation rather than contradicting it.

### Typography gaps — legibility, measure, scale, pairing, typeface choice, numerals

#### V77 `a07810dd` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "WCAG SC 1.4.10 Reflow requires content to be presentable without loss of information/functionality and without two-dimensional scrolling at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels (for vertical-scrolling content) or a height equivalent to 256 CSS pixels (for horizontal-scrolling content)."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/reflow.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Content can be presented without loss of information or functionality, and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions for: Vertical scrolling content at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels; Horizontal scrolling content at a height equivalent to 256 CSS pixels."

*Evidence:* Primary source (https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/reflow.html) confirms the claim word-for-word: "Content can be presented without loss of information or functionality, and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions for: Vertical scrolling content at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels; Horizontal scrolling content at a height equivalent to 256 CSS pixels." The claim correctly maps 320 px to WIDTH (vertical-scroll content) and 256 px to HEIGHT (horizontal-scroll content). Source is the W3C/WAI standards body itself — highest possible quality for a normative claim. Not outdated (WCAG 2.1, 2018, carried unchanged into 2.2). Not marketing. The only omission is the exception clause ("Except for parts of the content which require two-dimensional layout for usage or meaning"), which does not falsify the core requirement. Could not refute on any of the five checklist criteria.
*Counter-source:* None found. No credible source disputes these normative thresholds; the figures derive directly from 400% zoom of 1280x1024 (320x256 CSS px).

#### V78 `a0dba26a` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Fontaine reduces CLS by scanning your @font-face rules and generating fallback @font-face rules whose metrics (ascent-override / descent-override / line-gap-override) are crafted to match a local fallback font, so the fallback occupies the same space as the web font and swap causes no reflow."

**Source:** https://github.com/nuxt-modules/fontaine (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Reduces CLS by using local font fallbacks with crafted font metrics. ... scan[s] your `@font-face` rules and generate[s] fallback rules with the correct metrics."

*Evidence:* The nuxt-modules/fontaine repo (primary source) confirms the exact mechanism: "Reduces CLS by using local font fallbacks with crafted font metrics" and "[Nuxt/Fontaine] will scan your @font-face rules and generate fallback rules with the correct metrics," then appends the fallback (e.g. font-family: 'Roboto', 'Roboto fallback'). The generated fallback @font-face uses ascent-override, descent-override, and line-gap-override (plus size-adjust) — exactly as the claim states. Independent corroboration from Chrome for Developers / web.dev ("Improved font fallbacks" and "Framework tools for font fallbacks") describes the same technique and lists Fontaine as a framework tool that automates it. The standard CSS formulas (ascent-override = web font ascent / (UPM × size-adjust), etc.) make the fallback occupy the web font's line box so swap causes minimal/no vertical reflow. Source quality (primary repo + Chrome dev blog) matches the claim's strength; the technique is current (modern 2022+ best practice, actively maintained). Two minor imprecisions that do not rise to refutation: (a) the wording "crafted to match a local fallback font" is slightly muddled — the overrides are computed FROM the web font's metrics and applied TO the local fallback so it matches the web font, which the claim's result clause states correctly; (b) "swap causes no reflow" is marginally stronger than the repo's own framing ("reduce CLS"), since horizontal/variable-font edge cases can leave residual shift — but this is a qualification of degree, not a contradiction of mechanism. Net: well-supported, current, primary-sourced.
*Counter-source:* No credible source disputes the mechanism. Qualifying notes only: Vincent Bernat ("Fixing layout shifts caused by web fonts", vincent.bernat.ch/en/blog/2024-cls-webfonts) and others observe metric matching reduces but does not always perfectly zero out CLS (variable fonts, differing glyph widths). This qualifies "no reflow" as "near-eliminated," not refutes the claim.
