**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/resize-text.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Content satisfies the success criterion if it can be scaled up to 200% - that is, up to twice the width and height."

*Evidence:* VERBATIM CONFIRMED. The W3C Understanding doc for SC 1.4.4 (https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/resize-text.html) contains the exact sentence quoted: "Content satisfies the success criterion if it can be scaled up to 200% - that is, up to twice the width and height." I confirmed it word-for-word via WebFetch. The normative criterion reads: "Except for captions and images of text, text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200 percent without loss of content or functionality." Skeptical checks all pass: (1) Source quality — this is the PRIMARY authoritative W3C Understanding document, the canonical normative standard; can't get a better source for a [STRONG] standards claim. (2) Currency — I fetched the WCAG 2.2 version (https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/resize-text.html) and it retains the identical sentence; the criterion is unchanged across WCAG 2.0/2.1/2.2, so not outdated. (3) Not marketing/forum/cherry-pick — it is a W3C Recommendation. (4) Claim does not overreach the quote: it asserts exactly the 200% = "twice the width and height" threshold, which the quote states directly. One minor framing note (not a refutation): the full normative SC qualifies "200%" with "without loss of content or functionality" — the verifier should ensure the skill's statement of SC 1.4.4 includes that qualifier — but the narrow threshold claim under review (200% = twice width and height) is accurate as stated. No credible source disputes the 200% figure; the only contrary item found (w3c/wcag GitHub issue #4730) questions practical text-resizing UX, not the threshold value itself.
*Counter-source:* w3c/wcag GitHub issue #4730 questions practical resizing UX but does not dispute the 200% threshold; no source contradicts the figure.

#### V80 `a11ea1f5` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "There is no evidence base supporting specialized dyslexia fonts as an effective practice, and relying on them risks diverting resources from proven interventions."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5629233/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Currently, there is no documentation to support a specialized font is an evidence-based practice."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and corroborated, not refuted. PRIMARY SOURCE (Wery & Diliberto 2017, PMC5629233, Annals of Dyslexia, Springer) contains BOTH clauses near-verbatim: Clause 1 = exact quote "Currently, there is no documentation to support a specialized font is an evidence-based practice"; Clause 2 = "That time and resource could be use on other interventions that are more likely to improve students' reading ability." Study: n=12 elementary students with confirmed dyslexia, single-subject alternating-treatment design (OpenDyslexic vs Arial vs Times New Roman), negative effect sizes (IRD −49.65% to −88.65%), "no improvement in reading rate or accuracy." So Clause 2 is NOT an overreach of the quote — it is the authors' own stated conclusion. CORROBORATION (independent controlled studies): Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013 (eye-tracking, no improvement in reading time/fixations); Kuster et al. 2018 "Dyslexie font does not benefit reading in children with or without dyslexia" (participants preferred Arial); British Dyslexia Association recommends standard sans-serif + spacing/size, not special fonts. DISCONFIRMING EVIDENCE SOUGHT: only Marinus et al. 2016 found a benefit for Dyslexie font, but attributed it to inter-letter/word SPACING, not the special letterforms — a qualification consistent with the broader finding (spacing helps in any font), not a contradiction. Source is peer-reviewed, current (2013–2018, no reversal since), and is anti-marketing (debunks a commercial product), so checklist items 3/4/5 pass. CAVEAT (not refutation): "diverting resources" is the authors' recommendation rather than a measured outcome, and n=12 is small, but it is framed correctly as a "risk" and is backed by larger studies. Maps cleanly to a [MYTH] tag for "dyslexia-friendly fonts help"; what helps = size, spacing, sans-serif, line length.
*Counter-source:* Marinus et al. 2016 ("A Special Font for People with Dyslexia: Does it Work and, if so, Why?", Dyslexia) found faster reading with the Dyslexie font — but attributed the gain to letter/word spacing, not the special glyph shapes; this qualifies rather than contradicts the claim.

#### V81 `a1344640` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "font-display:swap causes layout shift / poor CLS because the fallback and web font differ in character size, shifting content when the swap happens — this is the modern motivation for metric-override fallbacks (TOPIC A: CLS from font swap)."

**Source:** https://developer.chrome.com/blog/framework-tools-font-fallback (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The biggest issue with `swap` is the jarring effect, where the difference in character sizes of the two fonts results in screen content shifting around... This leads to poor CLS scores, especially for text-heavy websites."

*Evidence:* Claim is faithfully supported by the primary source and corroborated by multiple independent authorities. WebFetch of developer.chrome.com/blog/framework-tools-font-fallback confirmed the exact quote: "The biggest issue with `swap` is the jarring effect, where the difference in character sizes of the two fonts results in screen content shifting around... This leads to poor CLS scores, especially for text-heavy websites." The same page proposes the metric-override fix (size-adjust, ascent-override, descent-override, line-gap-override via @font-face) and the Fontaine/Capsize tooling — exactly as the claim states. Independent corroboration: web.dev ("Best practices for fonts," "Optimize CLS"), Material Design (m3.material.io "Reduce reflow with web fonts"), DebugBear, Simon Hearne (2021), Vincent Bernat (2024) all confirm font-swap shifts count toward CLS when fallback and web-font metrics differ, and that metric overrides are the fix. No contradicting source found. Source quality (Google/Chrome primary + web.dev canonical) matches the claim's strength; technique is current (2024/2026-dated sources), not outdated, not marketing. The claim is properly conditional ("because the fallback and web font differ in character size") — the correct mechanism. Minor refinement only: the identical shift also occurs under font-display:block/FOIT since invisible text is laid out with fallback metrics, so swap is not the *unique* CLS culprit — but the claim attributes the shift to the metric difference, not to swap exclusively, so this does not refute it. Best tagged [CONVENTION] (named authority best practice), not a controlled study.
*Counter-source:* No credible contradicting source found. Nearest qualification: the same layout shift also occurs with font-display:block/FOIT (text laid out invisibly with fallback metrics), so swap is not uniquely responsible for CLS — but the claim attributes the shift to metric difference, which is accurate.

#### V82 `a17454a5` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The cache partition key is a 'Network Isolation Key' composed of the top-level site and the current-frame site, combined with the resource URL; sites are identified by scheme://eTLD+1 (ignoring subdomains and ports)."

**Source:** https://developers.google.com/web/updates/2020/10/http-cache-partitioning (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "With cache partitioning, cached resources will be keyed using a new "Network Isolation Key" in addition to the resource URL. The Network Isolation Key is composed of the top-level site and the current-frame site."

*Evidence:* All five components verified against primary/authoritative sources. Chrome's own blog (developer.chrome.com/blog/http-cache-partitioning — the migrated home of the cited 2020 developers.google.com article) states the cache key is "a combination of three elements: the top-level website, current-frame site, and URL of the resource" and that "Chrome uses top-level scheme://eTLD+1 and frame scheme://eTLD+1 for cache partitioning." The site definition (scheme + eTLD+1/registrable domain, ignoring subdomains, ignoring ports unlike same-origin) is independently confirmed by MDN and web.dev "same-site/same-origin" docs. The supplied quote directly supports items 1-2 (NIK = top-level + frame site, plus resource URL); the eTLD+1/subdomain/port detail (items 3-5) is not in the quote but is confirmed by the SAME primary source, so it is accurate elaboration, not overreach. Current and not outdated — all major browsers partition the cache (Chrome 86+, Safari, Firefox), so the downstream point that shared CDN/Google Fonts cache no longer helps cross-site is correct. Source is primary (Chrome team), not marketing.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found. PortSwigger Daily Swig, chromestatus, and Mozilla Bugzilla 1536058 corroborate rather than dispute; only nuance is the original URL now redirects to developer.chrome.com/blog/http-cache-partitioning.

#### V83 `a18deb7d` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[MYTH busted] The dyslexia-specific font OpenDyslexic did NOT improve readability or reading speed for dyslexic readers; mainstream fonts (Arial, Helvetica, Verdana) did better. Participants significantly preferred Verdana/Helvetica over OpenDyslexic."

**Source:** https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2513383.2513447 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The fonts designed specifically for dyslexia, OpenDys and OpenDys It., did not lead to a better or worse readability. As in [21], OpenDys did not lead to a faster reading."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported, current, and backed by primary peer-reviewed sources. (1) The supplied quote directly supports the core assertion: Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013 "Good Fonts for Dyslexia" (ACM SIGACCESS; 48 dyslexic readers, 12 fonts, eye-tracking) states "OpenDys did not lead to a better or worse readability... OpenDys did not lead to a faster reading." (2) The "mainstream did better" component matches the paper's recommended set (Helvetica, Courier, Arial, Verdana, Computer Modern) and its finding that "Courier, Helvetica, and Arial led to significantly shorter fixation durations than six other fonts," while OpenDyslexic was not in the fast group. (3) The preference component matches: "study participants strongly preferred Verdana or Helvetica over the OpenDyslexic alternative." Independent replication confirms currency: Wery & Diliberto 2017 (Annals of Dyslexia, PMC5629233) found OpenDyslexic gave WORSE fluency/accuracy than Arial and Times New Roman ("no improvement in reading rate or accuracy"); Kuster et al. found the same for Dyslexie ("no evidence that these fonts lead to improved reading performance"); International Dyslexia Association concurs. Contrary evidence is thin and non-refuting: one preliminary unreplicated 2022 eye-tracking study hinted at marginally better comprehension on long texts but explicitly showed no faster reading or better decoding. Minor wording caveat: "significantly preferred" — secondary sources say "strongly preferred"; unclear the paper ran a formal significance test on that exact contrast — but direction/magnitude are correct, so not a refutation.
*Counter-source:* Only non-refuting qualifier found: a preliminary, unreplicated 2022 eye-tracking study suggesting slightly better comprehension (not speed/decoding) on long texts with OpenDyslexic; does not contradict the claim. No credible source disputes the core finding.

#### V86 `a1dd6e4b` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Fontaine reduces CLS by scanning your @font-face rules and generating fallback @font-face rules whose metrics (ascent-override / descent-override / line-gap-override) are crafted to match a local fallback font, so the fallback occupies the same space as the web font and swap causes no reflow."

**Source:** https://github.com/nuxt-modules/fontaine (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Reduces CLS by using local font fallbacks with crafted font metrics. ... scan[s] your `@font-face` rules and generate[s] fallback rules with the correct metrics."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and current. Primary source (github.com/nuxt-modules/fontaine README) confirms the mechanism verbatim: "scan your @font-face rules and generate fallback rules with the correct metrics," with a worked example showing ascent-override: 92.77%, descent-override: 24.41%, line-gap-override: 0% on a generated local-fallback @font-face, plus automatic appending of the fallback to font-family. Independently corroborated by credible third parties: Chrome for Developers ("Improved font fallbacks" / "Framework tools for font fallbacks"), DebugBear, Speed Kit, and Aleksandr Hovhannisyan — so this is an established, well-documented technique, not an extraordinary or marketing-only claim. The same technique underlies Next.js next/font auto-overrides. Source quality matches claim strength.

Two minor caveats (qualify, do not refute): (1) The claim's absolute "swap causes no reflow" overstates it. The README's own playground shows CLS dropping from 0.34 to 0.013 — reduced, not zero — and explicitly notes residual shift remains (attributed to stylesheet load timing, fixable by inlining all CSS, not to metric mismatch). (2) The metric set matches vertical metrics (ascent/descent/line-gap) and AVERAGE advance width via size-adjust — which the claim's parenthetical omits — but not per-glyph widths, so multi-line text can still wrap differently and produce small residual shift. Accurate framing for a skill: "reduces / near-eliminates swap-induced reflow," not a literal zero guarantee.
*Counter-source:* developer.chrome.com/blog/font-fallbacks (corroborates, with the per-glyph-width limitation); github.com/nuxt-modules/fontaine README (shows non-zero residual CLS 0.013, qualifying the "no reflow" absolutism)

#### V87 `a1e6481b` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Subjective font preference does NOT predict reading performance: Preference Ratings vs Reading Time had a Pearson correlation of only -0.13 (near zero). Preference is not a proxy for reading speed."

**Source:** https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2513383.2513447 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Preference Ratings and Reading Time had a Pearson correlation of -0.13... which implies that there is almost no correlation between the reading time and the participants preferences."

*Evidence:* Claim is faithfully supported by the cited primary source and corroborated widely. DOI 10.1145/2513383.2513447 is the correct paper (Rello & Baeza-Yates, "Good fonts for dyslexia," ACM ASSETS 2013) — peer-reviewed eye-tracking study, 48 dyslexic readers, 12 fonts, measuring reading time, fixation duration, and 1-5 preference ratings. The claim restates the supplied quote ("Pearson correlation of -0.13... almost no correlation between reading time and participants' preferences") without overreach. Sign is coherent: lower reading time = faster, higher rating = preferred, so r=-0.13 is near-zero (slightly faster when preferred) = "not a proxy for reading speed," exactly as claimed. No contradicting source found; only corroboration: multiple summaries confirm the study deliberately recommends fonts on BOTH performance and preference because the two diverge; the wider literature reinforces it — Paterson & Tinker (1940): 70% preferred normal weight, no speed difference; Wallace et al., ACM ToCHI 2022 (10.1145/3502222): 77% believed their preferred font would be fastest, true for only 20%. Source quality matches a non-extraordinary, stable finding (reconfirmed 2022), not marketing/outdated. Consistent with prior typography findings (preference != reading speed; no inherent serif/sans advantage). Caveat (non-refuting): local PDF text extraction failed (no pdftotext/pymupdf/pip), so the literal "-0.13" digit rests on the supplied quote plus strong convergent corroboration rather than my own raw-PDF read; a secondary AI summary's phrase "negative and significant" refers to performance-vs-performance, not preference-vs-reading-time, and a detectable -0.13 is still practically near-zero.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found. Searches returned only corroboration (Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013 study summaries on dyslexia.com, ResearchGate, dyslexiahelp.umich.edu, editingresearch.byu.edu) and reinforcing primary studies (Paterson & Tinker 1940; Wallace et al. ACM ToCHI 2022, DOI 10.1145/3502222 — preferred font was fastest for only 20% despite 77% believing so).

#### V88 `a20e85f9` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The BDA recommends common sans-serif fonts (Arial, Comic Sans, Verdana, Tahoma, Century Gothic, Trebuchet, Calibri, Open Sans) for dyslexic readers — and nowhere in the guide does it recommend or even mention any specially-designed 'dyslexia font' (OpenDyslexic, Dyslexie). This is disconfirming evidence against the 'you need a special dyslexia typeface' belief: the leading dyslexia authority's own advice is plain sans-serif."

**Source:** https://cdn.bdadyslexia.org.uk/uploads/documents/Advice/style-guide/BDA-Style-Guide-2023.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Use sans serif fonts, such as Arial and Comic Sans, as letters can appear less crowded. Alternatives include Verdana, Tahoma, Century Gothic, Trebuchet, Calibri, Open Sans."

*Evidence:* Extracted the full text of the BDA Dyslexia Style Guide 2023 directly from the PDF (CDN blocked WebFetch with 403, so used the identical lbhfinspirehub.com mirror, saved to disk, decoded the embedded streams). VERIFIED BOTH PARTS: (1) The "Readable Fonts" section reads verbatim: "Use sans serif fonts, such as Arial and Comic Sans, as letters can appear less crowded. Alternatives include Verdana, Tahoma, Century Gothic, Trebuchet, Calibri, Open Sans." All eight fonts in the claim match exactly. (2) The full document — all five sections per its own bookmarks (Readable Fonts, Headings and Structure, Colour, Layout, Writing Style) — contains NO occurrence of "OpenDyslexic," "Dyslexie," or any reference to a specially-designed/dyslexia-specific typeface. The font guidance is entirely standard sans-serif plus size (12-14pt / 1-1.2em / 16-19px), inter-letter spacing (~35% of letter width), 1.5/150% line spacing, and left-aligned/no-justification. The claim's interpretation is technically an argument from silence (absence of mention, not an explicit recommendation against special fonts), but the claim is worded accurately — it asserts the BDA's advice IS plain sans-serif and doesn't mention special fonts, which is exactly what the document shows. It does not overstate this as "BDA condemns special fonts." Consistent with the controlled evidence (Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013: OpenDyslexic no reading-time/fixation benefit; de Leeuw 2010: Dyslexie no faster than Arial) and with preference≠speed. Source is primary, current (2023), full-text verified. Saved PDF: /home/anatoly/.claude/projects/-home-anatoly/7c93f7f4-c990-4019-9b53-b595930b21c2/tool-results/webfetch-1782521755120-rt8yq6.pdf
*Counter-source:* Minor caveat: the claim is an argument from silence (the guide's non-mention of special fonts is weaker than an explicit recommendation against them). A 2023 preference study (Broadbent: 58% of those expressing a preference favored OpenDyslexic) shows preference can run the other way, but preference is not reading speed and does not bear on what the BDA guide actually says. Neither weakens the verified factual claim about the guide's content.

#### V89 `a23656c0` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "SC 1.4.12 requires support for paragraph spacing of at least 2 times the font size and letter spacing (tracking) of at least 0.12 times the font size without loss of content or functionality."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Spacing following paragraphs to at least 2 times the font size ... Letter spacing (tracking) to at least 0.12 times the font size"

*Evidence:* Primary W3C source (https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html) confirms the claim verbatim. The normative SC 1.4.12 text requires that "no loss of content or functionality occurs" when setting "Spacing following paragraphs to at least 2 times the font size" and "Letter spacing (tracking) to at least 0.12 times the font size" (alongside line-height ≥1.5× and word-spacing ≥0.16×). The claim's two cited thresholds (paragraph spacing ≥2×, letter spacing ≥0.12×) and the "without loss of content or functionality" qualifier match the standard exactly. The claim's wording "paragraph spacing" = the standard's "spacing following paragraphs" — no semantic overreach. Source quality is maximal (the authoritative normative standard itself), current (WCAG 2.2, Oct 2023; criterion unchanged since WCAG 2.1, 2018). No contrary or qualifying evidence — this is a settled normative criterion, not a contested empirical claim. Correctly tagged [STRONG]/normative-standard. No conflict with the color skill (contrast), charting skill (tabular figures), or prior typography findings.

#### V90 `a23ca74f` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Under SC 1.4.8, line spacing (leading) must be at least space-and-a-half (150%) within paragraphs, and paragraph spacing must be at least 1.5 times larger than the line spacing."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/visual-presentation.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Line spacing (leading) is at least space-and-a-half within paragraphs, and paragraph spacing is at least 1.5 times larger than the line spacing"

*Evidence:* VERBATIM MATCH to the primary normative source. WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.8 Visual Presentation (Level AAA) states, word-for-word: "Line spacing (leading) is at least space-and-a-half within paragraphs, and paragraph spacing is at least 1.5 times larger than the line spacing." The claim restates this exactly: space-and-a-half = 150% line spacing, paragraph spacing >= 1.5x the line spacing. Confirmed at https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/visual-presentation.html and corroborated by multiple secondary sources (thewcag.com, halfaccessible playground, HK Digital Policy Office). The numbers are current in WCAG 2.1 and unchanged in 2.2. Source quality is the highest possible (the standard itself), matching the claim's strength.

CHECKLIST: (1) Supported by quote — yes, verbatim. (2) Contradicting evidence — none found; all sources agree. (3) Source quality — primary W3C normative text. (4) Not outdated — current in 2.1/2.2. (5) Not marketing.

TWO FRAMING NUANCES the skill should encode but which do NOT refute the threshold claim: (a) 1.4.8 is Level AAA (not AA), and the criterion requires only that "a mechanism is available to achieve" these properties — it does NOT mandate that default text be set this way. The claim's word "must be at least space-and-a-half" slightly overstates this as a hard default requirement rather than a mechanism-availability requirement. (b) Do not conflate with the stricter, more commonly cited SC 1.4.12 Text Spacing (Level AA, WCAG 2.1): line-height >= 1.5x font size, paragraph spacing >= 2x font size, letter-spacing >= 0.12em, word-spacing >= 0.16em with no loss of content. 1.4.8's "paragraph >= 1.5x line spacing" and 1.4.12's "paragraph >= 2x font size" are consistent (1.5 line x 1.5 = 2.25x font), but they are distinct criteria with distinct metrics. The claim correctly attributes its thresholds to 1.4.8 specifically.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source exists; all sources (W3C primary, thewcag.com, halfaccessible, HK Digital Policy Office) confirm the exact thresholds. The only correction is internal: 1.4.8 is AAA and is a "mechanism available" requirement, not a default-presentation mandate — a framing precision, not a refutation.

#### V91 `a2b2646e` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Fontaine reduces CLS by scanning your @font-face rules and generating fallback @font-face rules whose metrics (ascent-override / descent-override / line-gap-override) are crafted to match a local fallback font, so the fallback occupies the same space as the web font and swap causes no reflow."

**Source:** https://github.com/nuxt-modules/fontaine (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Reduces CLS by using local font fallbacks with crafted font metrics. ... scan[s] your `@font-face` rules and generate[s] fallback rules with the correct metrics."

*Evidence:* Claim verified against primary source plus two independent authorities. The nuxt-modules/fontaine README shows the exact mechanism: it scans @font-face rules and emits fallback @font-face rules with src: local('Segoe UI') and ascent-override/descent-override/line-gap-override (real example: ascent-override 92.77%, descent-override 24.41%, line-gap-override 0%), then appends the fallback to font-family. Chrome for Developers confirms "the web font and the adjusted fallback font will always have the same vertical dimensions"; MDN's ascent-override docs corroborate. Documented effect: playground CLS 0.34 -> 0.013. Source quality (primary repo + Google Chrome team + MDN) exceeds the claim's strength. The ONLY overstatement is "swap causes NO reflow": Fontaine's own docs concede residual shift (CLS 0.013, not 0; "there will still be a layout shift when the stylesheet loads"), and classic Fontaine overrides only vertical metrics — without size-adjust, minor horizontal glyph-width reflow can remain. Correct phrasing is "near-zero/minimal reflow." That is a refinement, not a refutation — mechanism, metric names, local-font matching, and CLS-reduction purpose are all confirmed accurate.
*Counter-source:* https://developer.chrome.com/blog/font-fallbacks

#### V92 `a458015f` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "There is no evidence base supporting specialized dyslexia fonts as an effective practice, and relying on them risks diverting resources from proven interventions."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5629233/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Currently, there is no documentation to support a specialized font is an evidence-based practice."

*Evidence:* Claim survives adversarial review. (1) Quote support: the source is Wery & Diliberto 2016, "The effect of a specialized dyslexia font, OpenDyslexic, on reading rate and accuracy," Annals of Dyslexia (peer-reviewed primary study). Its conclusion verbatim: "Currently, there is no documentation to support a specialized font is an evidence-based practice"; OpenDyslexic actually DECREASED fluency vs Arial/Times across all three tasks. The claim's first part is a faithful paraphrase, not an overreach. (2) The "risks diverting resources" clause is also in the source — the authors warn implementing such fonts without support "could waste resources and potentially damage students' academic self-esteem," so it is attributed caution, not invention. (3) Convergent controlled evidence: Kuster et al. 2018 (170 children, Dyslexie font, no benefit); Marinus et al. 2016 (any effect came from SPACING, "a dedicated letterform is not necessary"); Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013 (no performance gain, less preferred); British Dyslexia Association style guide recommends standard sans-serif + spacing, NOT specialized dyslexia fonts. (4) The only contrary hit (Franzen et al. AB004, Annals of Eye Science 2019) is a conference ABSTRACT — no sample size, no significance/effect sizes, and explicitly "reading speed was unaffected"; insufficient to dispute the consensus. (5) Not outdated (2016 source reinforced 2018/2022) and not marketing. Source quality matches claim strength. Maps cleanly to the task's expected [MYTH] tag.
*Counter-source:* https://aes.amegroups.org/article/view/5209/html (Franzen et al. AB004) — claims OpenDyslexic aids comprehension/visual processing, but it is a non-peer-reviewed conference abstract with no reported sample size or statistics and states reading speed was unaffected; too weak to refute the claim.

#### V93 `a4d1541a` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "WCAG SC 1.4.8 Visual Presentation is a Level AAA criterion (not AA), and its measure requirement caps line width at no more than 80 characters or glyphs (40 for CJK scripts)."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/visual-presentation.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Width is no more than 80 characters or glyphs (40 if CJK)."

*Evidence:* Primary W3C source (https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/visual-presentation.html) confirms BOTH halves of the claim verbatim. (1) Conformance level: the Understanding doc states SC 1.4.8 Visual Presentation is Level AAA — correct and stable across WCAG 2.0/2.1/2.2; it was never reclassified to AA or removed. It is correctly distinguished from SC 1.4.12 Text Spacing (the AA criterion), so no conflation. (2) Width requirement: the normative text reads exactly "Width is no more than 80 characters or glyphs (40 if CJK)" — matching the supporting quote word-for-word. The doc's rationale confirms the CJK halving: "CJK characters are approximately twice as wide... so the maximum line width for CJK characters is half that of non-CJK characters." Source quality (primary W3C normative Understanding doc) matches the claim's strength; claim is current, not marketing, not outdated. No contradicting source exists because the claim restates the standard's own text. Only nuance: WCAG allows exceeding 80 via a user-controllable mechanism, but "no more than 80" is the literal requirement, so "caps at 80" is faithful.

#### V96 `a530d279` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.12 Text Spacing (Level AA) requires that no loss of content or functionality occurs when the user sets line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size, with no other style property changed."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size ... No loss of content or functionality occurs by setting all of the following and by changing no other style property"

*Evidence:* The claim is faithfully supported by the primary normative source (W3C WAI Understanding doc) and the WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.12 normative text itself. Verified verbatim: "no loss of content or functionality occurs by setting all of the following and by changing no other style property: Line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size; ..." Conformance level is confirmed as Level AA. The specific number (1.5× font size for line height) is exact and matches the source. The "no other style property changed" clause in the claim maps directly to "by changing no other style property" in the normative text. Source quality is the highest possible (primary W3C standard) and currency is fine (WCAG 2.2 became a W3C Recommendation in Oct 2023, still current). Corroborated by independent secondary sources (Deque University, AAArdvark, BOIA, HK Digital Policy Office) which all cite line-height 1.5, paragraph 2×, letter-spacing 0.12×, word-spacing 0.16×.

ONE QUALIFICATION (does not refute, but should be carried into the skill): SC 1.4.12 requires that ALL FOUR spacing values be set SIMULTANEOUSLY ("by setting ALL of the following") — line-height 1.5×, paragraph spacing 2×, letter-spacing 0.12×, word-spacing 0.16× — not line-height alone. The claim isolates the line-height threshold; this is accurate as a description of one of the four required values and the verbatim no-other-property clause, but a reader must not infer that line-height 1.5× alone satisfies the criterion. Also note: the criterion does NOT require authors to USE these values; it requires content to survive WITHOUT loss when a user OVERRIDES spacing to these metrics (a tester applies them via CSS). The claim's phrasing ("when the user sets line height...") correctly reflects the user-override framing.

Tag: [STRONG] (normative WCAG standard). Numbers exact. No contrary evidence found.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found. All sources (W3C primary, Deque, AAArdvark, BOIA, HK Digital Policy Office) agree on the thresholds and Level AA. The only nuance is the "all four together" requirement, which qualifies rather than refutes the claim.

#### V97 `a5495fac` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[STRONG] Sans-serif, monospaced and roman styles produced significantly shorter FIXATION DURATIONS than serif/proportional/italic, but did NOT significantly change total reading TIME — so the speed-advantage conclusion is explicitly weak. Serif had no significant effect on Reading Time (W=11852, p=0.2021); only fixation differed (0.26s vs 0.24s, p=0.008). This supports 'no robust inherent serif/sans reading-speed advantage.'"

**Source:** https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2513383.2513447 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Although sans serif, monospaced and roman fonts lead to significant shorter fixation durations, we did not find a significant difference in reading time. Hence, our conclusions towards these characteristics are weaker."

*Evidence:* The claim's supporting quote is confirmed almost verbatim in the primary literature. I downloaded and text-extracted the TACCESS 2016 journal version (Rello & Baeza-Yates, "The Effect of Font Type on Screen Readability by People with Dyslexia," ACM Trans. Access. Comput., the extended version of the cited ASSETS 2013 paper, DOI 10.1145/2513383.2513447). It states verbatim: "Although for the tested fonts sans serif, monospaced, and roman fonts led to significantly shorter fixation durations for people with dyslexia, we did not find a significant difference in reading time. Hence, our conclusions on these characteristics are weaker." This directly supports all three core assertions: (1) sans/mono/roman gave significantly shorter fixation durations; (2) reading TIME showed no significant difference; (3) the speed-advantage conclusion is explicitly weak (authors' own word "weaker"). The fixation-duration data table I extracted is consistent with the claim's "0.26s vs 0.24s" magnitude: dyslexic-reader fixation means are low for sans/mono (Courier 0.22, Verdana 0.23, Arial 0.24, Helvetica 0.24) and higher for serif/italic (Times 0.25, OpenDys 0.26, Garamond 0.27, ArialIt 0.28). The interpretation "supports 'no robust inherent serif/sans reading-speed advantage'" is appropriately hedged ("supports," "no ROBUST advantage") and consistent with broader reading-science consensus; the dyslexia-population reading-time null is a supporting data point, not a contradiction. Source is primary, peer-reviewed ACM and is named in the research question itself as a key source — appropriate for the [STRONG] tag. Caveats not rising to refutation: the exact W=11852/p=0.2021 figures are from the ASSETS 2013 results tables, which I could not extract from the subsetted-font PDF pages (only abstract decoded cleanly), but direction and magnitude are independently confirmed and the numbers are clearly table-derived, not fabricated; and the study population is dyslexic readers, so the general "no inherent advantage" framing is a hedged extrapolation. Notably the claim does NOT overstate the paper — the paper's headline ("sans serif/roman significantly improved reading performance") could be misread as a serif/sans difference, but the claim correctly scopes to reading time and surfaces the authors' own disconfirming hedge.
*Counter-source:* No credible contradicting source found. The paper's own headline ("font type significantly improved reading performance," recommending sans-serif) is the only superficially countervailing element, but it is driven by the fixation-duration result, not reading time; the authors themselves qualify the reading-time/speed conclusion as "weaker," which is exactly what the claim reports. Broader reading-science consensus (and the prior typography findings) agree there is no robust inherent serif/sans reading-speed advantage.

#### V98 `a576fae2` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The fix is the @font-face metric-override technique: size-adjust, ascent-override, descent-override, and line-gap-override let a fallback font be tuned to match the web font's metrics, preventing the layout shift on swap."

**Source:** https://developer.chrome.com/blog/framework-tools-font-fallback (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Properties such as `size-adjust`, `ascent-override`, `descent-override`, and `line-gap-override`...can help override the metrics of a fallback font"

*Evidence:* The claim faithfully matches the source quote and is corroborated by multiple primary/authoritative sources. The cited Chrome for Developers blog states the four descriptors "in the @font-face rule can help override the metrics of a fallback font," and explicitly frames it against the swap-induced shift: "the difference in character sizes of the two fonts results in screen content shifting around." web.dev's "CSS size-adjust for @font-face" article and MDN document these as CSS Fonts Module L4/L5 @font-face descriptors. Independent corroboration: web.dev/Duda/Speed Kit/Vincent Bernat (2024) all describe the same technique — build a fallback @font-face from a local font (e.g. Arial) and tune size-adjust/ascent-override/descent-override/line-gap-override so the fallback metrically matches the web font, reducing/eliminating CLS on swap. Current, not outdated (properties standardized and broadly supported; 2024 sources still endorse). Not marketing. One minor qualification, not a refutation: the technique "reduces or eliminates" shift only to the degree the metrics are accurately matched (hence tooling like Fontaine/Fontsource auto-generate these values) — a perfect zero shift isn't guaranteed for arbitrary fonts, so "preventing" is slightly stronger than "minimizing," but the mechanism and intent stated in the claim are correct. Source quality (primary Chrome/web.dev/MDN) matches the claim's strength.

*Claim under review:* "The BDA recommends common sans-serif fonts (Arial, Comic Sans, Verdana, Tahoma, Century Gothic, Trebuchet, Calibri, Open Sans) for dyslexic readers — and nowhere in the guide does it recommend or even mention any specially-designed 'dyslexia font' (OpenDyslexic, Dyslexie). This is disconfirming evidence against the 'you need a special dyslexia typeface' belief: the leading dyslexia authority's own advice is plain sans-serif."

**Source:** https://cdn.bdadyslexia.org.uk/uploads/documents/Advice/style-guide/BDA-Style-Guide-2023.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Use sans serif fonts, such as Arial and Comic Sans, as letters can appear less crowded. Alternatives include Verdana, Tahoma, Century Gothic, Trebuchet, Calibri, Open Sans."

*Evidence:* Claim survives. (A) Font list confirmed: UBC Centre for Teaching & Learning's reproduction of the BDA 2023 guide names exactly the 8 fonts cited — "Arial, Calibri, Century Gothic, Comic Sans, Open Sans, Tahoma, Trebuchet, and Verdana." I also extracted the full text of the structurally-identical 2014 BDA guide (spldtrust mirror) directly: "Use a plain, evenly spaced sans serif font such as Arial and Comic Sans. Alternatives include Verdana, Tahoma, Century Gothic, Trebuchet." (B) The strong negative holds: my grep of the full 2014 guide text returned ZERO occurrences of OpenDyslexic, Dyslexie, "specially," "specialist," Sassoon, Sylexiad, or Barrington Stoke; the UBC reproduction explicitly states for 2023 "The document does not mention OpenDyslexic, Dyslexie, or any specially-designed dyslexia typefaces"; two WebSearch syntheses concur. The 2023 CDN PDF blocks all fetchers (403) and the bumc mirror used a non-uniform custom font encoding I could not fully decode, so I relied on three independent confirmations instead. (C) Interpreting this as disconfirming evidence against "you need a special dyslexia typeface" is fair and matches the controlled literature (Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013; Wery & Diliberto 2017). CAVEAT (does not refute): the BDA-as-organization DOES list OpenDyslexic and Dyslexie elsewhere — on its separate "New Technologies" page under "free fonts designed for dyslexia" — so the claim must stay scoped to "the Style Guide," which it is. Source quality matches strength: this is a [CONVENTION] claim about a named authority's guidance, primary-document-backed, current (2023, corroborated by 2014's identical stance), not a marketing or extraordinary scientific claim.

#### V100 `a5f3425c` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The 320 CSS pixel reflow threshold corresponds to a 1280 CSS pixel wide viewport zoomed to 400%, defining the test condition for text/content reflow."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/reflow.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "320 CSS pixels is equivalent to a starting viewport width of 1280 CSS pixels wide at 400% zoom."

*Evidence:* Verified against the primary source (W3C WCAG 2.1 Understanding SC 1.4.10 Reflow, https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/reflow.html). The supporting quote is verbatim accurate: "320 CSS pixels is equivalent to a starting viewport width of 1280 CSS pixels wide at 400% zoom." The doc reinforces it: "The 320 CSS pixels generally corresponds to a desktop browser window set to a width of 1280px and the browser viewport then zoomed to 400%." Math confirms: 1280 / 4 = 320. The normative SC text sets the reflow test at "width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels" (vertical scrolling content) and 256 CSS pixels height (horizontal). Source is the standards body itself (highest quality), current (WCAG 2.1 2018, unchanged in WCAG 2.2 2023), not marketing. Minor non-refuting nuance: the same doc notes real browsers show ~318px at 400% zoom on a 1280x1024 display due to browser chrome — an implementation caveat, not a contradiction of the CSS-pixel definition. Claim is [STRONG]/normative and correctly stated.

#### V101 `a60c06ff` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "SC 1.4.12 requires support for paragraph spacing of at least 2 times the font size and letter spacing (tracking) of at least 0.12 times the font size without loss of content or functionality."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Spacing following paragraphs to at least 2 times the font size ... Letter spacing (tracking) to at least 0.12 times the font size"

*Evidence:* VERIFIED against the primary normative source (W3C WCAG 2.2 Understanding, https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html). The SC 1.4.12 normative text states that "no loss of content or functionality occurs by setting all of the following": line height >=1.5x font size; "Spacing following paragraphs to at least 2 times the font size"; "Letter spacing (tracking) to at least 0.12 times the font size"; word spacing >=0.16x font size. The claim's two cited multipliers (paragraph 2x, letter 0.12x) and the "without loss of content or functionality" qualifier match verbatim. Checklist: (1) Quote supports claim exactly; citing only 2 of 4 thresholds is an accurate subset, not an overreach or misread. (2) No contradicting evidence — this IS the standard, not an interpretation of it. (3) Source quality is maximal: primary normative W3C standard, appropriate for a [STRONG]/normative tag. (4) Not outdated: SC 1.4.12 originated in WCAG 2.1 (2018), carried unchanged into WCAG 2.2 (W3C Recommendation Oct 2023). (5) Not marketing/speculation — official standards-body text. Only nuance: the claim paraphrases the conditional mechanism ("no loss occurs by setting") as "requires support for," which is a faithful plain-language rendering, not a distortion. Claim stands.

#### V102 `a60e33e3` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "OpenDyslexic actually performed worse than standard fonts (Arial, Times New Roman), with statistically significant deficits (IRD -49.65% to -88.65% on fluency; -53.89% to -75.81% on accuracy, CIs excluding zero), so the specialized font may hurt rather than help."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5629233/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Effect sizes showed OpenDyslexic performed worse, with IRD (Improvement Rate Difference) ranging from -49.65% to -88.65% on fluency measures and -53.89% to -75.81% on accuracy—all confidence intervals excluding zero, indicating statistically significant deficits."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED against the primary source (Wery & Diliberto, "The effect of a specialized dyslexia font, OpenDyslexic, on reading rate and accuracy," Annals of Dyslexia, 2016/2017; PMC5629233). All four exact IRD endpoint values in the claim appear verbatim in the results tables. Fluency (Table 2): letter naming Arial vs OD = -49.65% [CI -63.33, -32.98]; word reading Arial vs OD = -88.65% [CI -94.45, -77.57] — matches the cited -49.65% to -88.65% range. Accuracy (Table 3): word reading Arial vs OD = -53.89% [CI -67.52, -36.70]; nonsense word TNR vs OD = -75.81% [CI -85.09, -61.90] — matches the cited -53.89% to -75.81% range. All reported CIs exclude zero and are negative, so "statistically significant deficits" (i.e., OpenDyslexic reliably worse than Arial/TNR) is a correct reading of IRD-with-CI for a single-case alternating-treatment design (n=12 grade 3-6 dyslexic students). Design/methodology accurately described.

Corroboration, not contradiction: a WebSearch for disconfirming evidence turned up only studies AGREEING — Rello & Baeza-Yates (2013, eye-tracking, OpenDyslexic gave no reading-time or fixation benefit); Kuster et al. (2016/2018, "Dyslexie font does not benefit reading," readers preferred Arial). No credible source disputes the finding. This is the settled consensus, not outdated.

ONE legitimate qualification (does not rise to refutation): the authors note students called the font "new"/"different" but did NOT systematically control novelty/unfamiliarity as a confound, so part of the slower reading could be practice/familiarity rather than an inherent font property. The claim's hedge "MAY hurt rather than help" already accommodates this; the authors themselves use both "no improvement" and "decreased students' outcomes," and warn the font diverts time/money from evidence-based interventions. So the "may hurt" framing is defensible, not an overreach. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary study) matches the claim's strength. Tag-wise this correctly grounds a [MYTH] verdict on the dyslexia-font claim. Not refuted.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found. Searches surfaced only corroborating controlled studies (Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013; Kuster et al. 2016/2018). Sole caveat is internal to the cited paper: novelty/unfamiliarity not controlled as a confound, which softens but does not overturn the "may hurt" inference (claim is already hedged with "may").

#### V103 `a6145ead` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "WCAG SC 1.4.10 Reflow requires content to be presentable without loss of information/functionality and without two-dimensional scrolling at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels (for vertical-scrolling content) or a height equivalent to 256 CSS pixels (for horizontal-scrolling content)."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/reflow.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Content can be presented without loss of information or functionality, and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions for: Vertical scrolling content at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels; Horizontal scrolling content at a height equivalent to 256 CSS pixels."

*Evidence:* The W3C Understanding SC 1.4.10 Reflow page (fetched live) states verbatim: "Content can be presented without loss of information or functionality, and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions for: Vertical scrolling content at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels; Horizontal scrolling content at a height equivalent to 256 CSS pixels." The claim reproduces this exactly — both the 320px (vertical-scrolling/width) and 256px (horizontal-scrolling/height) thresholds and their directionality are correct. The W3C page also corroborates the rationale (320px = 1280px desktop at 400% zoom; 256px = 1024px at 400%). Source is the primary W3C WCAG 2.1 spec, the highest-quality authority for a normative standard; it is current (WCAG 2.1 normative, carried unchanged into 2.2). No contradicting source exists — these are fixed normative numbers. The claim does not overreach the quote and is not outdated or marketing.

#### V104 `a64d75d4` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[STRONG] Sans-serif, monospaced and roman styles produced significantly shorter FIXATION DURATIONS than serif/proportional/italic, but did NOT significantly change total reading TIME — so the speed-advantage conclusion is explicitly weak. Serif had no significant effect on Reading Time (W=11852, p=0.2021); only fixation differed (0.26s vs 0.24s, p=0.008). This supports 'no robust inherent serif/sans reading-speed advantage.'"

**Source:** https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2513383.2513447 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Although sans serif, monospaced and roman fonts lead to significant shorter fixation durations, we did not find a significant difference in reading time. Hence, our conclusions towards these characteristics are weaker."

*Evidence:* Every figure verified verbatim against the primary PDF (Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013, "Good Fonts for Dyslexia," ASSETS '13). The paper states: "We did not find a significant effect of Serif on Reading Time (W=11852; p=0.2021)" — exact match. And: "There was a significant effect of Serif on Fixation Duration (W=10547.5; p=0.008). Indeed, the fixation duration mean of the fonts with serif, mean=0.26 seconds... was significantly larger than the fixation duration mean of the fonts sans serif, mean=0.24 seconds" — exact match for "0.26s vs 0.24s, p=0.008". The supporting quote ("...we did not find a significant difference in reading time. Hence, our conclusions towards these characteristics are weaker.") is the authors' verbatim conclusion. The claim reproduces the authors' own self-hedge, so it is not an overreach. Source is a controlled eye-tracking study (97 subjects, 48 dyslexic, 12 fonts) — adequate for [STRONG]. Independent searches CORROBORATE rather than contradict: Boyarski 1998, Daxer 2022 (Times New Roman vs Helvetica), an NIH serif-size study, and a Cyrillic-reader study all report no significant serif/sans reading-speed difference. Minor non-refuting caveat: population is dyslexic+control and the abstract glosses these fonts as improving "reading performance," but the claim correctly isolates the significant fixation result from the non-significant reading-time result.
*Counter-source:* No credible contradiction found; independent controlled studies (Daxer 2022 OPO; Boyarski 1998; PMC4612630 serif-size RSVP study) reinforce the no-reading-speed-advantage conclusion.

#### V105 `a64e9970` — **REFUTED** · confidence: medium
*Claim under review:* "WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.12 Text Spacing (Level AA) requires that no loss of content or functionality occurs when the user sets line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size, with no other style property changed."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size ... No loss of content or functionality occurs by setting all of the following and by changing no other style property"

*Evidence:* Primary source (W3C WCAG 2.2 Understanding SC 1.4.12, verified via WebFetch of https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html) states the normative test as: "no loss of content or functionality occurs by setting ALL of the following and by changing no other style property:" — then requires FOUR properties set simultaneously: line height ≥1.5× font size; paragraph spacing ≥2× font size; letter spacing ≥0.12× font size; word spacing ≥0.16× font size (Level AA). The claim isolates only line height ("the user sets line height... to at least 1.5 times the font size, with no other style property changed"), which misreads the SC two ways: (1) the SC is a conjoined test of all four properties set together — you cannot conform by handling line height alone, yet the claim drops three of the four; (2) "by changing no other style property" means no property BEYOND those four, not "only line height" — the SC actually REQUIRES the three other spacing properties to also be changed, so the claim's "with no other style property changed" is wrong. The claim is contradicted by its own supporting quote, which contains "by setting all of the following." Correct elements: the 1.5× line-height threshold, Level AA, and "no loss of content or functionality" are accurate; only the conformance test (all-four-simultaneously) is misrepresented. This is an overreach/misread, not a faithful statement of the criterion.
*Counter-source:* https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html

#### V106 `a6bba0ff` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.12 Text Spacing (Level AA) requires that no loss of content or functionality occurs when the user sets line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size, with no other style property changed."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size ... No loss of content or functionality occurs by setting all of the following and by changing no other style property"

*Evidence:* Verified against the primary source (W3C WCAG 2.2 Understanding SC 1.4.12, fetched). Every load-bearing fact in the claim checks out: (1) Conformance level is AA — confirmed. (2) Normative threshold "Line height (line spacing) to at least 1.5 times the font size" — confirmed verbatim. (3) Normative phrase "no loss of content or functionality occurs by setting all of the following and by changing no other style property" — confirmed verbatim. (4) Source is the primary W3C standard — highest possible quality, current (WCAG 2.2 is the live recommendation), not marketing, not outdated. The claim's line-height number, level, and "no loss of content/functionality" language are all accurate and exactly sourced.

One framing nuance worth flagging (does NOT rise to refutation): the full SC requires ALL FOUR spacing properties be settable simultaneously — line-height ≥1.5×, paragraph spacing ≥2×, letter-spacing ≥0.12×, word-spacing ≥0.16× — and "changing no other style property" means changing nothing BEYOND those four. The claim isolates only line-height and phrases "no other style property changed" as if line-height alone is altered. That is an incomplete restatement of a conjunctive ("all of the following") requirement. However, it is not contradicted: setting fewer properties cannot cause more breakage than setting all four, so "no loss when line-height set to 1.5×" is a true (weaker) consequence implied by the full criterion. A web search likewise found no source disputing the 1.5× line-height threshold or the AA level; the only "misconception" sources flag is people forgetting the OTHER three properties — which supports the standard, not refutes the line-height fact. The claim states a true, well-sourced sub-requirement; the skill should additionally cite the other three thresholds and note all four must be set together to avoid the very incompleteness this claim exhibits.
*Counter-source:* https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html

#### V107 `a727f9ce` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "SC 1.4.12 requires support for word spacing of at least 0.16 times the font size without loss of content or functionality."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Word spacing to at least 0.16 times the font size"

*Evidence:* Primary normative source W3C WCAG 2.2 Understanding SC 1.4.12 states verbatim: "no loss of content or functionality occurs by setting all of the following... Word spacing to at least 0.16 times the font size." The claim reproduces both the 0.16x-font-size threshold and the "without loss of content or functionality" condition exactly. This is a Level AA criterion in WCAG 2.1/2.2 — current, stable, normative. WebSearch on w3.org confirmed the wording with no contradicting or qualifying source. Source quality (primary W3C standard) fully matches the claim's strength; the claim is neither an overreach nor a misread of the quote.

#### V108 `a780912a` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Under SC 1.4.8, line spacing (leading) must be at least space-and-a-half (150%) within paragraphs, and paragraph spacing must be at least 1.5 times larger than the line spacing."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/visual-presentation.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Line spacing (leading) is at least space-and-a-half within paragraphs, and paragraph spacing is at least 1.5 times larger than the line spacing"

*Evidence:* The supporting quote is a VERBATIM reproduction of the normative SC 1.4.8 Visual Presentation text, confirmed by fetching the primary W3C source (https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/visual-presentation.html). The bullet reads exactly: "Line spacing (leading) is at least space-and-a-half within paragraphs, and paragraph spacing is at least 1.5 times larger than the line spacing." The "150%" gloss is correct — W3C itself defines "space-and-a-half" as "the top of one line is 150% further from the top of the line below it than would be true when the text is 'single spaced.'" The attribution to SC 1.4.8 (not the AA-level SC 1.4.12 Text Spacing, which uses different numbers: line-height ≥1.5× font size, paragraph spacing ≥2× font size) is correct — the "paragraph spacing ≥1.5× line spacing" wording is uniquely 1.4.8's. Thresholds, attribution, and source quality all check out. Primary, authoritative, current (SC 1.4.8 persists unchanged in WCAG 2.1 and 2.2); not marketing.

TWO CAVEATS that qualify but do NOT refute the threshold claim: (1) SC 1.4.8 is Level AAA — the highest, aspirational tier rarely set as a compliance target — not A or AA. (2) The criterion does NOT require text to be PRESENTED this way by default; it requires that "a mechanism is available to achieve the following" — i.e. the user must be able to reach 1.5 spacing, e.g. via a stylesheet switcher. The claim's wording "line spacing MUST be at least space-and-a-half" slightly overstates this as a default-rendering mandate rather than a mechanism-availability requirement. A skill grounded on this should tag it [STRONG normative] but note "AAA, mechanism-availability not default." None of these caveats contradict the numeric threshold or its source, so the claim is not refuted.
*Counter-source:* https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/visual-presentation.html (same primary source; the only qualifications are the AAA level and the "mechanism available" framing, both stated on that page)

#### V109 `a7851b11` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[MYTH busted] The dyslexia-specific font OpenDyslexic did NOT improve readability or reading speed for dyslexic readers; mainstream fonts (Arial, Helvetica, Verdana) did better. Participants significantly preferred Verdana/Helvetica over OpenDyslexic."

**Source:** https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2513383.2513447 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The fonts designed specifically for dyslexia, OpenDys and OpenDys It., did not lead to a better or worse readability. As in [21], OpenDys did not lead to a faster reading."

*Evidence:* Primary source (Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013, ACM ASSETS, n=48, eye-tracking) directly supports the claim — more precisely than the supplied quote. Extracted from the full-text PDF: (1) Reading speed/readability — Table 1 shows OpenDys reading-time mean 29.17s = 103% of Arial's 28.35s (second-slowest), fixation not improved; paper states OpenDys "did not lead to a better or worse readability... did not lead to a faster reading." (2) Preference — EXACT stats: "Verdana is significantly preferred over... OpenDys (p = 0.002), OpenDys It. (p = 0.004)"; "Helvetica is significantly preferred over OpenDys It. (p = 0.010), OpenDys (p = 0.020)"; conclusion: "participants significantly preferred Verdana and Helvetica to other fonts." Preference means: Verdana 3.79, Helvetica 3.62 vs OpenDys 2.43-low. (3) Paper's recommended good fonts: "Helvetica, Courier, Arial, Verdana and Computer Modern Unicode" — OpenDys excluded. Source quality matches the [MYTH] strength (peer-reviewed, controlled, eye-tracking). Broader literature concurs (Kuster et al. 2018, Wery & Diliberto 2017, British Dyslexia Association) — no credible contradiction found. Caveat (not refuting): "did better" slightly overstates the speed dimension — OpenDys was statistically NEUTRAL (not significantly worse) on the main readability measure; only the preference difference reached significance, while reading-time was merely numerically slower. Skill-grounding note: cite this as [MYTH]/[STRONG] for the preference + no-speed-benefit findings; avoid implying mainstream fonts significantly beat OpenDys on reading speed.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found; corroborated by Kuster et al. 2018 ("Dyslexie font does not benefit reading"), Wery & Diliberto 2017, and the British Dyslexia Association style guide (recommends ordinary sans-serif, not special dyslexia fonts).

#### V110 `a7e21b3b` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The 320 CSS pixel reflow threshold corresponds to a 1280 CSS pixel wide viewport zoomed to 400%, defining the test condition for text/content reflow."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/reflow.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "320 CSS pixels is equivalent to a starting viewport width of 1280 CSS pixels wide at 400% zoom."

*Evidence:* Primary source confirmed verbatim. The W3C WCAG 2.1 Understanding doc for SC 1.4.10 Reflow (https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/reflow.html) states exactly: "320 CSS pixels is equivalent to a starting viewport width of 1280 CSS pixels wide at 400% zoom." The arithmetic is consistent (1280 / 4 = 320), and 320 CSS pixels is the normative width threshold used as the test condition for the Reflow criterion (for content that scrolls vertically, i.e. horizontal languages). The criterion is current — introduced in WCAG 2.1 (2018) and unchanged in WCAG 2.2. Source quality is the highest possible (W3C standards body, primary). Minor non-refuting nuance: there is a parallel 256 CSS pixel threshold for horizontal-scrolling content (vertical languages), but the claim correctly scopes itself to the 320px case and does not overreach.

#### V111 `a7e459d0` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "font-display:swap causes layout shift / poor CLS because the fallback and web font differ in character size, shifting content when the swap happens — this is the modern motivation for metric-override fallbacks (TOPIC A: CLS from font swap)."

**Source:** https://developer.chrome.com/blog/framework-tools-font-fallback (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The biggest issue with `swap` is the jarring effect, where the difference in character sizes of the two fonts results in screen content shifting around... This leads to poor CLS scores, especially for text-heavy websites."

*Evidence:* The claim is directly supported by the cited quote and corroborated by multiple independent authorities. The Chrome for Developers blog (Google/Chrome team, primary source for web-font rendering behavior) states the "swap" jarring effect comes from "the difference in character sizes of the two fonts" causing "screen content shifting around... poor CLS scores, especially for text-heavy websites" — matching the claim verbatim in mechanism (size difference -> reflow -> CLS) and motivation (metric-override fallbacks). Independent corroboration: DebugBear ("layout shifts if the fallback and web fonts are not metrically compatible"), Google Material Design 3 blog ("Reduce reflow with web fonts"), Sentry Blog ("Web Fonts and the Dreaded Cumulative Layout Shift"), Vincent Bernat (2024), and Simon Hearne — all attribute the shift to metric mismatch and prescribe size-adjust/ascent-override/descent-override/line-gap-override as the fix, with Next.js next/font and Fontaine cited as the tooling. Source quality matches claim strength (this is a mechanical/engineering fact, not an extraordinary claim needing an RCT). Currency is good: the @font-face metric-override technique is 2022-2024+ current best practice, not outdated. Not marketing. One precision check, not a refutation: swap does not *always* cause CLS — only when metrics differ — but the claim correctly conditions the shift on the size difference ("because the fallback and web font differ in character size"), so it is not an overreach. If the skill states it, the only added nuance worth noting is that metrically-matched fonts swap without shift, which is exactly why the override fix works. Properly tagged [CONVENTION]/established-mechanism from named authorities (Chrome team, web.dev family), not [STRONG] controlled-study and not [MYTH].
*Counter-source:* No credible source disputes the mechanism. The only qualification (swap does not universally trigger CLS — only on metric mismatch; e.g. DebugBear, Material 3) refines rather than refutes the claim, which already attributes the shift to the size difference.

Abstract: "Using a within-subject design, 48 subjects with dyslexia read 12 texts with 12 different fonts." The claim only asserts design + strength tag and makes no causal overreach (notably the study's actual finding is that OpenDyslexic did NOT help — it supports the dyslexia-font [MYTH] verdict). #### V114 `a8967c9f` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The cache partition key is a 'Network Isolation Key' composed of the top-level site and the current-frame site, combined with the resource URL; sites are identified by scheme://eTLD+1 (ignoring subdomains and ports)."

**Source:** https://developers.google.com/web/updates/2020/10/http-cache-partitioning (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "With cache partitioning, cached resources will be keyed using a new "Network Isolation Key" in addition to the resource URL. The Network Isolation Key is composed of the top-level site and the current-frame site."

*Evidence:* The claim is fully supported by the primary source (the cited developers.google.com URL 301-redirects to its canonical home, developer.chrome.com/blog/http-cache-partitioning — same Chrome-team article). Every component is verbatim-confirmed:

1. "keyed using a new 'Network Isolation Key' in addition to the resource URL" → confirms "NIK + resource URL."
2. "The Network Isolation Key is composed of the top-level site and the current-frame site." → confirms the two-site composition (matches the supplied supporting quote).
3. The eTLD+1 detail — which was NOT in the supplied supporting quote — is ALSO explicitly stated, so it is not an overreach: "The 'site' is recognized using 'scheme://eTLD+1' so if requests are from different pages, but they have the same scheme and effective top-level domain+1 they will use the same cache partition."
4. "ignoring subdomains and ports" is verbatim confirmed: "Because the key is created based on 'scheme://eTLD+1', subdomains and port numbers are ignored."

Adversarial checks all pass: (a) source is the primary/authoritative Chrome team doc — appropriate strength; (b) no contradicting evidence found — corroborated by chromestatus.com feature 5730772021411840, the GoogleChrome/http-cache-partitioning explainer, and PortSwigger; (c) not outdated — partitioning shipped in Chrome 86 (Oct 2020) and is now entrenched (Safari 2013, Firefox 2019, Chrome 2020), making the underlying "shared CDN cache no longer helps" point stronger, not weaker, over time; (d) not marketing — neutral technical documentation.

Minor honest caveat (does not refute): the article notes the NIK computation "may change in the future" (Chrome has explored further/triple-keying), so the exact key composition is the documented current behavior, not an immutable spec — but the claim as worded accurately describes the documented mechanism.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found. Corroborating sources: developer.chrome.com/blog/http-cache-partitioning (primary), chromestatus.com/feature/5730772021411840, github.com/shivanigithub/http-cache-partitioning, portswigger.net/daily-swig/google-chrome-partitions-browser-http-cache-to-defend-against-xs-leak-attacks.

#### V115 `a8bf095e` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Chrome partitions the HTTP cache so a resource cached while visiting one site is NOT served from cache on a different site, even when the resource URL is identical — defeating the historical shared-CDN caching benefit (e.g. a font fetched from a shared CDN on site A is re-downloaded on site B)."

**Source:** https://developers.google.com/web/updates/2020/10/http-cache-partitioning (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The same user visits a different page (https://b.example) which requests the same image (https://x.example/doge.png). Though the same image was loaded in the previous example, since the key doesn't match it will not be a cache hit."

*Evidence:* Claim fully supported by the primary source and corroborated independently. Primary source developer.chrome.com/blog/http-cache-partitioning (the old developers.google.com/web/updates/2020/10/ URL redirects here) states verbatim: "The same user visits a different page (https://b.example) which requests the same image (https://x.example/doge.png)... since the key doesn't match it will not be a cache hit." Cache key = Network Isolation Key (top-level site + current-frame site) + resource URL, so an identical URL on a different top-level site misses. The article explicitly notes services "that serve large volumes of highly cacheable resources across many sites (such as fonts and popular scripts) may see an increase in their traffic" — i.e. the shared-CDN benefit is defeated, exactly the claim. Independent corroboration: Addy Osmani (double-keyed caching), Simon Willison (Jan 2025), Stefan Judis, Jeff Kaufman ("Shared Cache is Going Away"), peakhour.io ("The CDN is Dead"). Cross-browser and current: Safari partitions since 2013, Chrome since v86 (Oct 2020), Firefox since Jan 2021. Measured cost: ~3.6% cache-miss increase, ~4% more network bytes, ~0.3% FCP. Checklist: (1) quote directly supports, no overreach; (2) no contradicting source found; (3) primary Chrome-team source, quality exceeds claim strength; (4) not outdated — more entrenched in 2026; (5) not marketing. One precision caveat (not a refutation): the behavior is cross-browser, not Chrome-only, so a skill should not frame it as Chrome-specific. Tag [STRONG].

#### V116 `a95383d3` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "A controlled alternating-treatment study found the OpenDyslexic font produced no improvement in reading rate or accuracy for children with dyslexia — directly contradicting the popular 'dyslexia-friendly font' marketing claim ([MYTH] per controlled evidence)."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5629233/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Results from this alternating treatment experiment show no improvement in reading rate or accuracy for individual students with dyslexia, as well as the group as a whole."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED, not refuted. (1) Quote is verbatim-accurate in PMC5629233 = Wery & Diliberto, "The effect of a specialized dyslexia font, OpenDyslexic, on reading rate and accuracy," Annals of Dyslexia (2016 online / July 2017 print; PubMed 26993270; DOI 10.1007/s11881-016-0127-1). (2) Study design matches the claim exactly: single-subject ALTERNATING TREATMENT design, 12 elementary students (grades 3-6) with confirmed dyslexia, OpenDyslexic vs Arial vs Times New Roman across letter-naming/word/nonsense-word tasks. (3) Claim is conservative — the study found NEGATIVE effect sizes (fluency -88.65% to -49.65%; accuracy -73.53% to -63.62%) and no participant preferred the font; "no improvement" understates it. (4) [MYTH] framing is corroborated by convergent controlled evidence: Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013 (eye-tracking; no reading-time benefit, least-preferred font) and Kuster et al. 2017 "Dyslexie font does not benefit reading in children with or without dyslexia" (same journal). (5) CONTRARY EVIDENCE found but does not refute the scoped claim: Franzen et al., Annals of Eye Science AB004, reports OpenDyslexic improves reading comprehension/visual processing — but in ADULTS (not children) and on comprehension (not rate/accuracy), and it's only a conference abstract. Population and outcome mismatch means it does not contradict this specific claim; synthesis should flag adults/comprehension as an open edge. Not outdated, not marketing, primary peer-reviewed source matches the [MYTH] strength.
*Counter-source:* Franzen et al., "The dyslexia font OpenDyslexic facilitates visual processing of text and improves reading comprehension in adult dyslexia," Annals of Eye Science AB004 (https://aes.amegroups.org/article/view/5209/html) — a positive result, but in adults and on comprehension/visual processing, not children's reading rate/accuracy; conference abstract only, so it does not refute the scoped claim.

#### V117 `a97daa0d` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The 320 CSS pixel reflow threshold corresponds to a 1280 CSS pixel wide viewport zoomed to 400%, defining the test condition for text/content reflow."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/reflow.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "320 CSS pixels is equivalent to a starting viewport width of 1280 CSS pixels wide at 400% zoom."

*Evidence:* Verified directly against the primary source (W3C WCAG 2.1 Understanding SC 1.4.10 Reflow, https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/reflow.html). The page states verbatim: "320 CSS pixels is equivalent to a starting viewport width of 1280 CSS pixels wide at 400% zoom." Math checks: 1280 / 4 = 320. The SC's normative requirement is content presentable "without loss of information or functionality, and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions for: Vertical scrolling content at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels." The companion equivalence is also confirmed: "256 CSS pixels is equivalent to a starting viewport height of 1024 CSS pixels at 400% zoom" (for horizontal-scrolling/vertical-text content). The claim's added phrase "defining the test condition for text/content reflow" is accurate — 1280px width at 400% zoom is the standard reflow test setup. Source is primary/authoritative (the standards body itself), current (WCAG 2.1, retained unchanged in 2.2), not marketing. No contradicting evidence found.

#### V118 `a9d3b82f` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "SC 1.4.12 requires support for word spacing of at least 0.16 times the font size without loss of content or functionality."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Word spacing to at least 0.16 times the font size"

*Evidence:* The claim faithfully restates the normative text of WCAG SC 1.4.12 Text Spacing. The W3C primary source (https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html) states the criterion requires that "no loss of content or functionality occurs by setting all of the following": line height to at least 1.5x font size, paragraph spacing to at least 2x, letter spacing (tracking) to at least 0.12x, and "Word spacing to at least 0.16 times the font size." The claim's "0.16 times the font size" and "without loss of content or functionality" framing both match the normative wording exactly. No nuance is lost: the SC is a user-override-survival requirement (page must not break when a user sets word-spacing to 0.16em), which "requires support for" accurately captures. Checklist: (1) Quote support — exact match. (2) Contradicting evidence — none; all 10 independent sources (Deque University, AccessibilityOz, AAArdvark, BOIA, DigitalA11Y) confirm 0.16x. (3) Source quality — W3C WAI Understanding doc is the highest-tier primary normative source. (4) Currency — WCAG 2.2 is the current standard; this threshold is unchanged from WCAG 2.1 (2018). (5) Not marketing. Tag would be [STRONG] (normative standard). No contradiction with color/charting/prior typography findings.
*Counter-source:* No credible contrary source found. All consulted sources (W3C, Deque, AccessibilityOz, AAArdvark, BOIA) corroborate the 0.16x word-spacing threshold; none dispute or qualify it.

#### V119 `a9d460b6` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "WCAG SC 1.4.8 Visual Presentation is a Level AAA criterion (not AA), and its measure requirement caps line width at no more than 80 characters or glyphs (40 for CJK scripts)."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/visual-presentation.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Width is no more than 80 characters or glyphs (40 if CJK)."

*Evidence:* Both parts of the claim verified against the primary normative source (W3C WCAG 2.1 Understanding doc) and 4 corroborating accessibility authorities. (1) Conformance level: W3C explicitly labels SC 1.4.8 Visual Presentation as "Level AAA"; corroborated independently by Appt.org, AAArdvark, TestParty, and Includia — none dispute it. (2) Width/measure requirement: the source states verbatim "Width is no more than 80 characters or glyphs (40 if CJK)," one of five normative sub-requirements (others: user-selectable fg/bg color, no justification, >=1.5 line spacing, 200% resize without horizontal scroll). The CJK=40 halving is explained because CJK glyphs are ~2x as wide at equal readability. The claim's term "measure" correctly denotes typographic line length. WCAG 2.1 is current and SC 1.4.8 carries unchanged into WCAG 2.2. Supporting quote matches the source exactly; no contradicting evidence found; source quality (primary normative standard) exceeds the claim's strength.

*Claim under review:* "SC 1.4.12 requires support for word spacing of at least 0.16 times the font size without loss of content or functionality."

**Supporting quote:** "Word spacing to at least 0.16 times the font size"

The claim reproduces both the exact threshold (0.16x font size) and the qualifying condition (no loss of content or functionality) accurately. This text is identical in WCAG 2.1 and 2.2 (current Recommendation), so it is neither outdated nor disputed.
