Minor framing nuance: the SC obliges authors to avoid breakage WHEN a user overrides spacing to >=0.16em, rather than to actively provide a word-spacing control; the claim's paraphrase is a standard rendering and preserves the exact number and condition, so not an overreach.

#### V121 `aa2a30dc` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The fix is the @font-face metric-override technique: size-adjust, ascent-override, descent-override, and line-gap-override let a fallback font be tuned to match the web font's metrics, preventing the layout shift on swap."

**Source:** https://developer.chrome.com/blog/framework-tools-font-fallback (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Properties such as `size-adjust`, `ascent-override`, `descent-override`, and `line-gap-override`...can help override the metrics of a fallback font"

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported, current, and primary-sourced. The four descriptors (size-adjust, ascent-override, descent-override, line-gap-override) are real CSS Fonts Module Level 5 @font-face descriptors. The primary source (developer.chrome.com/blog/framework-tools-font-fallback) directly supports the quote AND goes further: "You can prevent CLS by adjusting the dimensions of the fallback font to match that of the primary font" and "The biggest issue with swap is the jarring effect, where the difference in character sizes of the two fonts results in screen content shifting around. This leads to poor CLS scores." So the claim's "preventing the layout shift on swap" mirrors the source's own "prevent CLS" language — not an overreach. Independently corroborated by MDN (@font-face/ascent-override), web.dev (css-size-adjust), the W3C CSS Fonts Module Level 5 spec, and the Fontaine/Next.js next/font tooling that automates it. No credible source disputes the mechanism. Source quality (Chrome for Developers + W3C spec) matches the claim's [STRONG]/standards strength; not marketing, not outdated (active standard, 2024-2026). One refinement (not a refutation): vertical metrics (ascent/descent/line-gap) can be matched exactly, but size-adjust is the only horizontal lever and proportional fonts cannot be perfectly width-matched, so small residual shift can remain — Vincent Bernat (vincent.bernat.ch/en/blog/2024-cls-webfonts): "with a proportional font, it is not possible to achieve a perfect match"; the Chrome metric-override explainer notes "there may still be small layout shifts." CLS=0 is nonetheless achievable in practice (Bernat reached 0). A faithful skill should phrase it "tune fallback metrics to eliminate or sharply reduce swap CLS" and flag the horizontal-width caveat plus per-platform-fallback tuning, but the core claim stands.
*Counter-source:* https://vincent.bernat.ch/en/blog/2024-cls-webfonts (qualifies, does not refute: proportional fonts can't be perfectly width-matched via size-adjust, so small residual shift can remain; yet author still achieved CLS=0 with careful per-fallback tuning)

#### V122 `aa4e8f05` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "A controlled alternating-treatment study found the OpenDyslexic font produced no improvement in reading rate or accuracy for children with dyslexia — directly contradicting the popular 'dyslexia-friendly font' marketing claim ([MYTH] per controlled evidence)."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5629233/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Results from this alternating treatment experiment show no improvement in reading rate or accuracy for individual students with dyslexia, as well as the group as a whole."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported by the primary peer-reviewed source. PMC5629233 = Wery & Diliberto, "The effect of a specialized dyslexia font, OpenDyslexic, on reading rate and accuracy," Annals of Dyslexia (online 2016, vol. 67/2017). It is a controlled single-subject ALTERNATING TREATMENT design, n=12 elementary students (grades 3-6) with confirmed dyslexia, OpenDyslexic vs Arial vs Times New Roman across letter-naming, real-word, and nonsense-word tasks. The supporting quote matches verbatim. Findings were not merely null but NEGATIVE: fluency effect sizes -88.65% to -49.65%, accuracy -73.53% to -63.62%; zero participants preferred OpenDyslexic. So "no improvement in reading rate or accuracy" is accurate and conservative. Searched for contrary evidence: the one positive controlled study (Franzen et al., adults, Annals of Eye Science) found improved COMPREHENSION but EXPLICITLY states "reading speed was unaffected by OpenDyslexic" — consistent with, not contradicting, the rate/accuracy claim. Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013 found speed comparable to Arial (no improvement) and least preferred. No controlled study shows OpenDyslexic improving reading rate or accuracy. Source quality (primary, Springer peer-reviewed) matches the [MYTH] strength; not outdated (still BDA consensus, corroborated by Kuster et al. 2017 null result on Dyslexie font). The claim is correctly scoped to rate/accuracy and does not overstate. Could not refute.
*Counter-source:* Franzen et al. (Annals of Eye Science, adults) found OpenDyslexic improved reading COMPREHENSION in dyslexic adults — but explicitly found reading SPEED unaffected, so it does not contradict the rate/accuracy claim; it only qualifies the broader "fonts never help" generalization on the comprehension dimension.

#### V123 `aa520548` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The cache partition key is a 'Network Isolation Key' composed of the top-level site and the current-frame site, combined with the resource URL; sites are identified by scheme://eTLD+1 (ignoring subdomains and ports)."

**Source:** https://developers.google.com/web/updates/2020/10/http-cache-partitioning (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "With cache partitioning, cached resources will be keyed using a new "Network Isolation Key" in addition to the resource URL. The Network Isolation Key is composed of the top-level site and the current-frame site."

*Evidence:* Claim fully verified against the primary source (Chrome for Developers blog "Gaining security and privacy by partitioning the cache," the migrated home of the cited developers.google.com/web/updates/2020/10 article). The supplied supporting quote only covered the first half, but I confirmed every element is stated verbatim in the same primary article:

1. "The Network Isolation Key is composed of the top-level site and the current-frame site" — exact quote. Plus the worked example shows the full key tuple {top-level site, current-frame site, resource URL}, confirming "combined with the resource URL."
2. "The 'site' is recognized using 'scheme://eTLD+1'" — exact quote, confirming the scheme://eTLD+1 identifier.
3. "Because the key is created based on 'scheme://eTLD+1', subdomains and port numbers are ignored" — exact quote, demonstrated by their example where https://subdomain.a.example with an iframe at https://c.example:8080 still produces a cache hit. This confirms "ignoring subdomains and ports."

Checklist: (1) Quote support — claim is fully supported; the extrapolated "scheme://eTLD+1 ignoring subdomains and ports" detail beyond the supplied quote is itself directly attested in the same source. (2) Contradicting evidence — none. Independent sources (PortSwigger Daily Swig, chromestatus feature 5730772021411840, shivanigithub/http-cache-partitioning explainer) corroborate the triple-key model shipped in Chrome 86 (2020). (3) Source quality — official Google/Chrome engineering documentation (primary), sufficient for the claim's strength. (4) Currency — later refinements exist (an "is-cross-site bit" Intent-to-Experiment to simplify the frame-site component; Safari/WebKit uses double-keying on top-frame site only), but none contradict the documented Chrome model the claim describes, and the practical conclusion (cross-site shared-CDN cache reuse no longer works) is unchanged. (5) Not marketing — it is technical documentation. The detail that the claim describes Chrome specifically (other browsers' exact key composition varies) is the only minor caveat, but the claim does not over-generalize since it tracks the cited Chrome source.
*Counter-source:* No credible contradicting source found. Later Chrome refinements (chromium blink-dev "Intent to Experiment: is-cross-site bit in the HTTP Cache Partitioning Key") and WebKit's double-keyed (top-frame-site only) model differ in mechanism but do not refute the 2020 Chrome triple-key description the claim makes.

#### V124 `aa8e0eb4` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "A controlled alternating-treatment study found the OpenDyslexic font produced no improvement in reading rate or accuracy for children with dyslexia — directly contradicting the popular 'dyslexia-friendly font' marketing claim ([MYTH] per controlled evidence)."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5629233/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Results from this alternating treatment experiment show no improvement in reading rate or accuracy for individual students with dyslexia, as well as the group as a whole."

*Evidence:* Primary source PMC5629233 = Wery & Diliberto, "The effect of a specialized dyslexia font, OpenDyslexic, on reading rate and accuracy," Annals of Dyslexia (peer-reviewed, 2016). WebFetch confirms: single-subject ALTERNATING-TREATMENT design, 12 elementary students (grades 3-6, ages 9-12) with confirmed dyslexia/phonological-processing LD, normal vision, no comorbidities. The supporting quote is verbatim: "Results from this alternating treatment experiment show no improvement in reading rate or accuracy for individual students with dyslexia, as well as the group as a whole." Study actually found slightly NEGATIVE results vs Arial/Times New Roman across letter-naming, word-reading, nonsense-word tasks, and "none of the students indicated preferring" OpenDyslexic — so the claim ("no improvement") is if anything conservative, not an overreach. Source quality matches claim strength (primary peer-reviewed controlled study). Disconfirming search returned only CORROBORATING evidence: Kuster et al. Dyslexie-font study (Springer, Annals of Dyslexia) found no reading benefit in children with/without dyslexia; a 2020 systematic review concluded "no evidence that these fonts lead to improved reading performance. Font preference is not the same as reading performance." No credible source disputes it. Consistent with prior typography findings (preference ≠ reading speed). Minor: article dated 2016 not 2017 (journal-issue ambiguity, same study); small single-subject N=12, but alternating-treatment is a recognized controlled design and the [MYTH] tag is reinforced by the wider literature, not this study alone.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found. Disconfirming search yielded only corroborating sources: Kuster et al. (Dyslexie font, Annals of Dyslexia, Springer link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11881-017-0154-6) and a 2020 systematic review, both finding no reading-performance benefit from specialty dyslexia fonts.

#### V125 `aabbbe6e` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "OpenDyslexic actually performed worse than standard fonts (Arial, Times New Roman), with statistically significant deficits (IRD -49.65% to -88.65% on fluency; -53.89% to -75.81% on accuracy, CIs excluding zero), so the specialized font may hurt rather than help."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5629233/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Effect sizes showed OpenDyslexic performed worse, with IRD (Improvement Rate Difference) ranging from -49.65% to -88.65% on fluency measures and -53.89% to -75.81% on accuracy—all confidence intervals excluding zero, indicating statistically significant deficits."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported by the primary source PMC5629233 (Wery & Diliberto 2017). Verified IRD values match exactly: fluency Arial-vs-OD letter naming −49.65% [−63.33, −32.98] and word reading −88.65% [−94.45, −77.57]; accuracy TNR-vs-OD nonsense words −75.81% [−85.09, −61.90]. All CIs exclude zero, confirming statistically significant deficits in the stated direction (OD worse, not better). The paper itself states there is 'no evidence' of OpenDyslexic having a positive effect on reading speed or accuracy, and no participant preferred it. Direction and significance corroborated by multiple independent authorities (Edutopia, Australian Dyslexia Association, Nessy, Pimp my Type) and by a separate Dyslexie-font study (Kuster et al. 2017, Annals of Dyslexia), all finding dyslexia-specific fonts confer no benefit over Arial/Times New Roman. Minor caveat: the −53.89% lower accuracy bound was not directly visible in my fetch (partial table extraction), but every other figure matched exactly, so it is almost certainly a real value from the same table, not fabricated. The claim's hedge 'may hurt rather than help' is appropriately cautious for a single-case alternating-treatment design (small N), and is not an overreach. Source quality (primary peer-reviewed) matches claim strength; not outdated; not marketing.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found. Searched for studies showing OpenDyslexic improves reading; controlled reading-rate/accuracy studies consistently find no benefit. Creator/vendor preference claims exist but are self-report, not controlled performance data, and do not dispute the cited deficits.

#### V126 `aadcb231` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "SC 1.4.12 requires support for paragraph spacing of at least 2 times the font size and letter spacing (tracking) of at least 0.12 times the font size without loss of content or functionality."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Spacing following paragraphs to at least 2 times the font size ... Letter spacing (tracking) to at least 0.12 times the font size"

*Evidence:* W3C primary source (https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/text-spacing.html) confirms SC 1.4.12 normative text specifies exactly: "Spacing following paragraphs to at least 2 times the font size" and "Letter spacing (tracking) to at least 0.12 times the font size," under the condition "no loss of content or functionality occurs by setting all of the following." This matches the claim verbatim. Corroborated by multiple independent secondary sources (Deque University, BOIA, AAArdvark, TheWCAG). Source is the highest-quality possible (W3C normative standard), current (WCAG 2.2, values unchanged since 2.1), not marketing. The claim's phrasing "requires support for" is the correct legal interpretation: content must not break when a user applies these values, not that authors must set them by default — which the W3C source explicitly states ("Content is not required to use these text spacing values"). No contrary or qualifying evidence found.

#### V127 `ab009d28` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Under SC 1.4.8, line spacing (leading) must be at least space-and-a-half (150%) within paragraphs, and paragraph spacing must be at least 1.5 times larger than the line spacing."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/visual-presentation.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Line spacing (leading) is at least space-and-a-half within paragraphs, and paragraph spacing is at least 1.5 times larger than the line spacing"

*Evidence:* Verified against the primary source via WebFetch of https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/visual-presentation.html. The bullet is verbatim: "Line spacing (leading) is at least space-and-a-half within paragraphs, and paragraph spacing is at least 1.5 times larger than the line spacing." The claim's thresholds, "space-and-a-half (150%)" gloss, and attribution to SC 1.4.8 are all accurate and directly supported. Source is the highest-quality authority (W3C WAI), current (carried into WCAG 2.1/2.2), not marketing. Two caveats the claim should carry but that do NOT falsify it: (1) SC 1.4.8 is Level AAA, the highest/rarely-mandated tier, not A or AA; (2) the normative framing is "For the visual presentation of blocks of text, a mechanism is available to achieve the following" — i.e., a mechanism must be available for users to reach these values, not an absolute default-presentation mandate, so the claim's "must be" mildly overstates. Numbers and source attribution are correct; tag [STRONG]/normative with the AAA + "mechanism available" qualifiers.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found; only refinements — the official W3C text marks this AAA and frames it as "a mechanism is available to achieve," not an absolute requirement.

#### V128 `ab23cd55` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "There is no evidence base supporting specialized dyslexia fonts as an effective practice, and relying on them risks diverting resources from proven interventions."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5629233/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Currently, there is no documentation to support a specialized font is an evidence-based practice."

*Evidence:* Both clauses verified against the primary source. Wery & Diliberto 2017 (PMC5629233, Annals of Dyslexia, n=12 dyslexic students, OpenDyslexic vs Arial vs Times New Roman) states verbatim: "Currently, there is no documentation to support a specialized font is an evidence-based practice," and found "no improvement in reading rate or accuracy" (OpenDyslexic effect sizes −49.65% to −88.65%, i.e. worse). The "diverting resources" clause is NOT an overreach — it is directly in the paper: "That time and resource could be use on other interventions that are more likely to improve students' reading ability." Contrary evidence searched and found none; the literature converges: Kuster et al. 2018 (Annals of Dyslexia, n=170 dyslexic children — Dyslexie font no faster/more accurate than Arial, majority preferred Arial), Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013 (best fonts were standard sans), and the International Dyslexia Association and British Dyslexia Association both decline to endorse specialized fonts as superior. Source is a primary peer-reviewed study reinforced by multiple confirmations; quality matches the claim strength. "No evidence base" is correctly scoped to evidence-based practice (term of art) — subjective preference exists but preference is not effectiveness. This is the [MYTH] finding the research framing expected.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found. Searched specifically for controlled studies showing benefit; results converge against the fonts: Kuster et al. 2018 (link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11881-017-0154-6), Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013, International Dyslexia Association (dyslexiaida.org/do-special-fonts-help-people-with-dyslexia/). The only nuance — increased letter/line spacing does help dyslexic readers — reinforces rather than contradicts the claim, since the benefit comes from spacing, not the specialized glyph shapes.

#### V129 `ab608cf0` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "WCAG SC 1.4.4 Resize Text is a Level AA success criterion requiring that text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200% without loss of content or functionality."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/resize-text.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200 percent without loss of content or functionality."

*Evidence:* Verified against the W3C primary source (https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/resize-text.html), which confirms SC 1.4.4 Resize Text is Conformance Level AA with normative text: "Except for captions and images of text, text can be resized without assistive technology up to 200 percent without loss of content or functionality." The claim accurately reproduces the core requirement (200%, without assistive technology, no loss of content/functionality) and the AA level. Corroborated by multiple independent sources (DigitalA11Y, AAArdvark, TheWCAG, dock.codes). Criterion is unchanged across WCAG 2.0/2.1/2.2 (current, not outdated). Only deviation: the claim omits the "Except for captions and images of text" exception, but this does not make the claim false — it states the core requirement correctly and does not assert the criterion is exception-free. A skill should add the exception for completeness, but it is not a refutation. Source is the authoritative standards body; quality matches claim strength. Correctly tagged [STRONG]/normative.

#### V130 `ac1559b3` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[MYTH busted] The dyslexia-specific font OpenDyslexic did NOT improve readability or reading speed for dyslexic readers; mainstream fonts (Arial, Helvetica, Verdana) did better. Participants significantly preferred Verdana/Helvetica over OpenDyslexic."

**Source:** https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2513383.2513447 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The fonts designed specifically for dyslexia, OpenDys and OpenDys It., did not lead to a better or worse readability. As in [21], OpenDys did not lead to a faster reading."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and survives adversarial scrutiny. PRIMARY (Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013, ACM SIGACCESS, 48 dyslexic subjects, eye-tracking): the supplied quote is accurate — OpenDyslexic "did not lead to a better or worse readability... did not lead to a faster reading"; it was the least-preferred font and participants preferred Arial/Helvetica; Helvetica/Courier/Arial/Verdana gave significantly faster reading. INDEPENDENT CORROBORATION (Wery & Diliberto 2017, Annals of Dyslexia 67(2):114-127): alternating-treatment design vs Arial and Times New Roman found "no significant differences in either reading accuracy or reading speed," and no participant preferred OpenDyslexic. CONTRARY EVIDENCE WEIGHED: Franzen et al. (Annals of Eye Science) found OpenDyslexic improved reading COMPREHENSION — but that study itself reports reading SPEED was unaffected, so it does not touch this claim's scope (readability/reading speed). No credible controlled study shows OpenDyslexic improving reading speed; maker-cited studies (opendyslexic.org) are not independent. Minor, non-fatal precision quibbles: "did better" understates that OpenDyslexic's raw reading time was 'comparable' to Arial (it lost mainly on fixation measures and ranking), and "significantly preferred Verdana/Helvetica over OpenDyslexic" asserts a pairwise significance the snippet does not spell out (though direction — mainstream preferred, OpenDyslexic least-preferred — is firmly established). Source quality (two peer-reviewed controlled studies) matches the [MYTH] strength; not outdated; not marketing.
*Counter-source:* Franzen et al. (Annals of Eye Science, AB004) found OpenDyslexic improved reading COMPREHENSION in adult dyslexics — but same study states reading speed was unaffected, so it does not refute the speed/readability claim. opendyslexic.org/related-research cites favorable studies but these are maker-affiliated, not independent controlled evidence.

#### V131 `ac5d6190` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "WCAG SC 1.4.10 Reflow requires content to be presentable without loss of information/functionality and without two-dimensional scrolling at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels (for vertical-scrolling content) or a height equivalent to 256 CSS pixels (for horizontal-scrolling content)."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/reflow.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Content can be presented without loss of information or functionality, and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions for: Vertical scrolling content at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels; Horizontal scrolling content at a height equivalent to 256 CSS pixels."

*Evidence:* Verbatim match with the W3C primary source (https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/reflow.html). WebFetch of the primary URL returned the exact normative text: "Content can be presented without loss of information or functionality, and without requiring scrolling in two dimensions for: Vertical scrolling content at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels; Horizontal scrolling content at a height equivalent to 256 CSS pixels." The claim's mapping is correct (vertical-scroll→320px width, horizontal-scroll→256px height). Independent corroboration from Deque University, DigitalA11y, and includia reproduce identical figures. Note 1 explains the derivation (320px = 1280 CSS px viewport @ 400% zoom; 256px = 1024 CSS px @ 400% zoom), confirming the numbers. Source is the normative standard itself — primary, current (WCAG 2.1 2018, carried unchanged into WCAG 2.2), not marketing. Minor: the claim omits the "except for parts requiring two-dimensional layout" exception, but this is an omission of additive context, not a misstatement — the core requirement is stated accurately. Correctly tagged as a [STRONG]/normative-standard claim. No contradicting evidence found.

*Claim under review:* "SC 1.4.8 requires that text not be justified to both margins (i.e., favors left-aligned/ragged-right for accessibility)."

**Supporting quote:** "Text is not justified (aligned to both the left and the right margins)"

The supporting quote is exact and directly supports the claim. No contradicting evidence; WebAIM, BDA style guide, and WCAG technique G169 all favor left-aligned ragged-right over justified, so the "(i.e., favors left-aligned/ragged-right)" parenthetical matches consensus. Two precision caveats that qualify but do not refute: (1) SC 1.4.8 is Level AAA, not A/AA — claim omits this; (2) the criterion's stem reads "a mechanism is available to achieve the following," so it requires a mechanism to make text non-justified rather than an absolute default ban — claim's "requires that text not be justified" slightly compresses this. Source is primary and normative; text unchanged in WCAG 2.2. Recommend the skill tag this [STRONG] but note it is Level AAA and "mechanism-available" framed.

#### V133 `acc58a60` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The fix is the @font-face metric-override technique: size-adjust, ascent-override, descent-override, and line-gap-override let a fallback font be tuned to match the web font's metrics, preventing the layout shift on swap."

**Source:** https://developer.chrome.com/blog/framework-tools-font-fallback (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Properties such as `size-adjust`, `ascent-override`, `descent-override`, and `line-gap-override`...can help override the metrics of a fallback font"

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches. Primary source (Chrome for Developers, framework-tools-font-fallback) states verbatim: "Properties such as size-adjust, ascent-override, descent-override, and line-gap-override in the @font-face rule can help override the metrics of a fallback font," and frames metric-matching as "an effective strategy for preventing font loading layout shift" — so the claim's mechanism (tune fallback to match web-font metrics) and purpose (avoid swap CLS) are both directly stated, not overreach. Corroborated independently by MDN, the W3C CSS Fonts spec (which defines these descriptors normatively), DebugBear, Sentry engineering blog, Vincent Bernat, Aleksandr Hovhannisyan, and Type Project; the entire fallback-tooling ecosystem (Fontaine, Capsize, Next.js next/font, Fontsource) is built on it. No contradicting evidence found. Currency: descriptors shipped in Chromium ~2021-2022, broadly supported in 2026. Source quality (official Google engineering blog + W3C spec + MDN) exceeds the bar for what is documented standard CSS behavior, not an extraordinary claim. One non-refuting nuance: the claim says "preventing" while sources more carefully say "reduce or eliminate" — full elimination requires precise metric matching, otherwise a small residual shift remains; but the Chrome article itself uses "preventing" language, so the claim is consistent with its source. Recommend the skill phrase it as "reduces, and with exact metric matching eliminates, the swap shift" and tag [CONVENTION]/best-practice (the descriptors' effect is mechanically documented, but no controlled CLS study is cited). Composes cleanly with prior typography/color/charting findings; nothing contradicts them.
*Counter-source:* No credible source disputes the technique. Closest qualifier: sources (Chrome blog, web.dev) phrase the outcome as "reduce or eliminate" rather than absolute "prevent" — full prevention depends on achieving precise metric alignment, and a fallback can't perfectly match a variable/display web font in all cases. This qualifies wording, not the substance.

#### V135 `ace00fab` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Subjective font preference does NOT predict reading performance: Preference Ratings vs Reading Time had a Pearson correlation of only -0.13 (near zero). Preference is not a proxy for reading speed."

**Source:** https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2513383.2513447 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Preference Ratings and Reading Time had a Pearson correlation of -0.13... which implies that there is almost no correlation between the reading time and the participants preferences."

*Evidence:* VERBATIM CONFIRMED in the primary source. I extracted the full text of Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013, "Good Fonts for Dyslexia" (ACM ASSETS 2013, DOI 10.1145/2513383.2513447) from the University of Michigan-hosted PDF (dyslexiahelp.umich.edu) by decompressing the PDF streams. The paper states verbatim: "Preference Ratings and Reading Time had a Pearson correlation of -0.13, negative as expected (Table 1). However is close to 0, which implies that there is almost no correlation between the reading time and the participants preferences." This matches the claim's -0.13 figure and "near zero / not a proxy" interpretation exactly. Methodology context (also confirmed in text): within-subjects, 48 dyslexic adults, 12 fonts, eye-tracking; Reading Time and Fixation Duration had Pearson r=0.67 (p<0.001); Font Type had a significant effect on both reading time and on subjective preference ratings (χ²(11)=79.61, p<0.001) — so people DO have strong font preferences, those preferences just barely track reading speed. The claim is correctly scoped: it says preference doesn't predict speed, NOT that preference is irrelevant. Source quality is primary peer-reviewed and appropriate for this modest empirical claim; the broader literature corroborates (readers are poor self-judges of their reading speed). Two minor qualifications that do not refute: (1) population is dyslexic adults on screen, so the general headline is an extrapolation, though consistent with prior typography findings; (2) the -0.13 is a font-level descriptive correlation over ~12 condition means (Table 1), not a significance-tested participant-level coefficient — but the paper's own phrasing "almost no correlation" matches the claim's framing. No credible contradicting source found. Nothing conflicts with the colour or charting skills.

The quote ("Using a within-subject design, 48 subjects with dyslexia read 12 texts with 12 different fonts") directly supports the claim with no overreach — the claim only describes the design and tags it [STRONG], which is appropriate for a peer-reviewed controlled within-subject experiment and matches the research brief's loosened bar (controlled study => [STRONG]). Source quality (peer-reviewed primary ACM paper) matches claim strength; corroborated, not contradicted, by follow-up work (Wery & Diliberto 2017) and the British Dyslexia Association. Only minor methodological footnote (fixation duration as a readability proxy) — not a refutation. No credible contradicting source found.

This is settled, unchanged across WCAG 2.0/2.1/2.2. Part 2 (80/40 CJK width): the exact WCAG text reads "Width is no more than 80 characters or glyphs (40 if CJK)," matching the supporting quote verbatim. Minor nuance: WCAG frames this as "a mechanism is available to achieve the following" rather than an absolute page mandate, so "caps" slightly overstates the mechanism-availability framing — but the load-bearing facts (AAA conformance level and the 80-char / 40-CJK numbers) are exactly correct. No contrary or qualifying evidence found; source is the authoritative primary standard, matching the claim's strength.

W3C/WAI is the definitive normative authority for WCAG, so source strength matches this standards claim exactly. The claim accurately states the 200% = twice width/height threshold; it compresses the normative qualifiers (without AT, without loss of content/functionality, except captions and images of text) but is scoped to the threshold figure, which is precisely what the source states. #### V139 `aebbe0c2` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "SC 1.4.8 requires that text not be justified to both margins (i.e., favors left-aligned/ragged-right for accessibility)."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/visual-presentation.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Text is not justified (aligned to both the left and the right margins)"

*Evidence:* Verified against the primary source (W3C WCAG 2.1 Understanding 1.4.8, fetched 2026-06-27). SC 1.4.8 bullet 3 reads verbatim: "Text is not justified (aligned to both the left and the right margins)" — matching the supporting quote exactly. The criterion lists five visual-presentation conditions for blocks of text, and non-justified text is one of them, so the claim that 1.4.8 favors left-aligned/ragged-right is accurate and primary-sourced. Two qualifying caveats (do not refute): (1) It is Level AAA, the highest/least-claimed tier, not A or AA — "requires" could be read as baseline. (2) The exact framing is "a mechanism is available to achieve" non-justified text, i.e. satisfiable by a user toggle, not a hard prohibition on author-justified text. So "requires that text not be justified" is a mild overstatement of strictness, but the directional content (WCAG favors ragged-right for accessibility) is correct. Not contradicted by any source, not outdated, not marketing. The skill should state it as: "WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.8 (AAA) lists non-justified text as a visual-presentation condition — favors left-aligned/ragged-right [CONVENTION/normative]," noting the AAA level and mechanism-available framing.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found; the only nuances are the AAA conformance level and the "mechanism is available to achieve" framing, both within the same primary W3C document.

#### V140 `aee17377` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Subjective font preference does NOT predict reading performance: Preference Ratings vs Reading Time had a Pearson correlation of only -0.13 (near zero). Preference is not a proxy for reading speed."

**Source:** https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2513383.2513447 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Preference Ratings and Reading Time had a Pearson correlation of -0.13... which implies that there is almost no correlation between the reading time and the participants preferences."

*Evidence:* Verified verbatim against the primary-source PDF (Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013, "Good Fonts for Dyslexia," ACM SIGACCESS/ASSETS 2013, DOI 10.1145/2513383.2513447). Extracted exact text: "Preference Ratings and Reading Time had a Pearson correlation of -0.13, negative as expected (Table 1). However is close to 0, which implies that there is almost no correlation between the reading time and the participants preferences." Study design: 48 dyslexic participants, 12 fonts, eye-tracking reading time + 5-point Likert preference. r=-0.13 explains ~1.7% of variance and is non-significant at n=48. The claim quotes the paper accurately and the source is a peer-reviewed primary study, so its strength matches the claim. The only apparent counter (BYU blog noting preferred Arial/Helvetica were also fastest) is an aggregate font-ranking observation, not the within-person correlation, and the paper's authors themselves conclude "almost no correlation" — consistent with the broader "preference != reading speed" consensus in the prior typography findings. Caveat (not a refutation): single dyslexic-adult sample, so the generalization should stay scoped, which the claim does. Tag-wise this is [STRONG] (controlled study). DOI 10.1145/2513383.2513447 was 403 on direct fetch but the full-text PDF (dyslexiahelp.umich.edu mirror) confirmed the exact sentence.
*Counter-source:* None material. BYU editing-research blog (editingresearch.byu.edu) frames preference as aligning with speed at the aggregate font-ranking level, but this does not contradict the per-participant Pearson r=-0.13; the paper itself states "almost no correlation."

#### V141 `aef33d5f` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "OpenDyslexic actually performed worse than standard fonts (Arial, Times New Roman), with statistically significant deficits (IRD -49.65% to -88.65% on fluency; -53.89% to -75.81% on accuracy, CIs excluding zero), so the specialized font may hurt rather than help."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5629233/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Effect sizes showed OpenDyslexic performed worse, with IRD (Improvement Rate Difference) ranging from -49.65% to -88.65% on fluency measures and -53.89% to -75.81% on accuracy—all confidence intervals excluding zero, indicating statistically significant deficits."

*Evidence:* Claim verified against the primary source PMC5629233 = Wery & Diliberto (2017), "The effect of a specialized dyslexia font, OpenDyslexic, on reading rate and accuracy," Annals of Dyslexia 67(2):114-127. Single-subject alternating-treatment design, n=12 diagnosed dyslexic students (grades 3-6), comparing OpenDyslexic vs Arial vs Times New Roman across letter-naming, word-reading, and nonsense-word tasks; IRD (Improvement Rate Difference) with 95% CIs is the correct non-overlap effect size for this design. Fluency IRD values match the claim exactly: Arial vs OD -49.65% [-63.33, -32.98], TNR vs OD -67.73% [-73.60, -46.17], word reading -88.65% [-94.45, -77.57] — confirming the -49.65% to -88.65% range, all CIs excluding zero (statistically significant). Authors' verbatim conclusion: "no improvement in reading rate or accuracy for individual students with dyslexia, as well as the group as a whole"; "None of the participants reported preferring to read material presented in that font." Source quality is strong (peer-reviewed primary study) and matches the claim strength. Independently corroborated by the International Dyslexia Association ("Do Special Fonts Help People with Dyslexia?") and the broader literature (Rello & Baeza-Yates 2013; British Dyslexia Association style guide recommends standard sans-serifs, not OpenDyslexic). Not outdated; no credible source disputes it. TWO caveats that do NOT rise to refutation: (1) the accuracy range as stated in the claim (-53.89% to -75.81%) does not match verbatim the two principal accuracy IRD point estimates I could confirm (-68.18% [-78.64, -54.10] and -63.62% [-74.96, -48.66]); the claim's endpoints fall within those CIs and the study has multiple accuracy sub-measures, so the range is plausibly the full sub-measure span but was not confirmed verbatim. (2) "May hurt rather than help" is slightly stronger than the authors' own "no improvement" framing; the claim's "may" hedge keeps it defensible, but safest skill phrasing is "did not help / standard fonts matched or beat it." Core claim and [MYTH] usage are well-grounded.
*Counter-source:* No credible contradicting source found; corroborated by International Dyslexia Association (dyslexiaida.org/do-special-fonts-help-people-with-dyslexia) and British Dyslexia Association style guide. Only internal caveat: claim's accuracy range endpoints (-53.89% to -75.81%) not confirmed verbatim against the two principal accuracy IRDs in the paper (-68.18%, -63.62%), though within reported CIs.

#### V142 `af51f956` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Chrome partitions the HTTP cache so a resource cached while visiting one site is NOT served from cache on a different site, even when the resource URL is identical — defeating the historical shared-CDN caching benefit (e.g. a font fetched from a shared CDN on site A is re-downloaded on site B)."

**Source:** https://developers.google.com/web/updates/2020/10/http-cache-partitioning (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The same user visits a different page (https://b.example) which requests the same image (https://x.example/doge.png). Though the same image was loaded in the previous example, since the key doesn't match it will not be a cache hit."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported, current, and primary-sourced — I could not refute it on any of the five checklist axes.

1. SUPPORTED BY QUOTE: The cited Google/Chrome doc (the original developers.google.com URL 301-redirects to the canonical developer.chrome.com/blog/http-cache-partitioning — same content) contains the quote verbatim: cache key "{ https://b.example, https://b.example, https://x.example/doge.png }" and "Though the same image was loaded in the previous example, since the key doesn't match it will not be a cache hit." This exactly substantiates "identical URL on site B is a cache miss." No overreach.

2. NO CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE: Multiple independent sources confirm, none dispute: chromestatus.com feature 5730772021411840 ("Partition the HTTP Cache"), the shivanigithub/http-cache-partitioning explainer, Addy Osmani's "Double-keyed Caching," Stefan Judis "Say goodbye to resource-caching across sites," Peakhour "The CDN is Dead," and wicki.io "Time to Say Goodbye to Google Fonts." All corroborate that shared-CDN cross-site cache reuse is defeated.

3. SOURCE QUALITY MATCHES STRENGTH: Primary source is the browser vendor's own engineering blog (Google/Chrome) — the authoritative source for Chrome behavior — plus the formal chromestatus entry. Appropriate for this factual claim.

4. NOT OUTDATED — actually MORE entrenched: Shipped in Chrome 86 (Oct 2020). Far from being reversed, partitioning is now standard across all major browsers — Safari partitioned years earlier, Firefox shipped it (Network Partitioning, FF 85). The "fast-moving field reversal" risk is absent; the trend reinforced the claim.

5. NOT MARKETING: It is technical browser-behavior documentation describing a privacy/security mitigation (defeats cross-site cache side-channel/timing attacks), with measured costs disclosed (cache-miss rate +~3.6%, bytes-from-network +~4%, FCP +~0.3%) — the opposite of cherry-picked promotion.

Minor nuance (does not refute): some descriptions call it "triple-keyed" (top-level site + current-frame site + resource URL) rather than "double-keyed," but the operative consequence the claim asserts — same URL on a different top-level site = cache miss — holds in every account.

#### V143 `af5ff828` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[STRONG] Sans-serif, monospaced and roman styles produced significantly shorter FIXATION DURATIONS than serif/proportional/italic, but did NOT significantly change total reading TIME — so the speed-advantage conclusion is explicitly weak. Serif had no significant effect on Reading Time (W=11852, p=0.2021); only fixation differed (0.26s vs 0.24s, p=0.008). This supports 'no robust inherent serif/sans reading-speed advantage.'"

**Source:** https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/2513383.2513447 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Although sans serif, monospaced and roman fonts lead to significant shorter fixation durations, we did not find a significant difference in reading time. Hence, our conclusions towards these characteristics are weaker."

*Evidence:* Verified directly against the primary-source PDF (Rello & Baeza-Yates, "Good Fonts for Dyslexia," ASSETS 2013, DOI 10.1145/2513383.2513447). Every statistic is verbatim-correct. Section 5.1.3: "We did not find a significant effect of Serif on Reading Time (W = 11852, p = 0.2021)" and "significant effect of Serif on Fixation Duration (W = 10547.5, p = 0.008)... serif x̄=0.26s... sans serif x̄=0.24s" — matches the claim's W=11852/p=0.2021 and 0.26 vs 0.24/p=0.008 exactly. Section 6 supporting quote ("...we did not find a significant difference in reading time. Hence, our conclusions towards these characteristics are weaker") is verbatim. The pattern holds for all three contrasts: Monospace reading time p=0.159 n.s./fixation p<0.001 sig; Italics reading time p=0.09 n.s./fixation p=0.040 sig. The claim is faithful and actually MORE careful than the paper's own headline ("significantly improved reading performance"), which is carried solely by fixation duration; the claim correctly scopes to reading TIME. The dyslexic-reader population strengthens rather than contradicts the general "no robust serif/sans reading-speed advantage," and the paper independently supports the sibling "preference ≠ reading speed" finding (serif-preference p=0.999; ReadingTime-Preference r=-0.13). Controlled within-subjects eye-tracking study, 46/48 valid, proper non-parametric stats — justifies the [STRONG] tag. No contradicting evidence; consistent with prior typography, color, and charting findings.

#### V144 `afb45d54` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Chrome partitions the HTTP cache so a resource cached while visiting one site is NOT served from cache on a different site, even when the resource URL is identical — defeating the historical shared-CDN caching benefit (e.g. a font fetched from a shared CDN on site A is re-downloaded on site B)."

**Source:** https://developers.google.com/web/updates/2020/10/http-cache-partitioning (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The same user visits a different page (https://b.example) which requests the same image (https://x.example/doge.png). Though the same image was loaded in the previous example, since the key doesn't match it will not be a cache hit."

*Evidence:* CONFIRMED — claim is accurate, current, and primary-sourced. The cited web.dev URL 301-redirects to Google's canonical Chrome for Developers blog (developer.chrome.com/blog/http-cache-partitioning), which states the cache is keyed by a "Network Isolation Key" composed of "the top-level site and the current-frame site" IN ADDITION to the resource URL. The supporting quote is verbatim and on-point: for b.example requesting https://x.example/doge.png after a.example loaded the same image, "since the key doesn't match it will not be a cache hit" (key changes from {https://a.example, https://a.example, doge.png} to {https://b.example, https://b.example, doge.png}). No overreach. Corroborated by multiple independent authorities (Jeff Kaufman "Shared Cache is Going Away", Addy Osmani "Double-keyed Caching", Stefan Judis, peakhour.io, Chromium "Intent to Implement: Partition the HTTP Cache"), all citing the same jQuery/Google-Fonts-on-shared-CDN example. Timeline: Safari since 2013, Firefox v85, Chrome v86 (rolled out late 2020) — so the effect is universal, not Chrome-only, making the shared-CDN benefit fully obsolete by 2026. Measured impact per Google: ~3.6% cache-miss increase, ~0.3% FCP change. Not outdated, not marketing, primary engineering doc. Minor precision note (not a refutation): partitioning is by top-level site (eTLD+1), so it is specifically cross-SITE sharing that is lost; same-site subpages still share cache — the claim's "different site" wording is correct.
