No contrary or qualifying evidence found.

#### V146 `aff725e5` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "SC 1.4.8 requires that text not be justified to both margins (i.e., favors left-aligned/ragged-right for accessibility)."

**Source:** https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/visual-presentation.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Text is not justified (aligned to both the left and the right margins)"

*Evidence:* Verified against the primary source (W3C WCAG 2.1 Understanding doc). SC 1.4.8 Visual Presentation (Level AAA) lists verbatim as its 3rd requirement: "Text is not justified (aligned to both the left and the right margins)." The supporting quote is an exact match, so the claim is directly supported. The gloss "favors left-aligned/ragged-right for accessibility" is a fair reading (for LTR text, not justified = ragged-right). Source is primary/normative and current (unchanged in WCAG 2.1 and 2.2). Two precision caveats that do NOT refute: (1) it is Level AAA, not the more commonly-required AA baseline — a skill should tag it AAA; (2) the full criterion requires that "a mechanism is available to achieve" non-justified text rather than mandating non-justified content by default. Neither caveat contradicts the claim's substance. Tag as [STRONG] normative standard, noting AAA level.

### Font pairing & deliberate multiple-typeface communication

#### V147 `a0677743` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Clear typographic hierarchy aids scanning effectiveness: NNG calls the layer-cake pattern the most effective scanning behavior and links it to users successfully finding target information when present. Supports the [STRONG] 'hierarchy-aids-scanning' claim (distinct from which-fonts-to-pair conventions)."

**Source:** https://www.nngroup.com/articles/layer-cake-pattern-scanning/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "by far the most effective way to scan pages ... most of the time, it ensures that users will find the information they are looking for (if it is present on the page)"

*Evidence:* Verbatim quote confirmed from two independent fetches of the NNG article: "the layer-cake pattern is by far the most effective way to scan pages" and "most of the time, it ensures that users will find the information they are looking for (if it is present on the page)." The claim restates this faithfully; "when present" correctly captures NNG's "(if it is present on the page)" caveat (no truncation distortion). NNG also explicitly causally links typographic hierarchy to the effective pattern: "When headings and subheadings visually stand out on the page and are descriptive, users engage in an efficient scanning pattern," and "good page structure shifts users... toward the layer-cake... where content comprehension actually improves" — with two necessary conditions, the first being that subheadings are identifiable easily (the typographic standout). So the claim's equivocation of "typographic hierarchy" with "headings that visually stand out" is fair, not overreach. Source-quality caveat (the only soft spot): NNG is consultancy eye-tracking, not peer-reviewed, and the article reports no formal methodology (a 9-participant example is the only sample detail) — on its own thin for a strict [STRONG] "peer-reviewed/controlled" tag. However, the underlying hierarchy-aids-scanning phenomenon IS corroborated by peer-reviewed cognitive-psychology signaling research (Lorch & Lorch; Hartley & Trueman; ScienceDirect "Effects of topic headings on text processing: evidence from adult readers' eye fixation patterns"): eye-movement data show "signaling guided attention to relevant information and improved the efficiency and effectiveness of finding necessary information." Web searches surfaced only converging support and zero contradiction; the finding is current and the signaling literature is decades-deep and stable. Three refutation tests all fail: not unsupported by quote, not contradicted, and the [STRONG] tag is substantively earned via independent peer-reviewed corroboration even if NNG alone would be weak. Recommend the skill cite NNG for the applied/eye-tracking framing AND Lorch/Hartley signaling studies as the peer-reviewed backbone of the [STRONG] tag.
*Counter-source:* No credible contradicting source found. Closest caution: NNG is non-peer-reviewed consultancy research with opaque methodology (no sample sizes/statistics beyond a 9-participant example), so [STRONG] should lean on the peer-reviewed signaling literature (Lorch & Lorch; Hartley & Trueman; ScienceDirect eye-fixation studies) rather than NNG alone.

#### V148 `a0972c8c` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "In a controlled experiment, college students who read a 12-topic expository text WITH headings remembered the text's topics and their organizational structure better than students who read the identical text WITHOUT headings — controlled evidence that structural signals (headings) aid recall of content organization, the comprehension/scanning side of typographic hierarchy."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0361476X00910567 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "participants who received training and/or read the text with headings remembered text topics and their organization better"

*Evidence:* VERIFIED. Source correctly identified: Sanchez, R.P., Lorch, E.P., & Lorch, R.F. (2001), "Effects of Headings on Text Processing Strategies," Contemporary Educational Psychology 26(3):418-428 (DOI 10.1006/ceps.2000.1056; PMID 11414729). The pii S0361476X00910567 matches. Abstract confirms: college students read a 12-topic expository text with or without headings, and "participants who received training and/or read the text with headings remembered text topics and their organization better than participants who received no training and read the text without headings." This directly supports the claim that headings (a structural signal) improved recall of topics AND their organizational structure. Peer-reviewed controlled experiment by established signaling researchers (the Lorches) — sufficient for a [STRONG] tag. No contradicting evidence found; the broader signaling literature (Lorch, Meyer, Hyona) converges, and the claim is well-calibrated because the robust signaling effect is specifically on macrostructure/organization recall (exactly what the claim states), not total amount recalled. Not outdated (slow field, later replications). TWO QUALIFICATIONS that narrow but do not refute: (1) The design was 2x2 (outline-training x headings); the abstract's "and/or" co-manipulates training, though the headings main effect is the paper's titular focus and is the established finding. (2) The study tested PRESENCE of headings (a content/structural signal), not TYPOGRAPHIC rendering of hierarchy (font contrast/size/weight) — the bridge to "typographic hierarchy" is interpretive, but the claim hedges it correctly by labeling headings as "structural signals." The specific factual claim is accurate and properly sourced.

#### V149 `a1160aba` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "In a controlled experiment, college students who read a 12-topic expository text WITH headings remembered the text's topics and their organizational structure better than students who read the identical text WITHOUT headings — controlled evidence that structural signals (headings) aid recall of content organization, the comprehension/scanning side of typographic hierarchy."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0361476X00910567 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "participants who received training and/or read the text with headings remembered text topics and their organization better"

*Evidence:* Verified primary source. PII S0361476X00910567 = Sanchez, Lorch & Lorch (2001), "Effects of Headings on Text Processing Strategies," Contemporary Educational Psychology 26(3):418-428 (PubMed 11414729). PubMed abstract verbatim: "College students read and recalled a 12-topic expository text... Half of the participants read a text containing headings before every subsection; the other half read the same text without headings. The results were that participants who received training and/or read the text with headings remembered text topics and their organization better than participants who received no training and read the text without headings." The claim's substance (headings improve recall of topics + organizational structure) matches the finding exactly, and the supporting quote is the real abstract sentence. Peer-reviewed controlled factorial experiment = appropriate quality for a [STRONG] tag. No credible source disputes it; the broader Lorch text-signaling corpus corroborates (e.g., Lorch & Lorch 1996, "Effects of Headings on Text Recall and Summarization"). Honest qualifications (do not refute): (1) the headline result crosses headings with a TRAINING factor via "and/or" — the distinctly-worst cell was no-training/no-headings, so headings-alone among untrained readers isn't isolated in the one-sentence abstract, though headings was an independently manipulated factor and is the paper's subject; (2) the study measured free RECALL/memory, not "scanning"/eye-movements — the claim's "scanning" word slightly overreaches THIS study (scanning evidence is the separate Hyona & Lorch eye-fixation line); the memory-of-organization framing is accurate; (3) a known tradeoff in this literature (signals boost topic-STRUCTURE memory but can reduce within-topic DETAIL recall) is not contradicted because the claim limits itself to topics/organization. Not outdated (foundational, still cited), not marketing.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found. PubMed 11414729 confirms citation and abstract verbatim; corroborated by Lorch text-signaling literature.

#### V150 `a1a06a86` — **REFUTED** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Users do not read web text thoroughly word-by-word; they scan, with eye movement following a dominant F-shaped path (top horizontal sweep, a shorter second horizontal sweep, then a vertical scan down the left edge), established via eye-tracking of 232 users across thousands of pages."

**Source:** https://www.nngroup.com/articles/f-shaped-pattern-reading-web-content-discovered/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Users won't read your text thoroughly in a word-by-word manner. Exhaustive reading is rare."

*Evidence:* The claim's scanning core ("users don't read word-by-word; exhaustive reading is rare") is supported by the quote and accurate (232 users / thousands of pages / three-stroke F all match the 2006 NN/g article). But the load-bearing word "dominant" is an overreach contradicted on two fronts. (1) NN/g's OWN 2017 follow-up ("F-Shaped Pattern of Reading on the Web: Misunderstood, But Still Relevant") states "The F-Shaped Pattern Is Not the Only Scanning Pattern," calls it BAD and a consequence of unformatted content ("The F-shape is bad for users and businesses... Good web formatting reduces the impact of F-scanning"), lists five other patterns (layer-cake, spotted, marking, bypassing, commitment), and prescribes headings/hierarchy as the antidote that BREAKS the F. So the originating org does not treat it as a dominant law but as a failure mode of poor formatting. (2) Independent quantitative analysis by EyeQuant (99 sites, 46 participants each, 157,498 scan-phase fixations) found NO F-bias: "out of those 99 websites, we didn't find any individual heat maps that closely resembled an F" — the real bias was a central "blob" with slight leftward lean. Even the 2006 article hedges ("rough, general shape... not pixel-perfect," sometimes E or inverted-L). Source is non-peer-reviewed practitioner research presented as settled fact, and the framing is dated (2006, qualified 2017). For the typography skill, the honest inversion (F-pattern = what happens WITHOUT hierarchy; good hierarchy yields the layer-cake pattern) actually supports the skill's thesis while refuting this claim's "dominant" characterization.
*Counter-source:* NN/g's own 2017 follow-up (nngroup.com/articles/f-shaped-pattern-reading-web-content/) which says the F-pattern is "not the only scanning pattern," is "bad," and is broken by good formatting/headings; plus EyeQuant quantitative study (99 sites, 157,498 fixations) finding no F-shape bias ("simply a blob").

#### V151 `a26d57e5` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Controlled studies reviewed across many signal types (including headings and typographical cues) show signals reliably improve memory for the specific information they emphasize — concrete evidence that typographic emphasis/hierarchy aids retention of the cued content."

**Source:** https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF01320135 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Virtually all types of signals produce better memory for information they cue in a text, whereas memory for unsignaled information often is unaffected."

*Evidence:* Source confirmed genuine and quote verbatim: Lorch, R. F., Jr. (1989), "Text-signaling devices and their effects on reading and memory processes," Educational Psychology Review 1(3):209-234 (DOI 10.1007/BF01320135) — a peer-reviewed review synthesizing controlled studies. The supporting quote ("Virtually all types of signals produce better memory for information they cue in a text, whereas memory for unsignaled information often is unaffected") appears in the paper and directly supports the claim. The paper studies many signal types (titles, headings, overviews, summaries, typographical cues, number/importance/summary indicators), so "across many signal types, including headings and typographical cues" is accurate. Crucially, the claim is scoped correctly to "memory for the specific information they emphasize / the cued content" — it does NOT overclaim improved total recall or comprehension, which is where the literature is genuinely weaker. Contrary/qualifying evidence checked does NOT refute, only bounds: (1) over-signaling cancels the benefit — Lorch, Lorch & Klusewitz (1995, Contemporary Educational Psychology): "light" underlining improved cued recall but "heavy" cueing was no better than control, effect mediated by attention; (2) selective signaling can impair memory for UNSIGNALED content (half-signaled text → unsignaled recalled more poorly), so the "often unaffected" wording correctly hedges a real trade-off; (3) later multimedia-signaling meta-analyses (103 studies, N=12,201) support signaling benefits while debating magnitude/moderators. Checklist: quote supports claim (yes); not contradicted in direction; source quality matches (peer-reviewed review of controlled studies, [STRONG]); not overturned (replicated/extended 1995-2018); not marketing. Residual caveat for synthesis (not grounds to refute): evidence is 1980s-90s prose-reading, so generalizing to web/UI/dataviz hierarchy and to scanning/comprehension is an inferential bridge, and the robust finding is redistribution of attention to cued content (with a sparing-use and non-target-cost caveat), not a free boost to overall learning.
*Counter-source:* Lorch, Lorch & Klusewitz (1995), "Effects of Typographical Cues on Reading and Recall of Text," Contemporary Educational Psychology — shows over-signaling ("heavy" cueing) eliminates the memory benefit and that selective signaling can impair recall of unsignaled content (trade-off); qualifies but does not contradict the claim's direction.

#### V152 `a2cff0cb` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Controlled summarization experiments show that text topics introduced by a heading (signaled) are more likely to be included in readers' summaries than topics without a heading — direct evidence that headings shape readers' representation of a text's topic structure. This is STRONG controlled evidence that hierarchy/signaling markers aid topic identification, though it concerns headings as a structural device rather than typeface contrast per se."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0361476X99910378 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "In all experiments, topics were more likely to be included in a summary if they were signaled than if they were not signaled."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED, well-scoped. Source confirmed as Lorch, Lorch, Ritchey, McGovern & Coleman (2001), "Effects of Headings on Text Summarization," Contemporary Educational Psychology 26(3):171-191 (PII S0361476X99910378; "99" is the registration number, published 2001). The supporting quote is verbatim from the paper, and the article's own conclusion matches the claim: headings affect "how readers represent a text's topic structure," with signaling effects on recall mediated through topic-structure representation. (1) Quote supports claim — direct restatement, no overreach. (2) Source is primary, peer-reviewed, multi-experiment (college students plus 6th/8th graders), with controlled manipulation: headings on all topics / half / none. (3) The known qualifier in signaling research — that signals shift SELECTIVE recall and organization more than TOTAL comprehension — does NOT contradict the claim, because the claim is deliberately scoped to "topic identification," not comprehension; this is consistent, not contrary. The study's own trade-off finding (unsignaled topics LESS likely to be summarized under partial signaling) reinforces the topic-structure-steering effect rather than refuting it. (4) The [STRONG] tag is defensible for the narrow proposition (controlled, replicated; cf. Mayer's signaling principle, Dunlosky et al. 2013). (5) Critically, the claim is self-aware and honest: it explicitly states this "concerns headings as a structural device rather than typeface contrast per se," so it does NOT illegitimately extend to proving typeface-pairing or font-contrast effects. No credible contradicting source found. Only caveats are minor and already accounted for in the claim's hedging; the skill must not silently extend this finding from headings-as-structure to typeface contrast (the claim already says so).

#### V153 `a381b2c1` — **REFUTED** · confidence: medium
*Claim under review:* "Brown frames three combining strategies and is explicit that a SECOND family is not required: a single type family (exploiting its weights/widths/features) or a single typeface can supply 'a multitude of possibilities'; a superfamily (serif+sans from one designer) is a second option; multiple families a third. Weight/width within one family is the first lever. [CONVENTION — Tim Brown]"

**Source:** https://blog.typekit.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/combiningtypefaces.pdf (secondary)
**Supporting quote:** "Read any article or book about mixing fonts (this one included) and you['ll find] all of the following advice: stick with a single type family (making use of its various weights, widths and features); use a superfamily ... Even sticking with a single typeface (for example, the complete Proxima Nova family) provides a multitude of possibilities, particularly if that face o[ffers] ... weights, widths and styles."

*Evidence:* Source is Tim Brown's "A Pocket Guide to Combining Typefaces" (2013, Five Simple Steps; free Typekit PDF 2016) — a PRIMARY source for Brown's views (claim mislabels it "secondary," but that understates quality, so source is fine for [CONVENTION]). Verified the actual text via the vdoc.pub mirror. THREE distinct misrepresentations: (1) COUNT — the advice passage (Ch.3, p.26) lists FOUR items, not "three combining strategies": "stick with a single type family (making use of its various weights, widths and features); use a superfamily that includes sans and serif siblings; limit yourself to a specific historical period; use typefaces from the same type designer." The claim drops "historical period," conflates "superfamily" with "same type designer," and invents "multiple families" as a named strategy the list does not contain. (2) STANCE — Brown does not "frame" these as his endorsed model; he CRITIQUES them: immediately after the list he writes "That's all fine advice because it makes adequate combinations easy to identify. But it also robs us of the opportunity to truly understand why a combination works or doesn't, and can lead to a false sense of completion." The claim's "Brown frames... and is explicit that" omits this critique and presents conventional advice he is distancing from as his affirmative framework. (3) QUOTE SPLICING — the Proxima Nova "multitude of possibilities" line is on p.6 (intro), a separate passage from the p.26 advice list; the claim stitches the two non-contiguous passages into one quotation via ellipsis. The load-bearing core ("a second family is not required; single-family weight/width is a valid primary lever") IS genuinely Brown's view (Proxima Nova passage; he calls single-family advice "fine") and matches Bringhurst/Lupton convention — so a corrected, narrower claim would survive. But the claim AS WORDED ("three combining strategies," "Brown frames," "is explicit") is an overreach/misread of the cited passage's count, structure, and the author's critical stance. Refuting on overreach/misread of source, not on substantive falsity.
*Counter-source:* Tim Brown, "A Pocket Guide to Combining Typefaces" (2013), Ch.3 p.26 (four-item advice list + critique "robs us of the opportunity to truly understand... false sense of completion") and p.6 (Proxima Nova "multitude of possibilities"), verified via https://vdoc.pub/documents/a-pocket-guide-to-combining-typefaces-2mite2fs97kg and confirmed authorship via https://blog.typekit.com/2016/04/29/combining-typefaces-free-guide-to-great-typography/

#### V154 `a3e46927` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The heading effect is contrastive/relative, not absolute: when only half the topics carried headings, signaled topics were summarized even more often than when all topics had headings, while unsignaled topics were summarized less often than when no topics had headings — i.e., a marked-vs-unmarked contrast amplifies what reads as important. This supports the 'hierarchy by contrast' principle (selective emphasis communicates; uniform emphasis dilutes)."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0361476X99910378 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "This effect was magnified when the text was only half signaled: Signaled topics were more likely to appear in a summary if only half the text topics were signaled than if all of the topics were signaled; however, unsignaled topics were less likely to appear in a summary if half of the text topics were signaled than if none of the text topics were signaled."

*Evidence:* Claim's empirical core is a verbatim-accurate restatement of the source. PII S0361476X99910378 = "Effects of Headings on Text Summarization" (Lorch, Lorch, Ritchey, McGovern & Coleman), Contemporary Educational Psychology vol. 26 (2001) — a peer-reviewed journal, and the manipulation is specifically HEADINGS (a typographic/structural signal), so the source is both legitimate and on-point for a typography skill. Independent web search returned the supporting result in near-identical wording: "signaled topics were more likely to appear in a summary if only half the text topics were signaled than if all of the topics were signaled; however, unsignaled topics were less likely to appear in a summary if half of the text topics were signaled than if none of the text topics were signaled." Both halves of the claim map exactly to this. The interpretive gloss ("marked-vs-unmarked contrast amplifies what reads as important; selective emphasis communicates, uniform emphasis dilutes") is a sound, appropriately-hedged inference from a genuinely relative finding — the claim says "supports the principle," not "proves." Refutation attempts failed: not outdated (text-signaling cognition is stable; 2004 eye-fixation work and the signaling-principle meta-analysis corroborate it), not contradicted (later literature qualifies WHO benefits — strategic/higher-ability readers more than poor readers — a moderator point, not a denial of the contrast effect), not marketing/cherry-picked (primary peer-reviewed source). One honest caveat for the skill, not a refutation: the dependent measure is summarization of expository text (what readers judge important and reproduce), not visual scanning speed or aesthetic typographic hierarchy — so the mapping to "typographic hierarchy by contrast" is a strong analogy and should be labeled as evidence that emphasis is relative/contrastive, not as direct proof about font pairing or visual scanning. Tag: [STRONG] for the contrastive-emphasis finding within text-signaling research; supports the [CONVENTION] hierarchy-by-contrast principle by analogy.
*Counter-source:* No credible source contradicts the specific contrastive finding. Related literature (eye-fixation studies, signaling-principle meta-analysis showing small cognitive-load effects, reader-ability moderation) qualifies who benefits but corroborates rather than refutes the relative/contrast mechanism.

#### V155 `a44cf980` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "IBM Plex is a single unified superfamily spanning sans, serif, and monospace (plus a condensed sans) — one type system designed to cover heading, body, UI, and code roles from a common design, exemplifying superfamily pairing across functional differentiation rather than mixing unrelated families."

**Source:** https://www.ibm.com/plex/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Select Font IBM Plex Sans IBM Plex Sans Italic IBM Plex Mono IBM Plex Mono Italic IBM Plex Serif IBM Plex Serif Italic IBM Plex Sans Condensed IBM Plex Sans Condensed Italic"

*Evidence:* Claim is accurate and well-corroborated. The supporting quote (IBM's font selector listing Plex Sans, Sans Italic, Mono, Mono Italic, Serif, Serif Italic, Sans Condensed, Sans Condensed Italic) directly evidences a single family spanning sans + serif + mono + condensed. Independent corroboration: Wikipedia describes IBM Plex as "an open source typeface superfamily conceptually designed and developed by Mike Abbink at IBM in collaboration with Bold Monday... to be used for all brand material across the company," with four subfamilies — Sans, Serif, Mono, Sans Condensed — each in 8 weights + italics. Critically for the "common design" framing: Plex Mono is "a monospaced typeface based on IBM Plex Sans" and Plex Sans Condensed is "a condensed variant of IBM Plex Sans" — i.e. derived from shared design DNA, the defining property of a superfamily (vs mixing unrelated families). The single distributor (GitHub IBM/plex, one open-source package; Google Fonts hosts all variants) confirms it is one coordinated system, not separate fonts. The functional-differentiation framing is exactly how Plex is deployed in IBM's own Carbon Design System (Plex Sans for UI/body, Plex Mono for code). No credible source disputes or qualifies this. Source quality matches: claim is non-extraordinary, backed by the primary source (ibm.com/plex) plus multiple independent secondary sources, and current (2017 release, still IBM's active corporate typeface). One minor looseness — sans and serif are intentionally distinct designs, not literally "a common design" — but they share coordinated metrics/proportions, which is precisely what "superfamily" means, so the claim's use of the term is correct.

#### V156 `a57f4fd0` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Controlled summarization experiments show that text topics introduced by a heading (signaled) are more likely to be included in readers' summaries than topics without a heading — direct evidence that headings shape readers' representation of a text's topic structure. This is STRONG controlled evidence that hierarchy/signaling markers aid topic identification, though it concerns headings as a structural device rather than typeface contrast per se."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0361476X99910378 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "In all experiments, topics were more likely to be included in a summary if they were signaled than if they were not signaled."

*Evidence:* CONFIRMED, not refuted. The pii/DOI in the claim (S0361476X99910378 = 10.1006/ceps.1999.1037) is Lorch, Lorch, Ritchey, McGovern & Coleman (2001), "Effects of Headings on Text Summarization," Contemporary Educational Psychology 26(2):171-191 — a peer-reviewed, 3-experiment controlled study (college students + 6th/8th graders). Crucially, in THIS study "signaled" literally means "introduced by a heading": the manipulation was texts with headings on every subtopic, half, or none. So the claim's equation of signaled=heading is correct, not an overreach. The quote ("In all experiments, topics were more likely to be included in a summary if they were signaled than if they were not signaled") directly supports the central assertion, and the authors' own conclusion matches the claim verbatim — the study tested "whether headings influence readers' representations of the topic structure of a text" and concluded prior "signaling effects on text recall are mediated by effects on how readers represent a text's topic structure." Source quality matches the [STRONG] tag (leading authority R.F. Lorch, multi-experiment controlled design, replicated/extended 2013/2018/2024). Not outdated, not marketing. The claim is honestly hedged ("headings as a structural device rather than typeface contrast per se"), so it does not overreach into typeface-pairing. One non-refuting qualification: the effect is double-edged (half-signaling suppresses UNsignaled topics; heading effect largest when discussion is brief), so "aid topic identification" is better read as "shape/bias the reader's topic-structure representation" — but the claim's core proposition and its scope limits are accurate.
*Counter-source:* No credible contradicting source for the narrow claim. Broader literature (e.g., Educational Psychology Review, BF01320135) debates whether headings improve OVERALL comprehension/recall (mixed results) and documents the suppression of unsignaled topics — a qualification of, not a contradiction to, the topic-structure-representation claim the source actually makes.

#### V157 `a6306b63` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Brown insists pairing works on underlying COMPATIBILITY, not surface SIMILARITY — two shapes can look different yet share structure, which matters more than looking alike. This grounds the 'pair by contrast, not near-sameness' principle. [CONVENTION — Tim Brown]"

**Source:** https://blog.typekit.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/combiningtypefaces.pdf (secondary)
**Supporting quote:** "Remember that compatibility and similarity are not the same thing. For example, a square and a circle are very di[fferent], but both are strict geometric forms that have more in common with one another than either has with a more calligraphic shape."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and correctly attributed. Source confirmed via WebSearch as genuinely Tim Brown's "A Pocket Guide to Combining Typefaces" (Five Simple Steps 2013; free via Typekit/Adobe blog 2016); Tim Brown is a criteria-named authority for [CONVENTION] tags. The supplied quote ("compatibility and similarity are not the same thing... a square and a circle are very different, but both are strict geometric forms that have more in common with one another than either has with a more calligraphic shape") maps point-for-point onto the claim's paraphrase: compatibility-not-surface-similarity, look-different-yet-share-structure, structure-matters-more-than-looking-alike. Search independently surfaced Brown's adjacent framework terms ("compatible rhythm," superfamilies sharing "the same underlying structure"), and Google Fonts' superfamily page echoes "shared underlying structure," corroborating the passage's authenticity. No credible source contradicts "compatibility != similarity"; not outdated (stable typographic convention); not marketing. Minor caveat: the one interpretive sentence — that this "grounds the pair-by-contrast, not near-sameness principle" — is a mild stretch, since Brown stresses shared structure (compatibility) rather than Lupton/Bringhurst's contrast per se; but it is used only to support the negative half (surface sameness is not the goal), which the quote directly underwrites, and it is honestly tagged [CONVENTION] (expert practice, not proof). Verification limit: the primary PDF exceeds fetch size and is not text-indexed, so the exact sentence could not be pulled verbatim independently — but the bracketed OCR artifact ("[fferent]") indicates genuine extraction, not fabrication. All five refutation checks pass.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found; mainstream typography (Lupton, Bringhurst, Google Fonts superfamily guidance) agrees compatibility/shared structure differs from surface similarity.

#### V158 `a6496531` — **REFUTED** · confidence: medium
*Claim under review:* "Brown frames three combining strategies and is explicit that a SECOND family is not required: a single type family (exploiting its weights/widths/features) or a single typeface can supply 'a multitude of possibilities'; a superfamily (serif+sans from one designer) is a second option; multiple families a third. Weight/width within one family is the first lever. [CONVENTION — Tim Brown]"

**Source:** https://blog.typekit.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/combiningtypefaces.pdf (secondary)
**Supporting quote:** "Read any article or book about mixing fonts (this one included) and you['ll find] all of the following advice: stick with a single type family (making use of its various weights, widths and features); use a superfamily ... Even sticking with a single typeface (for example, the complete Proxima Nova family) provides a multitude of possibilities, particularly if that face o[ffers] ... weights, widths and styles."

*Evidence:* The substantive content is accurate conventional advice, but the claim misattributes Brown's stance on two specific points. (1) "Brown frames three combining strategies" overreaches: the supporting quote's own opening — "Read any article or book about mixing fonts (this one included) and you'll hear some or all of the following advice" — shows Brown is cataloging literature-wide advice, not presenting his own structured three-strategy framework. (2) "Brown is explicit that a SECOND family is not required" is contradicted by the full text of his guide. The vdoc.pub full-text source states plainly "Brown does not say a second font is unnecessary." Brown immediately critiques the single-family advice — it "makes adequate combinations easy to identify. But it also robs us of the opportunity to truly understand why a combination works or doesn't" — and his whole Pocket Guide to Combining Typefaces (Five Simple Steps, 2013) champions thoughtful COMBINING of different typefaces as the more valuable practice. The claim inverts his emphasis: he acknowledges single-family viability ("the complete Proxima Nova family ... a multitude of possibilities" via weights/widths/styles) only in passing while cataloging common advice; he does not advocate that you don't need a second family. The accurate, usable core — single family/superfamily/multiple families as recognized combining strategies, with weight/width within one family as a first lever, tagged [CONVENTION] — is sound and well-supported by the quote. But the claim's specific attribution wording ("Brown frames three combining strategies," "is explicit that a second family is not required") misreads Brown's rhetorical position and should be re-worded to attribute the convention to the general type literature (documented in Brown's guide) rather than asserting it as Brown's own thesis.
*Counter-source:* https://vdoc.pub/documents/a-pocket-guide-to-combining-typefaces-2mite2fs97kg (full text of Tim Brown, A Pocket Guide to Combining Typefaces, Five Simple Steps 2013): "Read any article or book about mixing fonts (this one included) and you'll hear some or all of the following advice..." followed by Brown's critique that such rules-based advice "robs us of the opportunity to truly understand why a combination works or doesn't"; source summary states "Brown does not say a second font is unnecessary."

#### V159 `a68ab276` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Controlled summarization experiments show that text topics introduced by a heading (signaled) are more likely to be included in readers' summaries than topics without a heading — direct evidence that headings shape readers' representation of a text's topic structure. This is STRONG controlled evidence that hierarchy/signaling markers aid topic identification, though it concerns headings as a structural device rather than typeface contrast per se."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0361476X99910378 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "In all experiments, topics were more likely to be included in a summary if they were signaled than if they were not signaled."

*Evidence:* Source verified: "Effects of Headings on Text Summarization," Lorch et al., Contemporary Educational Psychology (ISSN 0361-476X matches PII S0361476X99910378). The exact quote appears verbatim in the abstract: "In all experiments, topics were more likely to be included in a summary if they were signaled than if they were not signaled." The signaling device WAS headings specifically — design used texts with headings on all subtopics, half the subtopics, or none — so the claim's equation of "signaled" with "introduced by a heading" is accurate, not an overreach. The inference "headings shape readers' representation of a text's topic structure" is the authors' own conclusion ("signaling effects on text recall are mediated by effects on how readers represent a text's topic structure"), not the claimant's embellishment. [STRONG] tag is justified: multiple controlled experiments across college and 6th/8th-grade samples, peer-reviewed, by a recognized text-signaling researcher. Contrary-evidence search returned only corroborating follow-up work (eye-tracking heading studies), no disputes. Critically, the claim includes the correct honest caveat that this concerns headings as a structural device, not typeface contrast per se — preventing overreach into font-pairing claims. Only minor nuance (non-refuting): summary-inclusion-to-representation is an interpretation with possible alternative accounts, and the effect is partly contrastive, but both are consistent with the claim as worded.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found; follow-up literature (e.g., ScienceDirect S0959475204000039 eye-fixation study; S0361476X07000446 text-heading memory study) corroborates rather than disputes the finding.

#### V160 `a737eb36` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Controlled eye-tracking evidence: adding topic headings to multi-topic expository text improved comprehension processing of topic sentences AND raised recall, measured as more topics appearing in readers' written summaries. This is direct experimental support that structural/typographic hierarchy aids comprehension and recall, not just aesthetics."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0959475204000039 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The presence of topic headings facilitated the processing of topic sentences and increased the number of topics mentioned in the text summaries written after reading the texts."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED, with minor scoping caveats. Source confirmed as Hyönä, J. & Lorch, R.F. Jr. (2004), "Effects of topic headings on text processing: evidence from adult readers' eye fixation patterns," Learning and Instruction 14(2), 131-152 (peer-reviewed Elsevier; Lorch is a leading text-signaling researcher). The supporting quote is VERBATIM accurate against both the ScienceDirect and ERIC (EJ731658) abstracts: "The presence of topic headings facilitated the processing of topic sentences and increased the number of topics mentioned in the text summaries written after reading the texts," with the added line "The facilitatory effect of headings was reflected both in the fixations made during the first-pass reading as well as in the later look-backs directed to the topic sentences." Design = controlled within-subjects eye-tracking experiment (each adult read one text with topic headings signaling topic shifts, one without). So three claim legs hold: (1) it IS controlled eye-tracking evidence; (2) headings facilitated topic-sentence processing (authors' own word "facilitated," reflected in both first-pass fixations and look-backs); (3) more topics appeared in written summaries — a standard macrostructure recall measure, consistent with the broader signaling literature (Lorch & Lorch; Mayer signaling principle), so "raised recall" is a fair gloss. CAVEATS (qualify, do not refute): (a) "comprehension" is INFERRED from processing + summary recall, not a separate comprehension test (inference/T-F questions) — but summary topic-count is an accepted comprehension/learning outcome; (b) the manipulated variable was heading PRESENCE vs ABSENCE (the topic-naming words), NOT typographic STYLING (font/size/weight/contrast), so it directly evidences that STRUCTURAL headings aid processing and recall and does NOT isolate the contribution of typographic contrast — the claim's "structural/typographic" hedge keeps it honest, but for a font-pairing skill this should be cited as evidence for hierarchy/headings generally, not for type contrast specifically. No contradicting source found; finding aligns with the wider text-signaling literature. Recommend tagging [STRONG] for "clear headings/topic-structure signaling aids scanning, processing and recall," NOT [STRONG] for any specific font-pairing or type-contrast claim.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source located. Corroborating secondary index: ERIC EJ731658 (eric.ed.gov/?id=EJ731658) carries the identical verbatim abstract. Finding is consistent with the broader signaling literature (Lorch & Lorch; Mayer's signaling principle), which similarly treats topic recall in summaries as a learning/comprehension outcome.

*Evidence:* The claim is faithfully supported by the supplied quote and correctly tagged. (1) SUPPORT: The quote states verbatim "compatibility and similarity are not the same thing" and gives the square/circle vs. calligraphic example — two shapes that look very different yet "have more in common with one another" because both are strict geometric forms. The claim's paraphrase ("pairing works on underlying COMPATIBILITY, not surface SIMILARITY — two shapes can look different yet share structure, which matters more than looking alike") is an accurate restatement, not an overreach. (2) ATTRIBUTION/SOURCE: Confirmed via WebSearch that the cited PDF is Tim Brown's "Combining Typefaces: A Pocket Guide" (Five Simple Steps 2013, hosted on Typekit/Adobe blog). Brown is a named typography authority explicitly on the research question's approved-expert list (Web Font Specimen, modularscale.com, "Flexible Typesetting," Adobe/Typekit type evangelist). The metadata calls the source "secondary," but it is actually PRIMARY for Brown's own opinion — source quality exceeds what a [CONVENTION]-strength claim requires. (3) CURRENCY: 2013/2016; pairing craft principles are not fast-moving and Brown's compatibility framework remains widely cited — not outdated. (4) NO CONTRADICTION: No credible source disputes it; it is consistent with the Lupton/Bringhurst "contrast not conflict" convention. Not marketing or cherry-picked benchmark. One minor caveat: the second sentence ("grounds the 'pair by contrast, not near-sameness' principle") is a mild interpretive bridge — Brown's fuller position favors BOTH compatibility AND difference, not pure contrast — but the square/circle example does illustrate "difference is fine when structure is shared; near-sameness reads as a mistake," so the grounding is reasonable, not a misread. I could not pull the exact sentence verbatim from a third-party page (the PDF is not deeply web-indexed), but author, document identity, and conceptual content are all confirmed. Tag [CONVENTION — Tim Brown] is appropriate; claim is not presented as empirical proof.

**Source:** https://blog.typekit.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/combiningtypefaces.pdf (secondary)
**Supporting quote:** "One typeface will anchor the other(s). It['ll] help set the underlying tone of your experience, and act as a reference point for every element in your composition. Find it early and know it well. Make your body text typeface the anchor if possible, because this text represents a majority of your content ... and because text faces are built to withstand a variety of settings, whereas display faces can rarely be used at small sizes or at coarse resolutions."

*Evidence:* Claim is a faithful paraphrase of the cited Tim Brown source and is correctly tagged [CONVENTION — Tim Brown]. The supporting quote from Brown's "Combining Typefaces" (Pocket Guide, Five Simple Steps 2013; reissued free on the Typekit blog 2016) maps element-by-element to the claim: "One typeface will anchor the other(s)... Find it early and know it well" → anchor-first; "Make your body text typeface the anchor if possible" → body-text-as-anchor (the claim's "whenever possible" preserves Brown's "if possible" hedge, not an overreach); "this text represents a majority of your content" → majority rationale; "text faces are built to withstand a variety of settings, whereas display faces can rarely be used at small sizes or at coarse resolutions" → text-vs-display rationale. Attribution is independently confirmed: the Designmodo article (Zolani Stewart, designmodo.com/combine-typefaces) states "Brown suggests using the body text as the anchor in the appropriate settings (websites, magazines)," and the WebSearch summary of the guide confirms the "Anchor" concept. Source quality matches tag strength — Tim Brown was Type Manager at Adobe Typekit and author of Combining Typefaces and Flexible Typesetting (A Book Apart); this is an authoritative named-authority source appropriate for a [CONVENTION] tag, and the claim does not overclaim to [STRONG]. A competing "display-first" school exists (display sets the scene, body completes it), but it does not refute a claim that is explicitly scoped to Brown's method and labelled convention/taste — it reinforces the [CONVENTION] framing. Not outdated in a refuting way (the "coarse resolutions" rationale is mildly dated by hi-DPI screens but is accurately reported as Brown's reasoning; the method is still taught). Not marketing/cherry-picked — it is an educational guide.
*Counter-source:* Contrary "display-first" view (display font sets the scene, body text completes it) appears in pairing guides e.g. Pimp my Type (pimpmytype.com/pairing-fonts) and Pangram Pangram Foundry; Designmodo's Zolani Stewart calls Brown's body-text-as-anchor advice "curious" since body text is the least expressive face. These critique/compete with the method but confirm rather than refute its attribution and [CONVENTION] tag.

*Claim under review:* "Controlled eye-tracking evidence: adding topic headings to multi-topic expository text improved comprehension processing of topic sentences AND raised recall, measured as more topics appearing in readers' written summaries. **Supporting quote:** "The presence of topic headings facilitated the processing of topic sentences and increased the number of topics mentioned in the text summaries written after reading the texts."

*Evidence:* Claim accurately reflects the primary source: Hyönä, J. & Lorch, R. F. (2004), "Effects of topic headings on text processing: evidence from adult readers' eye fixation patterns," Learning and Instruction 14(2), 131-152 (ERIC EJ731658; ScienceDirect S0959475204000039). Controlled within-subjects eye-tracking experiment: each adult read one multiple-topic expository text WITH topic headings and one WITHOUT. Verbatim abstract finding: headings "facilitated the processing of topic sentences and increased the number of topics mentioned in the text summaries written after reading." The facilitation appeared in both first-pass fixations AND look-backs to topic sentences. The claim's two assertions map precisely: (1) "improved comprehension processing of topic sentences" = the eye-fixation facilitation; (2) "raised recall, measured as more topics in written summaries" = the summary-topic-count measure. Source quality is high (peer-reviewed Learning and Instruction; Hyönä and Lorch are leading eye-movement and text-signaling researchers); not outdated for this stable subfield; not marketing. No contradicting source surfaced. Two honest qualifications, not refutations: (a) the manipulation was heading PRESENCE vs ABSENCE — a structural/signaling device — so it supports headings/structural hierarchy directly but does NOT isolate typeface or font-pairing contrast; a skill should attribute this to headings, not font choice. (b) Direct measures were online processing + macrostructure recall (topics in summaries), so "comprehension" means processing + topic recall, not a separate comprehension-test score (a secondary summary also notes improved metacomprehension judgments). Claim is well-supported, current, and source-matched; tag [STRONG] for hierarchy/headings-aid-processing-and-recall, with the caveat that it concerns headings, not font pairing.

#### V164 `a7d685ef` — **upheld** · confidence: medium
*Claim under review:* "Brown frames three combining strategies and is explicit that a SECOND family is not required: a single type family (exploiting its weights/widths/features) or a single typeface can supply 'a multitude of possibilities'; a superfamily (serif+sans from one designer) is a second option; multiple families a third. Weight/width within one family is the first lever. [CONVENTION — Tim Brown]"

**Source:** https://blog.typekit.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/combiningtypefaces.pdf (secondary)
**Supporting quote:** "Read any article or book about mixing fonts (this one included) and you['ll find] all of the following advice: stick with a single type family (making use of its various weights, widths and features); use a superfamily ... Even sticking with a single typeface (for example, the complete Proxima Nova family) provides a multitude of possibilities, particularly if that face o[ffers] ... weights, widths and styles."

*Evidence:* Verified against the PRIMARY source (I downloaded the PDF and extracted text with PyMuPDF; the claim's "(secondary)" label is wrong — this IS Tim Brown's own guide). The load-bearing substance is verbatim-accurate:

- Page 5: "Even sticking with a single typeface (for example, the complete Proxima Nova family) provides a multitude of possibilities, particularly if that face offers many weights, widths and styles." → directly supports "single typeface/family supplies 'a multitude of possibilities' via weights/widths."
- Page 24: "stick with a single type family (making use of its various weights, widths and features); use a superfamily that includes sans and serif siblings" → supports "second family not required" and "weight/width within one family is the first lever" (it is listed first).

So the skill-grounding rule (a second family is optional; weight/width/feature within one family is the first lever; single family gives many possibilities) is solid, source quality is high (Brown's own guide), tag [CONVENTION — Tim Brown] is appropriately humble. Not refuted.

BUT three framing inaccuracies need correction by the synthesizer:
1. COUNT IS WRONG. Brown lists FOUR pieces of advice, not three: (a) single type family, (b) superfamily with sans+serif siblings, (c) "limit yourself to a specific historical period," (d) "use typefaces from the same type designer." The claim drops (c) and (d) and invents "multiple families a third" — which is NOT in Brown's enumerated list at all.
2. CONFLATION. The claim's "a superfamily (serif+sans from one designer)" fuses two distinct list items: superfamily AND "same type designer" are separate items in Brown's text. A superfamily is not defined as "from one designer" in the source.
3. STANCE IS MISREPRESENTED. Brown does not "frame" these as his strategies — he attributes them to "any article or book about mixing fonts" and you'll hear "some or all," then partly DISMISSES the checklist (Page 25): "it also robs us of the opportunity to truly understand why a combination works or doesn't, and can lead to a false sense of completion." His actual thesis is to work from design goals, not from this list.

Net: core convention TRUE and verbatim-sourced; the "three combining strategies" enumeration is a misframe that should be restated as "Brown lists common advice (single family / superfamily / shared historical period / shared designer) and notes a single family or even a single typeface already offers a multitude of possibilities — while cautioning that such checklists give a false sense of completion."
*Counter-source:* Same primary source, Tim Brown, "Combining Typefaces" (Typekit/Five Simple Steps), pp. 24-25: the advice list is four items attributed to "any article or book," not three strategies Brown frames; "multiple families" is not enumerated; Brown then partly critiques the checklist ("false sense of completion"). https://blog.typekit.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/combiningtypefaces.pdf

#### V165 `a7e3c5e9` — **upheld** · confidence: medium
*Claim under review:* "Controlled studies reviewed across many signal types (including headings and typographical cues) show signals reliably improve memory for the specific information they emphasize — concrete evidence that typographic emphasis/hierarchy aids retention of the cued content."

**Source:** https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF01320135 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Virtually all types of signals produce better memory for information they cue in a text, whereas memory for unsignaled information often is unaffected."

*Evidence:* Source verified: DOI 10.1007/BF01320135 = Lorch, R.F. Jr. (1989), "Text-signaling devices and their effects on reading and memory processes," Educational Psychology Review 1(3). The supporting quote is VERBATIM ACCURATE and is the review's headline conclusion, so the claim is faithfully supported by the quote (no misread). The claim is also carefully scoped to RETENTION of the cued content and does not overreach into comprehension or scanning. The direction is corroborated by later work (multimedia-signaling meta-analyses find small-to-medium positive effects favoring signaled material), so it is not contradicted.

Three real caveats that QUALIFY but do NOT reverse the claim — the skill must carry them: (1) Lorch 1989 is a NARRATIVE REVIEW, not a meta-analysis or single controlled study, and is 37 years old; tag it as strong-leaning convention, not gold-standard [STRONG]. (2) "reliably" overstates a literature that modern reviews call inconsistent (Signals and reading comprehension, System, 2000), and the benefit is DOSE-DEPENDENT — cued recall was better under LIGHT signaling than under control or HEAVY signaling; over-emphasizing everything eliminates the effect (Effects of Typographical Cues on Reading and Recall of Text, J. Educ. Psychol.). (3) There is a documented selective-attention TRADE-OFF (cost to memory for un-emphasized content in some studies); the quote's "unsignaled information often unaffected" is a soft, contested generalization. (4) Most studies measure RECALL, not comprehension or scanning — do NOT use this study to support "hierarchy aids scanning/comprehension" (the layer-cake eye-tracking claim needs different evidence). As written ("aids retention of the cued content"), the claim holds.
*Counter-source:* Signals and reading comprehension — theory and practice, System (2000), https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0346251X00000105 (notes inconsistent signaling findings; recall is not a reliable measure of comprehension); Effects of Typographical Cues on Reading and Recall of Text, J. Educ. Psychol., https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0361476X8571003X (light vs heavy signaling dose-dependence; attention-mediated, with cost evidence)

#### V166 `a85f3912` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Controlled eye-tracking evidence: adding topic headings to multi-topic expository text improved comprehension processing of topic sentences AND raised recall, measured as more topics appearing in readers' written summaries. This is direct experimental support that structural/typographic hierarchy aids comprehension and recall, not just aesthetics."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0959475204000039 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The presence of topic headings facilitated the processing of topic sentences and increased the number of topics mentioned in the text summaries written after reading the texts."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported, current, and the source matches the claim's strength. The paper is Hyönä & Lorch (2004), "Effects of topic headings on text processing: Evidence from adult readers' eye fixation patterns," Learning and Instruction 14(2):131-152 — a controlled within-subjects eye-tracking experiment (adults read matched expository texts with vs. without topic headings). The abstract (confirmed via ERIC EJ731658 and ScienceDirect S0959475204000039) states verbatim that headings "facilitated the processing of topic sentences and increased the number of topics mentioned in the text summaries written after reading the texts," with the facilitation visible in both first-pass fixations and later look-backs. Both halves of the claim (eye-tracking processing facilitation on topic sentences + raised recall measured as more topics in written summaries) are stated directly in the abstract — not an overreach or misread. Source quality is high: peer-reviewed primary study; Hyönä is a leading reading-eye-movement researcher and Lorch is the principal authority on text signaling. Far from being contradicted, the finding is corroborated: a signaling meta-analysis (~103 studies, N≈12,201) finds headings improve recall of high-importance information, and Lorch's broader corpus consistently shows topic signaling yields more topics in recall/summaries. Not outdated (text-structure research is slow-moving; a 2023 meta-analysis confirms), not marketing. Legitimate qualifications to attach as calibration, NOT refutation: (1) "comprehension" here = topic-sentence processing facilitation + recall BREADTH (count of topics), not deep inferential comprehension scores; (2) effect is moderated by prior knowledge and information importance per the meta-analysis; (3) headings confound a semantic topic label with visual prominence, so this is evidence that structural hierarchy/signposting aids comprehension/recall — supporting the [STRONG] hierarchy-aids-scanning point — but NOT direct evidence for font-pairing/typeface-contrast specifically. The claim correctly stays scoped to the hierarchy point and does not overreach into font-pairing.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found. Corroborating: Prinz-Weiß et al. (2023) headings/signaling meta-analysis (~103 studies, N≈12,201), Applied Cognitive Psychology https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/acp.4076 — confirms headings improve recall of high-importance info and is moderated by prior knowledge. The only caveat (not a counter) is that headings combine semantic + visual signaling, so this is not a clean test of typographic contrast alone.

#### V167 `a87ce023` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The 'layer-cake' scanning pattern (eye-tracking): users fixate mostly on headings and subheadings, dropping into body text only occasionally, producing alternating horizontal stripes in gaze data. This is direct eye-tracking evidence that distinct headings structure how users scan a page."

**Source:** https://www.nngroup.com/articles/layer-cake-pattern-scanning/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "fixations made mostly on the page's headings and subheadings, with deliberate occasional fixations on the (body) text in between"

*Evidence:* Claim is faithfully supported by the primary NNG source. NNG text (verified via WebFetch of nngroup.com/articles/layer-cake-pattern-scanning): "Fixations made mostly on the page's headings and subheadings, with deliberate occasional fixations on the (body) text in between. In an eyetracking heatmap or gaze plot, this pattern looks roughly like a set of horizontal stripes and blank spaces between them, resembling a layer cake." The claim restates this near-verbatim (headings-mostly, occasional body, horizontal stripes). NNG also explicitly states "The way in which the page is organized visually plays a big role in how people scan its content" and that the pattern requires headings that are "easily identifiable" visually — directly backing "distinct headings structure how users scan." No contradicting source found; the finding is corroborated by independent UX outlets (Mailchimp, Acquia, Instapage) and NNG's wider scanning-pattern corpus, and is complementary to (not contradicted by) the F-pattern. Not outdated: first documented 2004, re-confirmed in NNG's 2017+ scanning work. Source is a credible named authority (NNG) and the claim is appropriately scoped — it asserts only that headings STRUCTURE SCANNING (a descriptive gaze observation, which eye-tracking directly measures), not a controlled comprehension/task-success result. Caveat for skill framing (does not refute): this is NNG industry eye-tracking, not a peer-reviewed RCT, so it is observational [CONVENTION/evidence] that hierarchy organizes the eye, NOT [STRONG] proof that hierarchy improves comprehension. The claim as worded does not overreach into the comprehension leap, so it survives.

#### V168 `a8cf8a15` — **upheld** · confidence: medium
*Claim under review:* "Users do not read web text thoroughly word-by-word; they scan, with eye movement following a dominant F-shaped path (top horizontal sweep, a shorter second horizontal sweep, then a vertical scan down the left edge), established via eye-tracking of 232 users across thousands of pages."

**Source:** https://www.nngroup.com/articles/f-shaped-pattern-reading-web-content-discovered/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Users won't read your text thoroughly in a word-by-word manner. Exhaustive reading is rare."

*Evidence:* All checkable facts are accurate to the cited primary source (NN/g, Apr 16 2006). Verified verbatim: "232 users," "thousands of Web pages," the three-part F description (top horizontal sweep, shorter second sweep lower on the page, vertical scan down the left edge), and the scanning-not-word-by-word assertion ("Exhaustive reading is rare"). "Dominant" is the source's own framing. The central claim — users scan rather than read word-by-word — is robustly supported and STILL affirmed by NN/g's 2017 follow-up ("the F-shaped scanning pattern is alive and well... both on desktop and mobile"). The claim is correctly SCOPED to the study ("established via eye-tracking of 232 users"), not asserted as a universal law. CAVEAT (should be flagged in the skill, but does not refute): the F-shape is context-dependent, not universal. NN/g's 2017 article says it appears only with unformatted text + efficiency-seeking + moderate interest, lists five OTHER scanning patterns (layer-cake, spotted, marking, bypassing, commitment), and calls F-scanning "bad for users and businesses." EyeQuant's quantitative reanalysis (99 sites, 46 participants) found no F-bias at all. So the honest skill framing is: scanning-over-thorough-reading is [STRONG]; the specific "dominant F-shaped path" is one common but contested, context-dependent pattern, not a universal — the word "dominant" should be softened. The claim is faithful, well-sourced, current (qualified not retracted), and source quality matches; it is not unsupported, debunked, outdated, or marketing.
*Counter-source:* NN/g 2017 "F-Shaped Pattern... Misunderstood, But Still Relevant" (https://www.nngroup.com/articles/f-shaped-pattern-reading-web-content/) qualifies heavily but affirms the core; EyeQuant (https://www.eyequant.com/resources/eye-tracking-studies-does-the-famous-f-shape-pattern-really-exist) found no F-bias across 99 sites / 46 users.

#### V169 `a94371e9` — **REFUTED** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Users do not read web text thoroughly word-by-word; they scan, with eye movement following a dominant F-shaped path (top horizontal sweep, a shorter second horizontal sweep, then a vertical scan down the left edge), established via eye-tracking of 232 users across thousands of pages."

**Source:** https://www.nngroup.com/articles/f-shaped-pattern-reading-web-content-discovered/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Users won't read your text thoroughly in a word-by-word manner. Exhaustive reading is rare."

*Evidence:* The claim's facts are accurate (the source confirms "232 users... thousands of Web pages," the three-component F-description, and the quote "Exhaustive reading is rare" backs scanning-not-word-by-word). But the load-bearing word "dominant" fails on two counts. (1) Overreach beyond the supplied quote: that quote supports only scanning, never an F-shape, let alone a dominant one. (2) Contradicted by the SAME authority's later research — NN/g's 2017 follow-up (Pernice, "F-Shaped Pattern of Reading on the Web: Misunderstood, But Still Relevant") states verbatim "Scanning on the web does not always take the shape of an F," "There are many other possible scanning patterns" (it lists five others: layer-cake, spotted, marking, bypassing, commitment), and reframes F as a failure mode of poor formatting: "If your pages have big chunks of unformatted text, people will scan it in an F-shape," "The F-shaped scanning pattern is bad for users and businesses," and "Good design can prevent F-shape scanning." Even the original 2006 article hedges F as "a rough, general shape" that becomes an E or inverted-L depending on the page. So F is one of several patterns and is the anti-goal of good typography, not a "dominant" universal — which is the opposite of what the claim asserts and directly relevant because the typography skill targets layer-cake scanning produced by clear hierarchy. The scanning core (users scan, don't read word-by-word) is true and well-sourced; the distinctive "dominant F-shaped path" framing is refuted.
*Counter-source:* https://www.nngroup.com/articles/f-shaped-pattern-reading-web-content/ (NN/g 2017, Kara Pernice — same authority walking back the "dominant" framing)

*Evidence:* Every element verified VERBATIM against the primary source PDF (Tim Brown, "A Pocket Guide to Combining Typefaces," © 2013 Five Simple Steps, ed. Stephen Coles). Job-types list verbatim: "Is the chunk... supposed to be read comfortably? Or is its purpose to catch and direct readers' attention? Is it informational, meant for readers to pore over and reference? Is its purpose to help people navigate, or gather data quickly? Whatever it is you're trying to do, a typeface was made for that purpose." Geometric-sans claim verbatim: "Geometric sans serif typefaces are great for this job, and Soleil is no exception. Neutral and balanced, it clearly and quietly supplies structured information in the form of navigation, film titles and metadata." The FF Meta Serif detail — which was ABSENT from the provided supporting quote and which I specifically targeted — is also verbatim: "FF Meta Serif strikes a welcoming, conversational tone, while also conveying authority about the subject matter," and "Critiques and interviews are set in FF Meta Serif, a contemporary serif worthy of representing thoughtful, articulate reviews." The synthesis "each typeface appropriately addresses the jobs of its chunks of text" (p.61) supports "typeface signals the kind of content." Author confirmed Tim Brown (on the question's named-authority list, ex-Adobe Typekit type manager). The cited URL is the primary guide PDF itself, so source quality exceeds the [CONVENTION] bar. **Source:** https://www.nngroup.com/articles/layer-cake-pattern-scanning/ (primary)

*Evidence:* Quote is verbatim-accurate: the NNG article states "the layer-cake pattern is by far the most effective way to scan pages: most of the time, it ensures that users will find the information they are looking for (if it is present on the page)," and adds that when subheadings "visually stand out... and are descriptive, users engage in an efficient scanning pattern that allows them to quickly find the information that they need." So the claim's paraphrase is faithful, not an overreach. Layer-cake is a hierarchy mechanism (distinct, descriptive headings), correctly distinguished from which-fonts-to-pair conventions. The one real weakness — NNG is industry research, not peer-reviewed (layer-cake ~2004, F-pattern 2006, small samples), so NNG alone is [CONVENTION]-grade — does not kill the claim because the underlying proposition has independent peer-reviewed controlled support: Lorch et al. "Effects of headings and familiarity with a text on strategies for searching a text" and "Effects of topic headings on text processing: evidence from adult readers' eye fixation patterns" (ScienceDirect, eye-fixation studies), plus the text-signaling literature (Lorch, Educational Psychology Review): "virtually all types of signals produce better memory for information they cue." Searches for contradicting evidence found none; critiques of NNG eye-tracking target the F-pattern as an undesirable outcome and context-dependency, and NNG's own remedy is good headings to induce layer-cake scanning — reinforcing, not refuting, hierarchy-aids-scanning. Verdict: not refuted. Caveat for SKILL.md: anchor the [STRONG] tag to the academic signaling/headings literature (Lorch, Hartley), citing NNG layer-cake as applied corroboration rather than as the primary peer-reviewed source.
*Counter-source:* No source contradicts the claim. Closest qualifier: NNG itself is non-peer-reviewed industry research with small samples (n~9, 2004/2006 origins), so the [STRONG] tag should rest on peer-reviewed signaling/headings studies (Lorch et al., ScienceDirect/Educational Psychology Review) rather than on NNG alone — a citation refinement, not a refutation.

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and corroborated by independent sources beyond the cited quote. Wikipedia (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM_Plex) explicitly describes IBM Plex as "an open source typeface superfamily... designed and developed by Mike Abbink at IBM in collaboration with Bold Monday... to be used for all brand material across the company internationally" — directly supporting the "single unified superfamily / one type system covering heading, body, UI, and code roles" framing. Family structure confirmed verbatim: "comes in Sans, Serif, Mono, and Sans Condensed, all with Roman and true italics," matching the claim's sans+serif+mono+condensed-sans. "Common design" is supported: "IBM Plex Mono is a monospaced typeface based on IBM Plex Sans." Primary GitHub repo (github.com/IBM/plex) and IBM Design pages corroborate. The original supporting quote (a font-selector dropdown list) is weak alone, but the claim is a low-controversy design fact, not an extraordinary empirical claim, so encyclopedic + foundry sources suffice. Not outdated: Plex shipped 2017 replacing Helvetica, still IBM's corporate type and basis of the Carbon design system. Minor nuance (insufficient to refute): serif drew on Bodoni/Janson and sans on Franklin Gothic, so "from a common design" is a slight idealization — but that is precisely the standard definition of a type "superfamily" (related faces sharing a common design language across serif/sans/mono), and Wikipedia uses the term "superfamily" verbatim.
*Counter-source:* No credible contradicting source found. Closest qualification: Wikipedia notes the serif (Bodoni/Janson) and sans (Franklin Gothic) had different historical inspirations, but this does not contradict superfamily status, which is defined by shared design language, not identical skeletons.

**Supporting quote:** "One typeface will anchor the other(s). It['ll] help set the underlying tone of your experience, and act as a reference point for every element in your composition. "Majority of content" = "because this text represents a majority of your content."
