#### V175 `ac855e38` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The heading effect is contrastive/relative, not absolute: when only half the topics carried headings, signaled topics were summarized even more often than when all topics had headings, while unsignaled topics were summarized less often than when no topics had headings — i.e., a marked-vs-unmarked contrast amplifies what reads as important. This supports the 'hierarchy by contrast' principle (selective emphasis communicates; uniform emphasis dilutes)."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0361476X99910378 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "This effect was magnified when the text was only half signaled: Signaled topics were more likely to appear in a summary if only half the text topics were signaled than if all of the topics were signaled; however, unsignaled topics were less likely to appear in a summary if half of the text topics were signaled than if none of the text topics were signaled."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED. Source: Sanchez, Lorch & Lorch (2001), "Effects of Headings on Text Summarization," Contemporary Educational Psychology 26(3); PII S0361476X99910378 matches (received 1999, published 2001). Peer-reviewed, 3 experiments, replicated across college students and 6th/8th-graders. The supporting quote is VERBATIM from the abstract: "Signaled topics were more likely to appear in a summary if only half the text topics were signaled than if all of the topics were signaled; however, unsignaled topics were less likely to appear in a summary if half of the text topics were signaled than if none of the text topics were signaled." The factual core of the claim is therefore directly and exactly supported. The study is specifically about HEADINGS (per its title), so "headings" in the claim is accurate, not a loose reading of generic "signaling." The interpretation ("marked-vs-unmarked contrast amplifies what reads as important; selective emphasis communicates, uniform emphasis dilutes") is faithful to the data pattern — the heading effect is shown to be relative to the proportion of headed material, not absolute. Related Lorch/Sanchez papers (text recall, processing strategies) are consistent; no contradicting evidence found. CAVEAT (does not refute): the study measures topic inclusion in summaries (proxy for perceived topic-structure importance) and "contrast" = heading-present vs heading-absent, NOT contrast between typefaces — so it supports the hierarchy-by-contrast PRINCIPLE by analogy, not a literal test of font pairing. The claim is appropriately hedged as "supports the principle" and does not overstate this. Field is stable foundational comprehension psychology, so 2001 dating is not a weakness. This is a legitimate [STRONG] empirical anchor for the contrastive nature of typographic signaling, correctly framed.

#### V176 `aceda979` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "IBM Plex is a single unified superfamily spanning sans, serif, and monospace (plus a condensed sans) — one type system designed to cover heading, body, UI, and code roles from a common design, exemplifying superfamily pairing across functional differentiation rather than mixing unrelated families."

**Source:** https://www.ibm.com/plex/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Select Font IBM Plex Sans IBM Plex Sans Italic IBM Plex Mono IBM Plex Mono Italic IBM Plex Serif IBM Plex Serif Italic IBM Plex Sans Condensed IBM Plex Sans Condensed Italic"

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and accurate. Wikipedia (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM_Plex) explicitly defines IBM Plex as "an open source typeface superfamily conceptually designed and developed by Mike Abbink at IBM in collaboration with Bold Monday... to be used for all brand material across the company internationally," replacing Helvetica as IBM's corporate typeface in 2017. The family structure is confirmed verbatim: "The IBM Plex family comes in Sans, Serif, Mono, and Sans Condensed, all with Roman and true italics" — matching the claim's "sans, serif, and monospace (plus a condensed sans)." The "common design" / one-system framing is directly supported: "IBM Plex Mono is a monospaced typeface based on IBM Plex Sans," establishing shared design DNA across members. The source quote from ibm.com/plex (listing Plex Sans/Mono/Serif/Sans Condensed under one "IBM Plex" selector) supports the unified-system reading. The functional-role mapping (Mono=code, Sans=UI/body, Serif=editorial) is established type-system convention, accurate for Plex and consistent with its use as the basis of IBM's Carbon Design System. Source quality (primary ibm.com plus encyclopedic Wikipedia/Typographica/GitHub IBM/plex) matches the strength of this CONVENTION-grade factual claim; not outdated (2017 release, still IBM's active brand face). Minor nuance, not a refutation: Plex Serif is a transitional serif (inspired by Bodoni/Janson) coordinated as part of the system rather than literally derived from the Sans skeleton — but it is a designed-together member of the same superfamily, so "from a common design" as a coordinated type system is a fair characterization, not overreach.
*Counter-source:* No credible contradicting source found. WebSearch across Wikipedia, GitHub IBM/plex, Typographica, IBM Design Language, and Google Fonts uniformly describe Plex as a unified superfamily (Sans/Serif/Mono/Sans Condensed); none dispute or heavily qualify the claim.

#### V177 `ad04babd` — **upheld** · confidence: medium
*Claim under review:* "Controlled studies reviewed across many signal types (including headings and typographical cues) show signals reliably improve memory for the specific information they emphasize — concrete evidence that typographic emphasis/hierarchy aids retention of the cued content."

**Source:** https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF01320135 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Virtually all types of signals produce better memory for information they cue in a text, whereas memory for unsignaled information often is unaffected."

*Evidence:* Source verified: Lorch (1989), "Text-signaling devices and their effects on reading and memory processes," Educational Psychology Review 1(3), DOI 10.1007/BF01320135 — a peer-reviewed review of controlled experiments. The supporting quote is verbatim and accurate, and the review genuinely spans many signal types (headings, underlining, capitalization, overviews, summaries, signal words). The claim does NOT overreach: it is scoped to "memory for the specific information they emphasize / the cued content," which exactly matches the quote's caveat that unsignaled info is "often unaffected" (i.e., attention is redistributed, total recall is not raised). Skeptical cross-check found that newer evidence CORROBORATES rather than contradicts the scoped claim: meta-analyses report signaling retention g+ = 0.53 (Schneider/Beege et al.), d = .38 (Alpizar et al.), and a 2022 Educational Psychology Review meta-analysis (instructor- and learner-provided highlighting) found a memory benefit (g = 0.36) but NOT a comprehension benefit (g = 0.20). Dunlosky et al. (2013, 242 studies, ~169k participants) rated highlighting/underlining "low utility," but that concerns passive learner-generated highlighting and OVERALL learning, and even notes selective highlighting helps — a qualification, not a contradiction, of a claim already scoped to cued-item memory. Residual caveats for the skill: the effect is dose-dependent (Lorch's own heavy-signaling condition showed no benefit; emphasis must be selective), the benefit is to memory of the cued item specifically (not comprehension or total recall), and "hierarchy" is a mild stretch since signaling research tests emphasis cues, not visual heading-level hierarchy (though headings are one reviewed signal type). Frame as STRONG for "selective typographic emphasis aids memory of the emphasized content"; do NOT extend to "hierarchy improves comprehension."
*Counter-source:* Dunlosky et al. (2013) rated highlighting/underlining "low utility" for overall learning; 2022 Educational Psychology Review highlighting meta-analysis found memory benefit (g=0.36) but no comprehension benefit (g=0.20) — both qualify (selective use, cued-memory only, not comprehension) but do not refute the scoped claim, which modern signaling meta-analyses (retention g+=0.53) corroborate.

#### V178 `ad3a2a37` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The 'layer-cake' scanning pattern (eye-tracking): users fixate mostly on headings and subheadings, dropping into body text only occasionally, producing alternating horizontal stripes in gaze data. This is direct eye-tracking evidence that distinct headings structure how users scan a page."

**Source:** https://www.nngroup.com/articles/layer-cake-pattern-scanning/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "fixations made mostly on the page's headings and subheadings, with deliberate occasional fixations on the (body) text in between"

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported by the primary source and not an overreach. The supporting quote matches the NNG article verbatim ("fixations made mostly on the page's headings and subheadings, with deliberate occasional fixations on the (body) text in between"). NNG explicitly frames it as eye-tracking evidence: the pattern "looks roughly like a set of horizontal stripes" in "an eyetracking heatmap or gaze plot" — directly supporting the "alternating horizontal stripes in gaze data" wording. NNG names the cause that makes the claim's "distinct headings structure scanning" inference valid: the pattern occurs "when headings and subheadings visually stand out on the page and are descriptive," and is "by far the most effective way to scan pages." Source quality MATCHES claim strength: NNG (Nielsen Norman Group, article by Kara Pernice, Aug 4 2019, drawing on eye-tracking research from 2004 onward and the report "How People Read on the Web: The Eyetracking Evidence") is the authoritative lab for web eye-tracking — exactly the right primary source for an eye-tracking claim. Not outdated: scanning-pattern findings are stable perceptual/behavioral results, repeatedly reaffirmed (the related F-pattern was re-confirmed including on mobile). Not marketing/cherry-picked: research consultancy reporting its own studies, not a press release or benchmark. Contrary-evidence check found no credible dispute — the F-pattern (which appears when structure/headings are WEAK) is complementary, not contradictory, and actually reinforces that strong headings drive the more efficient layer-cake scan. ONE honest qualification for skill framing (does not refute the claim as worded): this is OBSERVATIONAL eye-tracking describing gaze behavior, not a randomized controlled experiment isolating headings as a variable or measuring comprehension/task-success gains. The claim is correctly scoped to "structure how users scan" (a description of where the eyes go), so it is accurate; the skill should NOT inflate it to controlled proof that headings improve comprehension. Within that scope, the claim is supportable as [STRONG] observational eye-tracking evidence that distinct headings shape scanning.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found. Searched for contradicting evidence; the most-cited adjacent finding (NNG F-shaped pattern, https://www.nngroup.com/articles/f-shaped-pattern-reading-web-content/) is complementary — it shows weak/absent headings produce a less efficient scan, reinforcing rather than refuting the layer-cake claim.

#### V179 `ad52220e` — **REFUTED** · confidence: medium
*Claim under review:* "Clear typographic hierarchy aids scanning effectiveness: NNG calls the layer-cake pattern the most effective scanning behavior and links it to users successfully finding target information when present. Supports the [STRONG] 'hierarchy-aids-scanning' claim (distinct from which-fonts-to-pair conventions)."

**Source:** https://www.nngroup.com/articles/layer-cake-pattern-scanning/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "by far the most effective way to scan pages ... most of the time, it ensures that users will find the information they are looking for (if it is present on the page)"

*Evidence:* Quote verified verbatim at the primary source (nngroup.com/articles/layer-cake-pattern-scanning): "Aside from reading every (or almost every) word, the layer-cake pattern is by far the most effective way to scan pages" and "most of the time, it ensures that users will find the information they are looking for (if it is present on the page)." So the DESCRIPTIVE part of the claim (NNG calls layer-cake most effective + links it to finding info) is accurate, not an overreach. REFUTATION is on the [STRONG] tag, not the facts. The skill's taxonomy defines [STRONG]=peer-reviewed/controlled research and [CONVENTION]=best practice from a NAMED authority. NNG is a named usability authority -> [CONVENTION]-grade. The layer-cake article is observational eyetracking: no sample size, no statistics, no hypothesis test, not peer-reviewed; the underlying 300-person/1.5M-fixation dataset is proprietary NNG lab work, not independently reviewed; and the "link to finding information" is NNG's stated interpretation/mechanism, not a measured controlled task-success outcome with controls. Hanging a [STRONG] label on this single NNG blog article is a wrong-grade-source-for-strong-claim mismatch. Notably the underlying proposition (headings/hierarchy aid scanning, search, retrieval) IS legitimately [STRONG]-supportable -- but via independent peer-reviewed work the claim does NOT cite (Hartley & Trueman, "The effects of headings in text on recall, search and retrieval"; Lorch et al. on headings + search strategies with eye-fixation evidence; Prinz-Weiss 2023, Applied Cognitive Psychology). No source contradicts the proposition; the defect is purely that the [STRONG] strength tag is attributed to a [CONVENTION]-grade source. Fix: keep the proposition, tag NNG layer-cake as [CONVENTION]/observational, and cite the controlled headings literature for the [STRONG] claim.
*Counter-source:* Peer-reviewed controlled evidence that would correctly carry the [STRONG] tag (and which the claim omits): Hartley & Trueman, "The effects of headings in text on recall, search and retrieval"; Lorch, Lorch et al., "Effects of headings and familiarity with a text on strategies for searching a text" (eye-fixation evidence); Prinz-Weiss et al. 2023, "Caption it! The impact of headings on learning from texts," Applied Cognitive Psychology. These show headings aid recall/search/retrieval under controlled designs. The cited NNG layer-cake article itself is observational/[CONVENTION]-grade, not the basis for [STRONG].

#### V180 `ad6ca009` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The 'layer-cake' scanning pattern (eye-tracking): users fixate mostly on headings and subheadings, dropping into body text only occasionally, producing alternating horizontal stripes in gaze data. This is direct eye-tracking evidence that distinct headings structure how users scan a page."

**Source:** https://www.nngroup.com/articles/layer-cake-pattern-scanning/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "fixations made mostly on the page's headings and subheadings, with deliberate occasional fixations on the (body) text in between"

*Evidence:* Claim is faithfully supported by the primary source and corroborated by independent controlled research. (1) DESCRIPTIVE PART verbatim-supported: NN/g (https://www.nngroup.com/articles/layer-cake-pattern-scanning/) states the layer-cake is "fixations made mostly on the page's headings and subheadings, with deliberate occasional fixations on the (body) text in between," forming "horizontal stripes and blank spaces between them, resembling a layer cake" — matching the claim's wording. (2) CAUSAL/INTERPRETIVE PART is NN/g's own framing, not an overreach: NN/g attributes the pattern to subheadings that "visually stand out" and are "descriptive," calling it "by far the most effective way to scan pages." So "eye-tracking evidence that distinct headings structure scanning" reports the source's conclusion. (3) Surviving adversarial caveat: NN/g's layer-cake itself is OBSERVATIONAL (gaze plots, small samples — e.g., 9 participants on one site, no control/significance testing), so on its own it shows where users LOOK, not a clean causal proof. BUT this gap is independently closed by a CONTROLLED eye-tracking study, Hyona & Lorch (2004), n=66, which found topic headings aid comprehension and topic-memory retention — convergent causal support. (4) No credible contradicting source: targeted searches for criticism/overstatement of the layer-cake found nothing disputing it; reaffirmed across multiple NN/g articles and third-party summaries. (5) Scoping note (does not refute the claim as written): "distinct headings" here means visually-standout + descriptive headings, achievable via weight/size/color within ONE family — this is NOT evidence for font PAIRING (a second typeface). The claim makes no pairing assertion, so it is clean, but the skill must not stretch it into a pairing justification. Strength labeling for the skill: treat the scanning pattern as direct-but-observational eye-tracking, and the headings-aid-comprehension causal claim as [STRONG] via Hyona & Lorch 2004; NN/g is industry research, not peer-reviewed RCT.
*Counter-source:* No source refutes the claim. The strongest qualifier is that NN/g's layer-cake data is observational (small samples, no control conditions), but the causal interpretation is independently corroborated by the controlled study Hyona & Lorch (2004, n=66) on topic headings aiding comprehension/memory.

#### V181 `ad869a83` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The heading effect is contrastive/relative, not absolute: when only half the topics carried headings, signaled topics were summarized even more often than when all topics had headings, while unsignaled topics were summarized less often than when no topics had headings — i.e., a marked-vs-unmarked contrast amplifies what reads as important. This supports the 'hierarchy by contrast' principle (selective emphasis communicates; uniform emphasis dilutes)."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0361476X99910378 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "This effect was magnified when the text was only half signaled: Signaled topics were more likely to appear in a summary if only half the text topics were signaled than if all of the topics were signaled; however, unsignaled topics were less likely to appear in a summary if half of the text topics were signaled than if none of the text topics were signaled."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED — claim holds. Source is real and peer-reviewed: "Effects of Headings on Text Summarization" by Lorch, Lorch, Ritchey, McGovern & Coleman, Contemporary Educational Psychology (S0361476X99910378). The signaling device in this study IS headings, so the claim's wording ("headings") matches the study — no overreach. The supporting quote is verbatim-accurate: two independent search snippets reproduced it word-for-word ("signaled topics were more likely to appear in a summary if only half the text topics were signaled than if all... unsignaled topics were less likely... if half... than if none"). The contrastive/relative interpretation matches the authors' own theory: a companion result ("unsignaled content recalled more poorly for the half-signaled text than for the unsignaled text, suggesting that signals direct processing") shows the literature treats signaling as resource-allocational — marking some items pulls processing toward them at the cost of unmarked items, i.e. "selective emphasis communicates; uniform emphasis dilutes." Source quality (controlled study, college + 6th/8th-grade participants) matches the claim's [STRONG] strength. Not outdated — foundational cognitive psychology of reading, still cited (cf. Lorch's "Text-signaling devices" review, Educ Psych Review). One honest caveat, not a refutation: the study is about text/semantic signaling and summarization behavior, not typographic contrast per se; the claim bridges to the "hierarchy by contrast" typography principle via the appropriately hedged word "supports," keeping it within bounds. No credible contradicting source found.

#### V182 `adaf49f0` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Brown's functional-differentiation thesis: every compositional 'chunk' is a job (prose to read, headings to catch attention, navigation, data to gather quickly), and you pick the face that fits that job — e.g. a geometric sans to 'clearly and quietly' supply navigation/structured information, an editorial serif (FF Meta Serif) for a 'conversational tone, while also conveying authority' in critique/body text. Typeface signals the kind of content. [CONVENTION — Tim Brown]"

**Source:** https://blog.typekit.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/combiningtypefaces.pdf (secondary)
**Supporting quote:** "Compositional chunks are like job openings ... Is its purpose to catch and direct readers' attention? Is it informational, meant for readers to pore over and reference? Is its purpose to help people navigate, or gather data quickly? Whatever [you]'re trying to do, a typeface was made for that purpose. ... Geometric sans serif typefaces are great for this job ... Neutral and balanced, it clearly and quietly supplies structured information in the form of navigation"

*Evidence:* Every load-bearing element of the claim is verified VERBATIM in the primary source — Tim Brown's "Combining Typefaces: A Pocket Guide" (the same PDF cited, blog.typekit.com, 2016). I downloaded the 10.6MB PDF, zlib-decompressed its 178 content streams, and reconstructed the text.

(1) Chunks-as-jobs thesis: "Compositional chunks are like job openings, and just as in the work world, the better you understand the job you need done, the more successfully you'll fill the position." And the four jobs the claim lists map exactly: "Is the chunk you're typesetting supposed to be read comfortably? Or is its purpose to catch and direct readers' attention? Is it informational, meant for readers to pore over and reference? Is its purpose to help people navigate, or gather data quickly? Whatever it is you're trying to do, a typeface was made for that purpose." (prose-to-read / catch attention / navigate / gather data — all four present).

(2) Geometric sans for navigation: "Geometric sans serif typefaces are great for this job, and Soleil is no exception. Neutral and balanced, it clearly and quietly supplies structured information in the form of navigation, film titles and metadata." — matches "clearly and quietly" + "structured information" exactly.

(3) FF Meta Serif (the part NOT covered by the claim's attached supporting quote, which I specifically went to check): "FF Meta Serif strikes a welcoming, conversational tone, while also conveying authority about the subject matter." And "Critiques and interviews are set in FF Meta Serif, a contemporary serif worthy of representing thoughtful, articulate reviews." — confirms editorial serif / "conversational tone, while also conveying authority" / critique-body use, verbatim.

(4) "Typeface signals the kind of content" is a fair paraphrase of the conclusion: "each typeface appropriately addresses the jobs of its chunks of text."

Adversarial checks all pass: (a) No overreach — even the FF Meta Serif phrasing not in the supplied snippet checks out word-for-word. (b) Nothing to contradict — the claim is correctly tagged [CONVENTION], i.e. named-expert craft guidance, NOT an empirical legibility claim, so it does not collide with the prior "no inherent serif/sans advantage" finding. (c) Source quality EXCEEDS the bar: the cited document is the PRIMARY source authored by the named authority (Tim Brown, Adobe Typekit's type lead), not secondary. (d) Not outdated — a 2016 craft convention about choosing a face per content-job; conventions of this kind don't expire and remain Brown's stated position. (e) Educational guide, not a benchmark or press release.

Minor non-fatal caveat to carry into SKILL.md: the source's concrete example uses Soleil (geometric sans) + FF Meta Serif within ONE case study (Art of the Title), so these are illustrative within a worked case, not a universal "always use geometric sans for nav" rule — but the claim's "e.g." correctly frames them as examples, so no overreach.

#### V183 `ae099e88` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "In a controlled experiment, college students who read a 12-topic expository text WITH headings remembered the text's topics and their organizational structure better than students who read the identical text WITHOUT headings — controlled evidence that structural signals (headings) aid recall of content organization, the comprehension/scanning side of typographic hierarchy."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0361476X00910567 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "participants who received training and/or read the text with headings remembered text topics and their organization better"

*Evidence:* VERIFIED against primary source. The article (ScienceDirect PII S0361476X00910567 = PubMed 11414729) is Sanchez, R.P., Lorch, E.P., & Lorch, R.F. Jr. (2001), "Effects of Headings on Text Processing Strategies," Contemporary Educational Psychology 26(3), 418-428. Verbatim abstract: "College students read and recalled a 12-topic expository text... Half of the participants read a text containing headings before every subsection; the other half read the same text without headings. The results were that participants who received training and/or read the text with headings remembered text topics and their organization better than participants who received no training and read the text without headings." Every concrete element of the claim is confirmed: controlled experiment, college students, 12-topic expository text, identical text with vs without headings, better memory for topics AND their organization. Source quality is sufficient — peer-reviewed primary experimental study, appropriate for a STRONG tag. Not outdated (stable, replicated text-signaling literature; Lorch corpus, Meyer structure-strategy). Not marketing. One honest caveat: it is a 2x2 design (training x headings) and the claim collapses it to the headings factor — but the abstract's own "and/or" phrasing means the well-performing set was {headings-only, training-only, both} vs the worst {neither}, so headings alone (untrained readers) sufficed to beat the no-headings baseline; the headings effect stands on the authors' own characterization. Two framing notes (not refutations): the measured outcome was free recall, not literal "scanning" (the claim hedges "comprehension/scanning," and recall/comprehension is exactly what was measured); and "headings" are structural section labels, so invoking this for "typographic hierarchy" is a mild but defensible extrapolation since the study did not manipulate font/weight/size contrast.
*Counter-source:* No credible source refutes this specific finding. Broader literature (e.g., signaling effects moderated by prior knowledge/reader skill) qualifies the GENERALIZATION of heading benefits but does not contradict this study's result. Full-text stats were inaccessible (ScienceDirect 403), so the independent significance of the headings main effect rests on the authors' verbatim abstract ("and/or read the text with headings"), which credits headings explicitly.

**Source:** https://blog.typekit.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/04/combiningtypefaces.pdf (secondary)

*Evidence:* Attribution and quote both verified. Independent web search confirms "Combining Typefaces: A Pocket Guide" is authored by Tim Brown (Five Simple Steps / Typekit blog) and returned the exact passage: compatibility and similarity "are not the same thing," with the square/circle example where both are "strict geometric forms that have more in common with one another than either has with a more calligraphic shape." This directly supports the claim that pairing works on underlying COMPATIBILITY (shared structure) not surface SIMILARITY, and that shapes can look different yet share structure. The [CONVENTION — Tim Brown] tag is correctly applied (named-authority craft, not controlled research). The prompt marks the source "(secondary)," but it is in fact the PRIMARY source — Brown's own guide — which strengthens sourcing. No contrary evidence found; typography craft of this kind is not date-sensitive. One minor interpretive bridge: the claim links Brown's compatibility idea to the Lupton/Bringhurst "pair by contrast, not near-sameness" principle. Brown's actual emphasis is subtler (different YET compatible via shared structure), but the claim preserves that nuance ("share structure") rather than misreading it — within CONVENTION latitude, not an overreach that warrants refutation.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found. Searches confirmed rather than disputed the attribution; the only nuance is that the claim's bridge to the Lupton/Bringhurst "pair by contrast" principle is a reasonable synthesis, not a contradiction.
