**Source:** https://fonts.google.com/specimen/Atkinson+Hyperlegible (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "special design principles to differentiate characters and make each one unique"

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported by the primary source and correctly scoped to DESIGN RATIONALE (not proven efficacy). Braille Institute's specimen (brailleinstitute.org/freefont) names disambiguation as the explicit organizing principle with labeled categories: "Unambiguous Letterforms" (B8 O0), "Clear Uprights" (1Iil), "Distinct Pairs" (EF pq), "Open Counters" (ebgs), "Spurs and Tails" (gmnr), framed by "For low-vision readers, certain letters and numbers can be hard to distinguish from one another." Every specific pair in the claim (B/8, O/0, 1/I/i/l, p/q) is confirmed verbatim. The supporting quote ("special design principles to differentiate characters and make each one unique") matches "core design goal is letterform disambiguation… making each character unique." Corroborated by PRINT Magazine, Dezeen, and Google Material Design ("rejects letterform norms that valorize uniformity… embraces letterform distinction"). Font dated 2019, still current (Atkinson Hyperlegible Next extends it) — not outdated. Foundry-origin sourcing is correct because the claim is ABOUT the designer's stated intent, so it is the appropriate primary source, not marketing fluff. CAVEAT (does not refute): supports topic (2) only as [CONVENTION]-level design rationale; the EFFICACY question (does it measurably cut reading errors/speed) has weak/mixed evidence — type designers note "scientific evidence… is minimal." So the claim is valid as worded but must not be elevated to [STRONG] proof of error reduction.
*Counter-source:* Efficacy is weak/mixed (not a refutation of the design-rationale claim): type designer commentary (e.g., alexjohnlucas.com/type/truth) and disabilityworld.org note scientific evidence that disambiguated/accessibility fonts measurably improve reading speed is minimal; supports keeping this as [CONVENTION] design rationale, not [STRONG] proven error reduction.

#### V221 `a92f036f` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The 'visual span' — the number of letters that can be recognized in a single glance/fixation — imposes a fundamental upper limit on reading speed; it measures ~10+ letters in central vision and shrinks to ~1.7 letters at 15deg eccentricity. This is direct vision-science support that glance-legibility of individual characters bounds reading throughput, the regime in which chart/axis/table text is read."

**Source:** https://legge.psych.umn.edu/sites/legge.psych.umn.edu/files/2020-08/psychophysics_of_reading._xx._linking_letter_recognition_to_reading_speed_in_central_and_peripheral_vision_legge_mansfield_chung_2001.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "We propose that the size of the visual span - the number of letters recognizable in a glance - imposes a fundamental limit on reading speed... The size of the visual span decreased from at least 10 letters in central vision to 1.7 letters at 15deg eccentricity"

*Evidence:* VERIFIED — verbatim support from a top-tier primary source. The PubMed abstract for Legge, Mansfield & Chung (2001), Vision Research (PMID 11248262) reads word-for-word: "the size of the visual span - the number of letters recognizable in a glance - imposes a fundamental limit on reading speed" and "visual span decreased from at least 10 letters in central vision to 1.7 letters at 15 degrees eccentricity." Both the "fundamental limit on reading speed" framing and the two cited numbers (10 central, 1.7 at 15deg) are quoted exactly, not inflated. Source quality matches a [STRONG] tag: peer-reviewed Vision Research; Gordon Legge is the leading authority on the psychophysics of reading (paper #XX in his named series). The theory is reinforced, not overturned, by later controlled work (Kwon/Legge/Dubbels 2007; the JOV "case for the visual span as a sensory bottleneck in reading"). Disconfirming searches surfaced only methodological criticism of a DIFFERENT paradigm — visual-attention-span dyslexia studies using linguistic stimuli (phonological/rapid-naming confounds) — not the central-vs-peripheral visual span finding in normal vision, so the cited values and core claim stand. ONE CAVEAT (qualification, not refutation): the experiments measured continuous reading (RSVP/sentence reading, words/min), and the literature acknowledges reading speed also depends on cognitive/lexical factors; the claim's tail "the regime in which chart/axis/table text is read" is a mild extrapolation since chart labels are isolated short strings glanced at, not continuous prose. But the mechanism (single-fixation letter-recognition capacity causally bounding reading throughput — the paper's literal title "Linking letter recognition to reading speed") applies at least as well to glancing at a label, so the extrapolation is defensible if phrased as "character legibility bounds reading throughput" rather than a literal measurement of chart-label glancing.
*Counter-source:* Disconfirming search returned only criticism of a separate visual-attention-span/dyslexia paradigm (linguistic-stimulus and phonological-retrieval confounds, e.g. ScienceDirect S0010945219302928 and PMC3617167), which targets VAS dyslexia studies, not Legge's central-vs-peripheral visual-span finding in normal vision; no source disputes the cited 10-letter / 1.7-letter values or the "fundamental limit" framing.

#### V222 `a96954d5` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[STRONG] Text is read at maximum (fluent) speed across a 10-fold 'fluent range' of angular x-height from approximately 0.2° to 2°, corresponding to physical x-heights of 1.4 mm (≈4 pt) to 14 mm (≈40 pt) at 40 cm. The speed-vs-size curve is a broad plateau with a sharp decline below the CPS and only a gradual decline for very large print — so once text clears the floor, making it bigger yields no reading-speed gain."

**Source:** https://www.researchgate.net/publication/51558557_Does_Print_Size_Matter_for_Reading_A_Review_of_Findings_from_Vision_Science_and_Typography (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "There is a 10-fold range of print size from approximately 0.2- to 2- for which people with normal vision can achieve maximum reading speed. This range corresponds to x-heights from 1.4 mm (4 points) to 14 mm (40 points) at a reading distance of 40 cm. We refer to this as the ﬂuent range of print size... the speed vs. print size curves in Figure 2 demonstrate a broad plateau for intermediate character sizes for which reading speed is fairly constant, a sharp decline in reading speeds for smaller characters, and a more gradual decline for very large characters."

*Evidence:* Claim is faithfully and near-verbatim supported by the cited primary peer-reviewed source: Legge & Bigelow (2011), "Does Print Size Matter for Reading?", Journal of Vision 11(5):8 (jov.arvojournals.org/article.aspx?articleid=2191906). The source states the "fluent range" spans a 10-fold range of angular x-height ~0.2 to 2 degrees = x-heights 1.4 mm (4 pt) to 14 mm (40 pt) at 40 cm, with a broad plateau of constant reading speed, sharp decline for smaller print, gradual decline for very large print. The claim's "sharp decline below the CPS" is consistent with the established MNREAD/Critical Print Size framework (CPS = smallest print supporting maximum reading speed; above it, bigger gives no speed gain) — independently confirmed by MNREAD literature (mnread.umn.edu/reading-measures; PMC4961000). No credible contradicting source found; the PNAS 2020 paper reconciles rather than refutes. Source quality matches the [STRONG] tag (peer-reviewed, primary, authoritative authors Legge & Bigelow), and the finding is foundational/non-outdated. Only minor caveat (not refuting): "4 pt/40 pt" is x-height-in-points, not font size, but the claim faithfully reproduces the paper's own wording.

#### V223 `a9822cb6` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Controlled peripheral-vision study: crowded digits read in the visual periphery are more legible when given a simpler/shorter digit skeleton (lower morphological complexity), i.e. simplicity aids glance/peripheral numeral legibility rather than added detail."

**Source:** https://dl.designresearchsociety.org/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1642&context=drs-conference-papers (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Crowded digits presented in the periphery of the visual field generally profit from a short morphological skeleton."

*Evidence:* Claim is faithfully supported. Source = Beier, Bernard & Castet (2018), "Numeral Legibility and Visual Complexity," DRS 2018 peer-reviewed conference proceedings. Sofie Beier is a named legibility researcher (cited in the skill criteria); Castet/Bernard are vision scientists studying peripheral vision/central-field loss. It is a CONTROLLED peripheral-vision study: three complexity-varied versions of digits 1-9, other physical params held constant, identified in random 3-digit strings in the peripheral visual field. Verbatim conclusion (confirmed across multiple independent sources): "Crowded digits presented in the periphery of the visual field generally profit from a short morphological skeleton. A simple morphological digit skeleton facilitates greater peripheral legibility than more complex skeletons by increasing its own legibility and increasing the legibility of adjacent digit skeletons." The claim ("simpler/shorter skeleton aids peripheral numeral legibility rather than added detail") restates this accurately and is correctly bounded to crowded peripheral conditions. Adversarial search surfaced only a compatible caveat (Beier & Larson 2010; closed-counter work): simplification must not produce confusable shapes — added detail helps when it prevents misreading (two-storey 'a', Johnston's looped 'l'). That is a disambiguation point, not a contradiction, and the claim does not overreach into "simpler is always better." Minor non-refuting nuance: claim says "glance/peripheral," slightly conflating brief-foveal glance with off-center peripheral; the study is strictly peripheral, but "peripheral" is the load-bearing scope and is stated correctly. Qualifies as [STRONG].
*Counter-source:* Beier & Larson (2010) and Beier/Oderkerk closed-counter work note simplification can hurt when it creates confusable shapes (added complexity aids disambiguation) — a compatible caveat, not a contradiction of the peripheral-crowded-digit finding.

**Supporting quote:** "The digits '3' and '9' should benefit from open apertures"

The paper is Beier & Bernard, "Numeral Legibility and Visual Complexity," DRS 2018 (peer-reviewed conference; Sofie Beier is a named [STRONG] authority in the brief). It is an empirical identification experiment: observers identified three complexity-varied versions of digits 1-9 in random three-digit strings shown in the peripheral visual field, other physical parameters controlled. The supporting quote "the digits '3' and '9' should benefit from open apertures" is verbatim from the study's results and sits alongside parallel measured recommendations for '1' (narrow, no bottom crossbar) and '7' (straight leg, no serif). The aperture conclusion is backed by measured data: "for the digits '3' and '9', the results showed evidence supporting the hypothesis that closed apertures adversely impacted performance," so it is a finding, not opinion. No contradicting source found; corroborated by Beier 2022 "Closed letter counters impair recognition" (ScienceDirect). Only caveat (not refuting): the directly measured condition is PERIPHERAL identification (motivated by crowding/central-field loss), so the claim's "small" reading is a reasonable but indirect extrapolation from "peripheral" — the skill should attribute the hard evidence to peripheral/crowded identification specifically.
*Counter-source:* None found. Searches surfaced only corroborating evidence (Beier 2022, "Closed letter counters impair recognition," ScienceDirect S0003687022000321) supporting the open-aperture principle; no credible source disputes it.

#### V226 `aa5119c0` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[STRONG] Controlled experiment: letters with CLOSED apertures (counters) are recognized less accurately than the same letters with OPEN apertures — direct empirical support for the rule that data-viz faces should have open apertures for small/glance legibility."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687022000321 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The findings showed that participants had more trouble identifying the letter if it was set in a font variation with closed apertures."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED, not refuted. The source is Beier & Oderkerk (2022), "Closed letter counters impair recognition," Applied Ergonomics (PubMed 35217404; primary, peer-reviewed; Sofie Beier is the named authority in this subfield). The paper's title states the claim's conclusion directly. It is a controlled within-subjects psychophysical experiment: one purpose-built serif font (Pyke), the aperture letters a/c/e/r/s/t/f drawn in three conditions (Open/Medium/Closed) with the 10 non-aperture letters (d,g,h,k,m,n,o,p,u,y) held identical as a control, presented flanked and unflanked in the parafovea at 2deg eccentricity. Finding: closed apertures recognized significantly less accurately than open — for flanked letters closed < open; for unflanked letters closed < both medium and open. So the claim's specific comparison (closed < open) holds in BOTH conditions. The supporting quote ("more trouble identifying the letter ... with closed apertures") matches verbatim. I searched specifically for disputes, replication critiques, and "myth" framings (two targeted searches) and found NO credible source contradicting or heavily qualifying it; the literature and the study's own framing consistently confirm the open-aperture advice. Three minor caveats, none refuting: (1) the parenthetical "(counters)" conflates aperture vs counter, but the paper's own title uses "counters" for the closed-aperture manipulation, so the claim mirrors its source; (2) single test font / 7 letters / parafoveal crowding bounds generalization, yet parafoveal+crowded reading is a faithful model of glance legibility, supporting the data-viz inference rather than weakening it; (3) exact participant N not extractable from abstract/PDF (no PDF-text tooling available), but controlled-experiment status is unambiguous. Source quality matches the [STRONG] tag; claim is current (2022) and correctly framed.
*Counter-source:* None found. Targeted searches for criticism, null results, or replication failure returned only sources that corroborate the open-aperture finding; no credible contradicting source exists.

#### V229 `ab0de7d0` — **upheld** · confidence: high

**Supporting quote:** "The Text version has higher x-height, higher stroke contrast, simplified letter constructions, open counters, and loose spacing."

*Evidence:* The verbatim quote is confirmed in the source (Peter Bilak, Typotheque, "A sans for small text. And another for large"), describing the Zed Text vs. Zed Display optical family. The article explicitly ties the attributes to glance legibility: "open counters to prevent confusion, allowing a reader to accurately decipher text at a glance," "loose spacing is probably the most important attribute of a text typeface," and "open apertures in 'a','c','e','r','s' increase recognition for glance-like reading." All four attributes the claim asserts (higher x-height, simplified constructions, open counters, loose spacing) appear in the quote. The claim selectively omits the quote's fifth item, "higher stroke contrast" — but that omission strengthens rather than weakens it: standard optical-sizing convention holds that SMALL sizes use LOWER, more even contrast (independent corroboration: Just Another Foundry "Size-specific adjustments," design-encyclopedia "Optical Size," Wikipedia "Legibility" — "thickened their thinnest strokes," "more consistent stroke widths," "higher x-height... larger counters... increased spacing... simplified fine detail"). The four retained criteria are mainstream optical-sizing/glance-legibility principles, independently corroborated, not idiosyncratic to one foundry. Source quality (respected foundry citing legibility research + convergent independent sources + Beier-style aperture rationale) matches the claim's modest strength ("grounds concrete design criteria"). Caveat for the skill: tag [CONVENTION], not [STRONG]; and do not import "higher stroke contrast" as a chart-text rule since general small-text convention favors lower contrast.
*Counter-source:* https://justanotherfoundry.com/size-specific-adjustments-to-type-designs ; https://design-encyclopedia.com/?T=Optical+Size ; https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Legibility — these corroborate the four retained attributes but contradict the omitted "higher stroke contrast" (small optical sizes use lower/even contrast), which the claim already excludes.

#### V231 `ab914436` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The CSS value tabular-nums (OpenType tnum) selects figures that are all the same width, allowing numbers to be easily aligned like in tables — the mechanical basis for using tabular figures in columns of numbers, axes, and data tables."

**Source:** https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/font-variant-numeric (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "tabular-nums ... activating the set of figures where numbers are all of the same size, allowing them to be easily aligned like in tables"

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and more precise than the MDN quote. MDN says tabular-nums activates "figures where numbers are all of the same size, allowing them to be easily aligned like in tables (tnum)." The claim restates as "all the same width" — which the authoritative Microsoft OpenType spec confirms verbatim: tnum "Replaces figure glyphs set on proportional widths with corresponding glyphs set on uniform (tabular) widths" (GSUB lookup type 1). So "same width" is the technically correct property (MDN's "same size" is the loose wording); the claim tightens, not overreaches. The extension — "mechanical basis for tabular figures in columns/axes/tables" — is the direct consequence of equal advance widths (digits occupy identical horizontal space, so they stack vertically in columns), confirmed by Type Network, PreussType, and Adobe Fonts docs. Claim correctly scopes tnum to the WIDTH axis only, not conflating it with the lining-vs-oldstyle (lnum/onum) HEIGHT axis. This is a mechanical/technical font-feature fact, not an empirical reading-speed claim, so MDN (secondary) + OpenType spec (primary, authoritative) exceeds the required source quality. Stable feature for decades; not outdated, not marketing. No credible source disputes or qualifies the equal-width mechanism. Tag: [CONVENTION]/technical-fact, properly sourced.
*Counter-source:* None found. Sought disconfirming sources across Microsoft Learn, PreussType, Type Network, Adobe Fonts; all corroborate that tabular figures have uniform advance width enabling column alignment. The only nit — MDN's "size" vs claim's "width" — resolves in the claim's favor per the OpenType spec.

#### V232 `abb1c401` — **upheld** · confidence: high
