**Supporting quote:** "Tabular figures ... all occupy identical horizontal spaces ... numbers have to align vertically ... spreadsheets, financial data, math, and things like lists of dates or phone numbers ... Proportional figures ... occupy horizontal spaces that are consistent with their design ... can't be used for any application that requires numbers to line up in vertical columns"

*Evidence:* Claim is definitional OpenType typography, faithfully paraphrased from the source quote and independently corroborated by multiple authorities. Type Network states tabular figures "all occupy identical horizontal spaces" for vertical alignment (tables, financial data, dates, phone numbers), while proportional figures "can't be used for any application that requires numbers to line up in vertical columns." Independent corroboration: Fonts.com/Fontology "Proportional vs. Tabular Figures" (proportional numerals "won't align in vertical columns... should not be used for tables"); Butterick's Practical Typography; Monotype; CreativePro (Ilene Strizver); MDN font-variant-numeric / CSS tabular-nums. No contradicting source found. This is a [CONVENTION]-strength definitional fact about glyph advance widths (OpenType tnum vs pnum), not an empirical perception claim, so no peer-reviewed study is required; source quality matches claim strength. OpenType figure mechanics are stable, not outdated. Only minor nuance: "cannot be used" is slightly absolute (proportional figures can be placed in a column but won't align) — but the sources use the same phrasing and the intent (alignment fails → unsuitable) is correct. Consistent with the charting skill's tabular-figures-for-number-columns rule.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source exists; searches for contradicting evidence returned only sources that agree (Fonts.com, Butterick, Monotype, MDN, CreativePro). Closest "qualification" is semantic: proportional figures can be physically placed in columns but simply fail to align — which the claim and all sources already convey.

#### V233 `abc43e23` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[STRONG] Controlled experiment: letters with CLOSED apertures (counters) are recognized less accurately than the same letters with OPEN apertures — direct empirical support for the rule that data-viz faces should have open apertures for small/glance legibility."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687022000321 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The findings showed that participants had more trouble identifying the letter if it was set in a font variation with closed apertures."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED — claim stands. Source is primary, peer-reviewed, controlled: Beier, S. & Oderkerk, C.A.T. (2022), "Closed letter counters impair recognition," Applied Ergonomics 101, 103709 (PII S0003687022000321; PubMed 35217404), by Sofie Beier — the exact named researcher the criteria request. Verbatim abstract confirms the supporting quote and the design: custom serif font Pyke, letters a/c/e/r/s/t/f rendered in Open/Medium/Closed aperture variants, single-letter recognition with and without flankers in the parafovea at 2° eccentricity. Result: closed apertures impaired identification accuracy (for unflanked letters, closed was worse than BOTH medium and open; for flanked, closed worse than open). The paper's own title — "Closed letter counters impair recognition" — directly endorses the claim. No contradicting evidence found; the study explicitly confirms long-standing legibility advice and Beier's broader body of work. Caveats that qualify but do NOT refute: (1) the claim's parenthetical equating apertures with counters is technically loose (aperture = the opening; counter = the enclosed space), but mirrors the paper's own conflation since closing the aperture closes the counter; (2) single typeface, one serif, 7 letters, 2° parafoveal single-letter task — so the core finding "closed apertures reduce recognition accuracy" is directly proven, while the application phrasing "data-viz [sans] faces should have open apertures for glance legibility" is reasonable convention-grade extrapolation layered on a strong core. The 2° parafoveal paradigm actually supports the glance-legibility use case. Source quality matches the [STRONG] tag; claim is current (2022) and accurate.
*Counter-source:* No credible contradicting source found. Searched for disputes/qualifications; the only limitations are scope (single serif typeface Pyke, 7 letters, 2° parafoveal task) which the study itself implies but which do not contradict the direction of the effect. PubMed 35217404 and the Elsevier Pure abstract both confirm the finding.

#### V234 `ac491395` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Open apertures on the letters a, c, e, r and s increase letter recognition for glance-like (brief, peripheral) reading — directly supporting letterform disambiguation as a driver of small-size legibility. Attributed to a peer-reviewed study (Beier & Oderkerk, 2021)."

**Source:** https://www.typotheque.com/articles/sans-for-small-text-sizes (secondary)
**Supporting quote:** "This tenet was confirmed in a study by Sofie Beier and Chiron Oderkerk from 2021, which concluded that open apertures in the letters 'a', 'c', 'e', 'r' and 's' increase recognition for glance-like reading."

*Evidence:* The underlying study is real and peer-reviewed, and the primary source confirms the claim's substance. Citation = Beier, S., & Oderkerk, C. A. T. (2022), "Closed letter counters impair recognition," Applied Ergonomics 101:103709 (PMID 35217404, DOI 10.1016/j.apergo.2022.103709). It tested aperture openness in seven letters (a, c, e, r, s, t, f) using the serif font Pyke in Open/Medium/Closed variants, presenting letters flanked/unflanked in the parafovea at 2 deg eccentricity. Conclusion: closed apertures impair recognition (open apertures aid it) for both flanked and unflanked letters — exactly the claim's direction and conditions ("brief, peripheral"). Two correctable defects, neither refuting the substance: (1) YEAR is 2022, not 2021 — the typotheque secondary source mis-dated it, and the claim inherited the error; (2) the study covered SEVEN letters, so the claim's "a, c, e, r, s" is an accurate but incomplete subset (drops t, f). "Glance-like reading" is a fair gloss for the parafoveal crowding paradigm; Beier uses that framing elsewhere. Consistent with her wider corpus (Oderkerk & Beier 2022, "Fonts of wider letter shapes improve letter recognition"). No credible contradicting evidence found; source quality (Applied Ergonomics, peer-reviewed) exceeds the claim's strength. For the skill: cite as Beier & Oderkerk (2022), tag [STRONG], and note all seven letters.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found. The only correction is to the citation: PubMed PMID 35217404 / ScienceDirect S0003687022000321 establishes the year as 2022 (not 2021) and the letter set as a, c, e, r, s, t, f (not just five).

#### V235 `ad14b3f5` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Open apertures on the letters a, c, e, r and s increase letter recognition for glance-like (brief, peripheral) reading — directly supporting letterform disambiguation as a driver of small-size legibility. Attributed to a peer-reviewed study (Beier & Oderkerk, 2021)."

**Source:** https://www.typotheque.com/articles/sans-for-small-text-sizes (secondary)
**Supporting quote:** "This tenet was confirmed in a study by Sofie Beier and Chiron Oderkerk from 2021, which concluded that open apertures in the letters 'a', 'c', 'e', 'r' and 's' increase recognition for glance-like reading."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported by a real peer-reviewed primary source, with two minor citation corrections needed (neither refuting). The study is Beier & Oderkerk, "Closed letter counters impair recognition," Applied Ergonomics 101:103709 (PubMed 35217404). Verified abstract: "To identify effects of open and closed apertures on the letters 'a', 'c', 'e', 'r', 's', 't' and 'f', we ran an experiment using the serif font Pyke... present a letter either with or without flankers in the parafovea at 2° eccentricity. The findings showed that participants had more trouble identifying the letter if it was set in a font variation with closed apertures." This confirms the claim's direction (open apertures aid recognition), its peripheral/parafoveal ("glance-like") framing (2° eccentricity, flanked/unflanked, short exposure), and includes letters a,c,e,r,s. CORRECTIONS for the skill: (1) the paper is dated 2022 (online Feb 22, 2022; vol. May 2022), NOT 2021 — Typotheque's "2021" is an off-by-one error, though its link points to the correct 2022 ScienceDirect article (S0003687022000321); no genuine 2021 Beier-Oderkerk aperture paper exists (the 2021 papers cover letter spacing/width and stroke contrast). (2) The study tested SEVEN letters (a,c,e,r,s,t,f); the claim/Typotheque cite five, omitting t and f — but the five named were among those tested and benefited, so the subset is incomplete, not false. Substantive science (apertures drive letter disambiguation under brief peripheral viewing → supports small-size/glance legibility) is sound and merits [STRONG]. Recommend the skill cite "Beier & Oderkerk 2022, Applied Ergonomics 101:103709" with the full 7-letter set rather than the secondary source's "2021 / a,c,e,r,s."
*Counter-source:* PubMed 35217404 / ScienceDirect S0003687022000321 — confirms the substance but corrects the date to 2022 and the letter set to a,c,e,r,s,t,f (7 letters).

#### V236 `ad90136b` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The face's core design goal is letterform disambiguation — making each character unique and pulling apart confusable shapes (its specimens show pairs like B/8, O/0, 1/I/i/l, p/q). This is direct design rationale for topic (2): disambiguated digits/letters to reduce reading errors."

**Source:** https://fonts.google.com/specimen/Atkinson+Hyperlegible (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "special design principles to differentiate characters and make each one unique"

*Evidence:* Both sub-claims verify against the primary source (Braille Institute, brailleinstitute.org/freefont, which Google Fonts mirrors). DESIGN RATIONALE: The supporting quote "special design principles to differentiate characters and make each one unique" is verbatim source language. The page lists explicit disambiguation principles: "Unambiguous Letterforms," "Clear Uprights" (similarly-shaped uprights made distinctly different), "Distinct Pairs," "Spurs and Tails ... increase differentiation." Corroborated independently by Adobe, Google Material Design (m3.material.io blog), Dezeen, and PRINT Magazine — all describe the font's purpose as maximizing character distinction for low-vision readers. SPECIMEN PAIRS: The Braille Institute specimen explicitly shows B8, O0, 1Iil, EF, pq — the claim's list (B/8, O/0, 1/I/i/l, p/q) is an exact subset. SCOPING IS HONEST: the claim says "design RATIONALE for ... to reduce reading errors," i.e. stated design intent, not proven efficacy. This matters because Atkinson Hyperlegible is widely noted to lack peer-reviewed legibility trials (it won Fast Company's Innovation by Design award, not a controlled study). The claim does not overreach into [STRONG] empirical proof — it correctly frames the font as design-intent evidence for topic (2), which the designer's own specimen authoritatively supports. Current (2019 font, adopted into Material Design 2025), primary-source-backed, appropriately scoped.
*Counter-source:* No credible source disputes the design intent. The one legitimate qualifier — that disambiguation EFFICACY (error reduction) lacks peer-reviewed proof — does not refute this claim because the claim asserts design rationale, not proven efficacy. Were it cited as [STRONG] empirical evidence of error reduction, it would be refutable; as design-intent [CONVENTION/rationale] it stands.

#### V237 `ada0178c` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[STRONG] Text is read at maximum (fluent) speed across a 10-fold 'fluent range' of angular x-height from approximately 0.2° to 2°, corresponding to physical x-heights of 1.4 mm (≈4 pt) to 14 mm (≈40 pt) at 40 cm. The speed-vs-size curve is a broad plateau with a sharp decline below the CPS and only a gradual decline for very large print — so once text clears the floor, making it bigger yields no reading-speed gain."

**Source:** https://www.researchgate.net/publication/51558557_Does_Print_Size_Matter_for_Reading_A_Review_of_Findings_from_Vision_Science_and_Typography (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "There is a 10-fold range of print size from approximately 0.2- to 2- for which people with normal vision can achieve maximum reading speed. This range corresponds to x-heights from 1.4 mm (4 points) to 14 mm (40 points) at a reading distance of 40 cm. We refer to this as the ﬂuent range of print size... the speed vs. print size curves in Figure 2 demonstrate a broad plateau for intermediate character sizes for which reading speed is fairly constant, a sharp decline in reading speeds for smaller characters, and a more gradual decline for very large characters."

*Evidence:* The claim is a near-verbatim, faithful restatement of the primary source (Legge & Bigelow 2011, "Does print size matter for reading?", Journal of Vision / ARVO — peer-reviewed, highly cited; both authors are named in the [STRONG] tier of the research criteria). Every quantitative element checks out against the supporting quote and independent corroboration:

(1) "10-fold fluent range, 0.2° to 2°" — confirmed verbatim by the ARVO journal page and a 2020 PMC review ("The fluent range extends over a factor of 10 in angular print size (x-height) from approximately 0.2° to 2°").
(2) "x-heights 1.4 mm (4 pt) to 14 mm (40 pt) at 40 cm" — confirmed verbatim ("the corresponding physical x-heights are 1.4 mm (4 points) and 14 mm (40 points)" at 40 cm). The slightly unconventional points-as-length-of-x-height usage is the paper's own wording, not a distortion by the claimant. Geometry checks: x-height at 0.2°/40cm ≈ 1.40 mm; 1.40 mm ≈ 3.97 pt.
(3) "Broad plateau, sharp decline below CPS, gradual decline for very large print" — matches the quote exactly and is the canonical MNREAD reading-speed-vs-print-size curve (reading speed rises to the Critical Print Size, then plateaus at Maximum Reading Speed; declines sharply for smaller, gradually for larger).
(4) "Once text clears the floor, bigger yields no reading-speed gain" — a sound inference from the plateau ("reading speed is fairly constant" / "levels off at MRS for print sizes larger than the CPS"); the claim also correctly preserves the slight downside at very large sizes ("only a gradual decline for very large print").

Disconfirming search turned up no credible contradiction. The one "dispute" in the literature is purely about the algorithm for fitting CPS from MNREAD curves (which candidate plateau to select), not about the existence of the plateau or the fluent range. Source quality matches the [STRONG] tag: foundational, stable vision-science finding (2011 but not in a fast-moving field; MNREAD framework remains standard), peer-reviewed, not marketing.

One scope caveat for the skill (does NOT refute the claim as written): this is a finding about CONTINUOUS READING SPEED of prose, whereas chart/table typography is mostly single-glance recognition of small labels and isolated numerals — so the CPS floor is a lower bound for legibility tasks, not a license to set chart text at 4 pt. The claim itself does not make that leap; it states the reading-speed finding accurately.
*Counter-source:* No genuine counter-source found. Corroborating: jov.arvojournals.org/article.aspx?articleid=2191906 (primary); pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9357187/ and PMC4961000 (MNREAD plateau/CPS). The only "dispute" (CPS-fitting algorithm selection) concerns measurement methodology, not the claim's substance.

#### V238 `ae5a0108` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[STRONG] The critical print size (CPS) — the smallest print below which reading speed declines sharply — has a consensus value of 0.2° x-height (visual angle) for normally sighted readers; at a 40 cm viewing distance this equals roughly a 9 pt Times New Roman body. The CPS varies across individuals/fonts/methods over about 0.15°–0.3°. This sets an evidence-based legibility floor for small chart/axis/table text."

**Source:** https://www.researchgate.net/publication/51558557_Does_Print_Size_Matter_for_Reading_A_Review_of_Findings_from_Vision_Science_and_Typography (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "There is a smallest print size below which reading speed begins to decline sharply, termed the critical print size (CPS). The CPS typically lies in the range from about 0.15- to 0.3- depending on the individual, stimulus factors such as font... Across studies, a consensus value for the critical print size for normally sighted readers is 0.2- x-height (Legge, 2007). At a viewing distance of 40 cm (16 inches), Times New Roman type, with an x-height fraction of 0.45 and a visual x-height of 0.2-, is equivalent to a physical body size of 9 points."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and accurate. The quote is from Legge & Bigelow (2011), "Does Print Size Matter for Reading?", a peer-reviewed review in Journal of Vision (confirmed at jov.arvojournals.org) — an appropriate [STRONG] source (Legge = leading vision scientist; Bigelow = renowned typographer). The quote directly supports every element: CPS = smallest print below which reading speed declines sharply; consensus 0.2 deg x-height for normal vision; range ~0.15-0.3 deg by individual/font/method; at 40 cm, Times New Roman (x-height fraction 0.45) at 0.2 deg = 9 pt body. Independent searches corroborate all of it: multiple sources confirm foveal CPS ~0.2 deg (one gives 0.16 deg fovea rising with eccentricity), fluent reading range 0.2-2 deg, and the 40 cm -> 1.4 mm x-height conversion. Math checks: 0.2 deg at 400 mm = ~1.40 mm x-height; /0.45 = 3.1 mm body = ~8.8 pt ~ 9 pt. Not outdated (CPS is stable vision science; 2020 PNAS Atilgan/Xiong/Legge refines but does not overturn the 0.2 deg lower bound). No marketing/cherry-picking. Two caveats the skill should note but that do NOT refute the empirical core: (a) CPS is measured on continuous/fluent reading (MNREAD/RSVP), whereas chart numbers are often single-glance, for which the absolute acuity threshold is lower (~0.1 deg) and CPS acts as a conservative comfort floor — so using it as a floor errs safe; (b) the "9 pt" is Times-New-Roman-specific — chart fonts with higher x-height ratios (Inter ~0.55, IBM Plex) reach 0.2 deg at fewer points, making the body-size floor font-dependent (the claim correctly attributes 9 pt to TNR). The "evidence-based legibility floor for small chart text" application is a reasonable CONVENTION layered on the STRONG empirical fact; the claim already hedges with the 0.15-0.3 deg range.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found. Closest qualifier is Atilgan, Xiong & Legge 2020 (PNAS, https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2007514117), which refines print-size/display-size constraints but reaffirms the 0.2 deg fluent-reading lower bound rather than disputing it. PMC9357187 (Digital Reading with Low Vision) also corroborates the 0.2 deg figure.

#### V239 `ae5f2f89` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The CSS value tabular-nums (OpenType tnum) selects figures that are all the same width, allowing numbers to be easily aligned like in tables — the mechanical basis for using tabular figures in columns of numbers, axes, and data tables."

**Source:** https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/font-variant-numeric (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "tabular-nums ... activating the set of figures where numbers are all of the same size, allowing them to be easily aligned like in tables"

*Evidence:* Claim verified — well-supported, current, source quality matches a mechanical/definitional claim.

1) QUOTE SUPPORT (no overreach): MDN (verbatim) defines `tabular-nums` as activating "the set of figures where numbers are all of the same size, allowing them to be easily aligned like in tables," and states it "corresponds to the OpenType value `tnum`." The contrasting `proportional-nums` = `pnum` = "numbers are not all of the same size." The claim restates this faithfully. The claim's word "width" is actually MORE precise than MDN's loose "same size" — and is the technically correct term (advance width), confirmed below.

2) INDEPENDENT CORROBORATION (primary spec): Microsoft's registered OpenType feature spec (learn.microsoft.com, features_pt) defines `tnum` as replacing "figure glyphs set on proportional widths with corresponding glyphs set on uniform (tabular) widths" via a GSUB lookup. PreussType/Type Network/RWT.io all agree: every digit 0–9 gets identical advance width (e.g. "1111" occupies the same horizontal space as "2340"), which is exactly what makes columns line up vertically. This is the "mechanical basis" the claim asserts.

3) SOURCE QUALITY vs CLAIM STRENGTH: This is a definitional/mechanical claim about what a CSS+OpenType feature does, not an empirical/perceptual claim. MDN (primary CSS docs) plus the Microsoft OpenType spec (the normative registry for the feature) are exactly the right authority tier; no peer-reviewed perception study is needed for "tnum yields equal-width digits." Not marketing, not a forum, not cherry-picked.

4) NO CONTRADICTION FOUND: Every source searched agrees; none dispute or qualify the core mechanism. The only minor caveat in the spec ("tabular widths will generally, but not always, be the default") concerns which figures a font ships as default — it does NOT contradict the claim, which is about what `tnum` selects when invoked.

5) CURRENCY: OpenType `tnum` and CSS `font-variant-numeric` are stable, long-standing standards; no outdating risk.

Caveat for skill phrasing (not a refutation): `tnum` guarantees equal advance WIDTHS, which gives integer/right-aligned column alignment; it does NOT by itself align decimal points or handle thousands separators — that needs decimal alignment / monospaced layout. The claim doesn't assert otherwise.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found. Microsoft OpenType spec (learn.microsoft.com/typography/opentype/spec/features_pt), PreussType, Type Network, and RWT.io all independently confirm tnum = uniform/tabular advance widths for column alignment. The only nuance (tabular often but not always the font default) does not contradict the claim.

#### V240 `ae76d718` — **REFUTED** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Letterspacing (tracking) — adding white space between letters — is the single most important attribute for improving ease of reading, per multiple studies; relevant to keeping small chart/axis/table text loosely spaced."

**Source:** https://www.typotheque.com/articles/sans-for-small-text-sizes (secondary)
**Supporting quote:** "It has been confirmed by numerous studies that tracking fonts, or adding more white space between letters, is the single most important attribute that contributes to significantly improved ease of reading."

*Evidence:* The claim faithfully paraphrases the Typotheque quote, but the quote is a superlative ("single most important attribute for improving ease of reading") that is (a) sourced only to foundry marketing and (b) contradicted by primary reading research. SOURCE QUALITY: typotheque.com/articles/sans-for-small-text-sizes is a type-foundry article explicitly promoting Typotheque's own "Zed Text/Display" small-size family; the "single most important" claim cites only a vague uncited "numerous studies" with no named study in that passage — insufficient for a superlative claim about reading science. CONTRADICTING PEER-REVIEWED EVIDENCE: (1) Chung (2002), Invest. Ophthalmol. Vis. Sci., "The effect of letter spacing on reading speed in central and peripheral vision" (PubMed 11923275) — reading speed peaks NEAR standard spacing and decreases for both narrower and WIDER spacing; increased spacing beyond standard does NOT improve reading speed in central or peripheral vision, despite reducing crowding. (2) "Wider Letter-Spacing Facilitates Word Processing but Impairs Reading Rates of Fast Readers" (Frontiers in Psychology, 2020, PMC7090332) — wider spacing IMPAIRED normal adult reading rate (209.9 → 200.4 wpm; one fast reader lost 27 wpm); single-word processing sped up but whole-text reading slowed due to more fixations/return sweeps. (3) Multiple studies report no speed/accuracy benefit for typically developing readers grade 3+. Genuine benefit of extra spacing is confined to specific populations/conditions — dyslexia, central-vision loss, very small/peripheral text (crowding) — not a universal "single most important" reading lever. The practical gloss "keep small chart text loosely spaced" can backfire, since over-tracking measurably slows normal readers; the defensible rule is ADEQUATE (non-cramped), near-default spacing, not loose tracking.
*Counter-source:* Chung S. (2002), "The effect of letter spacing on reading speed in central and peripheral vision," Invest. Ophthalmol. Vis. Sci. (PubMed 11923275); "Wider Letter-Spacing Facilitates Word Processing but Impairs Reading Rates of Fast Readers," Frontiers in Psychology 2020 (PMC7090332)

#### V241 `ae8d950e` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The CSS value tabular-nums (OpenType tnum) selects figures that are all the same width, allowing numbers to be easily aligned like in tables — the mechanical basis for using tabular figures in columns of numbers, axes, and data tables."

**Source:** https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/font-variant-numeric (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "tabular-nums ... activating the set of figures where numbers are all of the same size, allowing them to be easily aligned like in tables"

*Evidence:* Claim is fully supported and traces to the primary authority behind MDN. Microsoft's OpenType spec (features_pt, OpenType 1.9.1, updated 2024-05-29) defines tnum: "Replaces figure glyphs set on proportional widths with corresponding glyphs set on uniform (tabular) widths... Tabular figures are treated as equal-width numerals so that they align vertically, which increases the readability of financial type information." This matches the claim exactly. Notably the claim's wording ("all the same width") is MORE precise than the MDN quote it cites ("same size"); width/advance-width is the technically correct property, confirmed by the spec's "uniform (tabular) widths" and "equal-width numerals." The CSS mapping is correct: font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums activates the tnum OpenType feature (per MDN and Adobe CSS OpenType syntax docs). The extension beyond the quote's "tables" to "axes and data tables" is standard established data-viz practice (FT, Datawrapper/Lisa Charlotte Muth, IBM Carbon all specify tabular figures for number columns and chart numerics) and is not an overreach. No contradicting source exists — this is uncontested foundational typography. Source quality (Microsoft OpenType registry + MDN) matches the claim's strength, which is a basic mechanical fact, not an extraordinary claim. Current, not marketing, not speculation. Tag [STRONG] as a mechanical/normative fact. One trivial caveat that does not refute: tnum affects digit glyphs (figures); it is a no-op in already-monospaced designs, and a font must actually ship the feature for it to take effect — but the claim is about what the feature does, which is correctly stated.

#### V242 `aedf02d7` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "For simple text read in central vision (low error rate — the normal case for a sighted reader scanning a chart), letter recognition itself is the rate-limiting bottleneck for reading speed. This grounds the typography rule that making individual letterforms/digits easy to recognize at small sizes (open apertures, disambiguated forms) is what governs fast glance-reading, not stylistic preference."

**Source:** https://legge.psych.umn.edu/sites/legge.psych.umn.edu/files/2020-08/psychophysics_of_reading._xx._linking_letter_recognition_to_reading_speed_in_central_and_peripheral_vision_legge_mansfield_chung_2001.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "When the error rate is low, typically true for simple texts in central vision, letter recognition forms the bottleneck for reading speed."

*Evidence:* Quote verified VERBATIM in the cited primary source. Legge, Mansfield & Chung (2001), Vision Research, "Psychophysics of reading: XX. Linking letter recognition to reading speed in central and peripheral vision" (ScienceDirect S0042698900002959) contains the exact sentence: "When the error rate is low, typically true for simple texts in central vision, letter recognition forms the bottleneck for reading speed." The claim's first sentence is a faithful paraphrase preserving all three boundary conditions (low error, simple text, central vision). Source quality matches [STRONG]: Gordon Legge is the foremost reading-psychophysics researcher (author of Psychophysics of Reading in Normal and Low Vision, 2007); peer-reviewed primary source. Not outdated — the visual-span framework remains the dominant account 2001-2020s.

Two qualifications that DO NOT refute: (1) The full model attributes reading speed to the VISUAL SPAN (letters recognizable per glance), of which letter recognition is the determinant; "letter recognition itself" is a mild simplification, but the quote itself uses that exact framing for the low-error/central-vision regime. The Pelli (2007) "uncrowded span"/crowding refinement and the disconfirming Chung (2007) finding (training crowded-letter recognition reduced crowding but did NOT improve reading speed) concern the MECHANISM limiting the span in PERIPHERAL vision — they refine, not contradict, the central-vision claim being made. (2) The finding is about continuous-prose reading SPEED; applying it to glance-reading short chart labels/numerals is an extrapolation — but a conventional, reasonable one, and the claim properly hedges it as "grounds the typography rule," not "proves." The typographic specifics (open apertures, disambiguated forms) are a [CONVENTION]-layer inference; the paper does not test typeface features and the claim does not assert it does. The claim is well-supported, current, and accurately sourced.
*Counter-source:* Pelli & Tillman / Pelli 2007 "uncrowded span" and Chung (2007) "Learning to Identify Crowded Letters: Does It Improve Reading Speed?" (PMC2134936) refine the mechanism (crowding limits the visual span) and show a training intervention that reduced crowding did not speed reading — but these target the peripheral-vision/visual-span mechanism, not the central-vision low-error bottleneck claim; they qualify rather than refute.

#### V243 `af40190d` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The long-standing typographic CONVENTION that large counters/apertures aid legibility was the explicit hypothesis under test here — this paper is the evidence that converts that convention toward [STRONG]."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687022000321 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "An often-repeated piece of advice when choosing fonts for great legibility is to use fonts with large counters and apertures."

*Evidence:* Beier & Oderkerk (2022), Applied Ergonomics 101:103709, "Closed letter counters impair recognition" — read in full from the primary PDF. The paper makes the convention its EXPLICIT hypothesis: abstract states the "often-repeated piece of advice... use fonts with large counters and apertures," and the aim (p.1) is "We hypothesise that closed apertures will cause a negative impact on performances." It is a controlled, single-variable-isolated study (serif font Pyke; 7 aperture letters a,c,e,r,s,t,f; Open/Medium/Closed variants; flanked/unflanked at 2° eccentricity; N=18). Result: Closed apertures gave significantly lower glance-recognition than Open in both Flanked (p=.035, d=0.74) and Unflanked (p<.001, d=1.34) conditions, replicating Beier & Larson 2010 and Beier et al. 2018. The Conclusion explicitly states: "our findings confirm the often-repeated tenet within the design community that closed apertures hamper recognition," and recommends excluding closed-aperture fonts for glance reading. This is exactly "the evidence that converts that convention toward [STRONG]." Source quality matches: primary, peer-reviewed, reputable journal, leading legibility researcher, variable isolated, 2022 (current). Two legitimate scope caveats (NOT refutations): (1) Medium-vs-Open was non-significant (p=.999, d≈0.0–0.23) — the data support "avoid fully CLOSED apertures," not "bigger counter is always better"; a skill rule should say avoid closed apertures, not maximize. (2) The effect is on glance/letter RECOGNITION, not continuous reading speed — Bernard et al. 2016 (cited in the paper) found a peripheral-optimized open font improved letter/word recognition but NOT eye-mediated reading speed. But the claim is scoped to LEGIBILITY (glance recognition), which is precisely the regime the study confirms and precisely the regime that matters for small chart/axis/tick/label text. The claim's "toward [STRONG]" wording is appropriately hedged and accurate.
*Counter-source:* Bernard, Aguilar & Castet 2016 (PLoS One e0152506) and Beier et al. 2021 "Readability Research" only QUALIFY (recognition gains don't transfer to continuous reading speed), they do not contradict the legibility-scoped claim; the paper's own non-significant Medium-vs-Open comparison bounds it to "avoid closed," not "maximize aperture."

#### V244 `af9fb55a` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The long-standing typographic CONVENTION that large counters/apertures aid legibility was the explicit hypothesis under test here — this paper is the evidence that converts that convention toward [STRONG]."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003687022000321 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "An often-repeated piece of advice when choosing fonts for great legibility is to use fonts with large counters and apertures."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED, claim stands. Source is real and primary: Beier & Oderkerk (2022), "Closed letter counters impair recognition," Applied Ergonomics 101:103709 — a peer-reviewed controlled psychophysical experiment (lead author Sofie Beier, a named legibility authority), confirmed across PubMed (35217404), ScienceDirect (S0003687022000321), the Aarhus publisher repository, and the Readability Consortium PDF. (1) Quote support: the supporting quote is the paper's actual opening framing, and the study was explicitly built to test it — three aperture variants (Open/Medium/Closed) of the serif Pyke on letters a,c,e,r,s,t,f, shown alone or with parafoveal flankers at 2 degrees eccentricity. So "the convention was the explicit hypothesis under test" is accurate. (2) Finding is positive/confirmatory: closed apertures significantly impaired recognition vs open (and vs medium for unflanked letters), supporting the convention, not contradicting it. (3) Source quality matches the skill's own [STRONG] definition (peer-reviewed/controlled research), and the claim hedges with "toward [STRONG]," appropriate for a single study. (4) Current (2022). (5) Not marketing. Two QUALIFICATIONS to fold into the skill (refine, don't refute): (a) the paper manipulated aperture OPENNESS, not counter SIZE — it conflates "counters/apertures," but that conflation is the paper's own (title says "counters," intro says "counters and apertures"); strictly the open-aperture half of the convention is what is tested. (b) Medium-vs-Open apertures did NOT reach significance in either condition, so the evidence backs "avoid closed apertures," not "bigger is monotonically better"; the claim does not assert monotonicity, so this is a boundary note. No contradicting evidence found.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found. Closest qualifier is within the paper itself (Beier & Oderkerk 2022): Medium-vs-Open aperture difference failed to reach significance in both Flanked and Unflanked conditions — qualifies the strength to "open > closed" rather than "larger is always better," but does not refute.

#### V245 `afd81ffa` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "[STRONG] The critical print size (CPS) — the smallest print below which reading speed declines sharply — has a consensus value of 0.2° x-height (visual angle) for normally sighted readers; at a 40 cm viewing distance this equals roughly a 9 pt Times New Roman body. The CPS varies across individuals/fonts/methods over about 0.15°–0.3°. This sets an evidence-based legibility floor for small chart/axis/table text."

**Source:** https://www.researchgate.net/publication/51558557_Does_Print_Size_Matter_for_Reading_A_Review_of_Findings_from_Vision_Science_and_Typography (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "There is a smallest print size below which reading speed begins to decline sharply, termed the critical print size (CPS). The CPS typically lies in the range from about 0.15- to 0.3- depending on the individual, stimulus factors such as font... Across studies, a consensus value for the critical print size for normally sighted readers is 0.2- x-height (Legge, 2007). At a viewing distance of 40 cm (16 inches), Times New Roman type, with an x-height fraction of 0.45 and a visual x-height of 0.2-, is equivalent to a physical body size of 9 points."

*Evidence:* The factual core is directly quoted and independently confirmed from a strong peer-reviewed primary source: Legge & Bigelow (2011), "Does Print Size Matter for Reading?", Journal of Vision 11(5):8 (Legge = author of Psychophysics of Reading; Bigelow = type designer). Web search independently reproduces the paper's language: "a consensus value for the CPS for normally sighted readers is 0.2 degrees (12 min-arc)"; reading speed is "roughly constant in a ten-fold range between 0.2 and 2.0 degrees... below 0.2 degrees reading speed falls quickly"; and "the x-height of 9 point Times Roman at a reading distance of 40 cm subtends approximately 0.20 degrees." Conversion math checks out (0.2 deg at 400mm = 1.40mm x-height; /0.45 x-height fraction = 3.10mm em = ~8.8pt). The 0.15-0.3 deg range is taken verbatim from the supplied quote and matches the paper. [STRONG] tag is appropriate for peer-reviewed vision science. The only weakness is an application nuance, not a factual error: CPS is defined for CONTINUOUS reading speed (MNREAD/RSVP passages), not glance reading of isolated chart labels, and the paper does not study chart text — so "floor for chart/axis/table text" is an extrapolation, and 9pt is actually a permissive floor well below practitioner minimums like ONS 14px. The claim frames CPS honestly as a conservative legibility floor and represents all numbers correctly, so this caveat does not refute. No source disputes the 0.2 deg consensus.
*Counter-source:* No contradicting source found. Closest qualifier: the paper's own emphasis is that print size matters less than assumed above CPS (fluent range 0.2-2.0 deg), and CPS-as-floor is derived from continuous reading, not glance reading of chart text (PNAS 2020 "Reconciling print-size and display-size constraints on reading" extends but does not contradict the CPS framework).

#### V246 `afed8962` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "lining-nums (OpenType lnum) selects figures that all sit on the baseline, while oldstyle-nums (onum) selects figures where some digits (3, 4, 7, 9) have descenders — distinguishing lining figures (default for data) from old-style figures (suited to running text)."

**Source:** https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/font-variant-numeric (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "lining-nums ... activating the set of figures where numbers are all lying on the baseline ... oldstyle-nums ... activating the set of figures where some numbers, like 3, 4, 7, 9 have descenders"

*Evidence:* The claim accurately restates the MDN quote and is corroborated by multiple independent authoritative typography sources. CORE FACTS CONFIRMED: (1) Lining figures (OpenType lnum / CSS lining-nums) sit on the baseline, are uniform in height matching the capitals, and are the standard for tables/data/all-caps — confirmed by Type Network, RWT.io, Fonts.com (MyFonts), and Wikipedia's "Text figures." (2) Old-style figures (OpenType onum / CSS oldstyle-nums) have some digits descending below the baseline and are designed to blend with lowercase letters in running prose — same sources. (3) The lnum/onum CSS-to-OpenType feature-tag mapping in the claim is correct and matches the OpenType feature registry and Adobe/MDN docs. (4) The lining=data / old-style=running-text convention is universal across sources, not disputed by any credible one. SOURCE QUALITY: MDN is authoritative for CSS/OpenType web features; this is a stable, decades-old, basic typographic fact (Bringhurst, Butterick, OpenType spec), not an extraordinary claim — so MDN amply suffices. Not outdated, not marketing. MINOR IMPRECISION (does NOT refute): The canonical descending set in old-style figures is 3, 4, 5, 7, 9 (with 6, 8 ascending; 0, 1, 2 at x-height); MDN and the claim list "3, 4, 7, 9," omitting 5. But the exact descending digits are typeface-dependent and the claim hedges with "some digits," so this is a defensible simplification, not an error worth killing the claim over. The SKILL should state "some digits (commonly 3, 4, 5, 7, 9) descend" rather than reproducing MDN's incomplete list verbatim.
*Counter-source:* No credible contradicting source found. Closest caveat: Wikipedia "Text figures," Type Network, and Fonts.com give the descending set as 3,4,5,7,9 — meaning MDN's "3,4,7,9" omits 5 — but this is a minor, typeface-dependent imprecision, not a refutation of the lining-vs-oldstyle distinction.

### Typography & cognitive / mental load — what reduces the effort of reading

#### V247 `a07e4dff` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Processing difficulty (disfluency) is not uniformly beneficial: experiencing difficulty can be used either as a signal to invest more effort ('try harder') OR as an avoidance signal to disengage/switch away ('avoid and switch'). This bounds the disfluency-aids claim — the same hard-to-read input can prompt avoidance rather than deeper engagement."

**Source:** https://www.researchgate.net/publication/49732555_If_it's_hard_to_read_try_harder_Processing_fluency_as_signal_for_effort_adjustments (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Experiencing difficulties during information processing can either be used as signal for the increased need of cognitive effort ("try harder"), or as avoidance signal for future action selection ("avoid and switch")."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported. The supporting quote is a VERBATIM match to the abstract of Dreisbach & Fischer (2011), "If it's hard to read… try harder! Processing fluency as signal for effort adjustments," Psychological Research 75(5):376-383, DOI 10.1007/s00426-010-0319-y (PubMed 21210144) — a peer-reviewed Springer journal reporting 3 experiments. The abstract states verbatim: "Experiencing difficulties during information processing can either be used as signal for the increased need of cognitive effort ('try harder'), or as avoidance signal for future action selection ('avoid and switch')." The claim restates this faithfully and uses it only to BOUND the disfluency-aids claim, not to assert avoidance always dominates — so it is not an overreach. Adversarial check: this paper's own DATA supported the "try harder" side (low fluency triggered effort mobilization), so a skeptic could allege cherry-picking. That refutation fails because (a) the claim quotes the paper's framing, not its result, and presents both sides evenhandedly; (b) the "avoid/disengage" side is independently and directly demonstrated by Song & Schwarz (2008) "If it's hard to read, it's hard to do" (Psychological Science, PubMed 19000208) — disfluent fonts raised predicted effort/time (e.g., ~59-83% longer time estimates) and lowered willingness to engage; (c) Alter & Oppenheimer (2009) "Uniting the Tribes of Fluency" (PubMed 19638628) establishes disfluency as generally aversive (negative affect, frustration, downgrading). Source quality matches claim strength (moderate theoretical-bounding statement); current (dual-signal model remains standard); not marketing. The bounding inference correctly limits the Diemand-Yauman disfluency-aids-learning claim.
*Counter-source:* No credible source contradicts the dual-signal model; the strongest adversarial angle (the cited paper's data favored "try harder") is neutralized because the claim quotes the paper's framing and the avoidance side is corroborated independently by Song & Schwarz (2008) and Alter & Oppenheimer (2009).

#### V248 `a11a653e` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The broader literature on disfluent fonts and memory is mixed and unreliable, with results ranging from no effect on correct memory to outright memory costs, contradicting the original positive claims."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9733772/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "the effects of disfluent fonts on memory are mixed...others produced no effects on correct memory...and in some cases, have produced a memory cost."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported and current. Source = Huff, Maxwell & Mitchell (2022), "Distinctive Sans Forgetica font does not benefit memory accuracy in the DRM paradigm," Cognitive Research: Principles and Implications (PMC9733772), a peer-reviewed primary article. The supporting quote is reproduced nearly verbatim from its introduction: "the effects of disfluent fonts on memory are mixed... others produced no effects on correct memory... and in some cases, have produced a memory cost." "Mixed" is the article's own term, so the claim does not overreach. Independent corroboration (sought specifically to contradict, found none against): (a) Xie et al. (2018) meta-analysis — perceptual disfluency "produced no effect on later recall relative to non-distorted controls"; (b) the source's own 4 experiments (~300 participants) found Sans Forgetica had no effect on correct or false memory vs. Arial; (c) multiple independent failed replications of Diemand-Yauman et al. (2011): Cushing & Bodner 2022, Geller et al. 2020, Wetzler et al. 2021, Taylor et al. 2020, Roberts et al. 2023. To refute the claim one would need credible evidence that disfluent fonts reliably AID memory — no such evidence surfaced; the entire literature trend supports the "mixed/unreliable, contradicting original positive claims" characterization. Source quality (peer-reviewed + meta-analysis + replications) exceeds the claim's strength; not outdated; not marketing.
*Counter-source:* No credible counter-source found. Searched for evidence that disfluent fonts reliably aid memory; instead found only corroborating nulls/costs (Xie et al. 2018 meta-analysis; Wetzler et al. 2021; Geller et al. 2020; Cushing & Bodner 2022; Roberts et al. 2023). The only nuance is that some scholars frame the picture as "reliably null" rather than "mixed," but the claim's own wording (range from no effect to costs vs. original positives) matches the source's term "mixed" and does not misrepresent it.

#### V249 `a153c9ef` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "A 2020 peer-reviewed review (Thiessen, Beier & Keage, The Design Journal 23(5):797-815) concludes that the evidence on whether harder-to-read 'disfluent' typography improves learning is unresolved — i.e., the disfluency-aids-learning claim (from Diemand-Yauman/Oppenheimer/Vaughan 2011) is NOT robustly established."

**Source:** https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/14606925.2020.1810434 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "What is evident is that the discussion about whether or not disfluent reading materials support learning is far from resolved."

*Evidence:* Claim fully verified. The paper exists and is cited correctly: Thiessen, M., Beier, S. & Keage, H. (2020) "A Review of the Cognitive Effects of Disfluent Typography on Functional Reading," The Design Journal, vol. 23, pp. 797-815, DOI 10.1080/14606925.2020.1810434 — confirmed independently by SCIRP, Monash University research portal, ResearchGate, Elsevier Pure, and Taylor & Francis. The review's stated conclusion is that "the discussion about whether or not disfluent reading materials support learning is far from resolved," which exactly matches the supporting quote and faithfully grounds the claim's paraphrase ("unresolved / NOT robustly established") — no overreach. Source quality is sufficient: peer-reviewed review co-authored by Sofie Beier (a named typography-legibility authority). Contrary evidence REINFORCES rather than refutes the claim — the disfluency/desirable-difficulty replication record is heavily mixed-to-negative: Yue et al. (2013) meta-analysis, Kuhl & Eitel (2016), Rummer et al. (2016), Weissgerber & Reinhard (2017), Wetzler et al. (2021, Sans Forgetica), and the Sans Forgetica DRM-paradigm failure all report null or negative effects, so "not robustly established" is conservative. Claim is current (2020, consistent with 2021-2023 replications). Only unconfirmed micro-detail is the issue number "(5)" — a trivial bibliographic point that does not affect the substance. No credible source disputes the "unresolved" framing.

#### V250 `a17bd9fd` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Low perceptual fluency (hard-to-read/degraded stimuli) acts as an aversive signal that triggers mobilization of cognitive effort even when there is no response conflict. This is the proposed mechanism behind 'disfluency makes you try harder' — but it is demonstrated at the level of trial-to-trial cognitive control in lab tasks, NOT memory/retention, so it does not by itself validate disfluency-aids-learning claims."

**Source:** https://www.researchgate.net/publication/49732555_If_it's_hard_to_read_try_harder_Processing_fluency_as_signal_for_effort_adjustments (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Low perceptual fluency, which is known to evoke negative affective reactions, triggers the mobilization of cognitive effort even in the absence of response conflicts."

*Evidence:* Claim is well-supported, correctly bounded, and source-matched. (1) The verbatim PubMed abstract (PMID 21210144, Dreisbach & Fischer 2011, Psychological Research, doi:10.1007/s00426-010-0319-y) states exactly: "low perceptual fluency, which is known to evoke negative affective reactions, triggers the mobilization of cognitive effort even in the absence of response conflicts" — directly backing the claim's first sentence, including the "aversive signal" framing ("an aversive signal (here: low fluency) is not only used for effort prediction but also for effort adjustments," building on Botvinick 2007). (2) The bounding is accurate: the study ran three experiments analyzing trial-to-trial Fluency(N)×Fluency(N-1) sequential interactions on a within-session conflict/number-judgment task, with NO memory or retention test. Independent confirmation: Oliveira et al. (PMC10585938, "Easy to process, hard to control") states this line of work concerns "trial-by-trial cognitive control adjustments... within a single experimental session... not long-term learning or memorization." (3) The interpretive bridge ("does not by itself validate disfluency-aids-learning") is conservative and consistent with the disfluency replication-failure literature; the one contested point (Oliveira et al. find fluency can impair control, complicating directionality) reinforces rather than refutes the caution. Source is peer-reviewed Springer, matching the modest claim strength. Minor quibble only: "the proposed mechanism" is slightly strong since Alter et al. (2007, metacognitive difficulty -> analytic reasoning) is the mechanism more commonly cited for the Diemand-Yauman learning study, but the claim hedges with "proposed" and the point stands. Tag this [STRONG] for the trial-level effort-mobilization mechanism; it does NOT support a [STRONG] memory/learning claim — exactly what the claim itself asserts.
*Counter-source:* Oliveira et al., "Easy to process, hard to control: Transient and sustained processing fluency impairs cognitive control adjustments to conflict" (PMC10585938) — confirms this line is within-session trial-level control, not memory; complicates the directional mechanism but does not refute the claim. PubMed PMID 21210144 verbatim abstract confirms quote and paradigm.

#### V251 `a1a67ddd` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Disfluency effects on learning do not generalize across the type of disfluency manipulation, the timing of the test, or the memory outcome measured — undercutting any blanket 'harder-to-read fonts improve retention' rule."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S095947521730107X (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "The findings show that disfluency effects are not generalizable across disfluency manipulations, test time points, and memory outcomes."

*Evidence:* SUPPORTED. Primary source = "Is disfluency desirable for learning?" (Learning and Instruction, 2017; ScienceDirect S095947521730107X), a peer-reviewed primary study comparing perceptual disfluency (hard-to-read font), lexical disfluency (20% scrambled letters), and original text at immediate vs. 2-week delayed tests. (1) Quote→claim is a faithful paraphrase: original states disfluency effects "are not generalizable across disfluency manipulations, test time points, and memory outcomes"; claim restates this exactly. The study found effects flipped by dimension — illegible font reduced forgetting (delayed benefit) while scrambled letters reduced short-term recall — so the "does not generalize" framing is the study's own. (2) The "undercuts a blanket 'harder fonts improve retention' rule" addendum is a bounded, well-corroborated inference, NOT an overreach: it says a *blanket* rule is unjustified, not that disfluency never helps. (3) Broader literature corroborates rather than contradicts: Xie, Zhou & Liu (2018) meta-analysis (25 studies, 3,135 participants, Educational Psychology Review) found NO effect of perceptual disfluency on recall (d=-0.01) or transfer (d=0.03), only reduced judgments of learning (d=-0.43) and increased study time (d=0.52), with no moderators for recall; Sans Forgetica failures (Wetzler et al. 2021) and Yue/Castel-line replications agree. The one critical rebuttal found (Weissgerber & Reinhard 2021, "Null and Void?") disputes the meta-analysis's coding methodology but does NOT establish a general positive disfluency effect, so it doesn't rescue a blanket rule. Source quality (strong peer-reviewed journal, primary study + meta-analytic backup) matches the claim's moderate strength; not outdated; not marketing. Adversarial angle considered: this study itself found a real positive delayed-recall effect for the illegible font, but that supports condition-specificity, not a blanket benefit — so the claim stands.
*Counter-source:* Weissgerber & Reinhard (2021) "Null and Void? Errors in Meta-analysis on Perceptual Disfluency" (Educational Psychology Review, PMC7854329) critiques the Xie et al. (2018) meta-analysis coding/methodology — but it does NOT demonstrate a general positive disfluency effect, so it does not contradict the non-generalizability claim. No credible source establishing a reliable blanket "harder fonts improve retention" effect was found.

#### V252 `a20b735b` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Across four experiments with nearly 300 participants, the disfluent/distinctive Sans Forgetica font produced no benefit to correct memory (recall or recognition) of studied DRM list items relative to a standard Arial control font."

**Source:** https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s41235-022-00448-9 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Sans Forgetica consistently failed to improve correct memory for studied items... Sans Forgetica was ineffective at reducing the DRM false memory illusion."

*Evidence:* Claim is verbatim-supported by the primary source. Huff & Maxwell (2022), "Distinctive Sans Forgetica font does not benefit memory accuracy in the DRM paradigm," Cognitive Research: Principles and Implications (peer-reviewed, Psychonomic Society/Springer, open-access; PMC9733772). Abstract states: "Across four experiments, which included nearly 300 participants, Sans Forgetica was found to have no impact on correct or false memory of DRM lists relative to a standard Arial control font, regardless of whether font type was manipulated within or between subjects or whether memory was assessed via free recall or recognition testing. Our results indicate that Sans Forgetica is ineffective for improving memory accuracy even when accounting for associative memory errors." Every element of the claim maps directly: four experiments, ~300 participants, no benefit to correct memory, recall AND recognition tested, Arial control, DRM paradigm. The supporting quote about reducing false memory is also confirmed (no impact on false memory either). No condition in the study produced a positive effect. Source strength matches claim strength (primary peer-reviewed study, 2022, current). Three refutation angles tried (quote overreach, contradicting sources, source quality) all failed.

#### V253 `a2ef857c` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Sans Forgetica did not reduce false memories in the DRM paradigm; false recall/recognition rates were equivalent across Sans Forgetica and Arial, so the harder-to-read font did not protect against associative memory errors."

**Source:** https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s41235-022-00448-9 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Sans Forgetica was ineffective at reducing the DRM false memory illusion."

*Evidence:* Claim fully supported by the primary peer-reviewed source: Huff & Maxwell (2022), "Distinctive Sans Forgetica font does not benefit memory accuracy in the DRM paradigm," Cognitive Research: Principles and Implications (DOI 10.1186/s41235-022-00448-9; open-access PMC9733772). Four experiments, ~300 participants, within- and between-subjects, free recall and recognition. Exact results, all non-significant: false RECALL Arial 0.27 vs Sans Forgetica 0.32 (Exp 1A, t<1) and 0.27 vs 0.31 (Exp 1B, t<1); false RECOGNITION 0.66 vs 0.65 (Exp 2A, t<1) and 0.70 vs 0.66 (Exp 2B, t(114)=1.09, p=0.28). Correct memory also equivalent across fonts. The claim that SF "did not reduce false memories / rates equivalent across SF and Arial / did not protect against associative memory errors" matches the data exactly; if anything SF's false-recall numbers run slightly higher (0.31-0.32 vs 0.27), so it certainly did not reduce false memory. Source is primary, peer-reviewed, recent (2022), open access — quality matches claim strength; not marketing (it disconfirms Sans Forgetica's marketing). No contradicting evidence found; this is itself the relevant primary study. Bounded note: finding is specific to the DRM associative-illusion paradigm and the Sans Forgetica font.

#### V254 `a33a146d` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "The headline causal claim under scrutiny: across two studies, the authors assert that making text harder to read with a disfluent font improved learning/retention, i.e. that disfluency acts as a 'desirable difficulty.' This is precisely the claim later large replication attempts (Yue/Castel; Eitel & Kühl) failed to confirm."

**Source:** https://www.researchgate.net/publication/47644206_Fortune_favors_the_Effects_of_disfluency_on_educational_outcomes (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "In two studies we showed that making the text disfluent by using a hard to read font improved learning... It appears as though perceptual disfluency can function as a desirable difficulty in education."

*Evidence:* Both components of the claim are well-supported by peer-reviewed primary sources. (1) Original-paper portion matches the quote: Diemand-Yauman, Oppenheimer & Vaughan (2011, Cognition) ran two studies (Study 1 lab; Study 2 high-school classrooms), both reporting disfluent fonts improved retention, framed as a "desirable difficulty" (confirmed via ERIC EJ908264, PubMed 21040910, ScienceDirect S001002771000226X). (2) Replication-failure portion is documented: Yue, Castel & Bjork (2013, Memory & Cognition, PubMed 22976883) found disfluency affected metacognitive judgments but NOT memory; Eitel/Kühl et al. (2014, Applied Cognitive Psychology, doi 10.1002/acp.3004) found a benefit in Exp 1 that failed to replicate in Exps 2–4; "Fortune is fickle" (2015, Metacognition and Learning, doi 10.1007/s11409-015-9151-5) pooled 6 manuscripts/13 experiments/1000+ participants with null replications; Xie et al. (2018, Educational Psychology Review, doi 10.1007/s10648-018-9442-x) meta-analysis found null effects on recall (d=-0.01) and transfer (d=0.03), with only reduced judgments-of-learning (d=-0.43) and increased reading time (d=0.52). Adversarial checks: the 2018 meta-analysis was critiqued ("Null and Void?" 2021, doi 10.1007/s10648-020-09579-1) but only for coding/method errors, not for restoring a positive effect; later moderation studies find only narrow condition-specific benefits, which reinforce rather than refute the "failed to confirm the general effect" framing. No overreach of the quote, current literature, source quality matches claim strength. Correctly grounds "harder fonts aid memory" as a MYTH/non-replicable.
*Counter-source:* Yue, Castel & Bjork (2013) Memory & Cognition; Eitel, Kühl et al. (2014) Applied Cognitive Psychology; "Fortune is fickle" (2015) Metacognition and Learning; Xie et al. (2018) Educational Psychology Review meta-analysis — all CONFIRM the claim rather than refute it. The only counter to the meta-analysis ("Null and Void?" 2021) critiques its methodology but does not restore a positive disfluency-aids-learning effect.

#### V255 `a34288b4` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "In adults with age-related macular degeneration, doubling word/line spacing increased reading speed by ~26% with high-contrast text and ~46% with low-contrast text (both p<0.001), making spacing a measurable load-reducing lever for low-vision readers."

**Source:** https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0080325 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Double word/line spacing increased reading speed by approximately 26% (p < 0.001) with high contrast text, and by 46% (p < 0.001) with low contrast text."

*Evidence:* VERIFIED against primary source. Blackmore-Wright, Georgeson & Anderson (2013), "Enhanced Text Spacing Improves Reading Performance in Individuals with Macular Disease," PLOS ONE 8(11):e80325 (https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0080325; PMC3823704). Two independent fetches (PLOS direct + search summary/PMC) confirm: n=24 adults with binocular age-related macular degeneration (AMD); within-subjects two-way repeated-measures ANOVA; reading 51-word passages at 87.5% and 17.5% contrast. Double word/line spacing increased reading speed ~26% (p<0.001) at high contrast and ~46% (p<0.001) at low contrast, and "more than halved" reading errors. Claim is a near-verbatim restatement of the abstract with correct population, numbers, p-values, and direction (larger effect at low contrast). Properly bounded to low-vision/AMD readers, not generalized to average readers. Bounding caveats that do NOT refute: (a) outcomes were reading speed + error rate, not a direct cognitive-load measure (NASA-TLX/pupillometry), so "load-reducing lever" is a reasonable speed-as-effort-proxy inference; (b) the combined effect is driven overwhelmingly by LINE spacing (paper reports line-space effect sizes ~15-20x word-space), though the claim mirrors the paper's "word/line" phrasing; (c) small n=24, AMD-specific. Source is peer-reviewed and matches the claim's strength; not outdated (stable low-vision reading finding, corroborated by later crowding work e.g. Nature Sci Rep 2018 "Word Mode").

#### V256 `a35e7116` — **upheld** · confidence: high
*Claim under review:* "Across four experiments (~300 participants), the disfluent Sans Forgetica font produced no improvement in correct or false memory relative to a standard Arial control font in the DRM false-memory paradigm."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9733772/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Sans Forgetica was found to have no impact on correct or false memory of DRM lists relative to a standard Arial control font."

*Evidence:* Claim is fully supported by the primary source: Huff, Maxwell & Mitchell (2022), "Distinctive Sans Forgetica font does not benefit memory accuracy in the DRM paradigm," Cognitive Research: Principles and Implications (peer-reviewed, Springer/Psychonomic Society; PMC9733772 and link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s41235-022-00448-9). The supporting quote is verbatim from the abstract: "Sans Forgetica was found to have no impact on correct or false memory of DRM lists relative to a standard Arial control font." Per-experiment Ns confirmed: Exp1A=43, Exp1B=94, Exp2A=45, Exp2B=116, total=298 ("nearly 300" ≈ "~300", accurate). Four experiments, DRM false-memory paradigm, Arial control — all confirmed. Finding held regardless of within- vs between-subject manipulation and free-recall vs recognition testing. Source is a 2022 primary peer-reviewed null-result replication (not marketing, not outdated, not cherry-picked), consistent with the broader replication-failure consensus on Sans Forgetica/disfluency. The only quibble — whether 1A/1B/2A/2B count as "four experiments" vs "two with sub-parts" — is moot because the paper itself frames them as four. No contradicting evidence found.
